en.abandonner:
Attested around 1100, ~abandonner~ is a denominal verb built on the Old French noun ~bandon~, drawn from the phrase ~mettre à bandon~, “to put at bandon.” ~Bandon~ meant “power, jurisdiction, discretion,” and derives from ~ban~, a term of Frankish origin (*~ban~ / *~bannan~, “proclamation, command, the authority to summon”). To place something ~à bandon~ was to leave it open to anyone's power or will — at the disposal of all. The Frankish ~ban~ is cognate with English ~ban~ and German ~Bann~.

The logic of the meaning is quietly elegant: if a thing is surrendered to everyone's discretion, its owner has, in effect, let it go — and so “to put at the disposal of all” became simply “to abandon, to forsake.” The reflexive ~s'abandonner~ kept a softer trace of the original, “to give oneself over” to an emotion or a person. English borrowed the verb as ~abandon~, and the old noun survives fossilised in the French adverbial phrase ~à l'abandon~, “left untended, in neglect.” From the same Frankish ~ban~ French also holds ~bannir~ (“to banish”), ~banal~ (originally “belonging to the lord's ~ban~,” shared by all), and ~banlieue~.
en.aborder:
A French formation, ~a-~ (“to, toward,” from Latin ~ad~) + ~border~ (“to skirt, to come alongside the edge”), attested from 1306. ~border~ derives from ~bord~ (“edge, side; ship's plank, ship's side”), a borrowing from Frankish *~bord~, from Proto-Germanic *~burdą~ (“board, plank”). This is cognate with English ~board~ and German ~Bord~ — the same word that gives the nautical English ~aboard~.

The nautical scene is the key: to ~aborder~ a ship was to bring one's own ~bord~ up against another's — to come alongside, whether to dock, to board, or to attack. From this concrete “draw up to the side” grew the modern figurative senses: to ~aborder~ a person is to approach and accost them, and to ~aborder un sujet~ is to “tackle” or “take up” a topic, as if steering one's vessel toward its shore. The Germanic ~bord~ proved a rich import, seeding ~bord~, ~border~, ~bordure~, ~abordage~, ~abordable~, ~babord~ (“port side”), and ~tribord~ (“starboard”).
en.aboutir:
Unlike most verbs in this app, ~aboutir~ has no single Latin ancestor: it was built inside French itself, first attested around 1319. It is a parasynthetic derivative of the noun ~bout~ (“end, tip”), framed by the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad~, “to, toward”) and the second-group verbal suffix ~-ir~ — literally “to bring to an end / to a point.” The noun ~bout~ is itself a deverbal of Old French ~bouter~ (“to push, to strike”), borrowed from Frankish *~bōtan~ (“to push, beat”), of Proto-Germanic stock *~bautaną~ (“to beat”). Through that Germanic root it is cousin to English ~beat~ and to the ~butt~ of a goat.

The metaphor inside ~aboutir~ is a journey reaching its far ~bout~. Concretely, a street or pipe can ~aboutir~ somewhere — physically come out at that point; abstractly, an effort, a negotiation, or a project ~aboutit~ when it finally arrives at its result, hence “to succeed.” The negative ~ne pas aboutir~ is the everyday way to say a plan “came to nothing.” The same humble noun ~bout~ seeds a wide family: ~embout~ (“tip, nozzle”), ~about~ (a carpenter's butt-joint), ~rebouter~ (“to set a bone”), and the bone-setter ~rebouteux~ — all turning, like ~aboutir~, on the idea of an end pushed up against something.
en.abriter:
A denominal verb (attested 1489) from ~abri~ (“shelter”), which replaced an older verb ~abrier~ (“to cover, to shelter”). The deeper origin is debated: ~abrier~ is often linked to Late Latin ~aprīcārī~ (“to bask, to keep warm in the sun”), from ~aprīcus~ (“sunny, exposed to the sun”); some etymologists instead propose a Frankish source. Cognate, on the Latin account, with Italian ~aprico~ (“sunny”).

If the Latin derivation holds, the word's history is charming: a shelter was first a *sunny* spot, a warm nook where one came to bask, and only later the general idea of cover against wind, rain, or danger. The semantic slide runs from “warmth” to “protection.” ~Abriter~ supplanted ~abrier~ entirely, surfaced as a gardening term in the eighteenth century (sheltering tender plants), then broadened to its modern range — to shelter a person, to house an institution, even figuratively to harbour a secret. From ~abri~ French also keeps ~abribus~ (a bus shelter) and the reflexive ~s'abriter~, “to take cover.”
en.accepter:
From Latin ~acceptare~ (“to receive willingly, to take regularly”), the frequentative of ~accipere~ (“to receive, to take to oneself”), which is itself ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) plus ~capere~ (“to take, to seize”). The verb ~capere~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~keh₂p-~ (“to grasp, to seize”), and through the Germanic branch is a true cognate of English ~have~ and ~heave~. Italian ~accettare~ and Spanish ~aceptar~ are the Romance siblings; English ~accept~ is a borrowing from the same Latin stem.

Because ~acceptare~ is a frequentative, its built-in nuance is repeated or habitual taking — not a single grab but a settled willingness to receive. That shade of consent is what French kept: to accept is to receive something offered, and by extension to agree or to tolerate. The base ~capere~ is one of the most fertile roots in French, surfacing in ~capter~, ~capturer~, ~captif~, ~recevoir~ and ~concevoir~ (through ~capere~'s combining form ~-cipere~), and in ~capable~ and ~capacité~. Latin ~acceptare~ also gave the noun ~acceptation~ and, in commerce, the ~acceptation~ of a bill — a creditor's formal “taking” of a draft.
en.accompagner:
From Old French ~acompaignier~, derived from ~compaing~ / ~compagnon~ (“companion”) with the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~, “toward”). ~Compagnon~ comes from Late Latin ~companio~ (accusative ~companionem~), literally ~cum~ (“with”) + ~panis~ (“bread”) — “one with whom you share bread.” The Latin word was likely a calque of Gothic ~gahlaiba~ (~ga-~ “with” + ~hlaifs~ “loaf”), a fellow soldier of the same mess. Cognate with Spanish ~compañero~ and, via Old French, English ~company~.

So ~accompagner~ means, at root, to go alongside someone as a bread-sharer — to keep company. From there the sense fanned out: to escort, to go with, to add to (« accompagner un plat d'un vin »), and in music to support a soloist with a secondary part. The bread is still visible across the family: the same ~companio~ gave the Old French nominative ~compain~, which became the familiar modern ~copain~ (“buddy, pal”), and the collective ~compagnie~ (“company,” military, theatrical, or commercial). Few everyday words wear their convivial origin so plainly: to accompany someone is, etymologically, to break bread with them.
en.accomplir:
From Old French ~complir~, ~acomplir~, from Latin ~complēre~ (“to fill up, fill out, complete”) reinforced by the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”). ~Complēre~ itself is ~com-~ (“together, completely”) plus the bound verb ~plēre~ (“to fill”), from Proto-Indo-European *~pleh₁-~ (“to fill”). That root is enormously productive: it gives Latin ~plēnus~ (“full”), and is cognate with Greek ~plērēs~ (“full”), English ~full~, and English ~plenty~ (the last borrowed from the same Latin family).

The sense path runs from filling to finishing: to *fill out* a task is to *complete* it, and to complete it fully is to *accomplish* it — the prefix ~ad-~ adding a sense of carrying through to the end. Latin built a whole series on ~plēre~ by swapping prefixes — ~implēre~ (“to fill in”), ~explēre~ (“to fill out”), ~replēre~ (“to refill”) — and French inherited the lot. From the same root come French ~plein~, ~complet~, ~remplir~, ~accomplissement~, ~complément~, ~plénitude~, and even ~plus~ (from ~plūs~, “more,” the same idea of fullness).
en.accorder:
From Vulgar/Late Latin ~accordare~ (“to bring into agreement”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~cor~, genitive ~cordis~ (“heart”), with the verbal ending ~-are~. Latin ~cor~ comes from Proto-Italic *~kord~, from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ḱerd-~ (“heart”). The same root gave Greek ~kardia~ (“heart”), English ~heart~, and German ~Herz~, while the Latin compound surfaces as Italian ~accordare~ and Spanish ~acordar~.

Literally, ~accorder~ means “to bring heart to heart” — to make two hearts beat as one. From this image flow its modern senses: to make things agree (grammatical agreement, ~l'accord du participe~), to tune an instrument so its strings are in harmony, and, by extension, “to grant” a request, as though granting it from the heart. The noun ~accord~ (“agreement, chord”) was borrowed into English as ~accord~ and, musically, lies behind ~chord~. The same *~ḱerd-~ heart underlies a striking family of French words: ~courage~ (from ~cor~, the seat of feeling), ~cordial~, ~concorde~ and ~discorde~, ~record~ (originally “to call back to the heart, to remember”), and even ~croire~ (from ~credere~, “to put one's heart in”).
en.accroître:
From Middle French ~accroistre~, from Old French ~acroistre~ (“to grow, to increase”), from Latin ~accrēscere~ (“to grow larger, to increase”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and the verb ~crēscere~ (“to grow”). The Latin verb goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ker-~ (“to grow, to nourish”). The same root gave Latin ~creāre~ (“to bring forth, create”) and the divine name ~Cerēs~, goddess of grain. Through Latin it is cognate with English ~accrue~, ~crescent~, ~increase~, and even ~croissant~, the “growing” crescent-shaped pastry.

In Latin, ~crēscere~ described organic growth — plants, children, the moon waxing — and the prefixed ~accrēscere~ sharpened this to growth added onto something already there. Old French ~acroistre~ kept that nuance, and modern ~accroître~ still means to augment a quantity or intensity rather than merely to enlarge in size. A memorable quirk of its conjugation is the circumflex inherited from a vanished ~s~ (~accroistre~ → ~accroître~), and the past participle ~accru~ that distinguishes it from ~cru~. The root *~ker-~ flowered into a wide French family: ~croître~ itself, ~croissance~ (“growth”), ~accroissement~ (“increase”), ~recrue~ and ~recrutement~ (a “recruit” being fresh growth for an army), ~concret~, ~créer~, and ~croissant~ — all branches of the same idea of swelling and coming into being.
en.accueillir:
From Old French ~acoillir~, ~acueillir~ (“to receive, to gather, to attack”), from Vulgar Latin *~accolligere~, formed from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) plus Latin ~colligere~ (“to gather, to collect”). ~Colligere~ is itself ~con-~ (“together”) + ~legere~ (“to gather, to pick, to read”), which descends from PIE *~leǵ-~ (“to collect, to gather”) — the root that also gave Greek ~légein~ (“to pick out, to say”) and Latin ~lēx~ (“law”). Cognate with Italian ~accogliere~ and Spanish ~acoger~.

The base verb ~cueillir~ means “to pick, to gather” (flowers, fruit); add the directional ~ad-~ and the picture becomes “to gather someone toward oneself,” to take them in — hence “to welcome, to receive.” Old French ~acoillir~ could even mean “to set upon, to assail,” another way of moving toward someone, a sense now long gone. Modern ~accueillir~ has settled on hospitality, and yields the warm noun ~accueil~ (“welcome, reception”). From the same *~leǵ-~ root French keeps ~cueillir~, ~recueillir~ (“to collect, to gather up”), ~collecte~, ~lire~ (“to read,” via ~legere~), ~légende~, and ~élire~ (“to elect, to pick out”).
en.accuser:
From Latin ~accūsāre~ (“to call to account, to bring a charge against”), itself built on ~ad-~ (“toward, with regard to”) and the noun ~causa~ (“cause, lawsuit, legal case”) — so that to ~accuser~ someone was literally to bring a ~causa~ against them, to summon them to a legal reckoning. The same ~causa~ underlies ~excuser~ (“to clear of a charge,” from ~ex-~ + ~causa~) and, through Old French, English ~cause~ and ~accuse~. Italian ~accusare~ and Spanish ~acusar~ are direct cognates.

Latin ~causa~ also gives the grammatical term ~accusatif~ (the accusative case), through a chain of translation errors: Greek grammarians called it the case of the thing affected, but the Latin ~accūsātīvus~ misread the Greek as relating to ~accusation~. Modern French derivatives clustering around ~causa~ include ~accusation~, ~accusateur~, ~accusatoire~, and ~inaccusable~, alongside ~cause~, ~causer~, and ~à cause de~. The origin of ~causa~ itself before Latin is obscure and not securely reconstructed, so its prehistory is best left open. What is certain is the courtroom flavor at the root of ~accuser~: the word never simply meant to blame, but to lay a formal charge — and in French it still carries that weight of summoning someone to answer.
en.accéder:
From Latin ~accedere~ (“to approach, to draw near to”), formed from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward,” assimilated to ~ac-~ before c) and the verb ~cedere~ (“to go, to move, to withdraw, to yield”). ~Cedere~ comes from Proto-Italic *~kezdō~; its deeper origin is disputed, perhaps from a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to go away, to yield.” Through Latin compounds the verb fathered a whole English series — ~cede~, ~access~, ~recede~, ~precede~, and ~succeed~.

The Latin sense was physical: to ~accedere~ was to step toward something, to approach it. French kept that motion but turned it into reaching and entering: to ~accéder~ to a place is to get to it, and the noun ~accès~ is the way in. From there grew the figurative senses — to ~accéder à~ a request is to “come around” to it, to grant it; to ~accéder~ to a throne or an office is to attain it. The root ~cedere~ runs through a broad French family: ~céder~ (“to yield”), ~accès~, ~succéder~, ~procéder~, ~précéder~, ~excéder~, and the nouns ~succès~ and ~procédure~. To gain access, then, is simply to have walked all the way up to the door.
en.acheter:
From Old French ~achater~ (“to buy”), from Vulgar Latin *~accaptāre~, a reinforced form built with ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) on Latin ~captāre~ (“to seek to seize, chase after”). ~captāre~ is the frequentative of ~capere~ (“to take, seize, hold”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~kap-~ (“to grasp”). Through that root ~acheter~ is a distant cousin of English ~have~ and ~heave~, and of the borrowed ~capable~, ~captive~, and ~capture~. The Latin verb of buying proper, ~emere~, did not survive into French here.

The semantic path is one of narrowing: from “to grasp at, try to take” to “to obtain” and finally to the specific “to obtain in exchange for money” — to buy. This mirrors what happened to ~emere~ itself, which began meaning “to take” before specializing into “to buy” in Classical Latin. The pronunciation traveled too: medieval ~achater~ smoothed into modern ~acheter~. From the same ~capere~ family, French keeps a remarkable crowd of words — ~captif~, ~capable~, ~capter~, ~recevoir~ (through ~recipere~), and ~chasser~ (through *~captiāre~) — so that buying, capturing, and hunting are all, at the deepest level, manners of grasping.
en.achever:
From Old French ~achever~ (“to bring to a head, complete”), a derivative formed with the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~, “to, toward”) on ~chief~ (“head, end”), the Old French outcome of Latin ~caput~ (“head”). The verb belongs to the phrase ~venir a chief~ (“to come to a head, to bring to an end”). ~Caput~ descends from Proto-Italic *~kaput~ and Proto-Indo-European *~kaput~, and is a true cognate of English ~head~ and German ~Haupt~.

The core image is reaching the head or top of something: to achieve a task is to bring it to its culminating point, its ~chief~ end. From this grew the darker sense “to finish off,” to deal the death-blow that brings a wounded creature to its final point — French still says ~achever une bête~. English borrowed the verb as ~achieve~, where the sense drifted toward attaining success rather than merely ending. The same ~caput~ runs through a remarkable French family: ~chef~ (“head, leader, chef”), ~chief~'s modern reflex; ~chevet~ (“bedhead”); ~achèvement~ (“completion”); ~capitaine~; ~chapitre~; and ~cap~ (“headland”), each preserving the head as the foremost or culminating part.
en.acquérir:
From Latin ~acquirere~ (Vulgar Latin ~acquaerere~), a compound of ~ad-~ (“to”) and ~quaerere~ (“to seek, to ask for”). Old French had the inherited form ~acquerre~ (attested 1148); the modern ~acquérir~, reshaped on the model of other ~-ir~ verbs, appears around 1370. Cognate with Italian ~acquisire~ and Spanish ~adquirir~, and — through ~quaerere~ — with English ~acquire~, ~quest~, and ~query~.

The force of ~ad-~ turns a simple “seeking” into a seeking that attains its object: to ~acquérir~ is to seek something all the way to getting it. The supine ~acquisitum~ gave the noun ~acquisition~ and the past participle ~acquis~, which doubles as a noun meaning the knowledge or advantages one has gained. The bare root verb survives in French as the archaic ~quérir~ (“to fetch, to go seek”), still felt in ~aller quérir~. The same ~quaerere~ branches widely: ~conquérir~ (“to seek thoroughly, to win”), ~requérir~ (“to seek back, to demand”), ~enquérir~, and ~quérable~. Strikingly, Spanish let ~quaerere~ drift from seeking to wanting and then to loving — ~querer~ — while French kept the harder, more acquisitive edge of the original.
en.activer:
Attested from the 15th century, ~activer~ is a French formation built on the adjective ~actif~ (“active”) plus the verbal suffix ~-er~. ~Actif~ descends from Latin ~āctīvus~ (“active, practical”), a derivative of ~āctus~, the past participle of ~agere~ (“to drive, to do, to act”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive, to lead”). Through this root it is cognate with English ~act~, ~agent~, and ~agile~, with Greek ~ágein~ (“to lead, to drive”), and — surprisingly — with English ~ambassador~, by way of a long Celtic-Latin detour.

To ~activer~ is literally to render ~actif~, to set something in motion or speed it up. In everyday French it long meant chiefly “to hurry, to get a move on” — ~activer le pas~, “to quicken one's step,” or the colloquial reflexive ~s'activer~, “to bustle about.” The age of machines and screens then handed it a new technical life: one now ~active~ a switch, an account, a feature, or an alarm. The prolific ~agere~ feeds a wide French family alongside ~activer~: ~agir~, ~action~, ~acte~, ~agent~, ~agiter~, ~exiger~, and ~rédiger~ — every one of them a form of doing or driving.
en.adapter:
From Latin ~adaptare~ (“to fit to, to adjust”), made of ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) + ~aptare~ (“to fit, to make ready”), a frequentative of ~apere~ (“to fasten, to attach”). The participle ~aptus~ (“fitted, suitable”) gave French ~apte~ and English ~apt~; the whole family rests on PIE *~h₂ep-~ (“to join, to fit”). Cognate with Italian ~adattare~ and Spanish ~adaptar~, and the source, through French, of English ~adapt~.

The underlying image is mechanical — making one thing fit snugly onto another — and the verb kept it: to fit a part to a machine, then, by extension, to adjust oneself to circumstances, and in the arts to turn a novel into a film. The reflexive « s'adapter » (“to adapt oneself”) makes the organism, not the object, do the fitting. The same root *~h₂ep-~ produced a surprisingly varied family: ~apte~ and ~aptitude~ (“fitness, ability”), ~inapte~, and — through a learned Latin pun on “fitting a posture” — ~attitude~, borrowed from Italian ~attitudine~, itself from Latin ~aptitudo~. To adapt is, at heart, to fit.
en.admettre:
French ~admettre~ comes from Latin ~admittere~ (“to let in, to grant access, to allow”), formed from ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) plus ~mittere~ (“to send, to let go”). The form was remodeled on French ~mettre~, which itself descends from ~mittere~. The deeper origin of ~mittere~ is debated; the most accepted reconstruction traces it through Proto-Italic *~meitō~ to a Proto-Indo-European *~meyth₂-~ (“to exchange, to remove”), with a meaning that drifted from “to exchange” to “to let go, to send.” Italian ~ammettere~ and Spanish ~admitir~ are its Romance kin, and English borrowed the learned ~admit~ from the same Latin verb.

The Latin sense, “to let in,” is still the spine of the French word. Concretely ~admettre~ means “to allow entry” — to admit someone to a club, a school, a hospital. From “to let in” the meaning broadened to intellectual acceptance: to admit a hypothesis as true, to grant a point in argument, and finally to confess (~admettre une erreur~, “to admit a mistake”), the most reluctant kind of letting-in. The root ~mittere~ runs through a huge French family by way of ~mettre~ and its many compounds: ~mettre~ itself, ~admettre~, ~permettre~, ~promettre~, ~commettre~, ~soumettre~, plus the learned ~admission~, ~mission~, ~message~, and ~transmettre~. To admit, etymologically, is simply to let something — or someone, or some truth — come in.
en.adopter:
From Latin ~adoptāre~ (“to choose for oneself, to take by selection, to adopt”), compounded from ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~optāre~ (“to choose, to wish, to desire”). The origin of ~optāre~ is debated, but it is plausibly traced through Proto-Italic *~opeje-~ to a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to choose, to grasp,” perhaps the same that gave Hittite ~ēpzi~ (“seizes, takes”). The Latin verb is the direct source of Italian ~adottare~ and Spanish ~adoptar~, and of English ~adopt~.

The literal force is « choisir vers soi » — to choose someone toward oneself — which is exactly the original legal meaning: to take a child as one's own son or daughter, with full civil rights. Roman adoptio was a solemn juridical act, and the word kept that formal flavor before broadening. From the legal sense French extended ~adopter~ to informal taking-in of a child, then figuratively to embracing an opinion, voting a law (« adopter un texte »), and finally to taking up a style, a method, or a stray animal — all variations on deliberate choosing. From the same ~optāre~ French inherited ~opter~ (“to opt”) and ~option~, and from the related ~coöptāre~ (~com-~ + ~optāre~) the verb ~coopter~ (“to co-opt”).
en.adorer:
From Latin ~adōrāre~ (“to address a prayer to, to worship”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and the verb ~ōrāre~ (“to speak formally, to plead, to pray”). To ~adōrāre~ was thus literally “to speak toward,” to direct ritual words at a god. ~Ōrāre~ is traditionally tied to ~ōs~, ~ōris~ (“mouth”), from a Proto-Indo-European root for the mouth, the speaking aperture. Cognate, through ~ōrāre~, with English ~oration~ and ~orator~.

The original gesture was sacred and verbal: one ~adored~ a deity by uttering a formula toward it, the spoken prayer at the core of the act. Roman worship even kept a bodily echo — bringing the hand to the mouth in reverence. The religious sense survives intact (one ~adore~ God), but French wore the word down to everyday hyperbole, so that one may now ~adorer~ chocolate or a film with no thought of altars. From the same ~ōrāre~ French inherits ~oraison~, ~orateur~, ~oral~, and ~adoration~, while the ~ad-~ of approach recurs across countless verbs of turning-toward.
en.adresser:
From Old French ~adrecier~ (twelfth century), composed of the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~, “to, toward”) and ~dresser~ (“to straighten, to set up, to direct”). ~dresser~ comes from Vulgar Latin *~directiare~ (“to make straight”), built on Latin ~directus~ (“straight, direct”), the past participle of ~dirigere~ (~dis-~ + ~regere~, “to guide straight”). ~regere~ rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~reg-~ (“to move in a straight line, to direct”). Through this root ~adresser~ is cognate with English ~address~, ~direct~, and ~dress~, all sharing the Vulgar Latin *~directiare~.

In Old French through the seventeenth century, ~adresser~ meant “to straighten, to set upright, to direct.” The modern senses unfold naturally from that core: to direct a letter toward someone is to ~adresser~ it; to direct words at a person is to ~adresser la parole~ to them. The English noun ~address~ — first the act of directing, later the destination written on an envelope — followed the same path. From the root *~reg-~ French drew a broad family: ~droit~ (“straight, right,” from ~directus~), ~diriger~ (“to direct”), ~dresser~ itself, ~redresser~ (“to set straight again”), and ~adresse~ in both its senses, the postal address and the “skill” of one who does things rightly.
en.affecter:
From Latin ~affectare~ (“to strive after, to aim at, to pursue eagerly”), a frequentative of ~afficere~ (“to act upon, to influence, to affect”), itself from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward,” assimilated to ~af-~) and ~facere~ (“to do, to make”). ~Facere~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to set, to put, to make”). Cognate, through ~facere~, with Italian ~affettare~ and Spanish ~afectar~, and — through the root *~dʰeh₁-~ — with English ~do~ and ~deed~ and with Greek ~títhēmi~ (“I place”).

The Latin frequentative ~affectare~ meant “to keep doing toward,” hence “to strive after” and, with a darkening twist, “to pursue with a show one does not feel.” That second branch gave French ~affecter~ its sense “to feign,” as in affecting a calm one lacks. The other branch, from ~afficere~, gave “to affect, to move” and “to assign, to allocate.” The root *~dʰeh₁-~ is colossal: through ~facere~ it underlies ~faire~, ~défaire~, ~affaire~, ~facile~, ~fait~, and the suffix ~-fier~; through related forms it yields English ~fact~, ~factory~, and ~fashion~. To affecter a feeling is, at bottom, to ~make~ a face that does the feeling for you.
en.afficher:
From Old French ~aficher~, from Vulgar Latin *~affigicare~, a frequentative reshaping of Latin ~affigere~ (“to fasten to, to fix upon”), itself ~ad-~ (“to, onto”) plus ~figere~ (“to fix, to drive in, to plant”). ~figere~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~dʰeygʷ-~ (“to stick, to set up”). Cognate with Italian ~affiggere~ and Spanish ~afijar~, and — through ~figere~ — with English ~affix~ and ~fix~.

The sense is concrete and physical: to ~afficher~ was first to pin or nail something up against a wall, and only by extension to post a notice, then to display or flaunt anything at all — one can now ~afficher~ a price, an opinion, or a smug confidence. The doublet ~ficher~ shares the same source and the same idea of driving in (a stake, a card into a file), giving the noun ~fiche~, a slip or filing card, and the curt ~fichu~. From ~affigere~ directly come the nouns ~affiche~ (a poster) and ~affichage~ (posting or, today, a screen display). The PIE root *~dʰeygʷ-~, “to stick,” also surfaces far afield in the Germanic words for an earthwork — English ~dike~ and ~ditch~ — a heap of fixed, planted earth.
en.affirmer:
From Latin ~affirmare~ (“to make firm, to assert”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~firmare~ (“to strengthen, to make firm”), itself from ~firmus~ (“strong, steadfast, stable”). ~firmus~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~fermos~ and ultimately to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰer-~ (“to hold, to support”). Through that root it is cognate with Sanskrit ~dhárati~ (“he holds”) and, through ~firmus~ directly, with English ~firm~ and ~farm~ and with Italian ~fermare~ and Spanish ~afirmar~.

The metaphor inside the word is concrete: to ~affirmer~ something is to make it ~firm~, to stand it up so it will not topple. Latin ~affirmare~ already carried this sense in legal and philosophical contexts — to assert as solid truth — and the verb entered French in the thirteenth century with that meaning intact. From the same root *~dʰer-~ and its Latin offshoot ~firmus~ French inherited a large family: ~ferme~ (“firm”), ~fermer~ (“to close,” by way of making fast), ~affermir~ (“to strengthen”), ~firmament~ (the “fixed” vault of heaven), and the noun ~affirmation~. The doublet ~fermer~ and ~affirmer~ is striking — one shut a door, the other shored up a claim, yet both rest on the idea of making something hold fast.
en.affronter:
From Old French ~afronter~ (“to strike on the forehead, to face, to defy”), formed with the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~, “to, against”) on ~front~, from Latin ~frōns~, accusative ~frontem~ (“forehead, brow, front”). The underlying notion is ~ad frontem~, “to the forehead, face to face.” Cognate, through the same Latin noun, with Italian ~affrontare~, Spanish ~afrontar~, and English ~affront~ and ~front~.

The original sense was startlingly physical: to ~afronter~ someone was to strike them on the brow — to slap the face, the seat of one's dignity. From that blow grew two branches. One led to the noun ~affront~, an insult delivered “to the face,” preserving the sense of injured honor. The other softened the violence into mere confrontation: ~affronter~ now means to face, to brave, to stand up to a danger or an opponent. The forehead, in Latin thought, was where feelings showed and pride resided, which is why an attack ~ad frontem~ struck at honor itself. The same ~frōns~ gave French ~front~ (“forehead, front,” military and meteorological alike), ~frontière~ (“border,” the “front” edge of a land), ~affronter~, ~confronter~ (“to confront,” literally to set brow against brow), and ~effronté~ (“brazen,” one who has rubbed the shame off their forehead).
en.agir:
French ~agir~ is a learned borrowing from Latin ~agere~ (“to drive, to set in motion, to do, to act”), adopted directly into the language in the fifteenth century rather than inherited through the slow erosion of Vulgar Latin. The Latin verb goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive, to lead”). Its cognates are spectacularly far-flung: Greek ~ágein~ (“to lead, to drive”), Sanskrit ~ájati~ (“he drives”), and, through the same root, English ~act~ and ~agent~. Because it entered French as a bookish import, ~agir~ kept the clean, unworn shape of its Latin model, unlike the battered everyday verbs that descend by oral tradition.

The Latin sense was concrete and forceful — to drive cattle, to push something forward — and from that physical pushing came the abstract idea of doing and behaving. French ~agir~ carries both the general sense (“to act”) and, reflexively in ~il s'agit de~, the impersonal “it is a question of.” The same root *~h₂eǵ-~ seeds a huge family in French: ~agile~ (“nimble,” literally easily driven), ~agent~, ~action~, ~agiter~, ~ambigu~ (from “driving both ways”), and even ~examen~, originally the tongue of a balance that drives toward the true weight. A single image of setting things in motion thus underlies words for nimbleness, lawsuits, anxiety, and judgment alike.
en.aider:
From Old French ~aidier~, from Latin ~adiūtāre~ (“to help, to assist”), a frequentative of ~adiuvāre~ (“to help”), itself the prefix ~ad-~ (“toward”) joined to ~iuvāre~ (“to help, to please, to delight”). The frequentative form, suggesting help given again and again, is the one that won out across the Romance languages: ~adiūtāre~ yielded Italian ~aiutare~, Spanish ~ayudar~, Portuguese ~ajudar~, and French ~aider~, and, through Old French, English ~aid~.

Latin offered two verbs of helping, and French chose the more emphatic. ~Iuvāre~ alone could mean simply “to help” but also “to delight,” a shade preserved in the impersonal ~iuvat~ (“it is pleasing”); the prefixed, intensified ~adiūtāre~ pushed the meaning firmly toward active assistance. From the same Latin family French draws ~aide~ (both the action and the helper), ~aidant~, ~entraide~ (“mutual aid”), and the learned doublet ~adjuvant~ — a substance that “helps along” a medicine or vaccine — together with ~adjudant~, a military rank, both pointing straight back to ~adiuvāre~ and ~iuvāre~.
en.aimer:
From Middle French ~aimer~, refashioned by analogy with the stressed conjugated forms ~(j')aime~, ~(tu) aimes~, from Old French ~amer~, from Latin ~amāre~ (“to love”), from Proto-Italic *~amā-~. The deeper root is debated, but it is often linked to PIE *~am-~, a nursery word seen in *~amma~ (“mother”), a child's term of affection. Cognate with Italian ~amare~, Spanish/Portuguese ~amar~, and Catalan ~amar~; from the same Latin stock English borrowed ~amateur~ (“lover” of an art) and ~amorous~.

The split between Old French ~amer~ and modern ~aimer~ is a tidy lesson in sound change: a stressed Latin ~a~ diphthongized while an unstressed ~a~ did not, so the conjugation once alternated between ~aime~ (stressed) and ~amons~ (unstressed). Middle French leveled the paradigm in favor of the ~aim-~ stem, leaving ~aimer~. The root ~amāre~ blossomed into one of French's richest emotional families: ~amour~, ~amant~ (“lover”), ~amoureux~, ~amitié~ (“friendship,” via ~amīcus~, “friend”), ~aimable~ (the source of English ~amiable~), and ~amateur~. Even the verb's range — from mild liking to passionate love — mirrors that of its Latin parent, which covered every shade of affection.
en.ajouter:
French ~ajouter~ comes from Old French ~ajoster~ (“to bring together, to put side by side”), built ultimately on the Latin preposition and adverb ~juxtā~ (“near, beside”). The Old French verb reflects a Vulgar Latin *~adjuxtāre~, formed with the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) on ~juxtā~ — literally “to set next to.” The base ~juxtā~ itself derives from the Proto-Indo-European root *~yewg-~ (“to join, to yoke”), the same root behind Latin ~iungere~ (“to join”) and ~iugum~ (“yoke”), and behind English ~yoke~ and ~join~.

The meaning slid naturally from physical placement to arithmetic and rhetorical addition: to put one thing beside another is, in the end, to add it. Along the way Old French ~ajoster~ also meant “to bring into agreement, to fit together,” a sense that survives in its doublet ~ajuster~ (“to adjust, to fit”), relatinized straight from ~juxtā~ and kept distinct from ~ajouter~ in modern usage — one adds, the other fits. From the same ~juxtā~ French has the learned adjective ~juxtaposé~ and the noun ~juxtaposition~, while the deeper *~yewg-~ root surfaces in ~joindre~, ~jonction~, and ~conjuguer~ — fittingly, the very act of joining a verb to its endings.
en.alimenter:
Attested from the 14th century, ~alimenter~ is a French formation built on the noun ~aliment~ (“food, nourishment”) plus the verbal suffix ~-er~. ~Aliment~ comes from Latin ~alimentum~ (“nourishment”), a derivative of ~alere~ (“to nourish, to feed, to rear”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂el-~ (“to nourish, to grow”). Through that root it is cousin to a surprising crowd: Latin ~altus~ (“high,” literally “grown tall”), English ~old~ (originally “grown up”) and ~adult~, and even — via a Germanic branch of the same root — English ~ale~.

The metaphor inside ~alimenter~ is feeding, and it has spread well beyond the table. One still ~alimente~ a person or an animal, but one also ~alimente~ a fire with wood, a town with water, a bank account with deposits, and — figuratively — a quarrel, a rumor, or a debate, keeping it fed and alive. The Latin ~alere~ left a rich table of French words: ~aliment~, ~alimentaire~, ~alimentation~, the adjective ~adulte~ (“one who has finished growing”), and ~coalition~ (literally “a growing together”). The very word ~élément~, through Latin ~elementum~, has sometimes been traced to the same nourishing root — a fittingly elemental kinship.
en.aller:
~Aller~ is one of the most famous suppletive verbs in French, stitched together from three unrelated Latin sources. The present forms in ~v-~ (~vais~, ~vas~, ~va~, ~vont~) come from Latin ~vādere~ (“to go, to advance”), from Proto-Indo-European *~weh₂dʰ-~ (“to go forward, to wade”), the same root that gives English ~wade~. The future and conditional in ~ir-~ (~irai~, ~irais~) descend from Latin ~īre~ (“to go”), from PIE *~h₁ey-~. The infinitive ~aller~ itself, with its ~all-~ forms, has a debated origin.

For the third stem, scholars hesitate. The traditional guess derives it from Latin ~ambulāre~ (“to walk”), but the sound changes are hard to defend. A phonetically cleaner hypothesis traces it to a Vulgar Latin *~allāre~, drawn from ~allātum~, the orphaned supine of ~afferō~ (“to bring”). French Wiktionary calls the matter genuinely uncertain, so its ancestry is best left open. The result is a single everyday verb whose “I go,” “I will go,” and “to go” each look toward a different Latin word — a living museum of how Romance speakers rebuilt the idea of motion from spare parts, the future tense reaching back to one verb while the present strode off from another.
en.allier:
French ~allier~ descends from Latin ~alligare~ (“to bind to, tie to”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward,” assimilated to ~al-~) and the verb ~ligare~ (“to bind, tie”). ~Ligare~ traces back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~leyǵ-~ (“to bind”). The same ~alligare~ surfaces elsewhere in Romance — Italian ~allegare~, Spanish ~aliar~ — and, by way of Anglo-Norman, gave English its own ~ally~ and even ~alloy~, a true semantic twin of the metallurgical sense.

The binding image splits neatly in two. When people or states are “tied together,” ~allier~ means to ally; the noun ~alliance~ covers political pacts and the wedding ring alike. When metals are “bound together,” ~allier~ means to alloy, giving the noun ~alliage~. French even reborrowed the same Latin verb in learned form as ~alléguer~ (“to allege,” to bind in a citation), so that ~allier~ and ~alléguer~ are doublets from one source. The wider *~leyǵ-~ family is vast: through ~ligare~ come ~lier~ (“to tie”), ~lien~ (“bond, link”), ~ligament~, ~liasse~ (“bundle of papers”), and ~liage~; through the prefixed ~ob-~ form come ~obliger~ and ~obligation~ — every one of them, at root, a knot.
en.amener:
A French formation, ~a-~ (“to, toward,” from Latin ~ad~) + ~mener~ (“to lead, to conduct”), attested from around the tenth century. ~mener~ comes from Late Latin ~minare~ (“to drive animals”), a striking shift from Classical Latin ~minari~ (“to threaten”). The Romance cognate is Italian ~menare~; the underlying ~minari~ also gives French ~menacer~ (“to threaten”).

The semantic story is vivid: a Roman drover urged his beasts along with shouts and threats, so ~minari~ (“to threaten”) slid into ~minare~ (“to drive a herd”), and thence to the neutral “to lead, to conduct.” With the directional prefix ~a-~, ~amener~ means to lead a person or animal toward the speaker — to ~bring~ someone — as against ~emmener~ (with ~en-~), to lead them away. The contrast with ~apporter~ (for objects one carries) preserves the verb's animate, on-foot origin: properly one ~amène~ a guest but ~apporte~ a gift. From the same ~mener~ French built ~emmener~, ~ramener~, ~promener~, ~mener~ a life or a dance, and the menacing ~menacer~.
en.amuser:
Formed in the 12th century from the Old French verb ~muser~ (“to dawdle, to idle, to muse”) with the prefix ~a-~. The origin of ~muser~ is debated: one attractive theory links it to ~mus~ (“muzzle, snout”), so that ~muser~ would mean “to stand with one’s muzzle in the air,” gaping idly; others point to Medieval Latin ~musare~ or to a Germanic source meaning “leisure.” English ~muse~ and ~amuse~ are both borrowed from these French verbs.

The key to ~amuser~ is its older sense, which survives in the related amuser of trickery: to ~amuser~ someone first meant “to make them waste time, to distract, to keep them idly occupied” — even to deceive by diversion, as a feint ~amuse~ an enemy. Only later did idle distraction soften into pleasant distraction, giving the modern sense “to entertain, to amuse.” The reflexive ~s'amuser~ (“to have a good time, to enjoy oneself”) follows the same path. Traces of the old idea linger in ~amusette~ (“a trifle, a pastime”) and the wonderfully literal ~amuse-bouche~ or ~amuse-gueule~ (“appetiser,” something to “amuse the mouth” while one waits), as well as in the noun ~amusement~.
en.améliorer:
Modern French ~améliorer~ is a learned reshaping of Old French ~ameillorer~ (« to make better »), a verb built on ~meillor~, the older form of ~meilleur~ (“better”). The base goes back to Latin ~melior~ (“better”), the comparative of ~bonus~ (“good”), from Proto-Italic *~meljōs~ and ultimately the Proto-Indo-European root *~mel-~ (“strong, great”) — so the original sense was less “better” than “stronger.” Latin ~melior~ is cognate, through that root, with ~multus~ (“much”) and with Ancient Greek ~mála~ (“very”). In the Romance languages the comparative survived directly: Italian ~migliore~ and Spanish ~mejor~ are the everyday words for “better.”

The older verb ~ameillorer~, attested by the twelfth century, would by sound-law have given a modern *~améliorer~ with a different spelling; instead scholars in the late Middle Ages refashioned it on the Latin ~melior~, restoring the ~-lior-~ that ordinary phonetic wear had worn away — the modern form is therefore half-inherited, half-relatinized. From the same family French keeps ~meilleur~ itself, the noun ~amélioration~, and the rarer ~mélioratif~ (a word that makes a meaning “better” or more favorable), all turning on the idea of betterment that began as raw strength.
en.aménager:
Derived from ~ménager~ (“to manage, arrange, use sparingly”) with the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~, “to, toward”). ~Ménager~ comes from ~ménage~ (“household”), itself the deverbal of Old French ~manoir~ (“to dwell, remain”), from Latin ~manere~ (“to stay, remain”). ~Manere~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~men-~ (“to remain, stay”) and is cognate with Ancient Greek ~μένω~ (“I remain”) and, through Latin borrowings, with English ~remain~ and ~mansion~.

The chain is wonderfully domestic: ~manere~ “to stay” gave the place where one stays, the ~ménage~ or household, then ~ménager~ “to run a household, to manage carefully,” and finally ~aménager~ “to arrange a space, to fit out, to lay out.” To ~aménager~ a room is to organize and equip it; in modern usage it extends to landscaping and town planning (~aménagement du territoire~, “regional planning”). The same root ~manere~ scatters across French in ~maison~ (“house,” from ~mansio~, “a staying”), ~demeurer~ (“to dwell”), ~manoir~ (“manor”), ~permanent~, and ~rester~ — a whole vocabulary of staying put, all rooted in the idea of remaining.
en.analyser:
Derived from the noun ~analyse~ (“analysis”) with the verbal suffix ~-er~. ~Analyse~ entered French in the 16th century from Ancient Greek ~ἀνάλυσις~ (“a dissolving, a loosening-up”), a noun built on ~ἀναλύω~ (“to unloose, dissolve, resolve into parts”), from ~ἀνά~ (“up, throughout”) + ~λύω~ (“to loosen, untie, release”). The verb ~λύω~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~lewh₁-~ (“to cut off, separate, set free”), cognate with Latin ~luō~ (“to atone, pay off”) and with English ~loose~.

The image at the heart of the word is undoing a knot: to analyze is to ~loosen~ a tangle back into its separate threads, the reverse of ~synthèse~, which binds them together. Greek philosophers used ~ἀνάλυσις~ for working a problem backward to its first principles; mathematicians and chemists later borrowed it for resolving a whole into its elements. The same ~λύω~ underlies a learned family in French: ~analyse~ and ~analytique~, but also ~catalyse~ (a “loosening-down” that frees a reaction), ~paralysie~ (a “loosening beside,” a slackening of the limbs), ~dialyse~, and ~électrolyse~ — each, etymologically, a kind of releasing or dissolving.
en.animer:
From Latin ~animāre~ (“to give breath to, to bring to life”), from ~anima~ (“breath, air, life, soul”), which goes back through Proto-Italic to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂enh₁-~ (“to breathe”). The same root produced Latin ~animus~ (“mind, spirit, courage”) and Greek ~ánemos~ (“wind”). Cognates of the verb survive across the Romance languages: Italian ~animare~, Spanish and Portuguese ~animar~, and Occitan ~animar~.

The etymological core is breath as the sign of life: to ~animer~ something is literally to breathe a soul into it. From there the meaning fanned out — to enliven, to stir up, to lead and energize a group. Modern French keeps all these layers: an ~animateur~ may host a television show or run children's activities at a summer camp, while in film and computing ~animer~ means to make drawings appear to move, giving rise to ~animation~ and the borrowed ~anime~. The contrast between ~animé~ (“lively, animated”) and ~inanimé~ (“lifeless, inanimate”) preserves the original sense exactly. The prefixed ~ranimer~ and ~réanimer~ mean to revive — the latter is the ordinary medical term for resuscitation, an intensive-care ~service de réanimation~ being, etymologically, a place for breathing life back in. The same breath-root underlies ~animal~, ~unanime~, and ~magnanime~.
en.annoncer:
From Latin ~adnūntiāre~ (also written ~annūntiāre~), “to report, to make known,” from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~nūntiāre~ (“to announce”), a verb built on the noun ~nūntius~ (“messenger; message”). The deeper origin of ~nūntius~ is debated; one well-supported account ties it to a Proto-Indo-European root *~newH-~ (“to shout, to call out”), seen in Old Irish ~núall~ (“a loud cry”). The Romance siblings include Italian ~annunciare~ and Spanish ~anunciar~.

The heart of the word is the messenger: to ~annoncer~ is to bring word, to proclaim, and reflexively ~s'annoncer~ is for something to give early signs of itself (a storm, a good year). The same ~nūntius~ underlies a striking family in French: ~annonce~ (“announcement, advertisement”), ~dénoncer~ (“to denounce,” literally to report against), ~prononcer~ and ~énoncer~, and the ecclesiastical ~nonce~, the pope's ambassador — a messenger in the most literal sense. English drew ~announce~, ~denounce~, ~pronounce~, and ~nuncio~ from the same Latin spring.
en.apercevoir:
Derived from ~percevoir~ (“to perceive, collect”) with the prefix ~a-~, an allomorph of Latin ~ad-~ (“to, toward”). ~Percevoir~ continues Latin ~percipere~ (“to seize thoroughly, take in, perceive”), a compound of ~per-~ (“through, thoroughly”) and ~capere~ (“to take, seize”). ~Capere~ rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~kap-~ (“to grasp”). Through the same ~percipere~, English has ~perceive~, and through ~capere~ a vast cognate family.

The metaphor is mental grasping: to perceive is to take something in fully, and ~apercevoir~, with its directional ~a-~, adds the nuance of catching sight of, of perception suddenly directed at an object — “to glimpse, to catch sight of.” The reflexive ~s'apercevoir de~ moved further inward, to “to realize, to become aware,” the moment one mentally seizes a fact. The root ~capere~ seeds an enormous French vocabulary — ~recevoir~, ~concevoir~, ~décevoir~, ~capter~, ~captif~, ~capable~ — all turning on the idea of taking or holding. Historically ~apercevoir~ was often spelled ~appercevoir~ with a doubled consonant, before French standardized the single ~p~.
en.apparaître:
From Middle French ~apparoistre~, from Old French ~aparoistre~ (“to show oneself, become visible”), an inchoative reshaping built on Latin ~appārēre~ (“to appear, be visible”), via the Late Latin form ~appārēscere~ (“to come suddenly into view”). ~appārēre~ is ~ad-~ (“toward”) + ~pārēre~ (“to be visible, to obey”). The simple verb gave French ~paraître~, while the prefixed one gave ~apparaître~. Cognate with Italian ~apparire~, Spanish ~aparecer~, and — through Old French — English ~appear~.

The ~-aître~ spelling preserves the old ~s~ of ~apparoistre~ as a circumflex: the verb was written ~apparoître~ until 1835, then ~apparaître~, and since the 1990 reforms ~apparaitre~ is also allowed. The inchoative suffix ~-ēscere~ marked a beginning, so ~appārēscere~ meant not merely “to be visible” but “to start being visible,” which suits a thing that suddenly swims into sight — the nuance that still separates ~apparaître~ from a flat statement of presence. The root ~pārēre~ left a wide family in French: the present participle ~apparēns~ underlies ~apparent~, the noun ~appāritiō~ underlies ~apparition~, and the same verb survives in the old legal ~comparaître~ (“to appear in court”) and in ~transparaître~ (“to show through”).
en.appartenir:
From Old French ~apartenir~, from Late Latin ~appertinere~ (also ~appertĭnere~), a compound of ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~pertinere~ (“to pertain, to reach to, to belong”). ~pertinere~ is itself ~per-~ (“through”) plus ~tenere~ (“to hold”), which descends from Proto-Indo-European *~ten-~ (“to stretch”). Cognate with Italian ~appartenere~ and Spanish ~pertenecer~, and — through *~ten-~ — with English ~thin~ and ~tenant~.

The semantic path is vivid: ~tenere~ means to hold, ~pertinere~ to hold all the way through to something, hence to reach it, concern it, pertain to it; and ~appartenir~ adds ~ad-~ to direct that reaching toward an owner — what belongs to someone is what reaches and attaches to them. The PIE root *~ten-~, “to stretch,” yielded “hold” through the notion of keeping something taut, and it underlies a large French family: ~tenir~, ~contenir~, ~retenir~, ~maintenir~, ~obtenir~, and the nouns ~tenue~ and ~contenu~. The pronominal ~s'appartenir~, “to belong to oneself,” preserves the older flavor of the verb, used of a person free and answerable to no one — a fine reminder that to belong is, at root, to be held.
en.appeler:
From Old French ~apeler~ (“to call, name, summon”), from Latin ~appellāre~ (“to address, call upon, name”), a frequentative of ~appellere~ (“to drive toward”), from ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) + ~pellere~ (“to push, drive, beat”), from PIE *~pelh₂-~ (“to drive, push”). Cognate with Italian ~appellare~ and Spanish ~apelar~; through ~pellere~ and its participle ~pulsus~, English has ~appeal~, ~appellation~, ~compel~, ~pulse~, and ~propel~.

The surprise is the maritime metaphor underneath: ~appellere~ literally meant to drive a ship toward land, and from “to drive toward” the figurative ~appellāre~ came to mean to drive your voice toward someone — to address, summon, or name them. French ~appeler~ keeps that whole spread: to call out, to telephone, to name (~comment t'appelles-tu ?~, “what is your name?”), and the legal ~faire appel~ (“to appeal”), where one literally calls upon a higher court. The driving root ~pellere~ scattered widely in French through compounds — ~repousser~, ~pousser~ (via ~pulsāre~), ~propulser~, ~compulsion~, ~impulsion~ — and the verb's own offspring include the noun ~appel~, the reflexive ~s'appeler~ (“to be named”), ~rappeler~ (“to recall, remind”), and ~interpeller~ (“to hail, challenge”).
en.appliquer:
From Latin ~applicāre~ (“to attach to, join, bring into contact”), a compound of ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~plicāre~ (“to fold”). ~Plicāre~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~plek-~ (“to plait, fold”), the same root that yielded Greek ~plékein~ (“to braid”) and, through Germanic, English ~fold~ and ~flax~. Romance relatives include Italian ~applicare~ and Spanish ~aplicar~; the doublet ~appliquer~ borrowed straight from Latin sits beside the inherited ~plier~ (“to fold”).

The original image is wonderfully concrete: to fold or lay one thing flat against another, to press them into contact. From “lay against” grew “devote to” — to ~appliquer~ oneself is to fold one’s whole attention onto a task — and the abstract sense of putting a rule, a method, or a remedy into practice (to ~appliquer~ a law). The root *~plek-~ folds its way through a vast French family: ~plier~, ~ployer~, ~pli~, ~simple~, ~double~, ~complexe~, ~compliquer~, ~impliquer~, and ~explication~ — literally an “unfolding.” Even ~plaie~ (“wound”) and the embroidery term ~appliqué~, borrowed back into English for a folded-on ornament, trace to the same humble act of folding.
en.apporter:
From Latin ~apportāre~ (“to carry something to someone”), composed of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~portāre~ (“to carry”). ~Portāre~ derives from PIE *~per-~ (“to lead across, to pass over”), the same root behind Latin ~porta~ (“gate”) and ~portus~ (“harbor”). Through this root ~apporter~ is cognate with English ~ferry~, ~ford~, and ~fare~, and with Greek ~póros~ (“passage, way”).

Where ~porter~ simply means to carry, the prefix ~ad-~ adds direction: to ~apporter~ is to carry toward a destination or a person, as opposed to ~emporter~ (carry away) or ~rapporter~ (carry back). The root idea of passing across a threshold radiates through French in a large family: ~porter~, ~portail~, ~port~, ~porche~, ~importer~, ~exporter~, ~transporter~, ~rapport~, ~apport~, and ~déporter~. Even the seemingly distant ~opportun~ belongs here — it once described a wind driving a ship toward (~ob~) the ~portus~, the harbor. So ~apporter~, ~importer~, and ~opportun~ all hark back to the act of carrying something across a passage to where it is wanted.
en.apprendre:
From Latin ~apprehendere~ (“to seize, to grasp, to take hold of”), ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) + ~prehendere~ (“to grasp”). ~Prehendere~ itself is ~prae-~ (“before”) + an old root ~-hendere~ with no surviving simplex, from PIE *~gʰed-~ (“to seize, to grasp”), the very root that gives English ~get~. Old French reduced ~apprehendere~ to ~aprendre~. The fuller Latin form was later re-borrowed directly as a learned doublet, ~appréhender~ (“to apprehend, to dread”), so French keeps both the worn-down popular verb and its scholarly twin. Italian ~apprendere~ and Spanish ~aprender~ are cognates.

The semantic leap is a beautiful one: to learn is to seize knowledge with the mind, just as the hand grasps an object — the same metaphor English makes when it says one “grasps” an idea. From “to take hold of” French ~apprendre~ developed two faces that still coexist: one learns (acquires knowledge oneself) and one teaches (~apprendre quelque chose à quelqu'un~, “to impart knowledge to someone”), the same verb facing in opposite directions. It also means “to find out,” as in ~apprendre une nouvelle~ (“to learn of a piece of news”). From the ~prehendere~ root descend French ~prendre~, ~apprendre~, ~comprendre~ (“to grasp together, to understand”), ~surprendre~ (“to seize from above, to surprise”), ~apprenti~, and ~appréhender~.
en.approcher:
From Old French ~aprochier~ (“to bring near, to draw near”), from Late Latin ~appropiāre~ (“to come near”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and the adverb ~prope~ (“near”). Latin ~prope~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~prokʷe~, built on *~pro-~ (“forward, in front”). Cognate with Italian ~approcciare~ and, through English borrowing from Old French, with ~approach~ itself.

The sense has stayed remarkably stable: from the start ~aprochier~ meant to reduce the distance between two things, and modern ~approcher~ still does. The Latin adverb ~prope~ is unusually productive in French. Its comparative ~propior~ and superlative ~proximus~ gave ~proche~ (“near”) and the learned family ~proximité~, ~approximatif~, and ~rapprocher~ (“to bring closer together,” with an extra ~re-~). A nice detail is that ~approcher~ and the look-alike ~approprier~ are false relatives: the first rests on ~prope~ (“near”), the second on ~proprius~ (“one's own”), two distinct Latin words that French merely happened to dress in similar clothing. So when you ~approcher~ something, you are etymologically bringing it ~prope~ — near — and nothing more.
en.approprier:
From Latin ~appropriāre~ (“to make one's own”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to”) and ~proprius~ (“one's own, particular”). Latin ~proprius~ is generally traced to the phrase ~pro privō~, “for the individual,” involving ~prīvus~ (“single, one's own”). Cognate, through ~proprius~, with Italian ~appropriare~, Spanish ~apropiar~, and English ~appropriate~.

To ~approprier~ is to make a thing ~proprius~ — fitting, particular, one's own. Two threads run through its history. The reflexive ~s'approprier~ means to seize something as one's own, to take possession; this is the sense closest to English “appropriate.” But the transitive ~approprier~ kept the older idea of making a thing suitable to a situation — adapting, tailoring, fitting it. The shared ancestor ~proprius~ is one of Latin's most fertile roots in French: it gave ~propre~ (which means both “clean” and “one's own”), ~propriété~ (“property, ownership”), ~propriétaire~ (“owner”), ~approprié~ (“appropriate, suitable”), and ~exproprier~ (“to expropriate”). Note that ~approprier~ and ~approcher~ only look like twins: the former rests on ~proprius~ (“one's own”), the latter on ~prope~ (“near”), two unrelated Latin words.
en.apprécier:
From Late Latin ~appretiare~ (“to set a price to, value”), from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to”) and Latin ~pretium~ (“price, value, worth”). The noun ~pretium~ goes back to a suffixed form of the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to traffic in, sell, exchange”). Cognate with Italian ~apprezzare~ and Spanish ~apreciar~, and — through ~pretium~ — with English ~appreciate~, ~price~, and ~precious~.

The original act was commercial: to ~apprécier~ was to “put a price on” something, to appraise its monetary worth — a sense the verb still keeps in valuation and finance. From assessing value in coin grew the warmer modern sense of esteeming, enjoying, and being grateful for something. A pleasing tangle hangs off this root: from the same ~pretium~ Old French spun ~praise~, ~price~, and ~prize~, three English words that began as one before splitting apart. In French the family includes ~prix~, ~précieux~, ~priser~, ~déprécier~, and ~mépriser~ — this last a “mis-pricing,” to value too low, the very opposite of ~apprécier~.
en.appuyer:
From Old French ~apuier~ (also ~apoier~), from Vulgar Latin ~appodiare~ (“to support, prop up”), from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, against”) and Latin ~podium~ (“raised platform, support”). That ~podium~ was borrowed from Greek ~podion~ (“little foot, base of a vase”), a diminutive of ~pous~ (genitive ~podos~, “foot”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ped-~ (“foot”). Cognate with Italian ~appoggiare~, Spanish ~apoyar~, and Portuguese ~apoiar~, and — through *~ped-~ — with English ~foot~ and Latin ~pes~.

The charming detail is the foot hidden at the bottom: a ~podium~ was named by Greek architects who thought a low base beneath a wall or colonnade looked like a little foot to stand on. To ~appuyer~ is thus to set something against such a support, to “prop it on a footing.” From the physical act of pressing or leaning grew the figurative senses of backing a candidate or supporting an argument. The Italian sibling ~appoggiare~ gave music the ~appogiature~, a leaning grace-note. Through ~podium~ and Greek ~pous~ French also holds ~appui~, ~podium~ itself, ~podologue~, and the ~-pode~ of ~antipode~.
en.armer:
From Latin ~armāre~ (“to arm, to equip with weapons”), attested in French from the 10th century, derived from ~arma~ (“weapons, tools”). The Latin noun comes from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂er-~ (“to fit, to join”), through a form *~h₂(e)rmos~ (“fitting, that which is fitted together”) — so a weapon was first simply “gear, fitted equipment.” The same root underlies Latin ~ars~ (“art, skill”) and ~artus~ (“joint”), Ancient Greek ~ἀραρίσκω~ (~ararískō~, “to fit together”), and English ~arm~, ~art~, and ~harmony~.

The verb’s history is steeped in the medieval world of war and chivalry. Beyond “to arm,” ~armer~ acquired the feudal sense “to dub a knight” — to ceremonially fit a man out with his weapons and so make him a ~chevalier~. It later spread to any kind of equipping: one ~arme~ a ship (fitting it out for sea), ~arme~ a camera or a trap (setting its mechanism), and ~arme~ concrete with steel rods (~béton armé~, “reinforced concrete”). From the same Latin source come ~arme~ and ~armée~ (“army”), ~armure~ (“armour”), ~armurier~, ~armistice~, and, by a curious route through ~armarium~ (“a cupboard for tools”), the French ~armoire~ (“wardrobe, cabinet”).
en.arriver:
From Old French ~ariver~ (“to come to shore, land”), from Vulgar Latin *~arripāre~ (“to touch the shore, come to land”), from the phrase ~ad rīpam~ (“to the shore”), built on ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) + ~rīpa~ (“riverbank, shore”). Cognate with Italian ~arrivare~ and Catalan/Occitan ~arribar~; through the root noun ~rīpa~ it is kin to English ~river~ (via Old French ~rivière~) and to the geological term ~riparian~.

The original notion was wholly nautical: after a long voyage, a ship ~arriva~ when it finally touched the bank. From this image of coming ashore the verb generalized to reaching any destination by any means, then bleached further into the impersonal sense “to happen” — what ~arrive~ is what comes to pass, as in ~il arrive que~ (“it happens that”). The same ~rīpa~ gave French ~rive~ (“bank”), ~rivière~ (“river”), ~riverain~ (“riverside resident”), and the borrowed ~arriviste~ — one who has “arrived” socially by any means, a striver. So a word that once meant simply reaching the riverbank now spans both triumphant arrival and the sheer contingency of events.
en.arrêter:
From Middle French ~arrester~, from Old French ~arrester~, ~arester~ (“to stop, to halt, to fix”), from Vulgar Latin *~arrestare~, that is ~ad-~ (“to”) + ~restare~ (“to remain, to stay behind”). ~Restare~ is ~re-~ + ~stare~ (“to stand”), from PIE *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”). The literal force was thus “to make remain, to bring to a standstill.” Cognate with Italian ~arrestare~ and Spanish ~arrestar~; through Old French it gave English ~arrest~.

A persistent folk etymology tried to link the word to Germanic ~Rast~ or ~Rest~ (“rest, repose”), but the true ancestor is the Latin “standing” family, not the Germanic “resting” one. From the idea of bringing motion to a halt grew the noun ~arrêt~ — both a “stop” (un arrêt de bus) and, in law, a fixed and binding “decree” or court judgment, something settled and immovable. The verb also means to decide firmly (« arrêter une date ») and to detain a suspect. The same PIE root *~steh₂-~ underlies ~rester~, ~stable~, ~station~, ~constant~, and ~stature~ — a whole vocabulary of standing and staying put.
en.asseoir:
From Latin ~assidēre~ (also ~adsidēre~), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~sedēre~ (“to sit”), meaning “to sit beside, to settle.” ~Sedēre~ comes from Proto-Indo-European *~sed-~ (“to sit”), one of the most widespread of all roots, cognate with Sanskrit ~sī́dati~, Old Church Slavonic ~sěděti~, and English ~sit~, ~seat~, and ~settle~. The French verb is attested from the tenth century, and its tangled conjugation (~assieds~, ~assoit~, ~asseyait~) preserves traces of the Latin stems in fossilized form.

From the literal sense of placing someone in a seated position, ~asseoir~ developed a rich figurative life centered on the idea of settling something firmly in place. One ~assoit~ a reputation, an argument, or a tax — establishing it on a solid base. The same image runs through architecture and warfare, where a building or a camp is ~assis~ on firm ground. The English noun ~siege~, by way of Old French ~sege~ (“seat”), comes from this same family — a besieging army “sits down” before a city. From the root *~sed-~ descend French ~asseoir~, ~siège~, ~séance~ (“a sitting, a session”), ~résider~, and ~sédentaire~ (“given to sitting”).
en.assister:
From Latin ~assistere~ (“to stand by, to take one's place near”), a compound of ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~sistere~ (“to cause to stand, to place oneself”). ~sistere~ is a reduplicated formation on ~stare~ (“to stand”), from PIE *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”) — the very root behind the worked example ~ester~. Cognate with Italian ~assistere~ and Spanish ~asistir~, and, through ~assistere~, with English ~assist~.

The literal sense is bodily and spatial: to ~assister~ was to stand beside someone. From that single posture two modern meanings unfolded in opposite directions. Stand beside an event as a bystander and you are merely present — hence ~assister à~ a concert or a meeting, “to attend.” Stand beside a person to lend a hand and you are a helper — hence ~assister~ someone in need, “to aid.” French keeps both senses live, a rare case where one verb means both “to watch” and “to help.” The reduplicated ~sistere~ seeds a whole cohort of French verbs — ~résister~, ~insister~, ~persister~, ~consister~, ~subsister~ — while the parent ~stare~ gives ~stable~, ~station~, ~stade~, and ~rester~. Behind them all stands the same upright PIE *~steh₂-~, and behind ~assister~ in particular, the ~assistant~ who simply stands close by.
en.associer:
From Late Latin ~associāre~ (“to join to, to unite, to take as a companion”), from Latin ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) plus ~sociāre~ (“to join, to ally”), itself derived from ~socius~ (“companion, ally, partner”). The noun ~socius~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~sokjos~ to the Proto-Indo-European root *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”): a ~socius~ was originally “a follower,” one who goes along with another. From that same root flow Latin ~sequī~ (“to follow”) and a great train of its cousins, while ~socius~ itself underlies English ~social~ and ~society~, Italian ~socio~, and Spanish ~socio~.

That the word for “partner” should spring from “follower” is a quiet lesson in how alliances were imagined: to associate with someone was, at root, to fall in behind them. In 13th-century French ~associer~ named the act of allying among companions, and the sense has stayed close to that ever since — to bring people or things into partnership, to link them in the mind. The reflexive ~s'associer~ means to join forces or go into business together. From ~socius~ and its kin French also drew ~société~, ~social~, ~associé~, ~association~, and ~sociable~.
en.assumer:
Borrowed from Latin ~assumere~ (“to take to oneself, take up”), formed from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~sumere~ (“to take”). ~Sumere~ itself is a contraction of *~subemere~, that is ~sub-~ (“up from under”) plus ~emere~ (“to take, later to buy”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁em-~ (“to take, distribute”). The root is cognate with Lithuanian ~imti~ (“to take”) and, through Latin borrowings, with English ~example~ and ~redeem~ (Latin ~redimere~, literally “to take back”).

In classical Latin ~assumere~ meant to take something up for oneself — to adopt, to claim, to take on. French preserved this in the sense of taking on a role or burden: ~assumer ses responsabilités~ (“to shoulder one's responsibilities”). A modern nuance, “to come to terms with, to own,” has grown common — to ~assumer~ one's choices is to accept them without apology. Note that French ~assumer~ has nothing to do with the English false friend “to assume” in the sense of supposing; that meaning split off in English. The ~emere~ root seeds a wide French family: ~exemple~, ~rédemption~, ~prime~ (via ~praemium~), ~prompt~, and the legal ~péremption~ — a scattered descent from a verb that first meant simply “to take.”
en.assurer:
French ~assurer~ comes from Old French ~asseürer~ (“to make safe, to give confidence to”), from Late Latin ~assecūrāre~ (“to render secure”), formed with the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) on Latin ~sēcūrus~ (“safe, free from care”). The adjective ~sēcūrus~ is itself transparent: ~sē-~ (“without, apart from”) plus ~cūra~ (“care, worry”) — so to be secure is, literally, to be “care-free.” Through the Romance line ~assurer~ is cognate with Italian ~assicurare~ and Spanish ~asegurar~; the bare ~sēcūrus~ gave both English ~secure~ and, by a worn-down French route, English ~sure~.

Latin ~sēcūrus~ left two parallel descendants in French: the inherited, phonetically eroded ~sûr~ (“sure, safe” — once spelled ~seür~) and the learned borrowing ~sécurité~ (“security”), a doublet pair from one source. The verb ~assurer~ accordingly fans out across many senses — to make sure, to assert confidently, to provide insurance, to belay a climber on a rope — all turning on the idea of removing care or danger. From the same ~cūra~ root French keeps ~assurance~ (“insurance, confidence”), ~rassurer~ (“to reassure”), ~sûreté~ (“safety”), and ~curer~ — the carefree and the careful alike springing from one Latin worry.
en.attacher:
From Old French ~estachier~ (“to fasten, fix, stake up”), a verb built on ~estache~ (“stake, post”), itself from Frankish *~stakka~ (“stake, post”), from Proto-Germanic *~stakô~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)teg-~ (“pole, stick, stake”). The initial vowel later shifted under the influence of the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~, “to”). Cognate with Italian ~staccare~, Spanish ~estaca~ (“stake”), and — through the same Germanic root — with English ~stake~ and ~attach~ itself, which English borrowed back from French.

The earthy origin is a post driven into the ground: to ~attacher~ was first to tether a thing to a stake, and only later to bind, affix, or fasten in general, then figuratively to “attach” someone to a cause or person. The same base gives the perfect antonym ~détacher~ (Old French ~destachier~, “to un-stake, unfasten”), and from that pair English drew both ~attach~ and ~detach~. A surprising relative is ~tâche~ (“task”): many etymologists tie it to the same family, a “task” being originally something one is bound or pledged to do — a duty staked to a person, like an animal tethered to its post.
en.attaquer:
Attested in French from 1549, ~attaquer~ was borrowed from Italian ~attaccare~ (“to attach; to join; to assail”). The Italian verb arose by swapping the prefix of ~staccare~ (“to detach”) for ~a-~, and its tac- element is generally referred to a Germanic source — Frankish *~stakka~ (“stake, post”), the same root that gives English ~stake~. Crucially, ~attaccare~ first meant “to fasten, to attach,” and the military sense grew out of the phrase ~attaccare battaglia~, “to join battle,” literally “to fasten a fight onto” the enemy.

This makes ~attaquer~ an etymological doublet of native French ~attacher~ (“to attach”): the two are the same word, one inherited and one re-imported from Italy, which is why ~s'attaquer à~ and ~s'attacher à~ shadow each other so closely. The combative branch — to fall upon, to assail — eventually overshadowed the “fastening” sense in the borrowed form, leaving ~attacher~ to keep the literal meaning. From the same Germanic stock French also holds ~attache~, ~détacher~, and ~tache~ in some analyses, a small cluster all turning on the idea of pinning one thing fast to another.
en.atteindre:
From Old French ~ataindre~, ~ateindre~ (“to reach, to touch”), from Latin ~attingere~ (“to touch, to reach, to border on”), itself ~ad-~ (“toward”) + ~tangere~ (“to touch”), from Proto-Italic *~tangō~, from PIE *~teh₂g-~ (“to touch”). Cognate with Italian ~attingere~ (“to draw, to obtain”) and, through the same Latin root, with English ~tangent~, ~contact~, and ~intact~.

The core sense was always physical contact, and Latin ~tangere~ already stretched from “to touch” to “to reach” and “to affect.” French inherited and developed this range: one can ~atteindre~ a summit, a target, an age, or — figuratively — be touched by an illness, ~atteint~ d'une maladie. The notion of striking a mark gave the noun ~atteinte~ (“an attack, an impairment, a blow to”), as in « porter atteinte à » (“to undermine, to infringe upon”). The same PIE root *~teh₂g-~ seeded a wide family in French through learned Latin borrowings: ~tangente~, ~contact~, ~contagion~, ~intact~, ~tactile~, and ~contigu~ all preserve, under their abstract meanings, the ancient idea of one thing touching another.
en.attendre:
From Old French ~atendre~ (late eleventh century), from Latin ~attendere~ (“to stretch toward, to pay attention”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“toward”) and ~tendere~ (“to stretch”). ~tendere~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~ten-~ (“to stretch, to extend”), through which ~attendre~ is cognate with Ancient Greek ~teínō~ (“to stretch”), with Sanskrit ~tan-~ (“to stretch”), and with English ~thin~ and ~tend~. The underlying image is concrete: to attend is to stretch one's mind toward something.

In Latin, ~attendere~ literally meant to stretch (one's attention) in a direction — to heed. French inherited that sense, still visible in the old reflexive ~s'attendre à~ (“to expect”), but the everyday meaning drifted: someone whose attention is fixed on an awaited thing is, in effect, waiting for it, and so ~attendre~ became the ordinary verb “to wait.” English ~attend~ kept the original “pay attention” sense, making the two a neat false-friend pair. The root ~tendere~ produced a large French family: ~tendre~, ~étendre~, ~entendre~, ~prétendre~, ~tension~, ~intention~, ~tente~, and ~attention~ itself — all turning, one way or another, on the idea of stretching.
en.attirer:
Attested from the thirteenth century, ~attirer~ is formed from the verb ~tirer~ (“to pull, to draw”) with the prefix ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~, “to, toward”), so the word means to draw something toward oneself. ~Tirer~ comes from Vulgar Latin *~tīrāre~, whose ultimate origin is genuinely uncertain — one much-cited hypothesis derives it from a shortening of Old French ~martirer~ (“to make a martyr of, to torture”), from ~martyrium~, on the idea of drawing out and tormenting, but this remains disputed. Cognate with Italian ~tirare~ and Spanish ~tirar~.

In Old French ~atirer~ first meant “to arrange, to put in order, to adorn,” a sense English borrowed and still keeps in ~attire~ (“to dress, clothing”) — a surprising sibling of the everyday French verb. Only later did the pull of ~tirer~ reassert itself, giving the dominant modern meaning of attracting, whether a magnet drawing iron or a spectacle drawing a crowd. From ~tirer~ French keeps a busy family — ~tirage~, ~tiroir~ (“drawer,” a thing that is pulled), ~retirer~, ~soutirer~, ~étirer~, and the marksman's ~tireur~ — every one of them a kind of pulling.
en.attribuer:
From Latin ~attribuere~ (“to assign, to allot, to add to”), a compound of the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward,” assimilated to ~at-~) and the verb ~tribuere~ (“to grant, to allot, to divide”). ~tribuere~ is a denominative of ~tribus~ (“a tribe,” one of the divisions of the Roman people), so that to allot something was, originally, to distribute it among the tribes. Through this root ~attribuer~ is cognate with English ~attribute~, ~tribute~, ~contribute~, ~distribute~, and ~tribe~ itself.

The political-administrative origin still shows: ~attribuer~ is to apportion a share, a credit, a role, a cause — to assign a prize, attribute a painting to a master, ascribe a motive. The reflexive ~s'attribuer~ means to claim something for oneself. French carries the whole Latin family forward: ~attribut~ (“an attribute, a grammatical predicate”), ~attribution~ (“an allocation, a remit”), ~contribuer~ and ~contribution~, ~distribuer~ and ~distribution~, ~rétribuer~ (“to pay, to remunerate”), and ~tribu~ (“a tribe”). The notion of a ~tribut~ (“tribute”) — a payment apportioned to a ruler — and that of a ~tribunal~ (the platform of the ~tribus~) sit in the same etymological neighborhood, all turning on the idea of dividing and assigning shares.
en.augmenter:
From Latin ~augmentāre~ (“to increase”), a late verb built on ~augmentum~ (“an increase, growth”), itself from ~augēre~ (“to increase, to enlarge”). ~Augēre~ comes from Proto-Italic *~augeō~ and from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂ewg-~ (“to grow, to increase”). The root spread widely: English ~eke~ (“to eke out”), Ancient Greek ~αὐξάνω~ (auxánō, “to grow”), Lithuanian ~áugti~ (“to grow”), and, through Latin, English ~augment~, ~author~, and ~auction~ — the last literally a sale by increasing bids.

The Latin ~augēre~ already carried the idea of making something greater, and ~augmentāre~ simply intensified it; French kept the plain sense “to make or become larger.” The same fertile root yields a surprising spread in French: ~augmenter~ and ~augmentation~, of course, but also ~auteur~ and ~autoriser~ (an ~auctor~ being one who makes a thing grow), ~auguste~ and the name ~Auguste~ (“the venerable, the one who increases”), and even ~auxiliaire~ — an auxiliary being help that augments one's own forces. What began as the simple notion of growth thus branched into authority, majesty, and aid.
en.autoriser:
From Medieval Latin ~auctorizare~ (“to give authority to, to confirm”), from Latin ~auctor~ (“originator, author, guarantor”), itself from ~augere~ (“to increase, to make grow”) + the agent suffix ~-tor~. ~augere~ rests on Proto-Indo-European *~h₂ewg-~ (“to increase”), the source also of English ~author~, ~augment~, and ~auction~. The Old French intermediary was ~actoriser~, and ~autoriser~ stands as a doublet of ~octroyer~ (“to grant”), the popular outcome of the same Latin verb.

An ~auctor~ was originally one who “causes to grow” — who augments, vouches for, sets a thing on foot: a founder, a backer, a source of trust. To ~autoriser~ was thus to lend something the weight of such a guarantor, to make it valid and warranted; from this came both “to authorize, to permit” and the sense of conferring ~autorité~. The thread back to “growth” is invisible in modern usage yet binds a surprising kin: ~auteur~, ~autorité~, ~augmenter~, ~auguste~, ~enchère~ (“auction,” via ~auctio~), and even ~août~ (“August,” the month of ~Augustus~, “the increased, the venerable”).
en.avancer:
From Old French ~avancier~ (also ~avancir~), from Vulgar Latin *~abantiare~, a verb built on Late Latin ~abante~ (“before, in front”), itself a fusion of ~ab~ (“from”) and ~ante~ (“before”). ~Ante~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂énti~ (“in front, before”), which also gives Greek ~antí~ (“opposite, against”) and is cognate with English ~answer~ (originally a “swearing against”). The whole formation means, roughly, “to put forward, to move to the front.”

A charming etymological mishap haunts this word. Speakers came to feel that the ~a-~ of ~avancer~ was the ordinary prefix ~a-~, and that the rest was a stem ~vant~ — so when they wanted a noun for what lies ahead, they coined ~avant~ by clipping off that imagined prefix, even though the ~a~ was part of the root all along. The same false cut produced ~avantage~. Meanwhile ~avancer~ kept widening: from spatial movement forward it reached time (to advance a date), money (to advance a sum), and argument (to advance a claim). Kin in French include ~avant~, ~avance~, ~avantage~, ~avancement~, and ~devancer~.
en.avoir:
From Old French ~avoir~ (also ~aveir~), from Latin ~habēre~ (“to have, hold, possess”), from Proto-Italic *~habēō~, generally traced to Proto-Indo-European *~gʰh₁bʰ-~ (“to grab, to take, to hold”). Its Romance siblings are everywhere everyday verbs: Italian ~avere~, Spanish ~haber~, Portuguese ~haver~, Romanian ~avea~. Tempting as it looks, English ~have~ is not in fact a cognate — the resemblance is a coincidence, that verb belonging to a separate Germanic family.

Latin ~habēre~ began with the concrete sense “to hold,” then widened to “to possess.” In Romance, ~avoir~ went a step further and became an auxiliary, the engine of the compound past: ~j'ai mangé~ is literally “I have eaten,” a possession of the completed act. From the impersonal ~il y a~ (“there is, there are”) French even built an existential out of mere having. The same root threads through a rich family of borrowed and inherited words: ~habit~ (a way of “holding” oneself, hence both costume and custom), ~habitude~, ~habiter~ (“to dwell”), ~exhiber~, ~prohiber~, and, through the compound ~dēbēre~ (~dē-~ + ~habēre~, “to hold away from”), the verb ~devoir~ itself. To have, to owe, to dwell — all are ways of holding.

The same auxiliary turn happened on the far side of the linguistic frontier. German ~haben~ likewise became the engine of a compound past: ~ich habe gesungen~ (“I have sung”) mirrors ~j'ai chanté~ exactly. Yet here lies a fine irony — ~haben~ and ~avoir~ are not cognates at all. Despite their near-identical look and identical meaning, German ~haben~ descends from a different Proto-Indo-European root (*~keh₂p-~, “to grasp,” the source also of Latin ~capiō~ and so of French ~capter~, ~captif~, and ~accepter~), while ~avoir~ comes from *~gʰh₁bʰ-~. Two unrelated “have” verbs were independently pressed into the very same grammatical service.
