That convergence is a hallmark of the Western European ~Sprachbund~ — a zone where neighboring but unrelated languages, through centuries of contact, grow shared structures: the definite article, the relative pronoun, and precisely this split between ~être~ and ~avoir~ (German ~sein~ and ~haben~) as the two auxiliaries of the past (~je suis allé~ / ~j'ai mangé~ beside ~ich bin gegangen~ / ~ich habe gegessen~). These traits feel inevitable to a European but are unusual among the world's languages. That grammar, no less than vocabulary, can be borrowed across a frontier is the quiet lesson of ~avoir~.
en.avouer:
From Old French ~avoer~ (“to acknowledge, recognize, accept as one's own — especially to recognize as a protector or lord”), from Latin ~advocare~ (“to call to oneself, summon”), built from the prefix ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and ~vocare~ (“to call”). Some authorities note an alternate influence from ~advotare~ (related to ~votum~, “a vow”), paralleling ~dévouer~ from ~devotare~. The verb is kin to Italian ~avvocare~ and to the English borrowing ~avow~.

The medieval sense was feudal and legal: to ~avouer~ a lord was publicly to acknowledge him as one's protector, and to ~avouer~ a deed was to claim and own it. From “acknowledging as one's own,” the meaning slid toward acknowledging a fact about oneself — and so to the modern sense “to confess, admit,” often something one would rather not (~avouer un crime~, ~avouer sa défaite~). The same ~vocare~ runs through a large French family: ~avocat~ (“lawyer,” the advocate “called to” a case), ~vouer~ and ~dévouer~, ~évoquer~, ~invoquer~, ~provoquer~, ~convoquer~, and ~vocation~. A confession, in this old light, is the act of calling a truth toward yourself and owning it before others.
en.avérer:
From Medieval Latin or a Vulgar Latin *~adverare~, built on ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) plus the adjective ~verus~ (“true”). ~Verus~ is inherited through Proto-Italic *~wēros~ from Proto-Indo-European *~weh₁ros~ (“true, trustworthy”) — the same root that, through Germanic, gives German ~wahr~ (“true”) and, through Old French ~verai~, the English adverb ~very~, which once meant “truly.”

The original sense was juridical: to ~avérer~ a fact was to establish it as true, to prove or verify it, and Old and Middle French used it transitively in just that way. Modern French has largely shifted to the reflexive ~s'avérer~ (“to prove to be, to turn out”) — a thing ~s'avère~ exact, or false, or impossible. This produces a famous purist's quibble: since the verb already contains “true,” the common phrase ~s'avérer faux~ (“to prove false”) is, taken literally, a small contradiction (“to prove-true to be false”), though usage has long since accepted it. From the same ~verus~ French keeps ~vrai~ (its popular descendant), ~vérité~ (“truth”), ~vérifier~ (“to verify”), and ~véridique~ (“truthful”).
en.baisser:
From Middle French, from Old French ~baissier~ (eleventh century, “to lower”), from Vulgar Latin *~bassiāre~ (“to make low”), a verb derived from Late Latin ~bassus~ (“low, base, short”). The same ~bassus~ gave French the adjective ~bas~, Italian ~basso~ and Spanish ~bajo~, and, through musical Italian, English ~bass~ — the lowest voice. *~Bassiāre~ thus means, transparently, “to bring low.”

The verb has stayed close to its literal sense throughout: to lower a thing physically — to ~baisser~ the eyes, the head, a curtain, a window — and from there to turn down (a volume, a price) and to decline (the sun lowers, prices fall, strength wanes). The reflexive ~se baisser~ means to stoop or bend down. From the same ~bas~ family come ~abaisser~ (“to lower, to humble”), ~rabaisser~ (“to belittle”), ~baisse~ (“a fall, a drop”), ~soubassement~ (“a base, a footing”), and the bas-relief of sculpture, ~bas-relief~, a carving that stands only slightly proud of its background. The whole cluster turns on a single idea: lowness, and the act of bringing down toward it.
en.baser:
From the noun ~base~ with the suffix ~-er~ (15th century). The noun ~base~ comes, through Old French, from Latin ~basis~ (“pedestal, foundation”), itself borrowed from Ancient Greek ~βάσις~ (“a step, that on which one stands”). The Greek noun belongs to the verb ~βαίνω~ (“to go, to walk, to step”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷem-~ (“to go, to come”). Through that same root, ~base~ is distantly cognate with English ~come~ and Latin ~venire~ (“to come”).

The path of meaning is wonderfully concrete: a ~βάσις~ was first the act of stepping, then the thing one steps onto — a pedestal — and finally any foundation or supporting surface. From that physical sense the verb ~baser~ generalized to the abstract “to found, to ground,” as in an argument ~basé sur~ the evidence. The same Greek-Latin family yields a rich harvest in modern French: ~base~ in all its senses, ~basique~, ~baside~, and ~soubassement~ (“understructure”). English ~basis~, ~base~, and ~basement~ descend along the same line, making the French verb a near twin of an everyday English word.
en.battre:
From Latin ~battuere~ (“to beat, to strike, to thrash”), a verb almost certainly borrowed from Gaulish: native Latin words rarely begin with ~b-~, and the Celtic cousins are clear — Welsh ~bathu~ (“to coin money, to strike”), Old Breton ~bat~, and Irish ~bath~. The ultimate source is often traced to the Proto-Indo-European root *~bʰewh₂-~ (“to strike”). Rare in classical literature but vigorous in spoken Latin, ~battuere~ flourished in the Romance languages: Italian ~battere~ and Spanish ~batir~ are its direct heirs, and English ~batter~, ~battle~, and ~battalion~ all reach back to it through French.

The word's most charming offshoot is Late Latin ~battuālia~ (“the beatings”), the name for the practice combats of gladiators and soldiers; this became Old French ~bataille~ and English ~battle~ — so every battle is, etymologically, a drill in striking. ~Battre~ stayed earthily concrete in French: to beat eggs, beat a drum, beat a rival, beat a path. From it spread a wide family — ~battement~, ~bataille~, ~batterie~ (whether of guns, of a kitchen, or of a drum kit), ~combattre~, ~débattre~, ~abattre~, and ~rabattre~. Even a heated ~débat~ is, at root, two sides beating at each other. A modest verb for hitting things turns out to underlie much of the vocabulary of conflict.
en.blesser:
From Old French ~blecier~ (“to bruise, to injure”), from Gallo-Romance *~blettiare~ (“to make bruised, to make soft like overripe fruit”). The ultimate source is Germanic: Frankish, from Proto-West Germanic *~blaitijan~ (“to bruise, to make discoloured”), a factitive verb formed on *~blait~ (“pale, livid, discoloured”) with the causative suffix *~-jan~. The same Germanic root surfaces in Old English ~blātian~ (“to grow pale”).

The original image was vivid and physical: to ~blesser~ was to mark flesh with the dull, discoloured patch of a bruise — the very softness an apple shows when it turns ~blet~ (“overripe, bruised”), a French word from the same Germanic family. Over time the verb shifted from this narrow sense of bruising toward the general meanings “to wound” and “to injure,” whether by blade, bullet, or accident. By extension it also came to mean “to hurt” emotionally: harsh words can ~blesser~ as surely as a weapon, and one speaks of ~amour-propre blessé~ (“wounded pride”). Related French words include the noun ~blessure~ (“wound, injury”) and the participle ~blessé~ used as a noun for a casualty or injured person.
en.bloquer:
French ~bloquer~ is a denominal verb, coined in the fifteenth century from the noun ~bloc~ (“block, log”) — or its military relative ~blocus~. The noun ~bloc~ is not Latin at all: Old French ~bloc~ was taken from Middle Dutch ~blok~ (“tree-trunk”), from Proto-Germanic *~blukką~ (“beam, log”), traced by many to the Proto-Indo-European root *~bʰelǵ-~ (“plank, beam”). The same Germanic word gave English ~block~ and German ~Block~, so ~bloquer~ and to ~block~ are, satisfyingly, the very same word doing the same job in two languages.

The semantics follow the wood. To set a ~bloc~ in a doorway is to jam it shut, and from that ~bloquer~ generalized to stopping, jamming, or barricading anything — a road, a door, an account, a salary, a negotiation, or, in football, an opponent. French also distinguishes the borrowed Dutch ~blokken~ (“to cram, to swot”) in some dialects, but the mainstream verb is wholly about obstruction. From the same block of wood come ~blocage~ (“blocking, a mental block”), ~débloquer~ (“to unblock, to release funds”), ~blocus~ (“blockade”), and the borrowed-back ~blockhaus~ — a whole family piled up, log upon log, from one Germanic ~bloc~.
en.boire:
From Old French ~boivre~ (also ~beivre~, ~bevvre~), from Latin ~bibere~ (“to drink”). The Latin verb goes back to Proto-Italic *~pibō~, a reduplicated form whose initial ~p~ voiced to ~b~, and ultimately to the Proto-Indo-European root *~peh₃-~ (“to drink”). The kinship is spectacular: Sanskrit ~píbati~, Ancient Greek ~πῑ́νω~ (~pī́nō~), and Proto-Slavic *~piti~ all descend from the same root, as does — through a sister formation — Latin ~pōtiō~, source of English ~potion~.

The phonetic history is a small drama. The Old French stem ~bev-~ underwent labialization, passing through ~beuv-~ to ~buv-~, which is why the modern verb shifts shape across its conjugation: ~je bois~ but ~nous buvons~, ~ils boivent~ but ~je buvais~. The older ~beuv-~ form fossilized in ~beuverie~ (“drinking bout”). The same root waters a whole field of French words: ~boisson~, ~breuvage~, ~buvable~, ~buvette~, ~buveur~, and ~pourboire~ (literally “for drinking,” a tip). The everyday act named here is, etymologically, one of humanity's oldest spoken words.
en.bâtir:
From Old French ~bastir~ (“to baste, to interweave”), from Old Low Frankish *~bastjan~ (“to assemble, to interlace fibers”), from a Proto-Germanic root meaning bast, the fibrous inner bark used for tying and weaving. The word is cognate with German ~Bast~ and Dutch ~bast~ (“bast, inner bark”). The circumflex of ~bâtir~ marks a lost ~s~ that the older spelling ~bastir~ still showed.

The development is a small lesson in how houses were once made. From the sense “to weave, to lash together with bast,” the word moved to “to make a fence of stakes interwoven with branches,” the wattle of early construction, and from there simply to “to build, to erect.” French thus reached its grand modern verb — ~bâtir~ une maison, une cathédrale, une fortune — from a humble word for tying plant fibers together. (The competing guess that linked it to ~bât~, “packsaddle,” or ~bâton~, “stick,” is rejected by the TLFi.) The Frankish origin left a compact French family: ~bâtiment~ (“building”), ~bâtisse~ (“edifice”), ~bâtisseur~ (“builder”), and ~rebâtir~ (“to rebuild”) — all standing on a foundation of woven bast. The older sewing sense survives separately in ~bâtir~ meaning “to tack, to baste” cloth.
en.bénéficier:
From the noun ~bénéfice~, itself from Latin ~beneficium~ (“a kindness, favor, service”); the verb mirrors Medieval Latin ~beneficiāre~ (“to grant a benefice to”). ~Beneficium~ is built from ~bene~ (“well”) and a combining form of ~facere~ (“to do, to make”), so it means literally “a thing well done.” ~Facere~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to set, put, place”), shared with English ~do~ and ~deed~ and with Greek ~títhēmi~ (“I place”). Romance kin include Italian ~beneficiare~ and Spanish ~beneficiar~.

The Latin word named an act of generosity, a good turn done for another. In the Middle Ages the Church narrowed ~beneficium~ to a very specific gift: a ~bénéfice~ became an endowed ecclesiastical post and the income that came with it — a clergyman’s living. From that benefit-as-income the word broadened back out to commercial profit and, in the verb ~bénéficier~, to the general sense “to enjoy, to profit from” an advantage. The ~bene-~ element survives in ~bénir~ (“to bless”), ~bénévole~ (“volunteer,” literally “well-wishing”), and ~bienfait~ (“good deed”); the ~facere~ element seeds a teeming family — ~faire~, ~défaire~, ~parfait~, ~bienfaiteur~, and the everyday ~bénéfice~ itself.
en.cacher:
From Old French ~cachier~ (also ~quachier~), from Vulgar Latin *~coacticare~ (“to compress, to press together”), a frequentative formed on Latin ~coactare~ (“to constrain, to force”), itself from ~cogere~ (“to drive together, to compel,” from ~co-~ + ~agere~, “to drive”). Through ~cogere~ the verb is cognate with Italian ~quattare~ and with the French doublet ~cacher~ beside the learned ~coaguler~.

The original idea was purely physical: to press or squeeze something down. The path to “to hide” runs through that pressing — what is pressed flat or tucked away is concealed — and the sense “to hide” stayed comparatively rare until the 16th century, when ~cacher~ rose to displace older rivals for concealment such as ~musser~, ~esconser~, and ~repondre~. The pressing sense survives in the cousin ~écacher~ (“to crush, to flatten”). From the same Latin ~cogere~ family French keeps ~cachette~ (“hiding place”), ~cache~, ~cache-cache~ (the game “hide-and-seek”), ~cachot~ (“dungeon”), and ~cachotterie~ (“a small secret”) — a whole nest of words for hiding spun out of an ancient verb meaning, of all things, “to squeeze.”
en.calculer:
From Latin ~calculāre~ (“to reckon, compute”), from ~calculus~, the diminutive of ~calx~ (“limestone, pebble”). A ~calculus~ was a small stone — and specifically the little counters that Romans pushed along the grooves of an ~abacus~ to do sums. To calculate was, literally, to move pebbles. ~Calx~ is perhaps borrowed from Greek ~khálix~ (“gravel, small stone”), though the connection is uncertain. Italian ~calcolare~ and Spanish ~calcular~ share the root.

The charm of this etymology is how it fossilizes an ancient counting board inside a word for abstract arithmetic: every calculation still carries, etymologically, a handful of pebbles. The same ~calculus~ gives medicine its sense of a “stone” formed in the body — a kidney ~calculus~ — and gives mathematics the ~calculus~ of Newton and Leibniz, the supreme reckoning. French keeps ~calcul~ (both “calculation” and the medical “stone”), ~calculer~, ~calculateur~, and ~calculatrice~. From the parent ~calx~ in its limestone sense also descend ~chaux~ (“lime”) and ~calcaire~ (“limestone, calcareous”), so the humble pebble underlies both arithmetic and the white cliffs of chalk.
en.caractériser:
From the noun ~caractère~ plus the suffix ~-iser~. ~Caractère~ comes from Latin ~character~, itself borrowed from Ancient Greek ~χαρακτήρ~ (~kharaktḗr~), an “engraved mark, stamp, distinctive impression,” derived from ~χαράσσω~ (~kharássō~, “to engrave, scratch, sharpen”). Originally the word named both the engraver's tool and the mark it cut — a stamped sign that set one thing apart from another. The suffix ~-iser~ descends from Latin ~-izāre~, from Greek ~-ίζειν~ (~-ízein~), the ending behind English ~-ize~.

The semantic arc is elegant: a physical ~mark~ engraved on metal or wax became the figurative “stamp” of a personality, then the sum of traits defining a person, and finally any distinguishing feature at all. To ~caractériser~ is thus to identify the mark that sets something apart — to pin down its defining trait. The Greek root left French a sizeable family: ~caractère~, ~caractéristique~, ~caractérisation~, and ~caractériel~. English ~character~, ~characteristic~, and ~characterize~ are the exact cousins, and even the typographic ~character~ on a screen preserves the original sense of an engraved, distinctive sign.
en.casser:
French ~casser~ first appears around 1100 as ~quasser~ “to break into pieces,” taking its modern shape by about 1160. It descends from Latin ~quassāre~ “to shake violently, to shatter,” itself a frequentative — an intensive, repetitive form — of ~quatere~ “to shake.” That verb goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~kʷet-~ “to shake.” The same Latin source, by way of Old French, gave English ~quash~ “to crush, to annul.” But a second, homophonous Latin verb left its mark too: Late Latin ~cassāre~ “to annul, to make void,” derived from the adjective ~cassus~ “empty, vain.” The two converged so completely in French that one verb came to carry both meanings.

This fusion explains a remarkable split in sense. The ~quassāre~ side gives the physical meanings — to break a glass, and figuratively to wear out or exhaust. The ~cassāre~ side gives the legal one: “to annul, to overturn,” enshrined in the Cour de cassation, France's supreme court, which can quash a lower judgment. So when a verdict is set aside, it is, etymologically, declared empty rather than broken. From the shaking root also descend the rare ~quassation~ and French ~fracasser~ “to smash” and ~concasser~ “to crush,” alongside the everyday ~casse-tête~ “puzzle,” literally a head-breaker.
en.causer:
Modern French ~causer~ conceals two distinct verbs that fell together by sound. The first, “to cause,” was derived around 1275 from the noun ~cause~ plus the verbal suffix ~-er~, ~cause~ itself coming from Latin ~causa~ (“cause, reason, lawsuit”). The second, “to chat,” descends directly from Latin ~causari~ (“to plead a case, debate, dispute”), a verb built on that same ~causa~. So both ultimately rest on ~causa~, cognate with Italian ~cosa~ and Spanish ~cosa~ (“thing”) and with the English borrowing ~cause~.

The surprise lies in the second verb's drift. In Latin, ~causari~ belonged to the courtroom: to argue a legal cause. As the centuries passed, the solemn sense of pleading and debating softened into mere talking, then into friendly chatter — so that today ~causer~ most often means simply “to have a chat,” and to ~causer de la pluie et du beau temps~ is to make small talk. From ~causa~ in its “thing” sense Latin also gave Romance its everyday word for an object (Italian ~cosa~), while French preserved the learned ~cause~ alongside ~causerie~ (“an informal talk”), ~causette~, and ~accuser~ (literally to call something to one's cause).
en.cesser:
From Latin ~cessāre~ (“to delay, to hold back, to leave off, to be idle”), the frequentative of ~cēdere~ (“to go, to withdraw, to yield”), whose past-participle stem ~cess-~ supplies the longer verb. Latin ~cēdere~ comes, through Proto-Italic *~kesd-o-~ (“to go away, to avoid”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ḱesd-~. Because a frequentative names a repeated or lingering action, ~cessāre~ originally suggested keeping on holding back — hence “to tarry,” and from there “to stop.” Italian ~cessare~ and Spanish ~cesar~ continue the same verb directly.

The semantic drift is gentle: from “withdrawing” to “hanging back” to simply “ceasing.” French kept the literary, slightly formal ring of the word — ~cesser de~ + infinitive, or the elevated ~sans cesse~ (“ceaselessly”), where the bare noun ~cesse~ survives almost only in that fixed phrase. The base verb ~cēdere~ gave French ~céder~ (“to yield”) directly, so ~cesser~ and ~céder~ are doublets sprung from the same Latin root by different paths. The wider family is large and legal-flavored: ~cessation~, ~cession~, ~cessible~, ~incessant~, ~incessamment~, and, through the ~cēdere~ branch, ~accéder~, ~procéder~, ~succéder~. A pleasant surprise: the English ~cease~ and the courtroom ~cease and desist~ rest on the very same Roman idea of stepping back and leaving off.
en.changer:
From Late Latin ~cambiāre~ (“to exchange, barter”), a variant of Classical ~cambīre~, a word of Gaulish origin tracing to Proto-Celtic *~kambos~ (“bent, crooked”) and the PIE root *~kamb-~ (“to bend, to curve”). The notion of bending underlies that of swapping one thing for its counterpart. ~Changer~ is cognate with Italian ~cambiare~, Spanish ~cambiar~, Occitan ~cambiar~, and Catalan ~canviar~.

The sound history is itself memorable: when Latin ~-bi-~ before a vowel followed a bilabial, it palatalized to ~-ge-~, the same shift that turned ~simium~ into ~singe~ and ~rabia~ into ~rage~ — hence ~cambiāre~ became ~changer~. Borrowed straight from Gaulish into provincial Latin, the verb is a small monument to Celtic survival in everyday French. Its descendants form a busy family: ~change~ (money exchange), ~changement~, ~échanger~, ~rechange~, and ~changeur~. The learned Italian doublet ~cambiare~ also re-entered French finance, yielding ~cambiste~ (“foreign-exchange dealer”) and ~cambisme~ — so a Gaulish word for “bending” still names the people who trade one currency for another.
en.chanter:
French ~chanter~ descends, through Middle and Old French ~chanter~ (with the northern variant ~canter~), from Latin ~cantāre~ (“to sing”). ~Cantāre~ is the frequentative of ~canere~ (“to sing, to play, to recite”), so it originally meant something like “to keep singing”; it goes back to a Proto-Indo-European root *~keh₂n-~ (“to sing”). Cognates dot the family tree: Welsh ~canu~ (“to sing”) is a direct Celtic relative, Old Irish ~canaid~ another, and — more unexpectedly — English ~hen~, the “singer” among fowl, belongs to the same root. Italian ~cantare~ and Spanish ~cantar~ are its Romance twins, and English borrowed the learned ~chant~ from French itself.

The initial Latin c- softened to the French ch- sound in the north, which is why the verb is ~chanter~ and not the Picard ~canter~ — a split still visible in place and family names. The meaning held steady at “to sing,” but French spun off vivid idioms: ~faire chanter quelqu'un~ (“to make someone sing”) means to blackmail, and ~chanter~ in the dismissive ~si ça te chante~ means “if it appeals to you.” The root seeded a tuneful family: ~chant~, ~chanson~ and ~chansonnier~, ~chanteur~ and ~cantatrice~, ~enchanter~ (literally “to sing a spell upon,” from ~incantāre~), ~cantique~, and ~cantate~. To enchant, fittingly, is to sing magic into being.
en.charger:
From Old French ~chargier~ (“to load, to put in a cart”), attested around 1080, from Late Latin *~carricāre~ (“to load a wagon”), a verb built on Latin ~carrus~ (“wheeled cart, war chariot”). ~Carrus~ was itself borrowed from Gaulish ~karros~, from Proto-Celtic *~karros~ (“wagon”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ḱers-~ (“to run”). The same Romance verb survives as Italian ~caricare~, Spanish ~cargar~, and Portuguese ~carregar~.

The core image is concrete: to ~charger~ was first to pile goods onto a cart, and from that load the meaning fanned out — to burden a person, to fill a weapon (a gun is ~chargé~), to entrust someone with a task (~charger quelqu'un de~), and, in the modern age, to charge a battery or load a file. French kept a learned doublet, ~carguer~, a sailor's term for furling a sail. Strikingly, the humble Gaulish cart-word seeded a whole network of French and English vocabulary through that same “to run” root: ~char~, ~chariot~, ~charrette~, ~carrière~ (“career,” originally a racecourse), and the borrowed ~cargo~ and ~charge~ all trace back to the wheels that once rolled across Roman Gaul.
en.chercher:
From Old French ~cerchier~ (also ~cercer~, “to travel through, go around”), from Late Latin ~circāre~ (“to go around, traverse”), a verb derived from ~circus~ (“ring, circle”) and the preposition ~circā~ (“around, about”). ~Circus~ comes, through Greek ~kríkos~ / ~kírkos~ (“ring”), from PIE *~sker-~ (“to turn, to bend”). The same Old French ~cerchier~ crossed the Channel and gave English ~search~, which preserves the older form.

~Circāre~ first meant to move in a circuit around something, and the early verb described traversing a region — even moving one's hand about in all directions — before the dominant sense narrowed to “to look for, to seek.” In doing so ~chercher~ displaced the older ~querre~, which survives marginally as ~quérir~. The original idea of going around lingers in the noun ~recherche~ and the agent ~chercheur~, who circles a problem until it yields. From the same root *~sker-~ French keeps ~cirque~, ~cercle~, ~cerceau~, ~circuit~, and ~recherche~ itself — a whole vocabulary of rings and rounds standing behind the everyday act of searching.
en.choisir:
French ~choisir~ (attested around 1050) comes from Old French ~coisir~ (“to perceive, to discern, then to select”), borrowed from the Frankish verb *~kausjan~ (“to taste, to test, to try”). This is one of the many Germanic words the Franks left embedded in French. The Frankish form goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵews-~ (“to taste, to choose”), the very same root that gave Latin ~gustāre~ (“to taste”) — so ~choisir~ and ~goûter~ are distant relatives. Through that Germanic branch it is cognate with English ~choose~, German ~kiesen~ (“to choose, elect”), and Dutch ~kiezen~, all sharing the idea of testing a thing before taking it.

The semantic thread is intuitive: one tastes or tries, then discerns, then picks. Old French ~coisir~ first meant “to make out, to catch sight of,” and only gradually settled into the sense “to select,” pushing aside the older verb ~élire~ (from Latin ~ēligere~), which narrowed to formal “electing.” The shift from a verb of perception to a verb of decision mirrors how English ~choose~ itself developed from “taste, test.” From the same Germanic and Indo-European stock, French keeps the noun ~choix~ (“choice”), back-formed from the verb, and — by the Latin route — the gustatory family of ~goût~ and ~déguster~.
en.citer:
From Latin ~citare~ (“to set in motion, rouse, summon, call forth”), the frequentative of ~ciēre~ (“to move, stir, call”). The verb was borrowed into French as a learned word. ~ciēre~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~keih₁-~ (“to set in motion, to move”), making it cognate with Greek ~kīneîn~ (“to move, set in motion” — the source of ~cinéma~ and ~kinetic~) and with Sanskrit ~cyávate~ (“he stirs himself, sets out”). To cite is, etymologically, to put something or someone into motion.

That original kinetic sense explains the verb’s spread of meanings. To ~citer~ first meant to “summon” — to call a person before a court or to muster troops — and from “calling someone forth” came “calling forth someone’s words,” that is, to quote, and “calling out a name,” to mention. The motion sense stays vivid in the Latin-built relatives French inherited: ~exciter~ (“to excite, stir up,” from ~ex-~ + ~citare~), ~inciter~ (“to incite, urge on”), ~susciter~ (“to arouse, give rise to”), and ~solliciter~. The noun ~citation~ keeps both the courtroom summons and the quoted passage under one roof — two faces of a single act of “calling forth.”
en.classer:
From the noun ~classe~ (“class, category”), with the suffix ~-er~. The noun was borrowed from Latin ~classis~, a word that originally meant a “summons” or “call to arms” and then the categories of citizens that could be called up — the political-military divisions into which the Roman census sorted the population by wealth. Cognates of the verb include Italian ~classificare~ and Spanish ~clasificar~, and through the noun, English ~class~ and ~classify~.

The semantic journey runs from the muster-roll to the filing cabinet. From the Roman census-classes, ~classe~ generalized into any rank or category, then into the schoolroom ~classe~ and, by extension, the verb ~classer~: to sort, to file, to rank. The military memory lingers in French phrases — a ~classe d'âge~ is a cohort of conscripts, and ~classer une affaire~ is to file a case away, to close it. The noun also spawned ~classique~ (“classical,” first the literature of the top “class” of writers), and the verb gave ~classement~ (a ranking or league table), ~classeur~ (a binder or filing folder), and ~classification~. The prefixed forms ~reclasser~ (to reclassify, or to redeploy a worker) and ~déclasser~ (to downgrade) round out a family all descended from a Roman call to arms.
en.cliquer:
Formed in French from the onomatopoeia ~clic~, an imitation of a short, dry sound — the snap of a latch or the tap of a key — to which the verbal ending ~-er~ was added. It belongs to a small family of sound-words that includes ~claquer~ (“to slam, to clack”) and ~clicher~ (which gave ~cliché~, originally a printer's stereotype plate, named for the clicking noise the casting tool made). The pair ~clic~ and ~clac~ even survive together in the expression ~clic-clac~.

For most of its life ~cliquer~ named a faint mechanical noise and was rare. Its fortune was made by the computer mouse: the modern sense “to click” is a calque on English ~click~, itself an independent onomatopoeia of the same kind, so the two languages arrived at nearly identical words by imitating the same sound. From the family come ~cliquet~ (“a pawl, a ratchet”), ~cliqueter~ and ~cliquetis~ (“to jingle / a jingling”), and the borrowed ~cliché~. Few verbs show so clearly how a noise, frozen into a syllable, can be conjugated.
en.coller:
A denominal verb, formed in French directly from the noun ~colle~ (“glue, paste”). That noun comes from Vulgar Latin *~colla~, borrowed from Ancient Greek ~κόλλα~ (~kólla~, “glue”). The Greek word is the same one hidden inside English ~collagen~ (literally “glue-producer”) and ~protocol~, and it gives French ~collage~, ~colloïde~, and ~collagène~.

To ~coller~ is first of all to stick or glue one thing to another, but French has let the verb wander into a wealth of figurative and slang senses. Something that fits or works can ~coller~ (~ça colle~, “that works, that’s fine”); a person who clings to you ~colle~; and in everyday speech to ~coller~ someone is to stump them with a hard question — which is exactly why ~une colle~ became student slang for a tough quiz or oral exam, and why a school detention is also ~une colle~ (originally being “stuck” at school). A demanding examiner is even called a ~colleur~. From the same Greek-derived root come ~décoller~ (“to unstick,” and so “to take off” in an aeroplane), ~encoller~, and the noun ~colle~ in all its glue-bound and exam-bound meanings.
en.combattre:
A French compound of the prefix ~com-~ (a form of ~con-~, here “with, against”) and the verb ~battre~ (“to beat, to strike”) — literally, to beat against one another. French ~battre~ descends from Latin ~battuere~ (“to beat, to strike”), a word the Romans seem to have borrowed from Gaulish; its further origin is debated, perhaps a Proto-Indo-European root *~bʰat-~ (“to strike”), with a possible Celtic cognate in Welsh ~bathu~ (“to beat, to coin”).

The compound was already in Old French (~combatre~), and its sense has scarcely budged: to fight, to do battle, and figuratively to oppose or strive against (one ~combats~ an idea, a disease, an injustice). The whole ~battuere~ family is a battlefield in itself: from it French draws ~combat~, ~combattant~, ~bataille~ (“battle,” via Late Latin ~battālia~), ~bataillon~, ~débattre~ (“to debate,” to beat back and forth), ~abattre~ (“to fell, to slaughter,” whence ~abattoir~), ~rabattre~, and the simple ~battre~ of beating eggs or hearts. English borrowed much of the same cluster — ~combat~, ~battle~, ~battalion~, ~debate~, ~abattoir~ — all of it ringing with the old sound of blows struck.
en.combiner:
From Late Latin ~combīnāre~ (“to join two by two, to pair”), formed from ~com-~ (“together”) and the distributive numeral ~bīnī~ (“two each, in pairs”). ~Bīnī~ comes from Proto-Italic *~dwiznos~, built on ~bis~ (“twice”) — both ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root for “two,” *~dwóh₁~. Cognate, through that root, with English ~binary~ and ~twin~, with Latin ~duo~, and with the Italian and Spanish doublets ~combinare~ and ~combinar~.

The verb's literal sense — “to set things two by two” — is still visible: to ~combiner~ is to bring elements together so they pair up and work as one. From the same ~bis~ / ~bīnī~ stock French inherited a whole numerical family: ~binaire~, ~combinaison~, ~combinatoire~, and the everyday ~combine~ (a scheme or trick) and ~combinard~ (a schemer). The English ~combine~ is the same Latin verb, borrowed; in farming, a “combine harvester” is so named because it combines several operations. What began as a precise Roman way of counting in twos thus widened into the general idea of joining, arranging, and contriving — and, less flatteringly, of cooking up a clever ~combine~.
en.commander:
From Old French ~comander~ (eleventh century), from Vulgar Latin *~commandare~, an alteration of Latin ~commendare~ (“to entrust, to commit to one's care”), formed from ~com-~ (intensive) + ~mandare~ (“to hand over, to charge with a task”). ~Mandare~ itself is usually traced to ~manus~ (“hand”) + ~dare~ (“to give”) — to “give into the hand.” The Romance cousins are everywhere: Italian ~comandare~, Spanish ~comandar~ and ~mandar~, and, by Norman transmission, English ~command~.

The semantic slide is striking. In classical Latin ~commendare~ leaned toward “to entrust, to recommend,” but in Late Latin and Old French it hardened into “to order, to have authority over”: what you place in someone's hand as a charge becomes what you impose on them as a command. French preserves both faces of the word — ~commander~ a soldier is to give an order, while ~commander~ a meal is to place one's request, the older sense of “committing a task” still visible at the table. Kin from the same root crowd the language: ~commande~, ~commandement~, ~commandant~, ~recommander~, and, directly from ~mandare~, ~mander~, ~mandat~, and ~demander~.
en.commencer:
From Old French ~comencier~ (“to begin”), from Vulgar Latin *~cominitiāre~, from ~com-~ (an intensive of ~cum~, “with, together”) + ~initiāre~ (“to initiate, begin”), from ~initium~ (“a beginning, entrance”), itself from ~inīre~ (“to go in,” ~in-~ + ~īre~, “to go”). Cognate with Italian ~cominciare~, Spanish ~comenzar~, and Portuguese ~começar~; English ~commence~ was borrowed from the Old French verb.

The sense traces back to a literal act of entering: ~initium~ is the going-in, the threshold of a thing, and to ~initiāre~ was to set someone or something on its way — in religion, to admit to the rites (hence English ~initiation~). Vulgar Latin reinforced it with ~com-~ and reshaped it as *~cominitiāre~, simply “to begin.” From the same ~īre~ (“to go,” PIE *~ey-~) French draws a surprising scatter of words once you trace the path — ~initial~, ~initier~, ~initiative~ — and ~īre~ itself underlies ~issue~ (“a going out”) and the future stems of ~aller~. ~Recommencer~ (“to begin again”) layers ~re-~ on top, while the noun ~commencement~ names the start that ~commencer~ performs.
en.commettre:
From Latin ~committere~ (“to bring together, to join, to entrust, to perpetrate”), compounded from ~com-~ (“together, with”) and ~mittere~ (“to send, to let go, to throw”). Latin ~mittere~ goes back to a Proto-Indo-European root reconstructed as *~meit-~ (“to exchange, to remove”), whose sense shifted from “exchange, give” to “let go, send.” The Old French ~commetre~ inherited both branches of meaning. English ~commit~ is the learned borrowing of the same ~committere~, and Italian ~commettere~ and Spanish ~cometer~ are direct cognates.

The two faces of ~commettre~ trace back to ~com-~ + ~mittere~: from “to send together, to entrust” come the senses of appointing or charging someone with a task, while from a darker sense of “to bring about” comes the modern, dominant meaning — to ~commettre~ a crime, an error, a sin. The shared root ~mittere~ seeds an enormous French vocabulary: ~mettre~ itself, plus ~admettre~, ~permettre~, ~promettre~, ~soumettre~, ~transmettre~, ~mission~, ~message~, and ~messe~ (the Mass, from the dismissal ~ite, missa est~). The administrative offspring of ~committere~ proper — ~commis~, ~commissaire~, ~commission~, ~commissure~ — preserve the older idea of someone or something entrusted and brought together.
en.communiquer:
From Latin ~communicare~ (“to share, to make common, to impart”), the verb of ~communis~ (“common, shared by all”). ~communis~ is usually analysed as a Proto-Italic compound *~kom-moin-i-~, from *~kom-~ (“together”) plus a form of the Proto-Indo-European root *~mey-~ (“to change, to exchange”), the same root behind Latin ~munus~ (“duty, gift, public office”) and ~mutuus~ (“reciprocal”). It is a learned doublet of French ~communier~ (“to take communion”), which descends from the same Latin verb by the ordinary popular route. Cognate in sense with English ~common~ and ~mutual~, and with Gothic ~gamains~ (“common”).

In Latin, to ~communicare~ was to put something into the common stock — to share a meal, a duty, or a piece of news. French inherited both the sacred branch, where what is shared is the Eucharist (~communier~), and the secular branch, where what is shared is information (~communiquer~). The modern explosion of senses — to transmit, to connect, to get a message across — grew naturally from that core idea of holding something in common. The same root *~mey-~ feeds a large French family: ~commun~, ~communauté~, ~communisme~, ~communication~, ~municipal~, and ~immunité~, this last literally an exemption from the shared ~munus~, the public duty owed to the city.
en.comparer:
From Latin ~comparāre~ (“to pair, to match, to set side by side”), a compound of ~com-~ (“together, with”) and ~parāre~ (“to make ready, to furnish”), and closely tied to ~pār~ (“equal, a pair”). ~Parāre~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to produce, to procure, to bring forth”). Cognate through ~parāre~ with Italian ~comparare~ and Spanish ~comparar~, and through ~pār~ with English ~pair~ and ~peer~.

The original image is one of pairing: to ~comparāre~ was to bring two things together as a matched set, and from that act of setting equals beside each other grew the modern sense of weighing one against another. The kinship with ~pār~ keeps that idea of equality alive — a comparison still implies things placed on a level. From ~parāre~ French draws a remarkably broad family: ~préparer~ (“to make ready beforehand”), ~réparer~ (“to set right again”), ~séparer~ (“to make ready apart”), and ~parer~ itself, while ~pār~ yields ~pair~, ~paire~, ~pareil~, and ~comparaison~. Behind all of them lies the same quiet idea of furnishing, matching, and bringing things into order.
en.compliquer:
From Latin ~complicāre~ (“to fold together, to roll up”), formed from the prefix ~com-~ (“together”) and ~plicāre~ (“to fold”). ~plicāre~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~plek-~ (“to plait, to fold”), shared with Greek ~plékein~ (“to braid”) and, by way of Germanic, with English ~fold~ and ~ply~. French took ~compliquer~ as a learned borrowing, which kept it close to its Latin shape.

The original picture is physical: things folded over one another until they tangle. From that, ~compliquer~ moved naturally to the abstract sense of entangling a matter, multiplying its parts and difficulties — the literal “folded-together” becoming the figurative “involved, intricate.” English preserves the same image in ~complicate~ and in the doublet ~complice~/~accomplice~, a partner “folded in” to a plot. ~complicāre~ belongs to the broad *~plek-~ family that runs through French on the stem ~plicāre~: alongside ~compliqué~ (“complicated”) and ~complication~ stand ~appliquer~, ~impliquer~, ~expliquer~ (to “unfold,” hence to make clear), ~répliquer~, ~plier~, ~pli~, and ~ployer~. So to ~compliquer~ something and to ~expliquer~ it are exact opposites in their root image — folding together versus folding open.
en.compléter:
A denominal verb built on the adjective ~complet~ (“complete, full”), which comes from Classical Latin ~completus~, the adjectivized past participle of ~complere~ (“to fill up, to complete, to finish”). ~Complere~ is ~com-~ (intensive “fully”) plus ~plere~ (“to fill”), and ~plere~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~pleh₁-~ (“to fill”). That root is one of the most fruitful in the family: it is cognate with English ~full~ and ~plenty~, with Latin ~plenus~ (“full”) and Ancient Greek ~πλέως~ (~pleōs~, “full”).

The core image is of filling something to the brim — a ~complet~ thing is one that has been filled out, leaving nothing missing — and ~compléter~ is the act of topping it up, supplying what is lacking. French formed the verb from the adjective rather than inheriting it directly, which is why it has the learned ~-pl-~ of ~complet~ rather than a worn-down popular form. The same *~pleh₁-~ root spread widely into French: ~plein~ (“full”), ~remplir~ (“to fill”), ~plénitude~, ~complément~, ~supplément~, and even ~plus~ all draw on the idea of fullness or addition.
en.comporter:
From Latin ~comportare~ (“to carry together, to bring to one place”), a compound of the prefix ~com-~ (“with, together”) and ~portare~ (“to carry”). ~portare~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~per-~ (“to cross over, to pass through”), the same root behind ~portus~ (“harbor”) and ~porta~ (“gate”), both originally a passage one crosses. Through *~per-~ the verb is cognate with Ancient Greek ~peírō~ (“to pierce, to pass through”) and with English ~ford~ and ~fare~ (as in welfare and seafaring).

Latin ~comportare~ meant physically gathering things into one place; French ~comporter~ kept the idea of bringing together but turned it abstract — what a thing carries within itself. Hence its modern senses: a contract ~comporte~ several clauses (it includes them), a plan ~comporte~ risks (it entails them). The reflexive ~se comporter~ (“to behave”) is a separate development, borrowed from Italian ~comportarsi~, literally how one carries oneself. The root ~portare~ runs everywhere in French: ~porter~, ~apporter~, ~emporter~, ~transporter~, ~exporter~, ~supporter~, ~rapporter~, plus ~porte~ and ~port~ themselves — a whole vocabulary of carrying, doors, and harbors grown from a single idea of passage.
en.composer:
From Latin ~componere~ (“to put together, to assemble, to compose a work”), itself ~com-~ (“together”) + ~ponere~ (“to put, to place”). The regular phonetic descendant of ~ponere~ in Old French was ~pondre~, so ~componere~ should have given *~compondre~; instead the verb was remodeled on ~poser~ (“to set down, to lay”), a separate verb from Late Latin ~pausare~ (“to cease, to halt”), borrowed from Greek ~paûsis~ (“a stopping”). French ~poser~ thus carries the form of ~ponere~ but the body of ~pausare~, and ~composer~ inherited this hybrid. Cognate with Italian ~comporre~ and Spanish ~componer~, and, through ~ponere~, with English ~component~ and ~compound~.

The Latin sense “to arrange together” fanned out widely. One assembles musical or literary works (~composer une symphonie~), one constitutes a whole (the parts that ~composent~ a team), and one settles a quarrel by mutual concession — hence ~composer avec~ (“to come to terms with, to compromise with”). A distinctively modern branch is the telephone: to dial a number is to ~composer~ it, as if setting its digits in order like type in a printer's case — the same metaphor that gives the typesetter's ~composteur~. From ~ponere~ and its participle ~positus~ descends a vast family in French: ~poser~, ~position~, ~composant~, ~composition~, ~composite~, ~compositeur~, and the doublet ~pondre~ (“to lay an egg”), which preserves the unaltered sound shape that ~composer~ abandoned.
en.comprendre:
From Latin ~comprehendere~ (“to seize, to grasp, to take hold of together”), formed from the prefix ~com-~ (“together, wholly”) and ~prehendere~ (“to grasp, to seize”). That verb breaks down further into the element ~prae-~ and a root ~-hendere~, from the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʰend-~ (“to seize, to take”). Remarkably, that same PIE root gives English ~get~, so ~comprendre~ and ~get~ are deep cousins. Within Romance, ~comprendre~ stands beside Italian ~comprendere~ and Spanish ~comprender~.

The metaphor is one the French language never quite let go of: to understand is to ~grasp~. Latin ~comprehendere~ meant first to physically seize or enclose something, then to take it in with the mind, and French inherited both threads — ~comprendre~ can still mean “to include, to contain” as well as “to understand.” The ~prehendere~ root is unusually productive: French gets ~prendre~ (the shortened form) and with it ~apprendre~, ~surprendre~, ~entreprendre~, ~reprendre~, and ~prison~ (a place where one is “seized”), alongside the learned ~appréhender~, ~préhensile~, and ~répréhensible~.
en.compter:
From Old French ~conter~, from Latin ~computare~ (« to reckon, to calculate »), formed from ~com-~ (« together ») and ~putare~ (« to reckon »). ~putare~ originally meant « to prune, to clean », from ~putus~ (« clean »): to settle accounts was, figuratively, to « clean up » a tally. The same Latin verb is the source of Italian ~contare~, Spanish ~contar~, and — through Norman French — English ~count~.

Old French ~conter~ carried both senses « to count » and « to recount, to tell », since enumerating and narrating were one act. In the thirteenth century scribes split the word in two: a learned re-spelling ~compter~, restoring the ~p~ of Latin ~computare~, took the arithmetical sense, while ~conter~ kept the storytelling one — hence the modern pair ~compter~ « to count » and ~conter~ « to tell a tale ». The silent ~p~ of ~compter~ is therefore a deliberate etymological fossil. From the same root spring ~compte~, ~comptable~ (« accountant »), ~comptabilité~, and ~compteur~, while ~putare~ also yielded ~amputer~, ~disputer~, and ~réputation~.
en.concentrer:
Coined in French in the 17th century from the verb ~centrer~ (“to centre”) with the prefix ~con-~ (“together”), giving the sense “to bring together toward a centre.” ~Centrer~ rests on ~centre~, from Latin ~centrum~ (“centre, point of a compass”), itself borrowed from Ancient Greek ~kéntron~ (“sharp point, sting, goad, the fixed point of a compass”), a noun from ~kentéō~ (“to prick, to goad”). The image at the root is the sharp pivot-point that the other leg of a compass swings around.

The word's history is thus quite literally about points and centres. To ~concentrer~ is to draw things inward to a common centre — troops, efforts, attention, or a chemical solution, which one ~concentrer~ by boiling off water until the substance is dense. The reflexive ~se concentrer~ migrated into the mental sphere: to gather one's scattered attention to a single point, “to concentrate.” From ~centrum~ / ~kéntron~ come a wide family in French — ~centre~, ~central~, ~centrer~, ~excentrique~, ~concentrique~, ~concentration~, and ~concentré~ — all circling the idea of a fixed central point.
en.concerner:
From Latin ~concernere~ (« to mix, to sift together »), formed from the prefix ~con-~ (« together ») and the verb ~cernere~ (« to sift, to separate, to distinguish »). ~cernere~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~krinō~ to the Proto-Indo-European root *~krey-~ (“to sieve, to separate, to discriminate”), the same root that gave Ancient Greek ~krī́nō~ (“to separate, to judge”). It is thus a distant relative of English ~crisis~ and ~critic~.

The literal Latin image is agricultural: grain shaken through a sieve, the good separated from the chaff. From this physical sifting the verb drifted toward the mental act of distinguishing one thing from another, and in Medieval Latin ~concernere~ came to mean « to have regard to, to relate to. » French borrowed it as a learned word, and today ~concerner~ keeps only that abstract sense — « to touch upon, to be the business of. » The shared root *~krey-~ runs through a remarkable family of French words: ~certain~ (« sifted out, settled »), ~discerner~, ~secret~ (« set apart »), ~crime~, and even ~crible~, the sieve itself, which preserves the original gesture of separating grain.
en.concevoir:
From Old French ~conceveir~, from Latin ~concipere~ (“to take in, to take hold of, to conceive”), a compound of the prefix ~com-~ (“together, completely”) and ~capere~ (“to take, to seize”). ~Capere~ descends from Proto-Italic *~kapjō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~kap-~ (“to grasp, to seize”), the same root that gave, through Germanic, English ~have~ and ~heave~ and German ~haben~ and ~heben~. Italian ~concepire~ and Spanish ~concebir~ are its Romance siblings.

Latin ~concipere~ meant literally “to take together, to gather in,” whence two great branches: the bodily “to become pregnant, to conceive a child,” and the mental “to take an idea into the mind, to form a conception.” French ~concevoir~ keeps both, alongside the derived ~conception~. The root ~capere~ is astonishingly fertile in French, having seeded ~recevoir~, ~décevoir~, ~apercevoir~, ~percevoir~, ~capter~, ~capable~, and ~captif~, plus learned doublets in ~cept-~ such as ~concept~, ~accepter~, and ~excepter~. The shift from physical grasping to intellectual grasping is one humans make instinctively — we still speak of “grasping” an argument — so to ~concevoir~ a plan is, at heart, to take firm hold of it in the mind.
en.conclure:
From Latin ~concludere~ (“to shut up, to enclose, to bring to a close”), a compound of the prefix ~con-~ (“together”) and the verb ~claudere~ (“to close, to shut”). ~claudere~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~kleh₂w-~ (“hook, peg, key”), the same root behind Ancient Greek ~kleís~ (“key, bolt”). Through ~claudere~, ~conclure~ is cognate with English ~close~, ~clause~, ~include~, and ~exclude~, and with the very word ~claustrophobia~.

The metaphor inside the verb is spatial: to ~conclure~ is to shut things up together, to close a discussion off. From that came the two living senses — to bring an argument or an affair to its end (one ~conclut~ a treaty, a sale, a speech), and to draw an inference, the “closing” move of reasoning whereby premises are sealed into a result. The noun ~conclusion~ inherits both. French shows the same root in a whole network of closing-words: ~clore~ and ~clôture~ (“to close,” “a fence”), ~clé~ or ~clef~ (“key,” literally the thing that shuts), ~exclure~ and ~inclure~, ~reclus~ (“a recluse,” one shut away), and ~cloître~ (“a cloister,” the enclosed walk of a monastery) — all descendants of *~kleh₂w-~.
en.condamner:
From Latin ~condemnare~ (“to sentence, to convict, to blame”), a compound of the prefix ~con-~ (here intensive) and ~damnare~ (“to condemn, to inflict loss upon”), the verb of ~damnum~ (“loss, damage, fine, penalty”). ~damnum~ is traced to Proto-Italic *~dapno-~ and probably to a Proto-Indo-European root *~dap-~ (“to apportion, to share in exchange”), linking the idea of penalty to that of a cost paid out. Old French had ~condemner~, which kept its ~e~ until the sixteenth century, when the spelling shifted to ~condamner~ under the pull of native ~damner~ (“to damn”). Cognate with English ~condemn~ and ~damage~, the latter also from ~damnum~.

The oldest sense is judicial: a court ~condamne~ an accused, pronouncing the loss or penalty owed. From that core, French branched outward — to condemn morally (~condamner une pratique~), to doom (~condamné à mort~, but also ~condamné à échouer~, doomed to fail), and, more concretely, to block up or seal off (~condamner une porte~, to wall up a door so it can no longer be used). The same ~damnum~ underlies ~dam~ (in ~au grand dam de~, to the great detriment of), ~damner~, ~damnation~, and the borrowed ~dommage~ (“damage, pity”) — a family in which loss, penalty, and ruin keep close company.
en.conduire:
From Old French ~conduire~, from Latin ~conducere~ (“to lead together, to bring along, to hire”), a compound of ~con-~ (“together, with”) and ~ducere~ (“to lead, to draw”). The verb ~ducere~ descends from Proto-Italic *~doukō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~dewk-~ (“to draw, to pull, to lead”), the same root that surfaces in the Germanic family as English ~tow~ and ~tug~ and in German ~ziehen~ (“to pull”) and ~Herzog~ (“duke,” literally “army-leader”). Italian ~condurre~ and Spanish ~conducir~ are the parallel Romance descendants.

Latin ~conducere~ ran from “lead together” to commercial senses like “to hire” or “to rent,” but French kept the directional core: to lead, to guide, and from there to drive a vehicle and to manage an affair. The musical sense of conducting and the figurative ~conduire à~ (“to lead to, to result in”) both extend the idea of guidance. The Latin past participle ~conductus~ gave the noun ~conduit~ (a channel that “leads” water) and, via ~conduite~, the word for behavior — how one “conducts” oneself. The root *~dewk-~ seeds a large French family through ~-duire~: ~produire~, ~réduire~, ~traduire~, ~séduire~, plus ~duc~, ~conducteur~, and ~éduquer~.
en.confier:
From Latin ~confidere~ (“to trust fully, to have confidence in”), Frenchified in the fourteenth century on the pattern of the older verb ~fier~ (“to trust”). The Latin compound joins the intensive prefix ~con-~ and ~fidere~ (“to trust”), built on the noun ~fides~ (“faith, trust, loyalty, good faith”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~bʰeydʰ-~ (“to trust, to persuade”). Through this root ~fides~ is cognate with Greek ~peithō~ (“I persuade”) and, in the Germanic branch, with English ~bide~ and ~abide~.

To ~confier~ is to place something into another's keeping with complete trust — a secret, a child, a task. The sense sits squarely on ~fides~: you hand over the thing because you have faith in the one who receives it. From the same Latin ~fides~ French built one of its richest families: ~foi~ (“faith”), ~fidèle~, ~fidélité~, ~confiance~, ~confidence~, ~défier~ and ~méfier~ (“to dis-trust”), and ~fiançailles~ — a betrothal being a mutual pledge of ~foi~. The English doublets ~confide~ and ~confidence~, and the legal ~fidelity~ and ~faith~ itself, all flow from the same wellspring: the ancient idea that a bond between people rests on trust given and trust kept.
en.confirmer:
From Middle French ~confermer~, from Latin ~cōnfirmāre~ (“to make firm, to strengthen, to establish”), compounded from the intensive prefix ~con-~ and ~firmāre~ (“to make firm”), itself from the adjective ~firmus~ (“strong, steadfast, stable”). ~Firmus~ goes back to a suffixed form of the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰer-~ (“to hold firmly, to support”), which also gave Sanskrit ~dʰarma-~ (“law, that which is upheld”) and Greek ~thrónos~ (“seat, support”). Italian ~confermare~ and Spanish ~confirmar~ are siblings; English ~confirm~ is a borrowing of the same Latin verb.

The core image is solidity: to confirm is to make firm what was loose or uncertain — to shore it up. From “strengthen” the verb branched into “ratify, validate” (confirming a decision), “verify, prove true” (confirming a fact), and the ecclesiastical rite of confirmation, which strengthens the baptized in their faith. The same ~firmus~ family runs deep in French: ~ferme~ (“firm,” and the noun « farm », via Medieval Latin ~firma~, “a fixed payment”), ~fermer~ (“to close,” i.e. to make fast), ~affirmer~, ~firmament~ (the « fixed » vault of the sky), and the doublet adjective ~firme~ in the borrowed noun « une firme ».
en.confondre:
From Latin ~confundere~ (“to pour together, to mingle, to mix up”), a compound of ~con-~ (“together”) and ~fundere~ (“to pour”). Latin ~fundere~ goes back through Proto-Italic to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵʰew-~ (“to pour”), which also yields Ancient Greek ~χέω~ (“I pour”) and, in the Germanic branch, Gothic ~giutan~ (“to pour”) and English ~gut~. The supine stem ~fūsum~ supplies French ~fusion~, ~infuser~, and ~confus~.

The literal image is liquids poured into one vessel until they can no longer be told apart — and from that physical blending grew the abstract sense of mixing things up in the mind: to ~confondre~ two people is to fail to distinguish them. Old French also developed a stronger sense, “to ruin, to overwhelm, to put to shame,” which survives in the slightly archaic ~confondre l'ennemi~ (“to confound the enemy”) and in the past participle ~confondu~ used of someone struck dumb with astonishment or guilt. From the same ~fundere~ family come ~fondre~ (“to melt”), ~fonte~, ~profond~ is unrelated, but ~diffus~, ~effusion~, and ~refondre~ all share the root’s sense of pouring out and recasting.
en.confronter:
From Medieval Latin ~confrontare~ (“to border on, to share a frontier with”), a compound of the prefix ~con-~ (from ~cum~, “with, together”) and ~frons~ (“forehead, brow, front”). First attested in French around 1344, the verb thus literally meant “to stand front to front,” to place two things so their edges or faces meet. It is the source of Italian ~confrontare~, Spanish ~confrontar~, and English ~confront~.

The earliest French sense was territorial — two estates that ~confrontent~ are ones whose boundaries touch — and from this idea of edges meeting came the modern senses. To confront someone is to set yourself face to face with them; to confront two accounts or testimonies is to place them side by side so their faces can be compared. The legal sense of bringing witnesses face to face for cross-checking preserves this neatly. The root ~frons~, the brow one turns toward another, yields a wide French family: ~front~ (“forehead, front line”), ~frontière~ (“border,” literally the line one faces), ~affronter~ (“to face, to brave”), ~effronté~ (“brazen,” one without a brow to blush), and ~frontal~. In each, the forehead stands for the foremost, facing surface of a thing.
en.connaître:
First attested around 1050, from Old French ~conoistre~, later Middle French ~connoistre~ and ~cognoistre~, from Latin ~cognōscere~ (“to get to know, to become acquainted with, to recognize”). The Latin verb is the prefix ~co(m)-~ (“together, thoroughly”) reinforcing ~gnōscere~ (“to come to know”), itself from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵneh₃-~ (“to know, to recognize”). That root is one of the great cross-linguistic survivors: it underlies Ancient Greek ~γιγνώσκω~ (~gignṓskō~), Sanskrit ~jānā́ti~, and the Germanic words English ~know~ and German ~kennen~. Within Romance, ~connaître~ stands beside Italian ~conoscere~ and Spanish ~conocer~.

The modern circumflex of ~connaître~ marks a vanished ~s~ — the ~oist~ of ~conoistre~ — and the ~ai~ spelling, proposed in the seventeenth century as closer to the pronunciation, was only fixed by the 1835 spelling reform. French separates ~connaître~ (acquaintance, familiarity) from ~savoir~ (knowing facts), a distinction Latin already hinted at. The same PIE root *~ǵneh₃-~ feeds an enormous French vocabulary, much of it learned: ~connaissance~, ~reconnaître~, ~méconnaître~, ~notion~ (through Latin ~nōtiō~), ~noble~, and ~ignorer~, which is literally a “not-knowing.”
en.connecter:
Attested in French from around 1780, ~connecter~ comes from Latin ~conectere~ (also spelled ~connectere~), “to bind together, to join, to link,” from ~con-~ (“together”) + ~nectere~ (“to bind, to tie, to fasten”). The exact Proto-Indo-European source of ~nectere~ is debated — roots meaning “to bind” or “to knot” have been proposed — but its past participle ~nexus~ is secure and gave the noun ~nexus~ (“a binding, a tie”). Romance kin include Italian ~connettere~ and Spanish ~conectar~.

The word is, fittingly, about tying things together, and it entered French as a learned, almost scientific term before the electrical and then digital ages gave it new life: to ~connecter~ wires, then circuits, then — reflexively, ~se connecter~ — to log in, joining oneself to a network. The original Latin sense of fastening survives intact in this last use; logging in is, etymologically, knotting yourself to the machine. From ~nectere~ and its participle ~nexus~ French also draws ~connexion~ (and the variant ~connection~), ~connexe~, and ~annexer~, while the idea of a binding tie echoes too in the unrelated-looking but kindred imagery of the ~nœud~.
en.consacrer:
First attested in the twelfth century as ~cunsecrer~ (“to dedicate to God,” 1121), reshaped by 1155 into ~consacrer~ under the influence of the native verb ~sacrer~. The source is Latin ~consecrare~ (“to make sacred by dedicating to a god”), a compound of ~con-~ and ~sacrare~ (“to render holy”), built on the adjective ~sacer~ (“sacred, holy”). ~sacer~ descends through Proto-Italic *~sakros~ from a Proto-Indo-European *~seh₂k-~ (“to sanctify, to make a treaty”), and is cognate with ~sanctus~ (“holy”). Related forms include Italian ~consacrare~ and Spanish ~consagrar~.

The most arresting fact lies in ~sacer~ itself: in Latin the word meant both “sacred” and “accursed,” for whatever was handed over wholly to the gods — whether consecrated in honor or marked out for destruction — passed beyond the human sphere and became untouchable. To consecrate was thus to set something apart absolutely. French extended the religious sense to the secular: one can ~consacrer~ time, energy, or a whole life to a cause, and a usage “consecrated” by custom is one sanctioned by long use. From the same ~sacer~ come ~sacré~ (“sacred”), ~sacrer~ (“to crown, to anoint”), ~consécration~ (“consecration”), ~sacrifice~ (literally “a making sacred”), and ~sacerdoce~ (“priesthood”); English ~sacred~, ~consecrate~, and ~sacrifice~ share the root.
en.conseiller:
From Old French ~conseilier~ (“to advise, to speak privately to”), from Latin ~cōnsiliārī~ (“to take counsel, deliberate, advise”), from the noun ~cōnsilium~ (“counsel, plan, deliberation”). The verb is attested in French from the eleventh century. ~cōnsilium~ joins the prefix ~con-~ (“together”) with a root tied to ~calāre~ (“to call, summon, proclaim”), from Proto-Indo-European *~kelh₁-~ (“to shout, to call”) — so counsel was originally a “calling together.” Through the same Latin word came Italian ~consiglio~, Spanish ~consejo~, and, via Old French, English ~counsel~.

In early Old French ~conseilier a~ could mean “to speak in secret to,” a reminder that taking counsel was once a confidential, whispered affair before it became open advice-giving. The verb must be kept distinct from the homonymous noun ~conseiller~ (“counselor, councilor”), which descends from Latin ~cōnsiliārius~ (“adviser, assessor”). The family is large in French: the noun ~conseil~ (both “a piece of advice” and “a council”), ~déconseiller~ (“to advise against”), and ~conseillère~ (a female counselor). The shared root with ~calāre~ links counsel, distantly, to the idea of summoning voices together — fitting for a council called to deliberate.
en.conserver:
From Latin ~conservare~ (“to keep safe, to preserve, to maintain”), a compound of the intensive prefix ~con-~ and the verb ~servare~ (“to keep, to guard, to watch over”). ~servare~ is itself a denominal from ~servus~ / ~servos~ (“guardian, keeper”), and the family is usually traced to a Proto-Indo-European root *~ser-~ (“to protect, to watch over”). The verb passed regularly into French as ~conserver~. Related Romance forms include Italian ~conservare~ and Spanish ~conservar~, both meaning “to preserve,” and English borrowed the same Latin verb as ~conserve~.

The core idea has always been one of guarding something against loss or harm, and from “keeping watch” the sense broadened to “keeping” in general — keeping a secret, keeping food from spoiling, keeping a memory alive. Latin ~servare~ must be kept distinct from its look-alike ~servire~ (“to serve”), with which it is not certainly related despite the shared spelling. The same ~servare~ stem, with different prefixes, gave French a whole cluster of verbs of watchful keeping: ~réserver~ (“to set aside”), ~préserver~ (“to keep from harm”), and ~observer~ (“to watch attentively”). The noun ~conserve~ — a jar of preserved food — and the adjective ~conservateur~ (“conservative,” one who wishes to keep things as they are) descend directly from the same root.
en.considérer:
From Latin ~considerare~ (“to examine attentively, to contemplate”), a learned borrowing built from ~con-~ (“together, thoroughly”) and a stem linked to ~sidus~ (genitive ~sideris~, “star, constellation”). The word is widely read as belonging to the vocabulary of augury: to ~considerare~ was, by one old explanation, to study the stars closely — as a diviner scanned the night sky for omens. English ~consider~, Italian ~considerare~, and Spanish ~considerar~ all carry the same Latin word onward.

Whatever its exact prehistory, the “star” connection is made vivid by a telling contrast: beside ~considerare~ stood ~desiderare~ (“to long for”), built on ~de-~ + the same ~sidus~ — literally “to feel the absence of a star,” to miss what one no longer sees in the heavens. That verb became French ~désirer~, so that ~considérer~ and ~désirer~ are étymological siblings, one gazing at the stars and the other yearning for them. From ~sidus~ also come the learned adjectives ~sidéral~ and ~sidérer~ (“to stun,” as if star-struck), and the family ~considération~ and ~désir~.
en.consister:
From Latin ~consistere~ (“to stand together, to take a firm stand, to be composed of”), from ~con-~ (“with, together”) + ~sistere~ (“to set down, to make stand”), a reduplicated causative of ~stāre~ (“to stand”). All of this rests on Proto-Indo-European *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”), among the most prolific roots in the language, source also of English ~stand~ and ~stay~ and of Italian ~stare~. The learned Romance cognates are Italian ~consistere~ and Spanish ~consistir~.

The Latin verb pictured several things “standing together” — holding firm, coming to a halt, settling into a stable form. French borrowed it late and bookishly, keeping mostly the last of these: a thing ~consiste~ in that of which it is composed, in what gives it its standing substance, hence ~consistance~ for a mixture's body or firmness. The sense “to halt, to be stationed” faded, leaving the abstract “to be made up of.” From the same ~sistere~ and ~stāre~ French gathered a great crowd of words: ~assister~, ~résister~, ~insister~, ~persister~, ~exister~, ~constituer~, and the everyday ~rester~.
en.consommer:
From Latin ~consummare~ (“to bring to a sum, to complete, to accomplish”), a compound of ~con-~ (“together”) and ~summa~ (“sum, total, highest point”). ~Summa~ is the feminine of ~summus~ (“highest”), a superlative of ~superus~ (“upper”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~upo~ (“under, up from below”). So ~consommer~ originally meant to gather to a total, to carry through to the top. Italian ~consumare~ and Spanish ~consumar~ are cognates.

The great twist is a collision of two Latin verbs. ~Consummare~ (“to complete”) was confused in French with the lookalike ~consumere~ (“to use up, devour”), which separately yielded ~consumer~ (“to consume, burn up”). French eventually split the difference by sense: ~consommer~ keeps a notion of useful, purposeful using-up or completion, while ~consumer~ means sheer destruction. Hence one ~consommer~ a meal, fuel, or — strikingly — a marriage (to ~consommer~ a marriage is to complete it), whereas a fire ~consumer~ a house to ashes. From ~summa~ also come French ~somme~ (“sum”), ~sommet~ (“summit”), ~consommation~, and the cooking term ~consommé~, a broth “completed” by long reduction.
en.constater:
A denominal verb, coined in French from the noun ~constat~, which is in turn the Latin ~constat~ (“it is established, it is agreed”), the third-person singular of ~constāre~ (“to stand together, to be fixed, to be certain”). ~Constāre~ is ~con-~ (“together”) + ~stāre~ (“to stand”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”) — the same root behind ~être~, ~rester~, and ~stable~. The verb is first attested only in 1726, in the phrase « constater un fait » in the Mémoires de Trévoux.

The Latin ~constat~ was a fixed formula of legal and logical prose: « it stands established that… » To constater is therefore to record that something stands as a fact — to note, to observe, to certify officially. The legal and administrative flavor of the source clings to the modern word: a ~constat~ is still a formal written statement of findings (a constat d'huissier, a constat amiable after a car accident). From the same ~stāre~ / *~steh₂-~ root the French family is enormous — ~constant~ (“standing firm”), ~état~ and its doublet ~stade~, ~station~, ~stable~, ~rester~ — all turning on the image of standing fast.
en.constituer:
From Latin ~constituere~ (“to set up, establish, appoint”), a compound of the prefix ~con-~ (“together, with”) and the verb ~statuere~ (“to set up, to cause to stand”). ~Statuere~ is built on ~status~, the past participle of ~stare~ (“to stand”), and so reaches back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”) — the same vast root behind ~stable~, ~station~, and ~statue~. The Romance descendants are regular: Italian ~costituire~, Spanish ~constituir~, and French ~constituer~; English borrowed both ~constitute~ and, from the related ~statutum~, the word ~statute~.

The core image is of standing things up together — placing elements side by side so that they form a settled, standing whole. From that the verb drew its double life: a physical-cum-legal sense of “establishing, appointing, founding” (a Roman magistrate could ~constituere~ a guardian or a debt), and the sense French uses most today, “to make up, to compose” — the parts that together stand as the thing. From the same family come ~constitution~, ~statut~, and, through ~stare~ and ~status~, the noun ~état~ (“state”). To constitute something is to make its parts stand together as one.
en.construire:
From Latin ~construere~ (“to pile up, to build”), from the prefix ~con-~ (“together”) and ~struere~ (“to heap up, to arrange in layers”), past participle ~structus~. ~Struere~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~strew-~ (“to strew, to spread out”), the very same root that gives English ~strew~ and ~street~ (a “strewn,” paved way) and Latin ~sternere~ (“to spread flat”). Romance kept the verb as Italian ~costruire~ and Spanish ~construir~.

The image is wonderfully physical: the Romans took a root meaning “to scatter, to spread on the ground” and turned it upward, into “to pile in ordered layers,” hence “to build.” Old French reshaped ~construere~ into a third-group verb, and its meaning broadened from masonry to the building of sentences (~la construction d'une phrase~) and of arguments. The ~struere~ family is everywhere in French: ~construction~, ~structure~, ~instruire~ (“to instruct,” literally to build knowledge into someone), ~détruire~ (“to un-build, to destroy”), and ~industrie~. English ~structure~, ~instruct~, ~destroy~, and ~construe~ all rise from the same spread-out, then piled-up, ground.
en.consulter:
From Latin ~consultāre~ (“to deliberate, to take counsel, to consult”), the frequentative of ~consulere~ (“to deliberate, to take advice”), which is most likely ~con-~ (“together”) + a verb *~selere~ (“to gather, take”), from PIE *~selh₁-~ (“to take, seize”). The frequentative form expressed repeated or deliberate action, fitting a word about weighing matters at length. French borrowed it directly and learnedly, so the Latin shape survives almost intact. Cognate with Italian ~consultare~ and Spanish ~consultar~.

The oldest sense was political and collective: in Rome, ~consulere senātum~ meant “to gather the Senate” for counsel — which is why the chief magistrates were the ~cōnsulēs~, the “consuls.” Curiously, the verb ~consulere~ seems older than the noun ~cōnsul~ and probably gave rise to it, rather than the reverse. The meaning narrowed over time from “deliberating together” to the modern act of seeking expert advice or, more recently still, simply “referring to” a book or a clock — one consults a dictionary much as a Roman once consulted an oracle. Modern French relatives include ~consultation~, ~consultant~, and ~consulat~, while the deeper PIE root for “taking” surfaces, distantly, in English ~sell~ and ~sale~.
en.contacter:
A recent formation, attested only in 1842: French derived ~contacter~ from the noun ~contact~ with the first-conjugation suffix ~-er~. The noun ~contact~ was borrowed in the seventeenth century from Latin ~contāctus~ (“a touching, contact”), the past participle of ~contingere~ (“to touch, to reach, to border on”), which combines ~con-~ (“with, together”) and ~tangere~ (“to touch”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~tag-~ (“to touch, to handle”). Cognates through ~tangere~ include English ~tangent~, ~tact~, and ~contagion~, and Italian ~contattare~.

The verb had a rocky start: nineteenth- and twentieth-century purists long condemned ~contacter~ as an Anglicism modeled on English ~to contact~ and as needless slang, and it was still being printed in cautionary quotation marks as late as 1952. Usage won out, and today it is wholly standard for “to get in touch with” someone. The same Latin root *~tag-~ that gives the buried ~-tact~ of ~contact~ surfaces across a wide French vocabulary of touching: ~tact~ itself (both physical touch and social delicacy), ~tactile~, ~contagieux~, ~intact~ (literally “untouched”), and ~contigu~ (“adjoining, touching”). The newer relatives ~contactable~ and ~recontacter~ extend the verb in turn.
en.contenir:
From Latin ~continēre~ (“to hold together, to keep within, to contain”), ~com-~ (“together”) + ~tenēre~ (“to hold”). ~Tenēre~ is a stative verb from PIE *~ten-~ (“to stretch, to draw taut”) — to hold being conceived as keeping something under tension. The root reaches across Indo-European to Greek ~teínō~ (“I stretch”), Sanskrit ~tanóti~ (“he stretches”), and English ~thin~ (stretched out). Through Old French, ~continēre~ gave the modern ~contenir~; Italian ~contenere~ and Spanish ~contener~ are cognates.

The image of “holding together” unfolds in every modern sense. A vessel ~contient~ a liquid (holds it within); a hall ~contient~ a thousand people (takes them in); and figuratively one ~contient~ one's anger or a crowd (holds it back, restrains it) — hence the noun ~contenance~ (“composure,” the holding-together of one's bearing). What is held inside is the ~contenu~ (“contents”), and the thing that holds it is the ~contenant~ (“container”). The vast *~ten-~ family runs through French ~tenir~, ~contenir~, ~retenir~ (“to hold back”), ~maintenir~ (“to hold in the hand, to maintain”), ~soutenir~ (“to hold up, to support”), ~obtenir~, ~continu~ (“continuous”), and ~teneur~ — and, more distantly, ~tendre~ (“to stretch”) and ~tension~.
en.contenter:
From the French adjective ~content~ (“satisfied, pleased”), with the verbal suffix ~-er~. ~Content~ comes from Latin ~contentus~ (“contained, satisfied”), the past participle of ~continēre~ (“to hold together, to contain”), from ~con-~ (“together”) and ~tenēre~ (“to hold”), from Proto-Indo-European *~ten-~ (“to stretch”). Cognate, through *~ten-~, with English ~thin~ and ~tend~.

The metaphor is quietly beautiful: to be ~content~ is to be “contained,” held within bounds — desire kept from stretching past what one has. To ~contenter~ someone is therefore to hold their wishes inside a satisfying limit. The same ~tenēre~ gave French a great clan of holding-verbs and nouns: ~tenir~, ~contenir~, ~retenir~, ~maintenir~, ~obtenir~, ~contenu~, ~teneur~, and ~contenance~. The deeper PIE sense of “stretching” surfaces elsewhere too, in ~tendre~, ~tension~, and ~tente~ (a “stretched” canvas). So a contented person is, etymologically, one whose wants stay neatly held together rather than stretched thin.
en.continuer:
From Latin ~continuāre~ (“to join together, to make continuous, to prolong”), derived from ~continuus~ (“uninterrupted, connected”), itself from ~continēre~ (“to hold together”). ~Continēre~ is ~con-~ (“together”) plus ~tenēre~ (“to hold”), which descends from PIE *~ten-~ (“to stretch”). Through that root ~continuer~ is cognate with English ~thin~, ~tend~, and ~tenant~, and with Greek ~teínein~ (“to stretch”).

The link between “stretch” and “hold” lies in the idea of keeping something taut and unbroken: a continuous thing is one stretched out without a gap. From ~tenēre~ French inherited ~tenir~ and a great clan of relatives — ~contenir~, ~maintenir~, ~retenir~, ~soutenir~, ~obtenir~ — while ~continuus~ yields ~continu~, ~continuel~, ~continuité~, and the adverb ~continûment~. The same PIE root, by way of the “stretching” sense, also gives ~tension~, ~tendre~, and ~tendon~. So when one ~continue~ a task, one is, in the deep logic of the word, holding its thread stretched taut so that it does not snap — keeping the line unbroken from one moment to the next.
en.contraindre:
From Old French ~constreindre~, from Latin ~constringere~ (“to bind together, to fetter, to compress”), formed from ~com-~ (“together”) and ~stringere~ (“to draw tight, to bind”). ~stringere~ traces to a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to stroke, to press, to draw tight,” and gives English ~strain~, ~strict~, ~stress~, and even ~strangle~, plus Italian ~costringere~ and English ~constrain~ directly from the same Latin verb.

The physical image is one of tying tightly: to constrain someone was to bind them so they could not move freely. From that the verb passed to the figurative — to compel, to force a hand, to leave no choice. French keeps the family of ~-stringere~ verbs unusually intact, each with its own prefix and its own narrowed sense: ~étreindre~ (to embrace, to clutch), ~astreindre~ (to subject to an obligation), ~restreindre~ (to restrict), and ~contraindre~ (to compel) — a neat set of bindings loosened and tightened by their prefixes. The learned doublets ~constriction~ and ~constricteur~ (as in the snake) come from the same root by the bookish route. So when something is ~contraignant~, the word still whispers its origin: it is, quite literally, something that binds you tight.
en.contribuer:
From Latin ~contribuere~ (“to bring together, contribute, give as a share”), from ~con-~ (“together”) and ~tribuere~ (“to allot, assign, grant”). The verb ~tribuere~ derives from ~tribus~, the “tribe,” so its core sense was to “distribute among the tribes”; ~tribus~ is often connected to the Proto-Indo-European numeral root *~trey-~ (“three”), pointing to an archaic threefold division of the Roman people. Cognate with Italian ~contribuire~ and Spanish ~contribuir~, and — through ~tribuere~ — with English ~contribute~, ~distribute~, and ~tribute~.

The political origin is the memorable detail: long before it meant “to help,” ~contribuere~ described carving a populace up by tribe and assigning each its share of duties and taxes. From that fiscal sense — paying one's portion into a common pot — grew the modern meaning of voluntarily giving toward a shared end, whether money, effort, or an article. The same ~tribus~ underlies a wide French family: ~tribu~ itself, ~tribut~ (“tribute, levy”), ~attribuer~, ~distribuer~, ~rétribuer~, and ~tribun~, the Roman magistrate who was originally the head of a tribe. The noun ~contribution~ preserves the old tax sense in expressions referring to public levies.
en.contrôler:
From Old French ~contrerole~ / ~contre-rolle~, a clerk's “counter-roll”: a duplicate register kept alongside the original to verify it. The word joins ~contre-~ (“against, counter,” from Latin ~contra~) and ~rôle~ (“roll, scroll of parchment,” from Latin ~rotulus~, “little wheel,” diminutive of ~rota~, “wheel”). To ~contrôler~ an account was thus to check it against its mirror-copy. In the nineteenth century the sense of “mastery, command” was reinforced by English ~control~ — itself borrowed from this very French word centuries earlier and sent home with a new meaning.

The semantic arc runs from bookkeeping to power. The original act — comparing one register against another — generalized into “to verify, to inspect,” which is still the dominant French sense: a ticket ~contrôleur~ checks, a ~contrôle~ is an inspection or a roadside check, false. From the English re-import came the further idea of holding something in one's grip, of steering it. The ~rotulus~ branch flourished elsewhere too: French ~rôle~ kept its theatrical and administrative life (an actor's part was once literally the scroll bearing the lines), and ~rouleau~, ~enrôler~, and ~contrôle~ all descend from the humble Latin ~rota~, the turning wheel.
en.convaincre:
