From Latin ~convincere~ (“to refute, to prove conclusively, to convict”), formed from the prefix ~con-~ (an intensive use of ~cum~, “with, completely”) and ~vincere~ (“to conquer, to defeat”). ~Vincere~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~winkō~ and to the Proto-Indo-European root *~weik-~ (“to overcome, to fight”), reached through a nasal-infixed form *~wi-n-k-~. Cognate, through ~vincere~, with Italian ~vincere~ and Spanish ~vencer~ (“to win”), and with the borrowed English doublet ~convince~.

The Latin sense was forensic: to ~convincere~ someone was to defeat them decisively in argument, and so to prove them guilty or in error. French kept the verb close to this combative origin — to ~convaincre~ is, etymologically, to vanquish another's resistance with reasons until they yield. The same root *~weik-~ runs through a large family of French and English words built on ~vincere~: ~vaincre~ (“to defeat”), ~invincible~, ~victoire~, convaincre's near-twin ~convaincu~, and the legal ~conviction~. The Latin past participle ~convictus~ even gave English ~convict~, preserving the courtroom flavor that French ~convaincre~ has largely traded for the gentler work of persuasion.
en.convenir:
From Latin ~convenire~ (“to come together, to assemble, to agree”), a compound of ~con-~ (“with, together”) and ~venire~ (“to come”). ~venire~ descends from Proto-Italic *~gʷeniō~ and ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to go”). Cognate with Italian ~convenire~ and Spanish ~convenir~, and — through the same PIE root — with English ~come~ and Latin-derived ~convene~.

The literal sense, “to come together,” fans out beautifully in modern French. When people come together they reach accord, hence ~convenir~ “to agree, to admit”; when a thing comes together well with a situation it fits, hence “to suit, to be convenient.” The same gathering image gives ~couvent~ (a convent, those who have come together) and ~convention~ (an agreement reached in common). The PIE root *~gʷem-~ is among the most fertile in the language, underlying through ~venire~ a whole battery of French verbs — ~venir~, ~devenir~, ~revenir~, ~prévenir~, ~parvenir~, ~survenir~ — and, via the supine ~ventum~, the nouns ~avenue~ and ~événement~. The everyday ~ça me convient~ thus carries, fossilized within it, the ancient picture of two paths meeting.
en.correspondre:
French ~correspondre~ comes from Medieval Latin ~correspondēre~, formed from ~respondēre~ (“to answer, to respond”) with the prefix ~con-~ / ~cor-~ (“with, together”). ~Respondēre~ is itself ~re-~ (“back”) plus ~spondēre~ (“to pledge, to promise solemnly”), from Proto-Italic *~spondeō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~spend-~ (“to perform a rite, to make an offering”). The Greek cognates ~spéndō~ (“to pour a libation”) and ~spondḗ~ (“libation, solemn truce”) preserve the religious flavour; through the same Latin verb English gained ~respond~, ~sponsor~, and ~spouse~.

The literal idea is mutual pledging: to ~correspondre~ is etymologically “to pledge back together,” one party answering another in kind. From this came two great branches. The first is the abstract sense “to match, to agree, to be in harmony” — colours that ~correspondent~ to one another. The second is the sense “to exchange letters,” reciprocal answering carried on at a distance, which gave the noun ~correspondance~ and, by a charming extension, the ~correspondance~ one makes between two metro lines. The buried root surfaces across French in ~répondre~, ~époux~ and ~épouse~ (a spouse is one solemnly pledged), ~répons~, and ~spontané~. A solemn libation poured to seal an oath has become, in the end, a postcard.
en.corriger:
From Latin ~corrigere~ (“to set straight, reform, amend”), a compound of ~cum~ (“with, together”) — reduced to ~cor-~ before ~r~ — and ~regere~ (“to direct, guide, rule”). ~Regere~ descends from PIE *~h₃reǵ-~ (“to straighten, direct, make right”), one of the most productive roots in the language, source also of Latin ~rēx~ (“king”) and ~rēctus~ (“straight, right”). Through that root ~corriger~ is cognate with English ~right~ and German ~recht~, and with Italian ~correggere~ and Spanish ~corregir~. The literal Latin image is concrete: to “straighten thoroughly,” to bring a crooked thing back into line.

That spatial metaphor — making the bent straight — underlies every modern sense. To correct an error is to straighten what has gone awry; to correct a pupil or a fault is to guide conduct back onto the right path. French preserved the schoolroom sense of marking and grading work, ~corriger une copie~, alongside the moral one of ~se corriger~ (“to mend one's ways”). From the same root *~h₃reǵ-~ French draws a wide family: ~diriger~, ~régir~, ~règle~, ~droit~ (from ~dīrēctus~), ~roi~, and ~correct~ itself, the learned doublet of the past participle. The crooked line made straight runs quietly through them all.
en.coter:
Modern French ~coter~ is a denominal verb, drawn from the noun ~cote~. That noun was borrowed from Medieval Latin ~quota~ (clipped from ~quota pars~, “the share due from each person”), the feminine of Latin ~quotus~ (“which in order? what number?”), itself built on ~quot~ (“how many”). The chain reaches back to the Proto-Indo-European interrogative stem *~kʷo-~, the same pronominal root behind Latin ~quis~ and ~quod~. Through ~quota~, the noun ~cote~ is an etymological doublet of the international word ~quota~ and a cousin of English ~quote~, which passed through Medieval Latin ~quotare~ (“to mark off by numbers”).

From the bookkeeper's ~quota pars~ — each person's numbered share on a tax roll — ~cote~ branched into a surprising spread of senses: a figure marked on a plan (a dimension), a price quoted on the exchange, a horse's odds, a library shelfmark, and a grade given to a pupil's work. The verb ~coter~ inherited this whole range, so that today one may ~coter~ a security, a drawing, or an essay. The same numbering instinct lives on in ~quote-part~ (“share, portion”), ~quotient~, ~quotidien~ (literally “however many days there are,” hence “daily”), and ~quorum~ — all heirs of that small Latin question ~quot~, “how many?”
en.coucher:
From Old French ~couchier~ (also ~colchier~), from Latin ~collocare~ (“to place, lay in position”), formed from the prefix ~con-~ (“together, thoroughly”) and ~locare~ (“to place”), itself derived from ~locus~ (“place”). Old Latin had ~stlocus~, traditionally traced to Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ (“to put, place”), though that derivation is now treated cautiously. Latin ~collocare~ also gave Spanish ~colgar~ and Italian ~coricare~, and, through learned borrowing, English ~collocate~.

In Latin ~collocare~ meant to set something carefully in its place — to arrange, to position, even to invest money or marry off a daughter (placing her well). French ~coucher~ specialized toward one kind of placing: laying a body down, hence “to put to bed,” “to lie down,” and reflexively ~se coucher~ (“to go to bed”). From the horizontal it spread to the sun that ~se couche~ (“sets”), to writing something down (~coucher par écrit~, “to put in writing”), and to the noun ~couche~ (“a layer”). Beside it stands the learned doublet ~colloquer~ and the broad ~locus~ family — ~lieu~, ~local~, ~localiser~, ~louer~ (“to rent,” from ~locare~) — all built on the idea of a place.
en.couper:
From Old French ~colper~, ~couper~ (eleventh century), derived from the noun ~colp~ (modern ~coup~, “a blow”) with the verbal suffix ~-er~: to ~couper~ is literally to “separate with a blow.” The noun goes back through Vulgar Latin ~colpus~ to Late Latin ~colaphus~, itself borrowed from Greek ~kólaphos~ (“a blow on the cheek, a slap”). Cognates of the noun include Italian ~colpo~ and Spanish ~golpe~, both meaning “blow, strike.”

The striking thing — fitting for a word born of a slap — is how far the meaning travelled from violence. In Old French the sense had already moved from “to strike” to “to cut,” cutting being the result of a blow with a blade, and the original Greek “box on the ear” faded entirely. From there ~couper~ spread into a huge range of idioms: ~couper l'eau~ (to cut off the water), ~couper la parole~ (to interrupt), ~couper les ponts~ (to break off relations), and from 1610 the wine-merchant's ~couper~, to dilute or blend a wine with water or another vintage. Modern French keeps a rich family around the noun ~coup~ — ~coupe~ (a cut, and a cup or trophy), ~coupon~, ~découper~ (to cut out), ~couperet~ (the cleaver, and the guillotine's blade) — all ultimately a single Greek slap.
en.courir:
From Old French ~courre~, ~corre~ (“to run”), from Latin ~currere~ (“to run, to hasten”), from Proto-Indo-European *~ḱers-~ (“to run”). The infinitive ~courir~ is an analogical reshaping that, from the fourteenth century onward, displaced the older ~courre~ on the model of other verbs. Cognate, through ~currere~, with Italian ~correre~ and Spanish ~correr~, and — through the same PIE root *~ḱers-~ — with English ~horse~ and ~hurry~ and with the Latin-borrowed ~current~ and ~career~.

The old form ~courre~ has not vanished entirely: it survives, fossilized, in the hunting term ~chasse à courre~ (“hunting with hounds on horseback”), a phrase that still uses the medieval infinitive. ~Currere~ is one of Latin's most mobile roots, and French is full of its offspring: ~course~, ~coureur~, ~courrier~ (originally a “runner” who carried messages), ~courant~, ~concourir~, ~parcourir~, ~secourir~, and ~recourir~. The English ~courier~, ~course~, ~current~, ~occur~, and ~excursion~ all descend from the same running root, as does ~corridor~ — etymologically a “running-place.” Few Latin verbs have sprinted so far through the vocabulary of its daughter languages.
en.couvrir:
From Old French ~covrir~, from Latin ~cooperire~ (“to cover wholly, to cover over”), a compound of ~co-~ (an intensive form of ~cum~, “with, together”) and ~operire~ (“to close, to cover”). ~operire~ comes from a Proto-Indo-European compound *~op-wer-yo-~, joining *~op-~ (“over”) with the root *~wer-~ (“to cover, to shut”). ~couvrir~ is cognate with Italian ~coprire~ and Spanish ~cubrir~, and — through Old French ~covert~ — with English ~cover~ and ~covert~.

A neat irony hides in the family. The same root *~wer-~ produced a near-mirror verb, ~aperire~ (“to open, to uncover”), from *~ap-wer-yo-~: change the prefix from “over” to “off, away” and covering becomes uncovering. That ~aperire~ gave French ~ouvrir~ (“to open”) and English ~overt~ and ~aperture~ — so ~couvrir~ and ~ouvrir~, perfect opposites in meaning, are siblings built on one root. From ~cooperire~ French drew a rich harvest: ~couvert~ (“covered,” also a table setting and shelter), ~couverture~ (“blanket, cover”), ~couvercle~ (“lid”), ~découvrir~ (“to uncover, to discover”), and the vivid compounds ~couvre-chef~ (“headgear”) and ~couvre-feu~ (literally “cover-fire,” the medieval ~curfew~).
en.coûter:
From Middle French ~couster~, from Old French ~couster~, from Latin ~constāre~ (“to stand firm, to stand together,” and so “to be fixed at a price, to cost”), the infinitive of ~cōnstō~. The verb is built from the intensive prefix ~com-~ and ~stāre~ (“to stand”), the same root that gives English ~stand~ and ~stay~. Italian ~costare~ and Spanish ~costar~ are its everyday cousins, and English ~cost~ is borrowed, through Old French, from the very same source.

The semantic leap from “to stand” to “to cost” runs through the idea of a price at which something is fixed or settled: what a thing ~constat~ is what it firmly “stands at.” Old French ~couster~ kept this commercial sense, and modern French extended it figuratively — an effort, a sacrifice, or a mistake can ~coûter~ dearly without any money changing hands, as in the proverb ~coûte que coûte~ (“cost what it may, at all costs”). From the same Latin ~stāre~ descend a broad family of French words, including ~constant~, ~coût~ (the noun “cost”), ~constance~, and — through ~constāre~ itself — ~contraster~ and ~contraste~, where things are made to “stand against” one another.
en.craindre:
From Old French ~criembre~, ~creindre~ (attested as early as the ninth-century ~Cantilène de sainte Eulalie~), later reshaped to ~craindre~ on the model of other ~-aindre~ verbs like ~plaindre~ and ~fraindre~. The source is Gallo-Roman *~cremere~, an alteration of Latin *~tremere~ (“to tremble, to quake”) under the influence of a Gaulish form related to ~critos~ (“trembling, shudder”). The Latin verb descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~trem-~ (“to tremble”), cognate with Greek ~trémō~ (“I tremble”) and Italian ~tremare~. English ~tremble~ and ~tremor~ belong to the same family.

The shift from “to tremble” to “to fear” is intuitive: dread makes the body shake, so the physical tremor lent its name to the emotion. The Celtic colouring of the initial ~cr-~ is a rare visible trace of Gaulish on a Latin verb, a substrate fingerprint left as Latin settled over Gaul. From the same ~trem-~ root, but borrowed by the learned route, French also possesses ~trembler~ (through Late Latin ~tremulāre~), ~trémousser~ (“to wriggle”), ~tremblement~ (“trembling, earthquake”), and ~trémolo~. So the homely ~craindre~ and the literary ~trembler~ are, beneath their very different shapes, two reflexes of a single ancient shudder.
en.croire:
From Middle French ~croire~, from Old French ~creire~, ~croire~, from Latin ~crēdere~ (“to entrust as a loan; to trust, to have confidence in”). The Latin verb continues a Proto-Indo-European compound *~ḱred-dʰeh₁-~, literally “to place one's heart,” fusing *~ḱred-~ (the oblique stem of “heart”) with *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to put, set, place”). The same archaic compound surfaces far afield: Sanskrit ~śrad-dhā~ (“faith, trust”) and Old Irish ~creitid~ (“believes”) preserve the identical heart-placing image.

The earliest Latin sense was concrete and commercial — to ~crēdere~ was to entrust money to someone, to extend credit, trusting they would repay. From this lending of trust grew the sense “to have confidence,” and during the Christian era the strong religious meaning “to believe, to hold as an article of faith” developed and came to dominate. The metaphor at the bottom — belief as setting one's heart on something — is one of the oldest in Indo-European. From ~crēdere~ French inherited or borrowed a rich set: ~croyance~, ~croyant~, ~crédit~, ~crédible~, ~créance~, ~crédule~, and the liturgical ~credo~ (Latin for “I believe”).
en.croiser:
A denominal verb, derived from the noun ~croix~ (“cross”) with the verb-forming suffix ~-er~. ~Croix~ descends from Latin ~crux~ (genitive ~crucis~, “stake, cross”), the instrument on which the condemned were impaled or crucified. The same ~crux~ gave Italian ~croce~, Spanish ~cruz~, and — by way of Old Norse and Old Irish ~cros~ — English ~cross~ itself.

The ultimate origin of Latin ~crux~ is uncertain and best left open. The French verb means, fundamentally, “to put in the form of a cross”: one ~croise~ one's arms, two roads ~se croisent~ (intersect), and two travellers ~se croisent~ when their paths cross going opposite ways. From ~croix~ the language built a rich field: ~croisement~ (“crossing, intersection,” and in biology “crossbreeding”), ~croisière~ (“cruise,” a ship originally “crossing” to and fro on patrol), ~croisé~ (“crusader,” one who took the cross), and ~croisade~ (“crusade”). Through Latin ~crux~ the same root reaches English ~crucial~, ~crucify~, ~crusade~, and ~cruise~ — every one of them shaped, literally or figuratively, like a cross.
en.créer:
From Old French ~creer~, from Latin ~creāre~ (“to bring forth, to produce, to beget”). The verb belongs to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ḱer-~ (“to grow, to nourish”), and its oldest Latin sense was close to “to make grow.” That same root stands behind ~crēscere~ (“to grow, to arise”) and behind ~Cerēs~, the Roman goddess of grain and agriculture — so to “create” is etymologically to cause to flourish. Cognate verbs survive as Italian ~creare~ and Spanish ~crear~, and the family entered English as ~create~ and ~creature~.

In classical Latin ~creāre~ already covered both the divine act of bringing the world into being and the civic act of electing or appointing a magistrate, who was “created” by the people's vote. French ~créer~ inherited chiefly the productive, originating sense — to bring something new into existence — while keeping a foothold in officialdom, as when a post or a title is “created.” From the same Latin verb and the broader root come a rich family of French words, among them ~création~, ~créature~, ~créateur~, ~créatif~, and, through ~crēscere~, ~croître~ (“to grow”) and ~croissant~ (“growing; crescent; croissant”). The English ~recruit~, a fresh “growth” of new soldiers, springs from the same image of growing.
en.cuire:
From Gallo-Romance *~cocere~, a reshaping of Late Latin ~cocere~, itself an alteration of Classical Latin ~coquere~ (“to cook”). The earlier Latin form was *~quoquō~, dissimilated from Proto-Italic *~kʷekʷō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~pekʷ-~ (“to cook, to ripen”). The root is widespread: it gives Ancient Greek ~πέσσω~ (~péssō~, “to cook, to digest”), Sanskrit ~pácati~ (“he cooks”), and survives faintly in English ~cook~ (a Latin borrowing).

The verb has always covered both the cook’s fire and nature’s slow heat: one can ~cuire~ a meal, but fruit and grain also ~cuire~ as they ripen under the sun, faithful to the root’s double sense of “cook” and “ripen.” French extends ~cuire~ to the sting of pain — a wound or sunburned skin can ~cuire~ (“to smart, to burn”) — and to bitter experience, as in the warning ~il vous en cuira~ (“you’ll regret it, you’ll pay for it”). The same Latin ~coquere~ left a trail of French words: ~cuisine~ and ~cuisinier~, ~cuit~ and ~biscuit~ (“twice-cooked”), ~cuisson~, the archaic ~queux~ (“cook”), and ~cocotte~ (“a small cooking pot”).
en.cultiver:
Attested 1119 as a learned reshaping of Old French ~coutiver~, refashioned on Latin ~cultus~ (“tillage, cultivation, worship”) and more precisely on a reconstructed *~cultivus~ derived from ~cultiō~ (“cultivation”). The whole family rests on Latin ~colere~ (“to till, to tend, to inhabit, to worship”), from Proto-Italic *~kʷelō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kʷelh₁-~ (“to turn, to move around, and so to dwell”). Cognate, through that root, with English ~wheel~ and with Ancient Greek ~télos~ (“end, purpose”).

The semantic arc is remarkable: a root meaning “to turn / revolve” drifted to “to dwell where one turns about,” then to “to till the ground one dwells on,” and finally to “to tend and revere” — which is why ~colere~ fathered at once ~cultiver~ (the field), ~culture~ (the mind), ~colon~ and ~colonie~ (settlers who *inhabit*), and ~culte~ (worship). To ~cultiver~ a garden, a talent, or a friendship is the same gesture under three guises: patient tending. French even keeps coining on this stem — playful forms like ~culturer~ show the suffixation is still alive.
en.céder:
Borrowed from Latin ~cedere~ (“to go, walk, withdraw, yield”). The traditional analysis, going back to Pokorny, reconstructs ~cedere~ from an element *~ce-~ plus the root of ~sedere~ (“to sit”), giving a sense like “to go away by stepping aside.” The verb is cognate with the family seen in Ancient Greek ~ὁδός~ (“road, way”) and shares the broad Indo-European notion of going. From the idea of giving up one's place came the sense of conceding.

In Latin ~cedere~ first meant simply to move or walk, then to withdraw and so to give way; affairs were said to ~cedere~ well or badly, to “go” well or badly. French ~céder~ kept the yielding sense: to cede territory, to give up, to give in (~céder à la tentation~, “to yield to temptation”), and also to break or collapse under strain — a rope or a dam that ~cède~. Latin contrasted yielding with standing firm (~obstare~, ~resistere~). The same root threads through a large legal and motional French vocabulary: ~cession~, ~accéder~, ~procéder~, ~succéder~, ~précéder~, ~recéder~, and ~incessible~ — each preserving the original image of going, stepping, and giving way.
en.célébrer:
From Latin ~celebrāre~ (“to frequent, to throng, to solemnize, to praise”), derived from the adjective ~celeber~ (“crowded, populous, much-frequented”). The further origin of ~celeber~ is uncertain and best left unguessed, but the Latin verb is the clear and well-attested source of French ~célébrer~, English ~celebrate~, Italian ~celebrare~, and Spanish ~celebrar~.

The surprise is in that root adjective: ~celeber~ first meant simply “crowded” — a place thick with people. A spot that drew throngs was busy, then renowned, then famous, and a ceremony that drew throngs was a grand public occasion. So ~celebrāre~ slid from “to attend in great numbers” to “to mark with solemn pomp” and finally “to honor, to extol.” Both modern strands survive in French: one ~célèbre~ a mass or a marriage (the solemn, ritual sense) and one ~célèbre~ a victory or a hero (the festive, praising sense). From the same ~celeber~ come ~célèbre~ (“famous”) and ~célébrité~ (“fame, a celebrity”) — and the thread back to the beginning is plain, for to be a celebrity is, etymologically, to be surrounded by a crowd.
en.dater:
A fourteenth-century formation from the noun ~date~, with the suffix ~-er~. ~date~ comes from Latin ~data~, the feminine past participle of ~dare~ (“to give”), lifted from the formula ~data littera~ (“letter given”) — the phrase Roman and medieval scribes wrote before the place and day a document was issued, as in ~data Romae~ (“given at Rome”). ~dare~ descends from PIE *~deh₃-~ (“to give”). Cognate with Italian ~datare~ and Spanish ~datar~, and, through ~data~, with English ~date~.

So the very notion of a calendar “date” is fossilized inside a giving: a letter was “given” on such a day, and the participle naming the act of giving slid over to name the day itself. To ~dater~ a document is to record that moment of issue; from there the verb grew the intransitive sense “to be old-fashioned,” as in ~ça date~ — a thing so plainly of its time that it betrays its age. The same ~dare~ root runs deep in French through ~donner~, ~don~, ~dose~, and learned ~data~; and ~dater~ itself anchors a tidy family — ~datation~, ~datable~, ~antidater~, and ~postdater~ — every one of them still quietly turning on the idea of something “given” at a fixed point in time.
en.demander:
From Old French ~demander~ (attested around 980), from Latin ~dēmandāre~ (“to entrust, to commit; to charge with”), built from ~dē-~ (“fully, away”) and ~mandāre~ (“to commit to one's charge, to order”). ~mandāre~ itself is transparently ~manus~ (“hand”) plus a verb of giving, so its literal sense is “to put into someone's hand” — to entrust. ~manus~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~man-~ / *~meh₂-~ (“hand”). Cognate verbs survive as Italian ~domandare~ and Spanish ~demandar~, and the ~mandāre~ family entered English as ~mandate~, ~command~, ~commend~, and the legal ~demand~.

The semantic shift from “to entrust, to commit” toward “to ask, to request” happened within Latin and Romance: charging someone with a task slid into requesting something of them. French ~demander~ therefore lost the heavy sense of “entrust” and became the everyday verb for “to ask” — a question, a favour, a price. English ~demand~ kept a more forceful, insistent tone, which is why the French ~demander~ is a notorious false friend: ~je vous demande~ is a polite “I ask you,” not “I demand of you.” From ~manus~ and ~mandāre~ come a broad French family, including ~commander~ (“to order”), ~recommander~ (“to recommend”), ~mandat~ (“mandate; warrant”), and ~mander~ (“to summon”).
en.demeurer:
From Old French ~demorer~, ~demourer~ (“to delay, to remain, to dwell”), from Latin ~demorari~ (“to linger, to tarry, to stay”), a compound of the prefix ~de-~ and the deponent verb ~morari~ (“to delay, to tarry”), itself from the noun ~mora~ (“a delay, a pause”). Cognate with Italian ~dimorare~ (“to dwell, to reside”), Spanish ~morar~ (“to dwell”) and Portuguese ~morar~ (“to live, to reside”), all reflexes of the same family.

The Latin sense was strictly temporal — to be held up, to be delayed — and that meaning survived early on: the medieval expression ~sans demeurer~ meant “without delay.” But a thing that lingers in a place comes to belong there, and so ~demeurer~ drifted from “to tarry” toward “to remain” and finally “to dwell, to reside,” the meaning the verb still carries when one asks where someone ~demeure~. French preserves both threads: the residential one in the noun ~demeure~ (“a dwelling”), and the old temporal one in the legal locution ~mettre en demeure~ (“to serve formal notice,” literally to put someone in a state of delay or default) and in the adverb ~désormais~, whose buried ~-mais~ goes back to the same ~mora~. The participle ~demeurant~ even works as a connective, ~au demeurant~ meaning “for the rest, moreover.”
en.descendre:
From Latin ~dēscendere~ (“to climb down, to go down”), a compound of the prefix ~dē-~ (“down from”) and ~scandere~ (“to climb, to mount”). ~Scandere~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~skend-~ (“to leap up, to climb”), so the Latin verb is, literally, a “climbing-down” — a downward motion expressed, paradoxically, by a verb of ascent. The root shows up across Latin in ~ascendere~ (“to climb up”) and ~trānscendere~ (“to climb across”), and survives in English borrowings such as ~ascend~, ~descend~, and ~transcend~.

Attested in French from the eleventh century, ~descendre~ kept its plain sense of moving from a higher point to a lower one. By the eighteenth century it had acquired a figurative use — “to derive one's origin from,” as one ~descends~ from an ancestor — turning a movement through space into a movement through generations, down the branches of a family tree. The same root *~skend-~ underlies the noun ~descendant~ (“offspring”) and ~descente~ (“a going down, a raid”). The wider Latin family of ~scandere~ also reached French in the learned word ~scander~ (“to scan verse”), where the climbing sense survives in the metrical “stepping” through a line of poetry.
en.dessiner:
From Latin ~designare~ (“to mark out, to designate, to trace”), a compound of the prefix ~de-~ and the verb ~signare~ (“to mark with a sign”), itself from ~signum~ (“a sign, a mark”). The route into French was indirect: the Italian ~disegnare~ — which had specialized toward the sense “to draw” — reshaped the older French word, giving the modern ~dessiner~. The very same Latin ~designare~ also descends straight into French as ~désigner~ (“to designate, to point out”), so ~dessiner~ and ~désigner~ are a doublet. English ~design~ and ~designate~, Spanish ~diseñar~, and Portuguese ~desenhar~ belong to the same family.

The split is telling. Latin ~designare~ meant to mark something out — and that single act forks two ways: you can mark a thing in order to point to it (whence ~désigner~) or mark lines on a surface to depict it (whence ~dessiner~). The noun ~dessin~ (“a drawing”) and ~dessein~ (“a design, an intention”) were long spelled alike and only later prised apart, preserving in French the old kinship between drawing a figure and forming a plan. Through ~signum~, ~dessiner~ is also cousin to ~signe~, ~signal~, ~signer~, ~signature~, ~enseigne~ (“a shop sign”), and ~enseigner~ (“to teach,” literally to mark out for someone).
en.destiner:
From Latin ~destinare~ (“to make fast, fix, establish, resolve upon”), formed from the prefix ~de-~ and an element ~-stinare~ connected to ~stare~ (“to stand”). It thus belongs to the great family of the Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”), through a nasal-infixed derivative meaning roughly “to make stand firm.” The same root and the same ~-stinare~ element appear in Latin ~obstinare~ (“to stand firm against”), which gives French ~obstiner~, and at a deeper level it links ~destiner~ to Greek ~histēmi~ (“I cause to stand”) and to English ~stand~.

The metaphor is striking: to destine something is to make it ~stand fixed~, to settle it firmly in place for a purpose — fate as a thing nailed down in advance. Latin ~destinare~ ran from the concrete (“to fasten, to fix a target”) to the abstract (“to resolve, to set apart for”), and French kept the “set apart for a purpose” sense in ~destiner quelqu'un à une carrière~. The past-participle noun ~destinata~ underlies the idea of a fixed endpoint, surfacing in ~destination~ and ~destinée~, while the abstract noun gives ~destin~ (“fate, that which is fixed”). The same *~steh₂-~ root, so prolific in French, also yields ~stable~, ~constant~, ~obstiner~, and a host of words built on standing firm.
en.devenir:
From Latin ~dēvenīre~ (“to come down to, to arrive at, to fall into a state”), formed from the prefix ~dē-~ (“down, from”) and ~venīre~ (“to come”), the source of French ~venir~. Latin ~venīre~ descends from Proto-Italic *~gʷenjō~ and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to step”), one of the most productive verbal roots in the family. Through that root ~devenir~ is cognate with Italian ~divenire~ and Spanish ~devenir~, and, more distantly, with English ~come~ and German ~kommen~, which spring from the same PIE source.

The metaphor at the heart of the word is motion turned into transformation: to ~devenir~ something is, etymologically, to ~come~ to it, to arrive at a new state as one arrives at a place. Latin already used ~dēvenīre~ for falling into a condition, and French generalized it into the ordinary verb of becoming. The root *~gʷem-~ seeded a remarkable crowd of French words through ~venīre~ and its Latin relatives: ~venir~, ~avenir~ (“future,” literally what is “to come”), ~revenir~, ~convenir~, ~prévenir~, ~aventure~, ~revenu~, and ~souvenir~ — the last a memory that, etymologically, “comes up from below” into the mind.
en.devoir:
From Old French ~devoir~ (also ~deveir~), from Latin ~dēbēre~ (“to owe, to be bound to”). The Latin verb is a contraction of *~dēhibēre~, from ~dē-~ (“from, away”) plus ~habēre~ (“to have, hold”) — literally “to hold away from someone,” that is, to keep what is theirs and so to owe it. Through ~habēre~ it shares the root of ~avoir~. Romance cousins include Spanish ~deber~ and Italian ~dovere~, both meaning “to owe” and “must.”

The meaning traveled a clean path: from “to hold something belonging to another” (to owe), to the felt pressure of that obligation (“to be bound to”), to a pure modal of necessity (“must, ought”). French ~devoir~ carries all three at once — ~je te dois cent euros~ (I owe you), ~je dois partir~ (I must leave) — and, like ~pouvoir~, it froze into a noun: ~le devoir~ (“duty”), and in the plural ~les devoirs~ (“homework”). From the same Latin ~dēbēre~ and its noun ~dēbitum~ come ~dette~ (“debt”), the learned doublet ~débit~, and ~débiteur~; and English borrowed ~debt~, ~debit~, and — by way of the Old French phrase “to put in devoir” — ~endeavour~.
en.diffuser:
A 15th-century formation, built on the adjective ~diffus~ (“diffuse, spread out”) plus the verb-forming suffix ~-er~, created to render the Latin verb ~diffundere~ (“to pour out, to spread”). That verb is ~dis-~ (“apart, in different directions”) plus ~fundere~ (“to pour”), whose past participle ~diffusus~ gave ~diffus~. ~Fundere~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~ǵʰewd-~, an extension of *~ǵʰew-~ (“to pour”), cognate with Ancient Greek ~χέω~ (~kheō~, “I pour”) and Gothic ~giutan~ (“to pour”).

The image is liquid: ~diffundere~ meant to pour a fluid so that it spreads in every direction, and ~diffuser~ kept that sense of even, outward spreading — of light, heat, or a scent through a room. From there it took on “to circulate, to disseminate,” and in the twentieth century became the standard word for broadcasting by radio and television. French built a whole series of verbs on ~fundere~ for pouring in different directions: ~infuser~ (to pour in), ~perfuser~ (to pour through), ~transfuser~ (to pour across), and ~confus~ — literally “poured together,” hence muddled. The noun ~diffusion~ shares the same source.
en.diminuer:
French ~diminuer~ comes from Latin ~dēminuere~ (“to make smaller, to lessen”), formed from ~dē-~ (“down, from”) plus ~minuere~ (“to make small, to reduce”). The simple verb ~minuere~ rests on the adjective stem of ~minor~ / ~minus~ (“less”), and the whole family traces to a Proto-Indo-European root *~mey-~ (“small, to lessen”). That root is widespread: Gothic ~mins~ (“less”), Ancient Greek ~minúthō~ (“to diminish”), and Sanskrit ~minā́ti~ (“to lessen, to damage”) are all cousins, as is English ~minor~, borrowed from Latin. Italian ~diminuire~ and Spanish ~disminuir~ are its Romance siblings.

The classical sense, “to break into smaller pieces, to reduce,” narrowed cleanly into the modern “to decrease.” A delightful surprise lies in the relatives: the past participle ~minūtus~ (“made small”) gave French ~menu~ — both the adjective (“slender, fine,” as in ~menu peuple~, “the small folk”) and the noun ~menu~, originally a “detailed,” i.e. finely itemized, list, which English then borrowed back for restaurant cards. The same ~minūtus~ also produced ~minute~ (the time unit, a “small” division of the hour). Around this root cluster French ~diminution~, ~diminutif~, ~moindre~ (the irregular comparative, straight from ~minor~), ~mince~ (“thin”), and the learned ~minimum~ and ~minuscule~. To diminish, etymologically, is simply to make ever more ~menu~.
en.dire:
From Old and Middle French ~dire~, from Vulgar Latin *~dīgere~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~dīcere~ (“to say, to pronounce, to express in words”). The popular form *~dīgere~ arose by analogy with verbs in ~-gere~ whose past participles ended in ~-ctum~, a pattern ~dīcere~ shared (its participle ~dictum~). The verb goes back to Proto-Italic *~deikō~ and to Proto-Indo-European *~deyḱ-~ (“to show, to point out”). Through that root ~dire~ is cognate with English ~token~ and ~teach~, and with German ~zeigen~ (“to show”).

The leap from “to show” to “to say” is the heart of the story: to speak was first to point something out, to indicate it in words. That older sense of pointing survives all around the Latin family — in ~doigt~ (“finger,” from ~digitus~), in ~indiquer~, and in ~index~. From ~dīcere~ itself French inherited a crowd of words: ~dit~ (“said, saying”), the prefixed ~prédire~ (“to foretell”), ~contredire~ (“to contradict”), ~interdire~ (“to forbid,” literally “to say between”), and ~maudire~ (“to curse,” from ~maledīcere~, “to speak ill”). The learned ~diction~, ~dictée~, and ~verdict~ (“a true saying”) carry the original Latin ~dict-~ stem unworn.
en.diriger:
A learned fourteenth-century borrowing from Latin ~dirigere~ (“to set straight, align, guide”), formed from the prefix ~dis-~ (“apart, in different directions”) and ~regere~ (“to guide, rule, keep straight”). ~Regere~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₃reg-~ (“to move in a straight line, to direct”), one of the great roots of kingship and rightness. Through it ~diriger~ is cognate with English ~right~ and German ~recht~, and with Latin ~rex~ (“king,” the one who keeps the realm straight).

The governing image is the straight line. To direct is to set something on a straight course, whether a road, a gaze, an enterprise, or an orchestra; the reflexive ~se diriger vers~ (“to head toward”) keeps the literal sense of taking a straight bearing. The past participle of ~regere~ was ~rectus~ (“straight, right”), which gave French ~droit~ — so ~diriger~ and ~droit~ are cousins under the skin. Because French borrowed ~dirigere~ directly from Latin rather than inheriting it through the spoken chain, it kept its bookish shape. From the same *~h₃reg-~ root come a broad family: ~direct~, ~direction~, ~directeur~, ~dresser~, ~adresse~, ~régir~, ~règle~, and ~roi~.
en.discuter:
From Latin ~discutere~ (“to strike apart, to shatter, to break up by shaking”), compounded from ~dis-~ (“apart, asunder”) and ~quatere~ (“to shake, to strike”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~kʷēt-~ (“to shake”). That root also yielded the frequentative ~quassāre~ (“to shatter”), source of English ~quash~, and is cognate with German ~schütteln~ (“to shake”). Italian preserves the verb almost unchanged as ~discutere~.

The original sense was strikingly physical — to dash to pieces, to make something fly apart by shaking it. Late Latin already shifted ~discutere~ toward the figurative: to “shake apart” a question meant to examine it, to break a matter down into its elements. From there the modern senses follow naturally — to debate, to thrash out, and in everyday French simply to chat (« discuter avec un ami »). The image is a good one to remember: a discussion is, etymologically, a controlled shattering of a topic into examinable fragments. The same root underlies a cluster of French words sharing the idea of striking or shaking — ~discussion~, ~indiscutable~, ~percuter~ (“to strike, to collide”), ~percussion~, and ~secouer~ (“to shake”), the last descending from Latin ~succutere~.
en.disparaître:
First attested in 1509, ~disparaître~ was formed in French from the verb ~paraître~ (“to appear, seem”) with the prefix ~dis-~ (expressing reversal or removal), modeled on the Latin ~dispārēre~ (“to vanish”). That Latin verb had already produced an Old French ~disparoir~ (“to disappear”), but the older form was ousted by the new compound built on ~paraître~. The base ~paraître~ itself comes, through Old French ~paroistre~, from Vulgar Latin *~pārēscere~, an inchoative of Latin ~pārēre~ (“to come into view, to appear, to obey”). Spanish ~desaparecer~ and Italian ~disparire~ / ~sparire~ are its Romance counterparts; English ~disappear~ is a parallel ~dis-~ formation on ~appear~.

The key word is the prefix. Where ~paraître~ presents something to view and ~apparaître~ brings it forth, ~dis-~ undoes the showing: ~disparaître~ is, etymologically, “to un-appear.” Latin ~pārēre~ also carried the sense “to obey” — a servant who “appears” at command — which is why the same root gives French ~apparaître~, ~apparition~, ~apparent~, and the noun ~apparence~, alongside the more surprising ~parer~ in some of its uses. The whole family circles the act of becoming, or ceasing to be, visible. A neat detail: French rebuilt this verb from native parts in the Renaissance rather than simply inheriting the worn-down ~disparoir~, choosing transparency of form over the older sound-changed shape.
en.disposer:
From Latin ~dispōnere~ (“to set out, to arrange in different places”), from the prefix ~dis-~ (“apart, in different directions”) and ~pōnere~ (“to put, to place”). ~Pōnere~ is itself widely analyzed as an old compound of *~po-~ (“off, away”) and ~sinere~ (“to leave, to let”), with the past participle ~positus~. In its French form, ~disposer~ joined the great ~-poser~ family, where the descendants of ~pōnere~ blended with those of Late Latin *~pausāre~ (“to cease, to set down”).

The sense is one of placing things apart in good order: to ~disposer~ is to arrange, to lay out, to organize, and reflexively ~se disposer à~ is to get ready to act. From “having things arranged to one's liking” came the idiom ~disposer de~ (“to have at one's disposal”). The ~pōnere~ family blossomed into a regiment of French verbs — ~poser~, ~composer~, ~proposer~, ~exposer~, ~imposer~, ~déposer~ — plus nouns like ~position~, ~disposition~, and ~dépôt~. Curiously, ~disponible~ (“available”) preserves the original Latin ~n~ that ~disposer~ itself has lost, a small fossil of ~dispōnere~ hiding inside the modern word.
en.distinguer:
From Latin ~distinguere~ (“to separate, to mark off, to distinguish”), from ~dis-~ (“apart”) + ~stinguere~ (“to prick, to mark with points”). ~Stinguere~ rests on a Proto-Italic *~stengʷō~, from a Proto-Indo-European root *~steygʷ-~ broadly associated with sharp points and pricking. Through that family the word is connected to English ~stick~, ~sting~, and ~stigma~, and to Greek ~stízō~ (“to tattoo, to prick”). The image at the bottom is concrete: to distinguish was to set things apart by pricking marks between them.

From that literal marking-off the verb climbed to the mental act of telling one thing from another, and then to the social sense in ~distingué~, “distinguished, set apart by excellence” — the same gesture of marking out, now applied to merit. A neat in-house relative is ~éteindre~ (“to extinguish”), from Latin ~exstinguere~: the prick-and-mark root, with a different prefix, came to mean snuffing a flame, perhaps via the idea of pricking it out. French keeps the learned line in ~distinct~, ~distinction~, ~distinctement~, and ~indistinct~, all sharpened, etymologically, to a point.
en.distribuer:
From Latin ~distribuere~ (“to deal out, divide, apportion”), a compound of the prefix ~dis-~ (“apart, individually”) and the verb ~tribuere~ (“to assign, grant, allot”). That verb is itself drawn from ~tribus~, a “tribe” or division of the Roman people: to ~tribuere~ was originally to allot something among the tribes. French borrowed ~distribuer~ as a learned word directly from Latin, which is why it shows none of the wear of a verb passed down through Old French speech. Through ~tribuere~ it is cognate with English ~distribute~, ~tribute~, ~attribute~, and ~contribute~.

The semantic core is division followed by sharing out. From the civic act of apportioning duties among the Roman ~tribus~, ~distribuere~ generalized to any dividing-and-dealing-out: of goods, of letters, of roles in a play. Modern French ~distribuer~ covers handing out cards, delivering mail, and casting actors (~la distribution~ d'un film is its cast). The root ~tribus~ proved remarkably fertile: French inherited ~tribu~ (“tribe”) itself, ~tribut~ (“tribute”), ~attribuer~ (“to attribute”), ~contribuer~ (“to contribute”), ~rétribuer~ (“to remunerate”), and ~tributaire~ (“tributary”). It even survives in the unexpected ~tribunal~ and ~tribun~, since a ~tribunus~ was originally the head of a tribe — so the courtroom and the postal carrier share, distantly, the same ancestral division of Rome's people.
en.diviser:
From Latin ~dīvidere~ (“to force apart, to separate, to distribute, to share out”). The verb is an assimilated ~dis-~ (“apart”) prefixed to a root *~videre~ meaning “to separate,” which de Vaan traces to a Proto-Indo-European *~(d)widʰ-~ (“to separate, to divide in two”). This root is unrelated to ~vidēre~ “to see”; it is, however, the same one behind Latin ~viduus~ (“bereft, void”) and so behind ~vidua~ (“widow”) — division and widowhood share an idea of being set apart. Italian ~dividere~ and Spanish ~dividir~ are cognates, as is English ~divide~.

French actually inherited this verb twice. The popular, orally transmitted form became ~deviser~ (originally “to divide up,” later “to chat, to converse” — talk that goes back and forth, portioned out between speakers), while the learned, book-borrowed form gave ~diviser~, keeping the plain mathematical and physical sense of separating into parts. From the same source come ~division~, ~diviseur~, ~divisible~, ~divisibilité~, and ~divisionnaire~, plus, through ~vidua~, the surprising relatives ~veuf~ and ~veuve~ (“widower, widow”) and ~divorce~ — all turning on the ancient image of two things pulled asunder.
en.dominer:
From Latin ~dominārī~ (“to be master, to rule, to reign”), a verb derived from ~dominus~ (“master, lord of the household”), which in turn comes from ~domus~ (“house, home”). The chain reaches back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dem-~ (“house, household; to build”). The same ~dominus~ underlies Italian ~dominare~ and Spanish ~dominar~, while ~domus~ shows up across the family in Greek ~domos~ (“house”) and, by a Germanic detour, in English ~timber~ (originally “building material”).

The striking thing is how authority grew out of the home: the ~dominus~ was first simply the head of a ~domus~, the master of a household, and his domestic command broadened into mastery, rule, and at last sheer ascendancy. To ~dominer~ now ranges from a sovereign ruling a realm to a peak ~dominating~ a valley below — physical and figurative supremacy alike. The same root flowered into a large French family: ~maison~ (from ~mansiō~), ~domestique~, ~domicile~, ~domaine~, ~dame~ (from ~domina~), ~madame~, ~dominical~, and the borrowed ~dôme~, all gathered, ultimately, around the idea of the house and its lord.
en.donner:
From Old French ~doner~, from Latin ~dōnāre~ (“to give as a gift, to bestow”), itself built on the noun ~dōnum~ (“gift”). Both go back to Proto-Indo-European *~deh₃-~ (“to give”), by way of *~deh₃nom~ (“gift”). Through that root ~donner~ is cognate with Spanish ~donar~ and Italian ~donare~, with Sanskrit ~dā~ (“to give”), and with Greek ~dōron~ (“gift,” as in the name Theodore, “gift of god”).

A quiet upset lies behind the everyday verb. Classical Latin had two words here: plain ~dăre~ (“to give”) and ~dōnāre~ (“to make a gift of”). In Gaul, ~dăre~ was driven out early and almost completely, so French took its ordinary “give” from the more ceremonious ~dōnāre~ — where Spanish and Italian kept ~dăre~ alive in ~dar~ and ~dare~. From the same root French keeps ~don~ (“gift”), ~donateur~ (“donor”), ~donation~, and ~pardonner~ (“to forgive,” literally “to give thoroughly”), while the learned ~dose~ and ~anecdote~ reach back through Greek to the very same *~deh₃-~. The gift, in French, swallowed the verb of plain giving whole.
en.dormir:
From Middle French ~dormir~, from Old French ~dormir~ (attested around 1100), from Latin ~dormīre~ (“to sleep”), from Proto-Italic *~dormiō~ and Proto-Indo-European *~drem-~ (“to sleep”). Cognate, through the Latin verb, with Italian ~dormire~, Spanish and Portuguese ~dormir~, and Catalan ~dormir~; through the PIE root, with Old Church Slavonic ~drěmati~ (“to doze”) and Sanskrit ~drāti~ (“sleeps”).

Unusually for a common verb, the meaning never wandered: ~dormir~ has meant simply “to sleep” for two thousand years, with idiomatic extensions like ~dormir comme une souche~ (“to sleep like a log”) and ~qui dort dîne~ (“he who sleeps, dines”). The Latin root proved richly generative. From ~dormīre~ and its relatives French drew ~dormeur~ (“sleeper”), ~dormant~ (“sleeping, dormant,” as in ~eau dormante~, “still water”), ~endormir~ (“to put to sleep”), ~rendormir~ (“to send back to sleep”), and ~dortoir~ (“dormitory”). English borrowed many of the same: ~dormant~, ~dormitory~, and even ~dormouse~, the “sleeping mouse.” The architectural ~dormer~ window, too, descends from this root — once a window for a sleeping-room.
en.doter:
From Latin ~dotare~ (“to endow, to provide with a dowry”), a denominative verb built on the noun ~dos~, stem ~dot-~ (“dowry, marriage portion”). That noun goes back to Proto-Italic *~dōtis~ and to the Proto-Indo-European root *~deh₃-~ (“to give”). Through that root ~doter~ is cognate with a remarkable spread of words: Ancient Greek ~dōron~ (“gift”) and ~dosis~ (“a giving, a dose”), Latin ~donare~ (“to give”), and the English borrowings ~donate~, ~dose~, and even ~date~ (from the Roman dating formula “given on such a day”).

The verb began in the law of marriage: to ~doter~ a daughter was to settle a dowry on her, the property she brought to the household. From that contractual core the sense widened generously. To ~doter~ came to mean to furnish or equip with any advantage — funds, talents, qualities — so that today a region may be ~doté~ with resources, a person ~doté~ with charm, a school ~doté~ of a new library. French keeps the original noun ~dot~ (“dowry”) alongside the derived ~dotation~ (“endowment, allocation”). The same *~deh₃-~ root, entering French through learned Latin, also yields ~donner~, ~don~, ~donation~, ~dose~, and ~anecdote~ (Greek for something “not given out,” i.e. unpublished).
en.douter:
Attested in Old French from around 980, from Latin ~dubitāre~ (“to waver, hesitate, doubt”), a frequentative connected to ~dubius~ (“doubtful, uncertain”) and ultimately to ~duo~ (“two”). The image at the heart of doubt is being pulled between two ways: to doubt is to hover between two possibilities, unable to commit. Italian ~dubitare~ and Spanish ~dudar~ carry the same Latin root, and English ~doubt~ borrowed it through French.

The earliest French sense was not intellectual uncertainty but fear: in Old French ~douter~ meant “to dread, to fear” — to be of two minds before a danger. This sense survives intact in the compound ~redouter~ (“to dread”). Over time the verb shifted from fearing to questioning, from apprehension to mere uncertainty. The reflexive ~se douter de~ then swung almost to the opposite — “to suspect, to surmise,” to be fairly sure of something. The silent ~b~ in English ~doubt~ was reinserted by scholars to mirror Latin ~dubitāre~; French spelling never restored it. From the same root come ~doute~, ~douteux~, ~indubitable~, and ~dubitatif~.
en.dresser:
From Late Latin *~directiāre~ (“to straighten, to set upright”), a verb built on Latin ~directus~ (“straight, direct”), the past participle of ~dīrigere~ (“to lay straight, to direct”). That verb combines ~dis-~ with ~regere~ (“to rule, to keep straight”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₃reǵ-~ (“to straighten, to make right”) — the root behind English ~right~ and ~royal~. French ~direct~, ~droit~, ~adresse~, and ~dresser~ are all doublets descending from this single Latin family.

The core idea is “to make straight, to set up vertically,” and French ~dresser~ fans out from it impressively. One ~dresse~ a tent, a ladder, or a statue (raises it upright), ~dresse~ a table (sets it), ~dresse~ a list or a report (draws it up), and ~dresse~ the dishes on a plate (arranges them) — the source of culinary ~dressage~ (“plating”). Applied to animals and people, ~dresser~ means “to train, to break in, to tame,” straightening behaviour into obedience; a trained horse undergoes ~dressage~, the term now used in English for the equestrian discipline. Related French words include ~dressage~, ~redresser~ (“to straighten again, to set right”), ~adresse~ (both “address” and “skill”), and ~dresseur~ (“animal trainer”).
en.durer:
From Latin ~durare~ (“to harden, to endure, to last”), attested in French from the 11th century. ~Durare~ derives from the adjective ~durus~ (“hard”), whose deeper Indo-European origin is debated — one account ties it to a root meaning “long” (compare Ancient Greek ~dērós~, “long-lasting,” and Sanskrit ~dūrá~, “far”), another to *~deru-~ (“hard, firm,” as of wood). Whichever holds, the link between hardness and persistence is plain: what is hard endures. Romance kept the verb in Italian ~durare~ and Spanish ~durar~.

Latin ~durare~ already spanned the physical and the temporal — to make hard, but also to hold out and to last — and French settled firmly on the temporal: to ~durer~ is to go on in time, to last. A storm, a friendship, or a battery can ~durer~. The hardness sense lives on in the sibling adjective ~dur~ (“hard, tough”) and its kin ~durcir~ (“to harden”), ~dureté~ (“hardness”), and ~endurer~ (“to endure”). The time sense surfaces in ~durée~ (“duration”) and the preposition ~durant~ (“during”), which began life as a present participle of ~durer~ — “while X is lasting.”
en.débuter:
A French formation (16th century) from the noun ~but~ (“a goal, a target, an aim”) with the prefix ~dé-~ and the verbal ending ~-er~. The noun ~but~ is of debated origin: it may be a variant of ~bout~ (“an end, a tip”), or come from Old Norse ~butr~ (“a log, a block”), perhaps as the wooden mark one aimed at. Either way, ~débuter~ literally means to move away from the ~but~ — the term comes from games of bowls and target sports, where to ~débuter~ was to make the opening throw that knocks a ball off its mark.

From that opening cast the verb generalized to “to make a first move, to begin,” and especially to begin a public career: an actor, a singer, or a politician ~débute~ when first appearing before the public. French borrowed this sense out again as the noun ~début~ (“a beginning, a first appearance”), which English then took as ~debut~ along with ~debutante~ (a young woman making her ~début~ in society). The opposite end of the same game survives in ~buter~ (“to strike against, to stumble”) and the related ~butoir~ (“a buffer, a stop”), all circling the original notion of the mark struck at the start of play.
en.décider:
From Latin ~dēcīdere~ (“to cut off, settle, decide a question”), formed from the prefix ~dē-~ (“off, away”) and ~caedere~ (“to cut, to strike, to fell”). ~Caedere~ comes from Proto-Italic *~kaidō~, from PIE *~kh₂eyd-~ (“to cut, to hew”). The image is concrete and decisive: to decide is to cut a difficulty off at a stroke. Through the same root ~décider~ is related to English ~caesura~ and to the whole ~-cide~ family of words.

Where our deliberations feel like weighing, the Roman metaphor was a blade severing the tangle of a dispute — a resolution reached by cutting rather than by pondering. From the root ~caedere~ French inherited a sharp-edged vocabulary: ~concis~ (literally “cut short”), ~précis~ (“cut off in front,” hence “exact”), ~incisif~, ~circoncision~, and the murderous ~-cide~ suffix in ~homicide~, ~suicide~, and ~insecticide~ (an ~insecte~ being itself “cut into” segments, though from a different root). ~Décider~ thus shares its ancestry with both the surgeon's precision and the killer's blow — every decision a small, clean cut through uncertainty.
en.déclarer:
From Latin ~declarare~ (“to make clear, to announce, to reveal”), a compound of the prefix ~de-~ (here intensive, “thoroughly”) and ~clarare~ (“to make bright or clear”), a verb formed on the adjective ~clarus~ (“clear, bright, distinct”). ~Clarus~ descends from Proto-Italic and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~kelh₁-~ (“to call, to cry out”) — the same root behind Latin ~calare~ (“to proclaim”) and ~clamare~ (“to shout”). Italian ~dichiarare~ and Spanish ~declarar~ are the Romance siblings, and English ~declare~ is a borrowing from the same French stem.

The metaphor is light: to ~déclarer~ is to bring something out of obscurity into the open, to make it bright and plain to all. From this came the formal senses of announcing officially — declaring war, declaring one's love, declaring goods at customs, declaring income to the tax office. French ~déclaration~ carries the same weight, as in the ~Déclaration des droits de l'homme~. The underlying ~clarus~ seeds a luminous family in French: ~clair~, ~clarté~, ~clairière~ (a clearing where light enters the forest), ~clairvoyant~, ~éclairer~, and ~clarifier~ — all turning on the idea of brightness made manifest.
en.découvrir:
French ~découvrir~ is a transparent compound: the prefix ~dé-~ (“un-, reversal”) joined to ~couvrir~ (“to cover”), descending through Old French ~descovrir~ from Late Latin ~discooperīre~. The base ~couvrir~ comes from Latin ~cooperīre~ (“to cover over”), an intensive ~co(m)-~ plus ~operīre~ (“to close, to cover”), which rests on a Proto-Indo-European compound of *~op-~ (“over”) and the root *~wer-~ (“to cover, to enclose”). Curiously, the very same *~wer-~ root, with a different prefix, gives Latin ~aperīre~ (“to open, to uncover”) — so covering and uncovering are etymological twins.

The word's literal sense is simply “to uncover”: to remove the ~couverture~ from something. From taking the lid off came the figurative leap to “to find out, to reveal, to discover” — to uncover a truth, a land, a secret. English ~discover~ took exactly the same path from the same Late Latin source. The family is broad in French: ~couvrir~, ~couverture~ (“cover, blanket”), ~couvert~ (a place setting, what is “covered”), ~recouvrir~, and the nouns of finding, ~découverte~ (“a discovery”) and ~découvreur~. The age of exploration is, etymologically, an age of lifting lids — every ~découverte~ a veil pulled back.
en.décrire:
From Latin ~describere~ (“to copy off, to sketch, to describe”), a compound of the prefix ~de-~ (“down, off”) and ~scribere~ (“to write”). French refashioned the verb on the model of ~écrire~, giving ~décrire~. ~scribere~ originally meant “to scratch or carve marks,” from the Proto-Indo-European root *~skribʰ-~, an extended form of *~sker-~ (“to cut”). Through this root ~décrire~ is cognate with English ~scribe~ and ~script~, and — by an early Latin loan into Germanic — with English ~shrive~ and German ~schreiben~ (“to write”).

The buried image is one of cutting: the earliest writing was incised into wood, clay, or stone, and ~scribere~ named that scratching. In ~describere~ the prefix ~de-~ adds the idea of tracing something off or sketching it out, point by point — which is just what a description does, drawing a verbal outline of its subject. The same root left French a dense cluster of ~-crire~ verbs, each with its prefix: ~écrire~ (“to write”), ~inscrire~ (“to inscribe”), ~prescrire~ (“to prescribe”), ~souscrire~ (“to subscribe”), and ~transcrire~ (“to transcribe”), alongside the nouns ~description~, ~écriture~, and ~scribe~ itself.
en.décéder:
From Latin ~dēcēdere~ (“to go away, to withdraw, to depart”), formed from the prefix ~dē-~ (“away, from”) and ~cēdere~ (“to go, to yield, to withdraw”). ~Cēdere~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~kezdō~ and, by one reconstruction, to a Proto-Indo-European *~ḱesd-~ (“to go away, to drive off”); the deeper history is disputed. Through ~cēdere~ it is cognate with Italian ~cedere~, Spanish ~ceder~, and English ~cede~, ~concede~, and ~cease~.

The Latin ~dēcēdere~ was at first wholly neutral — “to withdraw, to step aside, to depart” — and only by gentle euphemism came to mean “to depart this life,” to die. French kept exactly this softened sense: ~décéder~ is the formal, administrative, and respectful word for dying, the verb of death certificates and obituaries, where the blunt ~mourir~ would jar. Its companion noun is ~décès~, from Latin ~dēcessus~ (“a departure”), and one who has died is ~décédé~. From the same ~cēdere~ French gathered a large family of measured movements: ~céder~, ~accéder~, ~procéder~, ~succéder~, ~précéder~, ~excéder~, and the nouns ~accès~, ~succès~, and ~procès~ — each a kind of going, yielding, or coming-to.
en.dédier:
Borrowed from Latin ~dedicare~ (“to proclaim, to consecrate, to dedicate”), a compound of ~de-~ (“completely, solemnly”) and ~dicare~ (“to proclaim, to set apart”), a durative variant of ~dicere~ (“to say,” the ancestor of French ~dire~). The chain reaches back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~deyḱ-~ (“to point out, to show”). It is cognate with English ~dedicate~, with Greek ~deíknumi~ (“to show”), and with Latin ~digitus~ (“finger,” the pointing thing) and ~index~.

The earliest sense was religious: to ~dedicate~ a temple was to point it out solemnly to a god, to declare it his. From that consecration the verb softened into the familiar “to dedicate a book to someone,” the modern offering of a work in another's honor. French keeps both the learned and the popular line: ~dédier~ for the solemn dedication, the noun ~dédicace~, and the back-formed ~dédicacer~ (“to autograph,” to sign a copy). Through the same root *~deyḱ-~ run a surprising company of words — ~dire~ and ~diction~, ~index~ and ~indiquer~ — all turning, at bottom, on the simple act of pointing and saying so.
en.défendre:
From Latin ~defendere~ (“to ward off, to repel, to defend”), attested in French around 1100. The verb is ~de-~ (“away”) plus *~fendere~ (“to strike, to thrust”), a root preserved only in compounds and traced to Proto-Indo-European *~gʷʰen-~ (“to strike, to kill”). Its twin compound is ~offendere~ (“to strike against, to offend”), and the pair survive together in English ~defend~ and ~offend~. The same PIE root lies, far back, behind a wide scatter of striking-words across the family.

The original idea was martial: to ~defendere~ was to strike a blow away from someone, to fend off an attacker — English keeps the bare core in ~fend~ and ~fender~. From “to keep harm away” the French verb branched in two directions. One is the familiar “to protect, to defend.” The other, surprising to learners, is “to forbid”: ~défendre~ à quelqu'un de faire quelque chose means to bar them from it — to ward off an action rather than a foe. So the same verb both shields and prohibits. Related French words include ~défense~ (which means both “defence” and “prohibition,” and also an elephant's tusk) and ~offenser~.
en.définir:
From Old French ~diffinir~, ~definir~, a learned borrowing from Latin ~dēfīnīre~ (“to bound, to limit, to determine precisely”), built from the prefix ~dē-~ and the verb ~fīnīre~ (“to finish, to set bounds”), itself from ~fīnis~ (“a boundary, a limit, an end”). The Old French scribes favored the spelling ~diffinir~, with ~dis-~ becoming ~dif-~ before the ~f~, partly to keep the word distinct from the classical sense of ~dēfīnīre~. Through ~fīnis~ the verb is kin to Italian ~definire~, Spanish ~definir~, and English ~define~ and ~finish~. The deeper origin of ~fīnis~ is uncertain, though it is often tentatively linked to ~fīgere~ (“to fix, to fasten”), as if a limit were a stake driven into the ground.

The Latin ~dēfīnīre~ meant literally “to mark off within boundaries,” and that spatial image still lives inside the abstract modern sense: to ~définir~ a word is to fence in its meaning, to say where it ends and where it does not. The verb entered French in the 13th century as a scholarly term and kept its precise, intellectual flavor. From the same ~fīnis~ family French inherited a wide harvest of words, among them ~fin~, ~finir~, ~final~, ~définition~, ~définitif~, ~indéfini~, and ~confiner~ — all turning, in one way or another, on the idea of a border or an end.
en.dégager:
Modern French ~dégager~ is built from the verb ~gager~ (“to pledge, to pawn”) with the privative prefix ~dé-~, which reverses an action. Its earliest attested form, ~desguagier~ (1174–76), meant quite literally “to redeem something pledged” — to recover from a pawnbroker the ~gage~ one had deposited as security. That noun ~gage~ was no Latin inheritance but a Frankish loan, *~waddi~ “pledge, deposit,” from Proto-Germanic *~wadi~ and ultimately the Proto-Indo-European root *~wedʰ-~ “to pledge, to redeem a pledge.” The same Germanic root surfaces in Dutch ~wedde~ “salary” and German ~Wette~ “a bet,” and, by a parallel borrowing into English through Norman French, in ~wage~. English ~wed~ descends from the same source: to marry was once to pledge oneself, the bridal vow being literally a pledge.

From the concrete medieval sense of buying back a pledge, ~dégager~ drifted toward abstraction. By around 1180 it could mean “to set free, to release,” and by the mid-thirteenth century “to clear away, to strip of a covering.” A pledge, once recovered, leaves one unencumbered — hence the modern senses of freeing, clearing, and emitting (a gas, a scent). The same Frankish family enriched French with ~gageure~ “a wager” and ~engager~ “to commit, to involve,” while ~dégagement~ names the very act of disengaging — the precise reversal of putting something in pledge.
en.délivrer:
Attested from the 11th century, from Late Latin ~dēlīberāre~ (“to set free”), built on ~līberāre~ (“to set at liberty”), itself from the adjective ~līber~ (“free”). ~Līber~ descends from Proto-Italic *~louðeros~ and Proto-Indo-European *~h₁lewdʰeros~ (“free,” originally “belonging to the people”), making it a distant cognate of Greek ~eleútheros~ (“free”) and German ~Leute~ (“people”).

Note that this Late Latin ~dēlīberāre~ (“to free”) is a different word from Classical Latin ~dēlīberāre~ (“to weigh, deliberate”), which gave French ~délibérer~ — the two are etymological twins that diverged. ~Délivrer~ kept the sense of releasing: freeing a captive, relieving someone of a burden, and, in the obstetric sense, delivering a mother of her child. The “handing over” sense of English ~deliver~ developed in the parallel Old French chain and survives in French ~livrer~ (“to hand over, deliver goods”) and the noun ~livraison~. From the root ~līber~ French draws ~liberté~, ~libérer~, ~libéral~, and ~délivrance~ — the whole vocabulary of freedom radiating from a single Latin adjective meaning simply “free.”
en.démarrer:
From a syncope of older French ~désamarrer~ (“to cast off moorings”), formed from the prefix ~dé-~ (reversal) and ~amarrer~ (“to moor, to make fast”), a nautical term borrowed from Dutch ~aanmarren~ (“to tie, to fasten”). ~démarrer~ thus began life on the water, with ~amarre~ (“a mooring rope”) and ~amarrer~ as its close relatives in French.

The original picture is purely maritime: to ~démarrer~ was to untie a ship from the quay so it could leave port. Once the lines were loosed, the vessel got under way — and it is that moment of getting under way, of breaking from rest into motion, that the verb kept as its core when it drifted ashore. By extension ~démarrer~ came to mean starting a car or an engine, launching a business, or kicking off any undertaking, and in cycling slang a rider who ~démarre~ suddenly accelerates away from the pack. The same idea of casting off survives in the everyday opposite ~amarrer~ and in the noun ~démarrage~ (“startup, getaway”). It is a quietly vivid metaphor: every time a French driver starts the engine, the language remembers a sailor slipping the ropes and pulling away from the dock.
en.démontrer:
From Latin ~dēmonstrāre~ (“to point out, show clearly, prove”), a compound of the prefix ~dē-~ (here intensive, “fully”) and ~mōnstrāre~ (“to show”). ~Mōnstrāre~ derives from ~mōnstrum~ (“a portent, a sign from the gods”), itself from ~monēre~ (“to warn, remind”), tracing back to the PIE root *~men-~ (“to think”). So at bottom “to demonstrate” means to make the mind take notice. Cognate with Italian ~dimostrare~ and Spanish ~demostrar~, and — borrowed — with English ~demonstrate~; the plainer French ~montrer~ shares the same ~mōnstrāre~ ancestor.

The Latin verb already split between simply showing and rigorously proving, and French kept ~démontrer~ on the side of proof: where ~montrer~ merely displays, ~démontrer~ establishes by argument. Mathematics adopted it wholesale, and the classroom flourish ~ce qu'il fallait démontrer~ (“which was to be proved”) still closes a proof, abbreviated ~CQFD~. The frightening cousin ~mōnstrum~ — a divine warning made flesh — gave French ~monstre~, so the family yokes the rational demonstration to the prodigy. From the same root *~men-~ come ~mention~, ~admonester~, and ~mémoire~ all keep the ancient sense of the mind remembering and being warned.
en.dénoncer:
From Old French ~denoncier~ (also ~denuntier~), from Latin ~dēnūntiāre~ (“to announce, to proclaim, to give formal notice”), a compound of ~dē-~ (here intensive) and ~nūntiāre~ (“to report, to bring news”), itself from ~nūntius~ (“messenger, message”). The origin of ~nūntius~ is uncertain; De Vaan tentatively traces it to a Proto-Indo-European root *~newH-~ (“to shout, to cry out”), fitting the herald who proclaims aloud. The Latin verb is cognate with Italian ~denunziare~ and Spanish ~denunciar~, and shares its second element with English ~announce~ and ~pronounce~.

In Latin and Old French the verb was neutral: simply “to make something known officially,” especially a treaty, a declaration of war, or a legal notice. Over time the sense darkened. To “give formal notice” against a person slid toward “to accuse, to inform on,” and modern French ~dénoncer~ now carries a strongly negative charge — to betray a wrongdoer to the authorities, or to publicly condemn an injustice. The older neutral meaning lingers only in formal registers, as when a state ~dénonce~ a treaty (declares it void). From the same ~nūntius~ stem come French ~annoncer~, ~prononcer~, ~énoncer~, and the noun ~nonce~ (a papal envoy — a messenger still).
en.dépasser:
A French compound of the prefix ~dé-~ and the verb ~passer~ (“to pass”), the ~dé-~ here marking the idea of going beyond or past a limit. ~Passer~ descends from Vulgar Latin *~passare~, a verb derived from the Latin noun ~passus~ (“a step, a pace”). That noun is itself the past participle of ~pandere~ (“to spread, to stretch out”), from Proto-Indo-European *~peth₂-~ (“to spread”). Cognates of ~passer~ are everywhere in Romance — Spanish ~pasar~, Italian ~passare~ — and English borrowed both ~pass~ and ~pace~ from the same source.

The underlying image is the stride: a ~passus~ was the distance covered by a step, so ~passer~ first meant to move step by step from one place to another, to go past. ~Dépasser~ sharpens this into “to go beyond” — to overtake a car, to exceed a limit, to surpass a rival, or even, figuratively, for a problem to be beyond someone (cela me ~dépasse~). The prefix ~dé-~ adds the sense of moving clear past a mark. From the same ~passer~ French built ~passage~, ~passant~, ~passerelle~, ~surpasser~, ~trépasser~ (“to pass away, to die”), and ~repasser~ (“to pass again,” hence “to iron”).
en.dépendre:
From Latin ~dependere~ (“to hang down, to hang from”), formed from the prefix ~de-~ (“down, from”) and the verb ~pendere~ (“to hang”), from Proto-Italic *~pendēō~, from the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)pend-~ (“to stretch, to pull, to draw”). Cognate forms include English ~pendant~, ~suspend~, and ~pendulum~, all sharing the image of something stretched and hanging.

French ~dépendre~ carries two senses that are really one. The concrete meaning — to ~dépendre~ a picture is to take it down off its hook — preserves the literal Latin “to hang from.” The abstract meaning grew by metaphor: that which hangs from something else relies on it, is suspended from it, and so to ~dépendre de~ someone is to “depend on” them, to hang from their support. The same root *~(s)pend-~ spread through a remarkable family, because in Latin ~pendere~ also meant “to weigh” and “to pay” — money was once weighed out. From it French inherited ~suspendre~, ~pendre~, ~pension~, ~dépense~, ~pendule~, and ~appendice~, while the “weighing” branch gave ~peser~ and, through Spanish, the ~peso~. The English ~pound~ and even ~spider~ (the “spinner,” a stretcher of thread) belong to the same ancient web.
en.déplacer:
A French formation, ~déplacer~ is built from the verb ~placer~ (“to place”) with the prefix ~dé-~, which here reverses or undoes an action — to ~déplacer~ is literally to un-place, to take a thing from where it was set. ~Placer~ derives from ~place~, from Old French ~place~, from Latin ~platea~ (“broad street, open square, courtyard”), borrowed from Ancient Greek ~πλατεῖα~ (~plateîa~, “broad way”), the feminine of “broad,” from the Proto-Indo-European root *~pleth₂-~ (“to spread out, to be flat”). Cognate with Italian ~piazza~, Spanish ~plaza~, and German ~Platz~.

The surprise is that the everyday French ~place~ began as a width: a ~platea~ was a wide thoroughfare, named for its flatness and breadth, and only later did the word narrow to a “spot” where a thing or person belongs. ~Placer~ assigns that spot; ~dé-~ revokes it. The same flat-and-broad root spread across French into ~place~, ~plat~, ~plateau~, ~plaine~, ~plan~, ~esplanade~, and ~remplacer~ (“to re-place, to replace”). Thus to ~déplacer~ furniture, a meeting, or oneself is, at root, to lift it off its flat appointed ground and set it elsewhere.
en.déployer:
From Old French ~desploier~ (“to unfold”), formed from the prefix ~dé-~ (“un-, reversal”) and ~ployer~ (“to bend, to fold”). ~ployer~ — a doublet of modern ~plier~ — descends from Latin ~plicāre~ (“to fold, to roll up”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~plek-~ (“to plait, to fold”). The same root gives English ~ply~, ~pleat~, and ~fold~, Greek ~plekein~ (“to plait”), and a whole French cluster including ~plier~, ~pli~, and ~déplier~.

The literal sense is the heart of the word: to deploy is to un-fold. A folded banner, a folded sail, a folded map — ~déployer~ spreads it out flat and open. From this concrete gesture the verb stretched into military language, where troops once tightly packed are unfolded across a field of battle, and then into everyday figurative use: one deploys energy, charm, or arguments, spreading them out for full effect. English ~deploy~ and ~display~ are in fact the same word arriving twice, both from this ~desploier~. The shared root *~plek-~ runs through a remarkably wide family of French words — ~simple~, ~double~, ~complexe~, ~exploit~, ~employer~, and ~appliquer~ among them — all turning, in one way or another, on the idea of folds laid one against another.
en.déposer:
Formed in French from the prefix ~dé-~ (“down, away,” from Latin ~de-~) and the verb ~poser~ (“to place, to set”). ~Poser~ has a famously tangled history: in form it continues Late Latin ~pausāre~ (“to halt, to cease,” borrowed from Greek ~pausis~, “a stopping”), seen in early Christian epitaphs such as ~hic pausat in pace~. But in meaning it merged with Latin ~ponere~ (“to put,” past participle ~positus~), so completely that all the French “-poser” verbs took over the senses of ~ponere~ — hence the nouns in ~-position~ rather than ~-pausation~.

With ~dé-~ added, the verb first meant “to set down what one was carrying,” then spread to “to lay aside,” “to entrust” (to ~déposer~ money at a bank), and figuratively “to depose,” to set a ruler down from the throne. The double parentage still shows in the family: from the ~pausāre~ side come ~pause~ and ~repos~ (“rest”), while the ~ponere~ side surfaces in ~déposition~, ~dépôt~, and the cousins ~exposer~, ~imposer~, and ~proposer~ — a whole conjugation built on a verb that, etymologically, means both “to rest” and “to put.”
en.dérouler:
From the verb ~rouler~ (“to roll”) with the privative, reversing prefix ~dé-~ (Latin ~dis-~), so literally “to un-roll.” ~Rouler~ comes from Old French ~roeler~, a derivative of ~roele~ (“little wheel”), from Latin ~rotella~, a diminutive of ~rota~ (“wheel”). ~Rota~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~Hrot-eh₂-~, from the root *~Hret-~ (“to run, to roll”), which also gave German ~Rad~ (“wheel”), Sanskrit ~ratha~ (“chariot”), and Lithuanian ~ratas~ (“wheel”).

The whole word is a small machine of meaning: a wheel (~rota~) turns, so a scroll or carpet ~se déroule~ when its rolled-up length is let out flat. From the literal “unroll” grew the figurative senses “to unfold, to take place over time” — events that ~se déroulent~ like a banner being released. The opposite ~enrouler~ rolls something back up. From the same ~rota~ come a rich family: ~roue~ (“wheel”), ~rouleau~, ~roulette~, ~rotule~ (“kneecap,” the little wheel of the knee), ~rotation~, ~ronde~, and even ~rôle~ — originally a ~rotulus~, a rolled-up sheet of parchment on which an actor's lines were written.
en.désigner:
From Latin ~designare~ (“to mark out, to point out, to designate”), a compound of the prefix ~de-~ (“out, off”) and a verb built on ~signum~ (“mark, sign, token, military standard”). The deeper origin of ~signum~ is genuinely disputed: some derive it from the Proto-Indo-European root *~sek-~ (“to cut”), as “something cut or carved out,” while others connect it to *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”), as the standard one follows. ~désigner~ is cognate with English ~design~ and ~designate~ and with Italian ~designare~, all resting on ~signum~.

The core image in ~designare~ is of marking something out so that it stands apart — pointing to it, singling it out. From this came both the act of indicating (to ~désigner~ a culprit, a successor, a meeting place) and, by a separate route through Italian ~disegno~, the artistic sense of “design” and “drawing.” French actually carries a doublet here: the learned ~désigner~ and the popular ~dessiner~ (“to draw”) both descend from ~designare~, the one keeping the sense of pointing out, the other the sense of tracing a figure. From the same ~signum~ French inherited ~signe~ (“sign”), ~signal~, ~signer~ (“to sign”), ~enseigne~ (“ensign, shop-sign”), and ~insigne~ (“insignia”) — a whole vocabulary of marks and tokens.
en.désirer:
From Latin ~desiderare~ (“to long for, to feel the want of”), which most analyses break down as ~de-~ plus the noun ~sidus~ (genitive ~sideris~, “star, constellation, heavenly body”). The verb passed through Old French ~desirer~ into the modern form. It is the close sibling of ~considerare~ (“to examine attentively”), built on the same stellar noun, and survives in Italian ~desiderare~ and Spanish ~desear~.

The stellar imagery is the memorable part, though its exact logic is debated. One traditional reading places the word in the mouths of augurs or sailors: to scan the night sky for an awaited star and not find it — to note its absence — was to feel ~desiderare~, the ache of something missing, while ~considerare~ was to study the stars present overhead. From “to miss what is absent,” the sense broadened to the general longing of modern ~désirer~. The Latin participle ~desideratum~ survives untranslated in English and French as a thing wished for, and the same ~sidus~ underlies the French family ~sidéral~ (“of the stars”) and ~sidérer~ (“to stun, to dumbfound”), once “to be struck by a baleful star.”
en.détailler:
Derived from ~tailler~ (“to cut, carve”) with the prefix ~dé-~. ~tailler~ comes from Old French ~taillier~, from Late Latin ~taliāre~ (“to cut”), from Latin ~talea~ (“a cutting, twig, slip for planting”). Through ~taliāre~, ~tailler~ is cousin to Italian ~tagliare~ and Spanish ~tajar~, and the noun ~taille~ ultimately gave English ~tally~ and ~tailor~ (the cutter of cloth). The prefix ~dé-~ here carries the sense of separation, breaking apart.

To ~détailler~ was first to “cut into pieces” — to divide a whole into its small parts. From dividing physical things grew two important commercial and intellectual senses. Cutting goods into small lots to sell piecemeal gave ~détail~ the meaning “retail” (selling in small quantities, opposed to ~en gros~, “wholesale”); ~vendre au détail~ is to retail. And describing a thing part by part — examining its every small piece — gave the everyday sense “to detail, to recount minutely.” The noun ~détail~ thus carries both ideas at once: a “detail” is a small part cut from the whole, whether of merchandise or of a description. Relatives in modern French include ~tailler~, ~taille~, ~tailleur~, ~détaillant~ (“retailer”), and ~entailler~ (“to notch”).
en.détenir:
From Latin ~detinēre~ (“to hold back, keep occupied, retain”), a compound of ~de-~ (here intensive or “away”) and ~tenēre~ (“to hold”), reshaped in French on the model of the inherited verb ~tenir~. ~Tenēre~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~tenēō~ to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch, draw taut”). The same root surfaces in Greek ~teínō~ (“I stretch”) and Sanskrit ~tanóti~, and ~tenēre~ itself lives on in Italian ~tenere~ and Spanish ~tener~.

French attests ~détenir~ early — around 1160 in the sense “to seize,” then “to hold someone back, restrain” by 1176, and by 1306 the now-dominant “to hold prisoner.” The trajectory runs from simply holding to holding against someone's will: to detain is to keep a grip on a person or thing that would otherwise slip free, which is why the same verb covers both holding a record and holding an inmate. The link between holding and stretching is the taut rope or sinew one keeps tight; from *~ten-~ French inherits a dense web of words, among them ~tenir~, ~tendre~ (“to stretch”), ~tension~, ~tenace~, ~contenir~, ~maintenir~, and the noun ~détenu~ (“detainee”), the one held fast.
en.déterminer:
From Latin ~determinare~ (“to bound, fix limits to”), from ~de-~ (here intensive) and ~terminare~ (“to limit”), itself from ~terminus~ (“boundary, boundary-stone”). The noun ~terminus~ traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ter-~ (“peg, post, boundary-marker”), which also gave Greek ~terma~ (“end-point, goal”).
