Cognate with Italian ~determinare~ and Spanish ~determinar~, and — through ~terminus~ — with English ~determine~, ~term~, and ~terminal~.

The surprise is the boundary-stone at the root: to ~déterminer~ is etymologically to “mark the limits” of a thing, to drive its boundary-posts so that it is fixed and unambiguous. The Romans even worshipped a god ~Terminus~, guardian of boundary-stones, honored at the festival of the ~Terminalia~. From the spatial act of bordering grew the abstract senses of settling a question, fixing a value, and resolving someone to act. The same ~terminus~ underlies a generous French family — ~terme~, ~terminer~, ~terminus~, ~terminal~, ~exterminer~, and ~indéterminé~ — all of them, in the end, about reaching or fixing a limit.
en.détruire:
From Old French ~destruire~, from Vulgar Latin *~destrugere~, a remodeling of Classical Latin ~destruere~ (“to tear down, to demolish”). The verb joins the prefix ~de-~ (here “un-, reversal”) and ~struere~ (“to pile up, to build”), from Proto-Italic *~strowō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~strew-~ (“to strew, to spread out”). Through that root it is cognate with English ~strew~ and, via the Latin compounds, with ~structure~ and ~construct~.

The etymology is beautifully exact: if ~struere~ is to build by piling up, then ~de-struere~ is to take the pile apart — to un-build. ~Détruire~ is thus the precise opposite of ~construire~ (~con-~ + ~struere~, “to pile together”), its twin and antonym in a single root. The family is tightly knit in French: ~construire~, ~instruire~ (literally “to build into,” hence “to teach”), ~structure~, ~industrie~, and the noun ~destruction~ all rest on the same ~struere~. There is a quiet irony in the shared root: to instruct, to construct, and to destroy are, etymologically, three ways of handling the same heap — building it up into the mind, into a wall, or pulling it back down.
en.développer:
From Old French ~desveloper~ (attested around 1170 as ~desvoleper~, “to bring out of what envelops it”), formed as the antonym of ~enveloper~ (“to wrap up”) with the separative prefix ~des-~. The shared core ~-veloper~ has an obscure origin that etymologists have never settled: it is also the root of Italian ~viluppo~ (“tangle, bundle”) and its derivative ~sviluppo~ (“development”), but no convincing Latin or Germanic source for the bare stem has won acceptance, so honest dictionaries leave it unexplained. English ~develop~ was borrowed from the French verb.

The image behind ~développer~ is concrete and tactile: to ~unwrap~ a parcel, to peel away a covering and let what was hidden come into the open. From that literal unwrapping the verb extended to “to expand, unfold, set out at length,” then to the modern senses of growing, elaborating an argument, processing photographic film, and — most recently — writing software. The opposite gesture survives intact in ~enveloper~ and in the noun ~enveloppe~. From the same family come ~développement~, ~développeur~, and the adjective ~développable~. The contrast between wrapping and unwrapping still animates the pair: where ~enveloper~ conceals, ~développer~ reveals.
en.dévoiler:
A French formation of the seventeenth century, built from the prefix ~dé-~ (a privative, “un-, removal of”) plus the verb ~voiler~ (“to veil, to cover with a veil”). ~Voiler~ is itself denominal, from the noun ~voile~, which descends from Latin ~vela~, the plural of ~velum~ (“sail, cloth, covering, curtain”). Through ~velum~ it is kin to English ~veil~ and ~reveal~ and to Spanish ~velo~.

The morphology is wonderfully transparent: where ~voiler~ puts a veil on, ~dé-~ takes it off, so ~dévoiler~ is literally “to un-veil.” The verb runs in step with the concrete and the figurative at once — to ~dévoiler~ a statue at its inauguration, and to ~dévoiler~ a secret, a plot, one's true intentions. Latin ~velum~ is the common ancestor of a small French cluster: the noun ~voile~ (notice that it is masculine, “the veil,” but feminine, “the sail,” the two meanings split by gender), the verbs ~voiler~ and ~révéler~ (from Latin ~revelare~, “to draw back the veil”), and ~vélum~ itself, the awning of an arena. To reveal and to unveil are, at root, the very same gesture.
en.effectuer:
From Latin ~effectuāre~, a verb built on ~effectus~ (“an accomplishment, a result, an effect”), itself the past participle of ~efficere~ (“to bring about, accomplish”). ~Efficere~ is ~ex-~ (“out”) plus a combining form of ~facere~ (“to make, to do”), from Proto-Italic *~fakiō~, from PIE *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to put, to set, to place”). Through that same root ~effectuer~ is cognate with English ~do~ and ~deed~ and with German ~tun~.

The Latin ~efficere~ described carrying a thing all the way through to its finished result, and the derived noun ~effectus~ named that result — the modern ~effet~. From this came the learned verb ~effectuer~, which keeps the precise, almost technical flavor of its origin: one ~effectue~ a calculation, a payment, or an experiment, bringing a defined procedure to completion. The PIE root *~dʰeh₁-~ is extraordinarily prolific: ~faire~ itself descends from ~facere~, and the family includes ~effet~, ~affecter~, ~parfait~ (“thoroughly done”), and ~fait~. A surprising relative is ~thème~, which reaches French through Greek from the same root meaning “something set down.”
en.emmener:
Compound of the prefix ~en-~ (from Latin ~inde~/~in-~, conveying motion away) and ~mener~ (“to lead, conduct”). ~Mener~ descends from Latin ~minare~ (“to drive animals by shouting and threatening”), a Late Latin development of ~minari~ (“to threaten”), which is built on ~minae~ (“threats”). So leading was first a matter of driving livestock forward with menacing cries.

The semantic journey is vivid: from threatening (~minari~), to herding beasts along by threats (~minare~), to simply leading or conducting anyone or anything (~mener~). Adding ~en-~ gives ~emmener~, to lead away, to take someone off with you — distinguished in careful French from ~amener~ (“to bring toward”) and ~ramener~ (“to bring back”): one ~emmène~ a person away to the cinema but ~amène~ them here. The ~minare~/~minari~ family is surprisingly large in French: ~menacer~ (“to threaten”), ~menace~, ~promener~ (“to take for a walk”), ~amener~, ~ramener~, and ~meneur~ (“leader, ringleader”) — a cluster of words whose shared ancestor was the herdsman's threatening shout.
en.employer:
From Old French ~emploier~, from Latin ~implicare~ (“to fold in, to enfold, to involve”), a compound of ~in-~ (“in”) and ~plicare~ (“to fold”). ~plicare~ descends from PIE *~pleḱ-~ (“to plait, to fold”). The verb is a doublet of the learned ~impliquer~, borrowed straight from Latin much later, while ~employer~ took the slow popular road through Old French. Cognate with Spanish ~emplear~ and Italian ~impiegare~, and, through ~implicare~, with English ~employ~ and ~imply~.

The semantic journey is a quiet marvel: from “to fold something in” grew the idea of “folding a thing into a task,” hence putting it to use, hence — of a person — giving them work. The same Latin ~implicare~ thus gave French both ~employer~ (to use, to hire) and ~impliquer~ (to involve, to entangle), two faces of one act of folding-in. ~plicare~ seeds a broad family: ~ployer~ and ~plier~ (its native French descendants), ~appliquer~, ~déployer~, ~replier~, and the noun ~pli~ (“a fold, a crease”) all share that root, as do English ~ply~ and ~plait~. From ~employer~ itself come ~emploi~, ~employé~, and ~employeur~ — the worker, after all, being someone “folded into” the work.
en.emporter:
A French compound: the prefix ~en-~ (from Latin ~inde~, “from there, away”) joined to the verb ~porter~ (“to carry”), attested already around the year 980 as ~enportet~. The base ~porter~ descends from Latin ~portāre~ (“to carry, to bear”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to lead through, to pass over”), the same root behind Latin ~porta~ (“gate”) and ~portus~ (“harbor”). Italian ~portare~ and Spanish ~portar~ are its siblings, and through the root English gained ~port~ and the kindred ~ford~.

The prefix ~en-~ carries the idea of taking something away with oneself, so ~emporter~ is to carry off — food “to go” is ~à emporter~. From physical carrying the verb won vivid figurative force: a storm or a fever may ~emporter~ a life, and to win a contest is to ~l'emporter~ (literally “to carry it off”). The reflexive ~s'emporter~ means to be carried away by anger, to lose one's temper. From the same ~portāre~ French drew a broad family — ~porter~, ~apporter~, ~rapporter~, ~transporter~, ~importer~, ~exporter~, ~supporter~, and the noun ~portée~ — all pivoting on the act of carrying.
en.emprunter:
From Vulgar Latin *~impromutuāre~, reshaped from Classical Latin ~promutuārī~ (“to borrow in advance”), built on ~mutuus~ (“lent, borrowed, reciprocal”). ~mutuus~ derives from the Proto-Indo-European root *~mei-~ (“to change, to exchange”), the same root behind English ~mutual~, French ~muer~ and ~mutuel~, and Latin ~mūtāre~ (“to change”), source of ~muter~ and ~commun~.

The etymology fits the idea perfectly: borrowing is an exchange you promise to reverse, a thing given on the understanding it will change hands back again. From the financial sense of taking a loan, ~emprunter~ broadened to borrowing of every kind — borrowing a book, borrowing words from another language, borrowing a friend's umbrella. Most evocatively, French uses it for roads and paths: one ~emprunte~ a route, a corridor, a staircase — literally “borrowing” the way one travels, as if the road were lent for the duration of the journey. The related noun ~emprunt~ names both a loan and a linguistic loanword. The deep root *~mei-~ thus links the humble act of borrowing to the grand idea of change and exchange that also gives ~mutuel~, ~commun~, and ~migrer~.
en.empêcher:
From Old French ~empeechier~, from Late Latin ~impedicare~ (“to fetter, to entangle, to catch by the feet”), a compound of ~in-~ (“in, into”) and ~pedica~ (“a shackle, a foot-fetter”), itself from ~pes~ (genitive ~pedis~, “foot”). ~pes~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ped-~ (“foot”), making ~empêcher~ a distant cousin of English ~foot~, ~pedal~, and ~pedestrian~, and a closer relative of English ~impeach~, which entered through the same Old French verb.

The original picture is vividly physical: to ~empêcher~ someone was to shackle their feet so they could not move — to entangle and hold them fast. From that literal fettering the meaning lifted into the abstract “to prevent, to hinder,” which is all that survives in modern French. Its etymological twin tells a charming story: ~dépêcher~ began as the opposite gesture, “to un-fetter,” to free the feet and so to hurry — whence the reflexive ~se dépêcher~ (“to hurry up”) and the noun ~dépêche~ (a message “sent in haste”). English ~impeach~, by contrast, drifted from “to impede” toward “to accuse,” crossing paths along the way with unrelated Latin ~impetere~. The root *~ped-~ thus underlies an unexpected pair: to be ~empêché~ is to have one's feet tied, while to ~dépêcher~ is to set them free.
en.encourager:
Formed in Old French from the noun ~courage~ with the prefix ~en-~ (“to put into, to make”) and the verbal suffix ~-er~ — literally “to put courage into.” ~Courage~ derives from ~cœur~ (“heart”), from Latin ~cor~ (“heart”), via a Vulgar Latin ~coraticum~. ~Cor~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ḱerd-~ (“heart”), source of Ancient Greek ~kardía~, Sanskrit ~hṛ́daya~, German ~Herz~, and English ~heart~ itself. For the medieval French, the heart was the seat of courage, so to embolden someone was, quite literally, to put heart into them.

The metaphor is the same one English keeps in “to hearten” and “take heart,” and it shows how concrete the abstraction once was. To ~encourager~ runs from rousing a wavering soldier to gently spurring a child or backing a worthy cause. Its opposite, ~décourager~, takes the heart back out. From the same root come a cluster of heart-words in French: ~cœur~, ~courage~, ~cordial~, ~accord~, ~courroux~, and ~écœurer~ (“to sicken, to disgust” — literally to turn the stomach, or heart). The medical prefix ~cardio-~, borrowed from Greek, descends from the very same Indo-European root.
en.engager:
From Old French ~engagier~, derived from ~gagier~ (“to pledge”) with the prefix ~en-~ (“in, into”). The base noun is ~gage~ (“pledge, security”), which French took from Frankish *~wadi~, from Proto-Germanic *~wadją~ (“pledge”), in turn from the Proto-Indo-European root *~wedʰ-~ (“to pledge, bind”). The Frankish word also passed into Medieval Latin as ~vadium~. Through this Germanic source, ~gage~ is a doublet of the borrowed English ~wage~, and a cousin of English ~wed~ and German ~Wette~ (“bet, wager”).

To ~engager~ was first to put something “into pledge” — to pawn it, to bind it as security. From this concrete sense of staking a guarantee grew the whole modern fan of meanings: to bind oneself by a promise (to commit), to bind another to oneself by hiring, and to draw a person or thing into an action (to involve, to engage in battle). The reflexive ~s'engager~ kept the strongest flavor of the original pledge — “to commit oneself.” The same family yields ~gage~, ~gager~ (“to wager”), ~dégager~ (“to release, free up”), and the borrowed-back ~engagement~. That a word for emotional and professional commitment began as a banker's word for a pawned object is a fitting reminder that to promise is, etymologically, to put something on the line.
en.enlever:
Formed within French from the prefix ~en-~ and the verb ~lever~ (“to lift, raise”). ~Lever~ comes from Latin ~levāre~ (“to lift, lighten, relieve”), a verb built on the adjective ~levis~ (“light, not heavy”), from the PIE root *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ (“light, nimble”). Through that root ~enlever~ is distantly cognate with English ~light~ (in weight) and, via Latin borrowings, with ~levity~ and ~elevate~; Italian and Spanish keep the parent in ~levare~ and ~llevar~. The prefix ~en-~, from Latin ~inde~ or ~in~, adds a sense of motion away — so the compound means roughly “to lift up and off.”

The core idea is a lifting that becomes a taking-away. To lift a thing off a surface is to remove it, and French let the sense fan out: one can ~enlever~ a stain, a coat, a difficulty, or — more darkly — a person, giving the noun ~enlèvement~ its double life as both “removal” and “abduction.” The same lightening root colors a surprising spread of vocabulary: ~léger~, ~alléger~, ~soulever~, ~élever~, ~prélever~, and ~levée~ all carry the memory of ~levāre~. Even ~levain~ (“leaven”), the agent that lifts dough, belongs to the family — a reminder that to raise and to make light were once one and the same act.
en.enregistrer:
From the noun ~registre~ (“register, record book”) with the prefix ~en-~ (“to put into”) and the first-conjugation verb suffix ~-er~. The noun ~registre~ comes from Old French ~registre~, from Medieval Latin ~registrum~, an altered form of Late Latin ~regesta~ (“things recorded, a list”), the neuter plural of the past participle of Latin ~regerere~ (“to carry back, record”), from ~re-~ (“back”) and ~gerere~ (“to carry, bear”). Cognate with English ~register~ and ~registry~, and — through ~gerere~ — with English ~gesture~ and ~digest~.

So to ~enregistrer~ is literally to “put into the record book,” and the surprise lies in that book: the original meaning of ~regerere~ was “to carry back,” a clerk physically carrying entries back to be filed. The doubled ~-r-~ of ~registrum~ is unetymological, picked up in Medieval Latin by analogy with other nouns in ~-istrum~. The verb broadened from formal registration of acts and births to the modern senses of recording sound, capturing data, and saving a file on a computer. From the same ~gerere~ family French inherited ~geste~, ~gérer~, ~digérer~, and ~suggérer~, while ~registre~ itself spread into Dutch, German, and the Scandinavian languages.
en.enrichir:
French ~enrichir~ is a parasynthetic verb — formed in a single stroke from the adjective ~riche~ (“rich”) with the prefix ~en-~ (“to make, to put into”) and the verbal suffix ~-ir~, literally “to make rich.” The adjective ~riche~ is itself a Germanic loan: Old French took it from the Frankish *~rīki~ (“powerful, mighty”), from Proto-Germanic *~rīkijaz~. Through that Germanic family ~riche~ is cognate with German ~reich~, Dutch ~rijk~, and English ~rich~, and with Gothic ~reiks~ (“ruler, mighty one”). The Germanic word was itself borrowed early from Celtic, akin to Gaulish *~rīx~ and Old Irish ~rí~ (“king”), all from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₃reǵ-~ (“to rule, to direct in a straight line”) — the same root as Latin ~rēx~ (“king”).

The original force of the word was “powerful,” not “wealthy”: in early Old French a ~riche~ man was a mighty one, and only in the thirteenth century did the sense of material prosperity prevail. ~Enrichir~ thus once leaned toward “to make mighty” before settling on “to make wealthy” and, by extension, “to enhance” — one enriches a soil, a language, or a uranium sample. From the same stock French keeps ~riche~, ~richesse~ (“wealth”), and the borrowed ~Reich~, while the deeper root surfaces in ~roi~, ~régir~, and ~royal~.
en.enseigner:
From Old French ~enseignier~ (attested around 1050), from Vulgar Latin *~insignare~, an alteration of Latin ~insignire~ (“to mark, to distinguish, to make conspicuous”), compounded from the prefix ~in-~ and ~signum~ (“mark, sign”). ~Signum~ is, by one influential account, traced to the Proto-Indo-European root *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”) — the standard one follows — though some scholars favor a root meaning “to cut.” Cognate with Italian ~insegnare~ and Spanish ~enseñar~ (“to teach”).

The path from “marking” to “teaching” is the heart of the story: to *~insignare~ was to set a sign upon something, to point it out — and to point something out to a learner is to teach it. French preserves both ends. The original “marking” sense survives in ~enseigne~ (a shop's “sign,” and once a military “ensign” or standard-bearer), while the verb ~enseigner~ moved wholly into instruction. From the same ~signum~ spring ~signe~, ~signal~, ~signaler~, ~désigner~, and ~insigne~, and English ~ensign~, ~sign~, and ~insignia~. A teacher, etymologically, is one who sets up signposts — marking the road so the pupil may follow it.
en.entendre:
From Old French ~entendre~ (« to pay attention, to understand, to hear »), from Latin ~intendere~ (« to stretch toward, to direct one's attention »), formed from ~in-~ (« toward ») and ~tendere~ (« to stretch »). ~tendere~ rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch”), the same root behind English ~tend~, ~tension~, and ~thin~, and behind French ~tendre~ and ~tension~. Italian ~intendere~ and Spanish ~entender~ are its close Romance kin.

The original metaphor is bodily: to ~intendere~ was to stretch the mind toward something, as a bow is drawn toward its mark. From « directing attention » the verb split two ways. In medieval usage it came to mean « to understand » — a sense French still keeps in ~entendement~ and the phrase ~bien entendu~ — and then, by a further drift, « to hear », the everyday meaning today. Its noun ~entente~ (« understanding, agreement ») gave English the diplomatic ~entente~. From the same root *~ten-~ spring ~tendre~, ~tendance~, ~attendre~ (« to await »), ~prétendre~, and ~étendre~ (« to spread out »).
en.entourer:
A denominal verb formed from ~entour~ (“surroundings, what is round about”), which is ~en~ (“in”) + ~tour~ (“a turn, a going-around”). ~Tour~ comes from Latin ~tornus~ (“a lathe, a turner's tool for tracing circles”), borrowed from Ancient Greek ~τόρνος~, from Proto-Indo-European *~terh₁-~ (“to rub, bore, turn by twisting”). Cognate, through ~tornus~ and the verb ~tornāre~, with Italian ~tornare~, Spanish ~tornar~, and English ~turn~ and ~tour~.

The image is geometric: to ~entourer~ is to set things all around, as a circle is traced by a turning point. An older form ~entourner~ survived into the 16th century before being smoothed to ~entourer~. From it French draws ~entourage~ (the people round about a person) and ~entour~ in the literary ~à l'entour~ (“round about”). The deeper ~tour~/~tornus~ family is vast: ~tourner~, ~tour~ (both the masculine “turn/circuit” and the feminine “tower” by a different route), ~détour~, ~contour~, ~tournoi~, ~tornade~ (via Spanish), and ~tour~ in ~faire le tour~. All circle back to the carpenter's lathe — a tool that makes roundness by turning, the very gesture of surrounding.
en.entraîner:
From Old French ~traîner~ (~traïner~, “to drag, to trail”), prefixed with ~en-~ (from Latin ~in-~). The base verb descends from Vulgar Latin *~traginare~, a derivative of *~tragere~, itself reshaped from Classical Latin ~trahere~ (“to pull, to draw, to drag”). The deeper origin of ~trahere~ is uncertain — perhaps a Proto-Indo-European *~dʰregʰ-~ — but its past participle ~tractus~ seeded an enormous Latin and English family. Cognate descendants of the same base appear in Italian ~trainare~ and Spanish ~entrenar~ (“to train”).

The literal sense was physical: to drag something along behind oneself, as a current ~entraîne~ a swimmer or a horse drags a cart. From “to pull along” grew the figurative “to lead, to carry away, to bring about as a consequence,” and then the modern athletic sense “to train, to coach” — to repeatedly put someone through their paces, drawing them toward fitness. English borrowed this last sense as ~train~. The shared root ~trahere~ / ~tractus~ underlies a wealth of French words, among them ~traire~ (“to milk”), ~trait~, ~tracter~, ~traction~, ~abstrait~, ~extraire~, and ~portrait~ — all turning on the idea of drawing or pulling.
en.entreprendre:
Formed in Old French from the prefix ~entre-~ (“between, among”), from Latin ~inter~, and the verb ~prendre~ (“to take”). ~Prendre~ descends from Latin ~prehendere~ (“to grasp, seize”), itself ~prae-~ (“before”) plus a root ~-hendere~ from PIE *~gʰed-~ (“to seize, take”). Through that root it is cognate with English ~get~ and ~forget~ and with Greek ~khandánō~ (“to hold, contain”). The compound is often read as a calque of the Frankish *~underneman~ (literally “to take among / take under”), the same notion that English keeps in ~undertake~ — to take a task upon oneself.

The image is of reaching in among possibilities and seizing one — taking something on. French let ~entreprendre~ mean to begin a venture, a project, even an effort against someone (~entreprendre quelqu'un~). From it grew the powerhouse noun ~entreprise~ (“undertaking, business firm”) and the agent ~entrepreneur~, which English then borrowed back wholesale for the figure who launches ventures. From the ~prehendere~ root French also keeps ~prendre~, ~apprendre~ (“to take to oneself,” hence “to learn”), ~comprendre~ (“to take together,” hence “to understand”), ~surprendre~, and ~prison~ — a whole web of taking, learning, and grasping.
en.entrer:
From Old French ~entrer~, from Latin ~intrāre~ (“to enter, to go inside”), a denominal verb from ~intrā~ (“within, inside”). ~intrā~ goes back through *~interus~ (“inner,” the source also of ~interior~) to the Proto-Indo-European *~h₁énteros~ (“inner, what is inside”), itself from *~h₁én~ (“in”) — the same little particle behind English ~in~ and Latin ~in~. The Romance cousins all line up: Italian ~entrare~, Spanish ~entrar~, Portuguese ~entrar~.

The verb has stayed close to its plain spatial sense — “to go in” — for two thousand years, which is part of why it remains one of the most common verbs in French. Its figurative reach grew, though: one can ~entrer~ a profession, ~entrer~ data into a machine, or ~entrer~ into a discussion. The ~intrā-~ / ~inter-~ pair seeded a large family in French: the literal ~entrée~ (“entrance,” and the dish that “enters” a meal), ~rentrer~ (“to re-enter, to go back home”), ~entrer~'s causative ~introduire~ (“to lead in”), and the cluster ~intérieur~, ~interne~, ~intime~ — every one of them, at bottom, about being or going “inside.”
en.entretenir:
A compound coined in Old French (12th century) from ~entre~ (“between, mutually”) and ~tenir~ (“to hold”), literally “to hold between” or “to hold together.” ~Entre~ comes from Latin ~inter~ (“between”), and ~tenir~ from Latin ~tenere~ (“to hold, to keep”), which goes back through Proto-Italic to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch”). That root also yielded Ancient Greek ~teínō~ (“I stretch”), Sanskrit ~tanóti~, and English ~thin~ — holding conceived as keeping something taut.

The earliest sense was reciprocal: parties who ~entretenir~ one another held together, supported each other. From “holding together” the verb branched in two directions. One leads to material upkeep — to ~entretenir~ a house, a garden, or a car is to keep it in good condition by sustained care; ~entretien~ is its noun, “maintenance.” The other leads to the social and conversational: to ~entretenir~ someone is to keep them in talk, hence ~s'entretenir avec~ (“to converse with”) and ~entretien~ in the sense of “interview.” The same root *~ten-~ underlies a sprawling French family — ~tenir~, ~retenir~, ~contenir~, ~maintenir~, ~tendre~, ~tension~ — all turning on holding, stretching, and keeping.
en.envisager:
From ~en-~ + ~visage~ (“face”) + ~-er~, attested from the sixteenth century. The noun ~visage~ comes from Vulgar Latin *~visaticum~, a derivative of Latin ~visus~ (“sight, appearance”), the past participle of ~vidēre~ (“to see”), from Proto-Indo-European *~weyd-~ (“to see, to know”). The verb thus literally meant “to bring before one's face,” “to look someone in the face.” Through the same root *~weyd-~ it is cognate with English ~vision~ and ~view~, and, more distantly, with English ~wit~ and ~wise~ and Greek ~idéā~.

The development is a tidy metaphor. To ~envisager~ someone was first to gaze at their ~visage~; from “having a face before one's eyes” the verb drifted to the mental sense of “having something in view,” then to “to contemplate, to consider” a possibility — to picture it inwardly as one would a face. English borrowed the word as ~envisage~ with this same abstract meaning. The root *~weyd-~ seeded a large French family, including ~vision~, ~visible~, ~viser~, ~aviser~, ~prévoir~, and ~évident~, all of which turn, one way or another, on the act of seeing.
en.envoyer:
From Latin ~inviāre~ (“to send on a journey, to travel over”), ~in-~ (“in, onto”) + ~via~ (“road, way”). ~Via~ comes from Proto-Italic *~wijā~; the PIE source is debated, but it is plausibly a doublet of English ~way~, both descending from a root for travelling or pursuing. To send someone was, literally, to “put them on the road.” Italian ~inviare~ and Spanish ~enviar~ are direct cognates, and ~via~ itself survives unchanged as the English and international preposition ~via~ (“by way of”).

The Latin sense “to set on the way” narrowed in French to “to dispatch, to send” — a letter, a messenger, a signal. A curious wrinkle lies in the conjugation: the future and conditional stems (~enverrai~, ~enverrais~) do not follow the verb's own pattern but were reshaped in the 18th century by analogy with ~voir~ (“to see”), an unrelated verb that merely sounded similar — so ~envoyer~ borrows the future of a stranger. The everyday ~envoyer chercher~ means “to send for,” and the noun ~envoi~ is the act or the parcel sent; the past participle gives the ~envoyé~ (“envoy, dispatched agent”), borrowed into English as ~envoy~. The ~via~ root also yields French ~voie~ (“way, lane”), ~voyage~, ~dévoyer~ (“to lead astray”), ~convoyer~ (“to convey, to escort”), and ~renvoyer~ (“to send back”).
en.espérer:
From Latin ~sperare~ (“to hope, to look forward to”), formed from the noun ~spes~ (“hope”). Through Proto-Italic *~spēs~, the word traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~speh₁-~ (“to prosper, to turn out well”), the same root that lies behind Latin ~prosperus~ (“favorable, thriving”), source of French ~prospérer~. Its Romance siblings are everywhere: Spanish ~esperar~, Italian ~sperare~, Portuguese ~esperar~ — though in Spanish the verb has split its labor, also meaning “to wait,” since waiting and hoping are close kin.

The core sense — to look forward to a good outcome — has held remarkably steady from Latin into French. Old French had the form ~esperer~ with an initial vowel that French later dropped in the noun, giving ~espoir~ (the everyday word) beside ~espérance~ (a loftier, more sustained hope, drawn from the present participle). The negative prefix ~dé-~ yields the dark twins ~désespérer~ and ~désespoir~, the loss of all hope. Theological French ranks ~l'espérance~ among the three theological virtues, alongside faith and charity. From the same Indo-European root of thriving come ~prospère~, ~prospérité~, and ~prospérer~ — so that to ~espérer~ and to prosper are, far back, the same impulse toward things turning out well.
en.essayer:
A denominal verb from the noun ~essai~ (“a trial, an attempt, an assay”), with Old French forms ~essaiier~ and ~assaier~. The noun ~essai~ comes from Late Latin ~exagium~ (“a weighing”), built on the verb ~exigō~ / ~exigere~ (“to weigh, to measure, to drive out”), which is ~ex-~ (“out”) + ~agere~ (“to drive, to do”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive”). That root also gave Greek ~ágō~ (“to lead”) and Sanskrit ~ájati~. From the same ~exagium~ come Italian ~saggio~, Spanish ~ensayo~, and, through Anglo-Norman ~assai~, the English ~assay~ and ~essay~.

The key image is the scale. To weigh a coin or an ore was to ~exigō~ it — to “drive it out” to its exact measure — so ~exagium~ named the test of a metal's purity, and ~essayer~ first meant “to assay, to test.” From testing a thing it broadened to “trying” anything: trying on clothes (~essayer une robe~), trying out a car, or simply attempting a deed. The same family yields French ~essai~ (a trial, and the literary “essay”), ~essayage~ (“fitting,” of garments), ~essayiste~ (“essayist”), and ~essayeur~ (“assayer”) — and, through ~agere~, the immense crop of ~agir~, ~acte~, and ~agent~.
en.ester:
From Old French ~ester~ (“to stand, to be standing, to remain”), from Latin ~stāre~ (“to stand”), from Proto-Italic *~stāō~, from PIE *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”). Cognate with Italian ~stare~ and Spanish ~estar~, which survive as everyday verbs, and — through the same root — with English ~stand~ and ~stay~.

In Old French, ~ester~ was fully conjugated and meant “to stand” or “to remain.” Its territory was gradually carved up by other verbs: ~rester~ (~re-~ + ~ester~, “to remain behind”) took over the sense “to stay,” while ~être~ absorbed others. Most strikingly, the past participle of ~être~ — ~été~ — descends not from Latin ~esse~ (“to be”) but from ~status~, the past participle of ~stāre~ (“to stand”), the same root as ~ester~: ~être~ is a suppletive verb, and its “having been” is, etymologically, a “having stood.” Stripped of these everyday roles, ~ester~ survives in modern French only as a defective legal term, in the fixed expression ~ester en justice~ (“to appear before a court, to bring suit”), conjugated in almost no tenses — a fossil of a once-central verb. The same PIE root *~steh₂-~ underlies a vast family of French words, among them ~stable~, ~station~, ~statue~, ~constant~, and ~rester~ itself.
en.estimer:
French ~estimer~ comes from Latin ~aestimāre~ (older ~aestumāre~), “to value, to appraise, to reckon the worth of.” Its origin is debated. The traditional analysis splits it into ~aes~ (“bronze, copper, money”) plus an element meaning “to cut,” glossing it as “one who cuts the bronze” — that is, a minter weighing out coin; the linguist de Vaan instead links it to Proto-Indo-European *~h₂eys-~ (“to seek”). Through Old French the word fanned out into doublets: learned ~estimer~ kept the Latin sense, while a popular line gave Old French ~aesmer~, source of English ~aim~ (originally “to estimate, to calculate a target”); English also borrowed ~esteem~ and ~estimate~, and Italian ~stimare~ and Spanish ~estimar~ are cognate.

The semantic arc runs from the marketplace to the heart. At first ~estimer~ meant to set a monetary value — to appraise — and that commercial sense lives on in ~estimer~ un bien (“to value a property”) and in ~estimation~. From “to reckon worth” grew the gentler “to judge, to reckon” (~j'estime que~, “I reckon that”) and finally the warm “to hold in high regard, to esteem” a person. The same root yields ~estime~, ~inestimable~, ~surestimer~, and ~sous-estimer~. If the old etymology is right, a feeling as lofty as esteem began at the mint, with someone literally cutting and weighing metal to fix a price.
en.examiner:
From Latin ~exāmināre~ (“to weigh carefully”), from the noun ~exāmen~. ~Exāmen~ is a contraction of *~exagmen~, from ~ex-~ (“out”) plus the root of ~agere~ (“to drive, set in motion”) and the instrumental suffix ~-men~ — literally “that which drives out” or “the means of driving.” ~Agere~ traces to Proto-Indo-European *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive, lead”), the root behind English ~act~ and ~agent~.

The surprise lies in what ~exāmen~ first named: the tongue or needle of a balance, the slender pointer driven by the weights that shows when the scale is true. To examine, then, was to read the balance-needle — to weigh a thing precisely and judge its worth. From the scales the sense generalized to any careful scrutiny, the testing of a candidate, the inspection of evidence. Curiously, ~exāmen~ also meant a “swarm of bees,” another mass set in motion by the same root. The PIE *~h₂eǵ-~ gives French an enormous family through ~agere~ and its compounds: ~agir~, ~agent~, ~action~, ~exiger~, ~exact~ (a doublet relative of ~examen~), ~rédiger~, and ~naviguer~.
en.exclure:
From Latin ~exclūdere~ (“to shut out, to exclude”), which passed through Old French ~excluder~ and Middle French ~exclure~ to reach its modern form. The Latin verb is a compound of the prefix ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~claudere~ (“to close, to shut”). ~claudere~ descends from Proto-Italic *~klaudō~ and Proto-Indo-European *~kleh₂u-~ (“hook, peg, bolt” — the things that lock a door). Cognate, through ~claudere~, with Italian ~escludere~ and Spanish ~excluir~; through the root, with Ancient Greek ~kleís~ (“key, bolt”).

The image is concrete: to ~exclure~ is to shut someone or something out the door, ~ex~ + ~claudere~, and the figurative “to exclude, to leave out, to rule out” grew naturally from that. The Latin ~claudere~ is one of the great hinges of French vocabulary, surviving in a swarm of related verbs: ~clore~ and ~clôturer~ (“to close, to enclose”), ~conclure~ (“to conclude,” to close together), ~inclure~ (“to include,” to shut in), ~reclure~ (whence ~reclus~, “recluse”), and the noun family ~clôture~, ~clé~/~clef~ (“key”), and ~clavier~ (“keyboard”). So an exclusion is, etymologically, a locking-out, and a conclusion a locking-together.
en.exercer:
From Old French ~essercer~ (attested 1121–34), from Latin ~exercēre~ (“to keep busy, train, practice, work”), a compound of the prefix ~ex-~ (“out, away”) and ~arcēre~ (“to enclose, hold in, keep off”). Latin ~arcēre~ goes back, through Proto-Italic ~arkeō~, to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂erk-~ (“to hold, contain, protect”), the same root behind ~arx~ (“citadel”) and ~arca~ (“chest, box”). Italian ~esercitare~ and Spanish ~ejercer~ are its Romance cousins, and English ~exercise~ enters through the same Latin verb.

The inner logic is vivid: if ~arcēre~ means “to hold in,” then ~exercēre~ is literally “to hold out of an enclosure” — to let loose, to put to work — which is exactly what one does when driving penned animals to labor or marching soldiers from camp onto the drill field. From that image of setting energy in motion grew the senses of training, practicing a skill, and wielding a power, all of which ~exercer~ keeps: one exercises a profession, exercises authority, or exercises the body. The same root family yields French ~exercice~, ~exorciser~ (a different ~ex-~ compound, but the Latinate feel is kin), and the more distant ~arche~ and ~coffre~ of the storage sense. The surprising kinship is that a verb of vigorous activity hides, at its heart, a word for shutting things in.
en.exiger:
From Latin ~exigere~ (“to drive out, to demand, to exact, to weigh precisely”), compounded from ~ex-~ (“out, thoroughly”) and ~agere~ (“to drive, to lead, to do”). ~Agere~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive, to set in motion”). Cognate, through ~agere~, with Italian ~esigere~ and Spanish ~exigir~, and — through the root *~h₂eǵ-~ — with Greek ~ágō~ (“I lead”), Sanskrit ~ájati~ (“drives”), and English ~act~ and ~agent~.

The Latin verb carried a striking double sense: literally “to drive out,” but also “to weigh exactly,” from the image of driving a scale's beam level — whence the adjective ~exact~ (“driven to precision”). French ~exiger~ kept the forceful side, “to demand as one's due,” the creditor driving out what is owed. The root *~h₂eǵ-~, through ~agere~ and its participle ~actus~, is among Latin's most productive: French owes it ~agir~, ~agent~, ~acte~, ~action~, ~exact~, ~rédiger~, and ~naviguer~, while English gathers ~act~, ~agent~, ~agile~, ~exact~, ~exigent~, and ~ambiguous~ from the same driving root. To exiger a thing is to drive it out of someone, forcefully and to the last measure.
en.exister:
From Latin ~existere~, also written ~exsistere~ (“to step forth, to come into being, to appear”), a compound of the prefix ~ex-~ (“out, forth”) and ~sistere~ (“to cause to stand, to set down”). ~sistere~ is a reduplicated form of ~stare~ (“to stand”), from Proto-Indo-European *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”), one of the great roots of the family. Through it ~exister~ is cognate with Ancient Greek ~hístēmi~ (“to make stand”), with English ~stand~ and ~stay~, and with the whole Romance family of standing verbs.

The literal Latin sense was vivid: to ~exsistere~ was to stand out, to step into view, to emerge — existence imagined as a coming-forth from hidden ground into the visible. French borrowed the verb in learned form, keeping the philosophical sense “to be, to have being” while letting the original spatial image fade. The same root *~steh₂-~ gives French an enormous progeny: ~être~ (whose past participle ~été~ comes from ~status~, not from ~esse~), ~rester~, ~stable~, ~station~, ~statue~, ~constant~, ~instant~, ~assister~, ~résister~, and ~consister~ — a vast family in which everything ultimately stands.
en.expliquer:
From Latin ~explicare~ (« to unfold, to unroll »), formed from ~ex-~ (« out ») and ~plicare~ (« to fold »). ~plicare~ rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~pleḱ-~ (“to fold, to plait”). Through this root ~expliquer~ is kin to French ~plier~ (“to fold”) and ~ployer~, and to a crowd of English borrowings — ~ply~, ~apply~, ~deploy~, ~employ~, ~explicit~ — all carrying the buried metaphor of folding and unfolding.

The Latin picture is concrete: a papyrus scroll rolled up, then ~explicare~ — unrolled — so its text lies open to be read. From « unrolling a scroll » the verb moved to « laying a matter open », and so to the modern sense « to make clear, to account for ». A fellow word travelled the opposite way: ~compliquer~ (« to fold together ») describes what is tangled, while ~expliquer~ undoes the tangle. The same root *~pleḱ-~ branches widely in French: ~pli~ (« a fold »), ~plier~, ~appliquer~, ~impliquer~, ~déployer~, ~exploiter~, and ~simple~ itself (« once-folded »).
en.exploiter:
From Vulgar Latin *~explicitāre~, a frequentative of Latin ~explicare~ (“to unfold, unravel, explain”), itself ~ex-~ (“out”) plus ~plicare~ (“to fold”), from Proto-Indo-European *~plek-~ (“to plait, fold”). It surfaces in Old French as ~espleitier~ / ~esploitier~ (“to accomplish, carry out”) and shares its root with English ~exploit~ and, through the same Latin ~plicare~, with Spanish ~explotar~.

The path runs from the literal to the figurative: from “unfolding” a thing, to “carrying it out,” to “making it yield a profit.” Early Old French ~espleitier~ meant “to accomplish,” and the related noun gave English its ~exploit~, a notable feat or achievement. By 1560 the modern ~exploiter~ had settled into “to make productive, to turn to account” — a farm, a mine, a resource — and only later acquired the darker, pejorative sense of using a person unfairly. The same ancestral *~plek-~ branches widely in French through ~plicare~ and its kin: ~plier~ (“to fold”), ~ployer~, ~appliquer~, ~compliquer~, and ~expliquer~, that last one the learned doublet of ~exploiter~ — both descend from ~explicare~, one borrowed straight from Latin, the other worn down through popular speech.
en.explorer:
French ~explorer~ comes straight from Latin ~explorare~ (“to observe, examine, scout out, explore”). The verb is transparently ~ex-~ (“out”) plus ~plorare~ (“to cry out, weep, wail”). The deepest origin of ~plorare~ is uncertain — etymologists do not assign it a secure Proto-Indo-European root — so we stop, honestly, at the Latin. Through ~explorare~ the word spread across Europe: Italian ~esplorare~, Spanish ~explorar~, and, via French, English ~explore~ and ~exploration~.

The surprising part is buried in ~plorare~, “to cry out.” The ancient — and quite plausible — explanation makes ~explorare~ a hunters' term: to ~explorer~ a region was first to scout it by sending up a shout, the cry that flushed or signalled game. From that very physical sense of beating the bush, the verb climbed to the cool, deliberate “investigate, examine” of the scholar, the surgeon (who ~explore~ a wound), and the geographer. The same tearful root ~plorare~ is hiding in plain sight in two other French verbs: ~déplorer~ (“to deplore, lament,” to weep ~de-~ thoroughly) and ~implorer~ (“to implore,” to weep ~in-~ toward someone in entreaty) — so that ~explorer~, ~déplorer~, and ~implorer~ form a small trio all founded on a cry.
en.exposer:
A French compound of the prefix ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~poser~ (“to place, to put”), formed to render the Latin verb ~exponere~ (“to set out, to expose”), which had evolved regularly into Old French ~espondre~. The ~poser~ element comes from Late Latin ~pausare~ (“to pause, to set down”), whose Romance descendants absorbed the senses of Latin ~ponere~ (“to place”), past participle ~positus~. Through ~positus~ the family is cognate with English ~pose~, ~position~, and ~compose~, and Latin ~ponere~ also gave Spanish ~poner~ and Italian ~porre~.

The literal meaning is to “set out,” to place something where it can be seen — and from this grew several modern senses at once: to ~exposer~ a painting is to display it (whence an ~exposition~), to ~exposer~ an idea is to set it forth, and to ~exposer~ someone to danger is to put them out where the danger can reach them. The construction with ~poser~ rather than the inherited ~espondre~ shows French rebuilding a Latin verb from transparent native parts. The same ~poser~ generated a large family: ~déposer~, ~composer~, ~disposer~, ~proposer~, ~imposer~, and the noun ~position~.
en.exprimer:
French ~exprimer~ comes from Latin ~exprimere~ (“to press out, to squeeze out, to portray vividly”), a compound of ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~premere~ (“to press”). ~Premere~ traces to Proto-Indo-European *~per-~ (“to strike, to press”), the same root that, through Latin, gives English ~press~ and ~print~. Italian keeps ~esprimere~ and Spanish ~exprimir~ (“to squeeze”), preserving the very physical core sense.

The metaphor at the heart of the word is wonderfully concrete: to ~exprimer~ is to press something out, the way one squeezes juice from a fruit — and indeed ~exprimer le jus d'un citron~ is still good French. From this literal squeezing the Romans drew the figurative “to press a meaning out into words, to portray,” and that abstract sense, “to express, to convey,” is what dominates modern French. The buried ~premere~ runs through a large family: ~comprimer~, ~imprimer~ (“to print,” literally to press onto), ~opprimer~, ~supprimer~, ~déprimer~, and the nouns ~pression~ and ~expression~. So when one ~exprime~ a feeling, one is, etymologically, wringing it out under pressure until it spills into speech.
en.extraire:
From Latin ~extrahere~ (“to draw out, to drag forth”), compounded of ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~trahere~ (“to pull, to draw”). The form ~extraire~ shows the regular Old French reshaping of ~-trahere~ on the model of ~traire~ (“to draw, to milk”). The verb ~trahere~ is traced to the Proto-Indo-European root *~tragʰ-~ (“to draw, to drag”); it left Italian ~trarre~ and the learned ~estrarre~, Spanish ~traer~, and is held — though the correspondence is debated — to be akin to English ~draw~ and ~drag~.

The sense is wonderfully concrete: ~extrahere~ meant to haul something physically out — a tooth, a thorn, an army from a trap. French kept that literal pull (to ~extraire~ a tooth, ore from a mine, juice from a fruit) and added abstract ones, from extracting a passage out of a book to taking a square root in mathematics. The participle stem ~tract-~ of ~trahere~ seeded a huge family in French: ~trait~, ~traiter~, ~traîner~, ~attirer~, ~abstraire~, ~soustraire~, ~distraire~, and the bookish ~extraction~ and ~extrait~ — all turning on the idea of drawing or pulling.
en.exécuter:
From Latin ~exsecūtus~, the past participle of ~exsequī~ (“to follow out, to pursue to the end, to carry through”), compounded from ~ex-~ (“out, thoroughly”) and ~sequī~ (“to follow”). Latin ~sequī~ rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”), one of the most fertile roots in the European languages. Italian ~eseguire~ and Spanish ~ejecutar~ are cognates; through the same ~sequī~, French is kin with English ~execute~, ~sequence~, ~pursue~, and ~suite~.

The core image is following a thing all the way through to its end — and from “to follow through” came “to carry out, to accomplish.” The grim sense of “to put to death” is a specialization: to ~exécuter~ a sentence was to carry it out, and the phrase contracted onto the prisoner. The reflexive ~s'exécuter~ keeps the gentler meaning, “to comply, to do what is required.” The root ~sequī~ scatters across French: ~suivre~ (“to follow”), ~suite~, ~conséquence~, ~poursuivre~, ~persécuter~, ~obséquieux~, and ~second~ (“following, the one that follows first”). Modern relatives of ~exécuter~ itself include ~exécution~, ~exécutif~, ~exécutant~, ~exécutable~, and ~inexécutable~.
en.fabriquer:
Attested from the twelfth century, ~fabriquer~ is a learned reshaping of Latin ~fabricāre~ (“to build, to construct, to fashion”), itself derived from ~fabrica~ (“workshop, craft, skilled production”) and ultimately from ~faber~ (“artisan, smith, worker in hard materials”). ~Faber~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~fafro-~ and probably to a Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰabʰ-~ (“to fit together, to be skilful”). Cognate with English ~forge~ and ~fabric~, and — through that same root — with Armenian ~darbin~ (“smith”).

The popular, inherited descendant of ~fabricāre~ in French is in fact ~forger~ (by way of ~fabrica~ → ~faverge~ → ~forge~): ~fabriquer~ and ~forger~ are doublets, one borrowed from the books, the other worn smooth by centuries of speech. The learned form kept the broad Latin sense of skilled making, which is why ~fabriquer~ today covers honest manufacture as readily as the dishonest kind — one can ~fabriquer~ a chair or ~fabriquer~ a false alibi. The shared workshop root survives across French in ~fabrique~, ~fabrication~, ~fabricant~, ~préfabriqué~, and ~forgeron~, every one of them, at bottom, a thing made by a skilled hand.
en.faciliter:
From Italian ~facilitare~ (“to make easy”), borrowed in the sixteenth century, from Italian ~facile~ (“easy”), from Latin ~facilis~ (“easy, feasible”). The adjective ~facilis~ was built from the verb ~facere~ (“to do, to make”) plus the suffix ~-ilis~ — literally “do-able” — and traces, through Proto-Italic *~fakiō~, to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to put, to place, to do”). The same root gives Greek ~tithēmi~ (“I place”) and English ~do~ and ~deed~.

The core idea has always been ease: to ~faciliter~ something is to render it ~facile~, to smooth its path. French already had the learned adjective ~facile~ when it reached across the Alps for the Italian verb, Italy being the prestige source of so much Renaissance vocabulary. The fertile root *~dʰeh₁-~ — “to do, to make” — seeded a vast French family far beyond ease: ~faire~ itself, ~facteur~, ~facture~, ~faculté~, ~difficile~ (literally “not easy”), and ~fait~, the bare past participle “done” that became the noun “fact.” Even ~affaire~ (à + ~faire~, “to do”) and the suffix ~-fier~ in countless verbs descend from this same Latin ~facere~. To facilitate, then, is etymologically to make the making easy.
en.faillir:
From Old French ~faillir~ (“to be lacking, to fail, to err”), from Vulgar Latin *~fallīre~, a remodelling of Classical Latin ~fallere~ (“to deceive, to trick, to escape notice”). The Latin verb is often traced to a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to stumble, to deceive,” and it is plausibly cognate with Ancient Greek ~σφάλλω~ (~sphállō~, “to make stumble, to trip up”), though the precise reconstruction is disputed. Through Latin ~fallere~, ~faillir~ is kin to Italian ~fallire~ (“to fail, to go bankrupt”) and Spanish ~fallar~, and, by way of Old French, to English ~fail~ and ~fault~.

The sense shifted from deceiving someone to letting them down, then to falling short oneself. French kept a vivid trace of the older meaning: ~il s'en faut~ (“it is lacking”) and the impersonal ~falloir~ (“to be necessary”) spring from the very same Latin ~fallere~, the idea of something missing hardening into the idea of something required. Modern ~faillir~ is now used above all with an infinitive to mean “to nearly do” — ~j'ai failli tomber~, “I almost fell” — a ghost of failure that never quite happened. The same family yields ~faute~, ~faillite~ (“bankruptcy”), ~défaillir~, ~infaillible~, and the borrowed ~fallacieux~.
en.faire:
From Old French ~faire~, from Latin ~facere~ (“to make, to do”), from Proto-Italic *~fakiō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to put, place, set”). Romance cousins include Italian ~fare~ and Spanish ~hacer~. Through the same root come Greek ~títhēmi~ (“I place,” source of ~thesis~ and ~theme~), German ~tun~, and English ~do~ and ~deed~ — so the French “to make” and the English “to do” are, deep down, the same word.

The shift from “to put, set” to “to make, do” is natural enough: to make a thing is to set it in place. French ~faire~ swallowed an enormous range of senses, from manufacturing to weather (~il fait beau~) to causation (~faire faire~, “to have done”). A phonetic curiosity hides in its conjugation: the past historic ~fis~, ~fit~ comes not from the infinitive stem but from the Latin perfect ~fēcī~, whose long vowel left the telltale ~-i-~. From ~facere~ and its participle ~factum~ flow a vast progeny: ~fait~, ~façon~, ~facile~, ~facteur~, ~facture~, ~affaire~, ~défaire~, ~satisfaire~, and the learned ~-fier~ verbs (~modifier~, ~simplifier~) — every one a way of making.
en.falloir:
From Old French ~faloir~, an offshoot of ~faillir~ (“to fail, to be lacking”), from Vulgar Latin *~fallīre~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~fallere~ (“to trip up, to deceive, to be wanting”). ~Fallere~ goes back to a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to stumble,” the same source as Greek ~sphallein~ (“to throw down, to make fall”) and Sanskrit ~skhalate~ (“he stumbles”). The impersonal ~falloir~ split off from ~faillir~ under the pull of its third-person form ~faut~ (Latin ~fallit~), remodeled on the pattern of ~valoir~.

The semantic journey is remarkable: from “to be lacking” the impersonal “it lacks” drifted into “it is needed,” and so a verb of deficiency became French's chief verb of necessity, ~il faut~ (“one must”). The notion of falling short survives across its relatives: ~défaillir~ (“to faint, to give way”), ~faillite~ (“bankruptcy,” a merchant who has failed), and ~faute~ and ~défaut~ (“fault, flaw, lack”). English borrowed the same family in ~fail~, ~fault~, ~false~, and ~fallacy~. So the polite ~il faut~ that opens a thousand French sentences is, etymologically, a quiet confession that something is missing.
en.favoriser:
From the noun ~faveur~ (“favor, goodwill”), itself from Latin ~favor~ (“favor, support, applause”), a noun built on the verb ~favēre~ (“to be favorable, to be well-disposed toward”). The verb was coined in French by attaching the productive verbal suffix ~-iser~ to the stem of ~faveur~, on the model of Latin ~favor~. Related Romance forms include Italian ~favorire~ and Spanish ~favorecer~, both expressing the same idea of granting one's favor.

Latin ~favēre~ originally belonged to the language of religion, where it meant to look kindly upon a rite, and the formula ~favēre linguīs~ (“to favor with the tongues”) actually commanded onlookers to keep a reverent silence so as not to spoil a ceremony with ill-omened words. From this notion of benevolent regard the family broadened to mean showing preference, supporting, or advantaging — the senses ~favoriser~ carries today, including a faintly negative one of unfair preferment. The same root nourishes a tight cluster of French words: ~faveur~, ~favorable~, ~favori~ (borrowed back from Italian ~favorito~), ~favoritisme~, and the everyday ~favori~ of a web browser. Even English ~favor~ and ~favorite~ are, through Old French, members of this household descended from a Roman idea of divine and social goodwill.
en.fermer:
From Old French ~fermer~ (“to close, to fasten, to make firm, to build”), from Latin ~firmare~ (“to strengthen, to make firm, to secure”), a verb built on the adjective ~firmus~ (“firm, steadfast, solid”). ~firmus~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰer-~ (“to hold, to support”), the same root that gives Sanskrit ~dharma~ (“that which upholds, law, order”). Through Latin ~firmus~, the French verb is cognate with Italian ~fermare~ and Spanish ~firmar~ (“to sign”), and with English ~firm~ and ~confirm~.

The semantic leap is the surprise here: ~firmare~ meant “to make firm, to secure,” and a door was secured by being fastened shut — so “to make fast” slid into “to close.” In Late Latin the closing sense took hold, and Old French ~fermer~ went on to displace the older ~clore~ (from Latin ~claudere~), which became homonymically tangled with ~clouer~ (“to nail”) and retreated into technical use. A folk etymology once linked ~fermer~ to ~fer~ (“iron”), imagining iron bars, but scholars reject it. The same root *~dʰer-~ underlies ~ferme~ (“firm,” and the noun “farm”), ~fermeté~, ~affermir~, ~confirmer~, and ~fermeture~ — and even the agricultural ~ferme~ traces back through the idea of a fixed, secured holding of land.
en.figurer:
From Latin ~figurare~ (“to form, to shape, to fashion”), derived from ~figura~ (“shape, form, figure”), itself built on the verb ~fingere~ (“to mould, to shape, especially in clay”). ~fingere~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeyǵʰ-~ (“to knead, to form, to build”), the same root that gives Latin ~fictus~ (“shaped, feigned”) and, in the Germanic branch, English ~dough~ and the ~-dy~ of Old English placenames. Cognate with Italian ~figurare~ and Spanish ~figurar~.

The root meaning is hands working clay, and ~figura~ kept that sculptural sense of an outline or moulded shape. From “to give shape to,” French ~figurer~ branched in several directions: to represent or depict (a drawing ~figure~ a scene), to be present in a series — to “appear in a list,” to be included among others — and, reflexively, ~se figurer~, to picture something to oneself, to imagine. The same family is rich in French: ~figure~ (face, figure), ~figuratif~, ~figurine~, ~défigurer~ (to disfigure), ~préfigurer~, and ~configurer~. Through its sibling ~fingere~ come ~feindre~ (“to feign”) and ~fiction~ — a reminder that the line between shaping something and making it up has always been thin.
en.financer:
Denominal verb from the noun ~finance~, which arose in Old French (around 1280) from the verb ~finer~ (“to bring to an end; to settle, pay”), an altered form of ~finir~ (“to finish”), with the suffix ~-ance~. All of this rests on Latin ~fīnis~ (“boundary, limit, end”). The early ~finance~ meant the ending or settling of a matter — and since settling an affair so often meant paying it off, Old French ~fin~ itself could mean “money.” Cognate with Italian ~finanza~ and Spanish ~finanza~, and — borrowed — with English ~finance~ and ~final~, all anchored in ~fīnis~.

The slide from “ending” to “money” is the heart of the story: to ~finer~ a debt was to make an end of it by payment, so the noun drifted from “settlement” to “payment,” then by the fifteenth century to “monetary resources,” as in ~trésorier des finances~ (“treasurer of finances”). Medieval Latin ~financia~ even named a tax or levy. The modern verb ~financer~ — to fund, to provide money for — is the natural offspring. From the same ~fīnis~ French keeps a broad family: ~finir~, ~fin~, ~final~, ~définir~, ~confiner~, and ~affiner~ — boundaries and endings everywhere, with money the great settler of ends.
en.finir:
From Old French ~finir~ (with the variants ~fenir~ and ~finer~), from Latin ~fīnīre~ (“to limit, to bound, then to finish”), itself derived from the noun ~fīnis~ (“boundary, limit, border, end”). The deeper origin of ~fīnis~ is disputed: it is often referred — perhaps from an earlier *~fignis~ — to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeygʷ-~ (“to stick, set up”), the root behind Latin ~fīgō~ (“to fix”), a boundary being something “fixed” in the ground; an alternative links it to ~findō~ (“to split”). Cognate verbs are Italian ~finire~ and Spanish ~finir~.

The path of meaning runs from space to time: a ~fīnis~ was first a physical border between fields or peoples — hence the plural ~fīnēs~ for a country's “territory” — and from “to set a limit” the verb slid to “to bring to an end.” The same root spread widely in French: the noun ~fin~ (“end”), ~final~, ~finale~, ~finalité~, ~finir~'s cousin ~définir~ (“to define,” to set the ~fīnēs~ of a meaning) with ~définition~, ~infini~ (“endless”), ~finitude~, and the commercial ~finance~ — originally the “settling, ending” of a debt by payment.
en.fixer:
From Latin ~fixus~, the past participle of ~figere~ (“to drive in, to plant, to fasten”). ~Figere~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeygʷ-~ (“to stick, to set up”), which also yielded, through Germanic, English ~ditch~ and ~dike~ and German ~Teich~ (“pond”) — all things dug into or stuck in the ground. The Latin participial stem ~fix-~ gave Italian ~fissare~ and Spanish ~fijar~, and the same ~figere~ produced the diminutive ~fibula~ (“clasp, brooch”), a pin that fastens.

The earliest French sense stayed close to the Latin: to ~fixer~ was to make something fast, to set it firmly in place, then to settle the eyes upon it — hence ~fixer du regard~, “to stare at.” From “to make immovable” flowed “to determine, to settle definitively,” as in ~fixer une date~ or ~fixer un prix~. In the twentieth century French re-borrowed the modern repair sense from English ~to fix~, layering a fresh meaning atop the old. From the same root come ~fixe~, ~fixation~, ~fixe~ as a noun (a regular salary), ~préfixe~, ~suffixe~, and ~crucifier~ (literally “to fasten to a cross”). To ~fixer~ something is, at bottom, to plant it where it cannot move.
en.fonctionner:
Unlike most verbs in this app, ~fonctionner~ is young and home-grown: it is a denominal coinage, attested around 1787, formed directly on the French noun ~fonction~ (“function, role, duty”). That noun comes from Latin ~functio~ (“a performing, an execution”), the action-noun of the deponent verb ~fungi~ (“to perform, discharge, carry out an office”), whose past participle was ~functus~. Through Latin, ~fonction~ is the close kin of ~défunt~ (“deceased”), literally one who has ~discharged~ life — the ~de-~ marking completion of the duty of living.

The sense development is neat. ~Fonction~ first named a duty or office one performs; the verb ~fonctionner~ then meant “to perform one's function,” and from there spread to machinery and systems — a clock, an engine, an institution that ~works~ by doing what it is meant to do. The word arrives just as the mechanical age does, and it carries that flavour: when a French speaker says ~ça fonctionne~, the metaphor is of a thing duly discharging its office. From the same ~fungi~ / ~functio~ root come ~fonctionnaire~ (“civil servant,” one charged with a function), ~fonctionnel~, ~dysfonctionnement~, and the mathematical ~fonction~.
en.fonder:
From Old French ~fonder~, from Latin ~fundare~ (“to lay a foundation, to establish”), a denominal verb built on the noun ~fundus~ (“bottom, base, the ground of an estate”). ~fundus~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~funðos~ to a Proto-Indo-European *~bʰudʰmḗn~ (“bottom”), the same root that yielded English ~bottom~ and Ancient Greek ~puthmḗn~ (“bottom of a vessel”). The Romance cousins are everywhere: Italian ~fondare~ and Spanish ~fundar~, both “to found.”

To found something is literally to give it a ~fundus~ — to lay its lowest part, its base, before anything can be raised on top. From this concrete sense of laying a footing came the abstract one of establishing an institution, a city, or a doctrine, and figuratively of grounding an argument “on” solid reasons. French kept the underlying noun too, as ~fond~ (“bottom, background”) and ~fonds~ (“fund, capital, a piece of land”), and the doublet relationship there is striking: the financial ~fonds~ and the scenic ~fond~ are the same Latin ~fundus~ wearing two spellings. Other descendants include ~fondation~ (“foundation”), ~fondement~ (“basis”), ~profond~ (“deep,” literally “toward the bottom”), and ~foncier~ (“pertaining to land”). The English ~fund~, ~found~, ~foundation~, and ~profound~ all trace back to the same Latin base.
en.forcer:
From Vulgar Latin *~fortiare~, built on Late Latin ~fortia~ (“strength, force”), which was the substantivized neuter plural of the classical adjective ~fortis~ (“strong, brave, firm”). Attested in French since the eleventh century, ~forcer~ is cousin to Italian ~forzare~ and Spanish ~forzar~, and the whole family is kin to the English borrowings ~force~ and ~fort~.

The verb's path follows the noun ~force~, which was carved from that neuter plural ~fortia~ — literally “strong things.” To ~forcer~ originally meant to apply strength, and from there it branched: to force a door, to force a lock, to force someone's hand, to force a plant in a hothouse, even to overstrain (~se forcer~, “to push oneself too hard”). In hunting, ~forcer~ a stag is to run it to exhaustion. The same ~fortis~ underlies a robust French lineage: ~fort~ (“strong”), ~force~, ~forcené~ (“frenzied,” originally “out of one's senses”), ~renforcer~, ~effort~, and ~confort~ (which reached English as ~comfort~ before returning). What began as a Roman soldier's praise — ~fortis~, brave and steadfast — became the verb for any exertion of power, gentle or brutal.
en.former:
From Latin ~formare~ (“to shape, to fashion, to form”), derived from ~forma~ (“shape, figure, mold, beauty”). The deeper origin of ~forma~ is uncertain; one long-standing hypothesis derives it from Ancient Greek ~morphē~ (“shape, form”), perhaps with a swapping of consonants and possibly transmitted through Etruscan, though this remains unproven. Cognate with Italian ~formare~ and Spanish ~formar~.

The verb began concretely — to give matter a shape, to mold — and then climbed into the abstract: to constitute (« former un gouvernement »), to develop a skill, and especially to train or educate a person, so that ~formation~ in modern French means “training” as much as “formation.” The same ~forma~ branched into a remarkably productive family of French words: ~forme~, ~format~, ~formule~, ~formel~, ~uniforme~, ~conforme~, ~déformer~, ~réformer~, and ~transformer~. From a single word for “shape,” French built a whole architecture of forming, reforming, and transforming.
en.fournir:
From Old French ~fornir~ (also ~furnir~), borrowed from Frankish *~frumjan~ (“to accomplish, carry out, provide”), from a Germanic base *~fruma~ (“first, foremost; advantage, benefit”). The verb is cousin to Italian ~fornire~ and to the borrowed English ~furnish~ (which English took straight from this Old French verb), and through its Germanic root to German ~fromm~ (“pious, devout,” earlier “useful, upright”). The unexpected nasal ~n~ of the modern stem arose under the influence of the near-synonym ~garnir~ (“to garnish, equip”).

The original idea was “to carry through, accomplish, make ready,” which slid easily into “to provide what is needed, to equip.” From “equipping” came the modern core senses: to supply, to provide, to furnish. The noun ~fourniture~ (“supply, supplies”) and the agent ~fournisseur~ (“supplier”) carry the commercial weight today, while ~fourni~ as an adjective means “well-stocked” or, of hair and beards, “thick, abundant.” It is a tidy irony that English ~furnish~ — which in English narrowed toward outfitting a room with furniture — and French ~fournir~ — which stayed broad, “to supply anything” — are the very same Frankish word, sent down two paths. Kin in French include ~fourniture~, ~fournisseur~, ~fournil~, and ~fourni~.
en.frapper:
From Old French ~fraper~ (attested 1178), most likely from Frankish *~hrapōn~ (“to snatch, seize quickly”), of Germanic origin. The same Germanic stem ~hrap-~ surfaces in Dutch ~rapen~ (“to gather, seize”), German ~raffen~ (“to snatch up”), and Old Norse ~hreppa~ (“to obtain, reach”), all turning on the idea of grabbing something swiftly and forcefully. Romance absorbed the word too, giving Italian ~frappare~. The initial ~f~ is the telltale mark of a Frankish loan reshaped in Gallo-Romance mouths.

The meaning shifted from a quick snatching to a sudden, forceful blow — the gesture of striking. From there ~frapper~ spread generously: one hits a nail, a clock strikes the hour, a coin is struck (~frapper monnaie~), and, figuratively, a sight or a fact strikes you — ~ça m'a frappé~, “that struck me.” It even yields ~frappé~ for an iced, “shaken” drink. Modern relatives include ~frappe~ (a strike, or typing), ~frappant~ (“striking, remarkable”), and ~frappeur~. The Germanic input here is a reminder that beneath Latinate French runs a strong Frankish current, left by the Germanic people who gave France its very name.
en.gagner:
From Old French ~gaaignier~ (“to earn, win, cultivate, forage”), from Late Latin *~wadaniāre~, borrowed from Frankish *~waidanjan~ (“to forage, graze, pasture, hunt for food”). This rests on a Germanic base for hunting and pursuit, *~waiþō~ (“pasture, hunting ground”), and ultimately the Proto-Indo-European root *~weyh₁-~ (“to chase after, pursue, strive for”). Through the same Germanic source come German ~Weide~ (“pasture”) and Old Norse ~veiðr~ (“hunt”), while English ~gain~ was borrowed straight from the Old French verb.

The history is a journey from field to fortune. The earliest sense was agricultural and pastoral — to work the land, to graze animals, to forage — the toil by which medieval people drew sustenance from the earth. From “to get one's living from the land” the meaning broadened to “to earn,” then to “to obtain, acquire,” and finally to the triumphant modern “to win.” The compound ~gagne-pain~ (“breadwinner,” literally “earn-bread”) preserves the older laboring sense beautifully. The French family includes ~gain~, ~gagnant~ (“winner”), ~regagner~ (“to regain”), ~gagne-pain~, and the dialectal ~gagnage~ (“grazing land”) — a quiet memory of the pastures where the word was born.
en.garantir:
From Old French ~garantir~, a denominal verb formed around 1100 from the noun ~garant~ (“warrantor, protector”) plus the verbal suffix ~-ir~. ~garant~ first appears as ~guarant~, of Frankish origin: from ~warand~, related to the Germanic verb ~warjan~ (“to defend, ward off, vouch for”), from Proto-Germanic *~warjaną~, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *~wer-~ (“to cover, protect”). The initial Germanic ~w-~ regularly became ~gu-~ then ~g-~ in central Old French, while the northern dialects kept the ~w-~ — which is why English borrowed both ~warrant~ (from Norman ~warant~) and ~guarantee~ (from the southern ~garant~) as a doublet of the same word.

The core idea is standing surety: a ~garant~ is someone who vouches for you and shields you from loss, and to ~garantir~ is to give that protective pledge. From the legal sense of warranting title and quality, the meaning broadened to “to assure, to safeguard, to protect from” — one can ~garantir~ a result, or ~garantir~ oneself from the cold. The same Frankish stem ~warjan~ gave French ~guérir~ (“to heal,” originally “to protect, defend”), and the family includes ~garantie~ (“warranty, guarantee”) and ~garde~. German preserves a relative in ~gewähren~ (“to grant, vouchsafe”).
en.garder:
From Old French ~guarder~ (later ~garder~), from Medieval Latin *~wardare~, itself a borrowing from Frankish *~wardōn~ (“to watch, to guard”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~wer-~ (“to perceive, to watch out for”). The Frankish initial ~w-~ regularly became ~gu-~ then ~g-~ in central Old French, while the Norman dialect kept the ~w-~ that English borrowed: so French ~garder~ and English ~guard~ are doublets of one and the same Germanic word, and English ~ward~ and ~warden~ preserve the older form. German ~warten~ (“to wait on, to tend”) is a cognate.

The root idea is keeping watch, and from vigilant attention the verb spread to keeping, retaining, and looking after: to guard a fortress, to keep a secret, to look after a child. The reflexive ~se garder de~ (“to beware of”) holds the original sense of wariness. The prefixed ~regarder~ — ~re-~ + ~garder~ — drifted from “to watch over” to simply “to look at,” and gave the noun ~regard~ (“gaze”) and ~égard~ (“consideration”). The Germanic root spreads through French ~garde~, ~gardien~, ~garderie~, ~sauvegarder~, and ~avant-garde~, and feeds back into English as ~reward~ and ~regard~.
en.glisser:
Attested in Old French as ~glicier~, ~glisser~ arose from a blend of two verbs: ~glier~ (“to glide”), from the Frankish *~glidan~ (“to glide”), crossed with ~glacer~ (“to freeze, to make slippery”), from Latin ~glaciāre~. The Germanic *~glidan~ is a true cognate of English ~glide~ and is kin to German ~gleiten~; the icy half pulls in the family of ~glace~ (“ice”) and ~glacier~.

The two ancestors merged so neatly because they describe the same sensation — moving smoothly and uncontrollably over a slick surface, as one does on ice. From that core, ~glisser~ fanned out across French: a foot ~glisse~ on a wet floor (to slip), a sled ~glisse~ down a slope (to slide), a hand ~glisse~ into a pocket (to slip in quietly), and a remark can ~glisser~ over a subject without dwelling on it. The reflexive ~se glisser~ means to slip or sneak in. Modern descendants include ~glissade~ (“a slide, a glissade” — borrowed straight into English ballet and mountaineering vocabulary), ~glissant~ (“slippery”), and ~glissement~ (“a sliding, a shift”). It is one of those French words whose Frankish, Germanic blood still shows beneath the Romance surface.
en.guider:
A reshaping, under the influence of the noun ~guide~, of Old French ~guier~ (“to guide, to lead”), which goes back to the Old Frankish ~witan~ (“to show the way, to watch over”). This Germanic verb belongs to a family meaning “to know, to see, to point out”: it is cognate with Old English ~wīsian~ (“to guide, to direct”), German ~weisen~ (“to show, to point”), and, more distantly through the same Indo-European root, with Latin ~vidēre~ (“to see”). The reshaped form was reinforced by Italian ~guidare~ and Spanish ~guiar~, which English in turn borrowed as ~guide~.

The Frankish root joins seeing and showing: one who can see the path can point it out, and so ~guider~ means to lead another along a way, whether a road, a course of study, or a moral life. The initial ~gu-~ is the tell-tale Germanic ~w-~ as adapted by Old French, the same shift seen in ~guerre~ (from ~werra~) and ~garder~ (from ~wardōn~). From ~guide~ and ~guider~ French built a tidy family: ~guidage~, ~guidance~, ~guidon~ (the handlebar that steers a bicycle), and the modern ~téléguider~ (“to guide from afar, to remote-control”).
en.générer:
From Latin ~generare~ (“to beget, to produce, to generate”), a derivative of ~genus~ (“birth, origin, kind, race”), which descends from Proto-Italic *~genos~ and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵenh₁-~ (“to beget, to produce”). The same root gave Ancient Greek ~génos~ (“race, stock”), Sanskrit ~jánas~, and — through Germanic — English ~kin~ and ~kind~. Romance kept the verb alive as Italian ~generare~, Spanish ~generar~, and Portuguese ~generar~.

French borrowed ~générer~ learnedly from Latin rather than inheriting it through ordinary sound change, which is why it keeps its Latin shape so transparently. Its meaning has stayed close to the source — to bring into being, to produce — though modern usage leans heavily on technical and scientific senses: a machine, a reaction, or a computer program may ~générer~ output. The Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵenh₁-~ is astonishingly fertile in French, lying behind ~genre~, ~général~, ~généreux~, ~génération~, ~génie~, ~gendre~, and even ~gens~ — a whole family clustered around the ideas of birth, kind, and lineage that the original root named.
en.gérer:
From Latin ~gerere~ (~gestum~) (“to carry, to bear, to produce, to conduct, to manage, to perform”), whose perfect and supine stem in ~gest-~ arose because an older ~s~ became ~r~ between vowels by the regular Latin sound change called rhotacism. According to de Vaan, ~gerere~ goes back through a post-PIE *~h₂ǵ-es-~ to the same Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive, to lead”) that gave Latin ~agere~ (“to drive, to do”), the ancestor of French ~agir~. The verb was a learned borrowing, taken straight from Latin rather than inherited through the slow erosion of everyday speech.

In Latin, ~gerere~ ranged from the concrete “to carry on one's person” to the figurative “to carry out, to administer,” as in ~bellum gerere~ (“to wage war”) and ~rem gerere~ (“to manage an affair”). French kept the administrative sense: to ~gérer~ is to handle, to run, to take charge of. The ~s/r~ split left a rich double family in modern French: from the ~ger-~ side come ~gérer~, ~gérant~, ~ingérer~, ~digérer~, and ~suggérer~; from the ~gest-~ side come ~gestion~, ~geste~, and ~gesticuler~. So a manager who oversees ~la gestion~ of a company and an actor who studies his ~gestes~ on stage are, etymologically, both simply carrying something.
en.gésir:
From Old French ~gesir~ (also ~jesir~), “to lie, to be lying down,” from Latin ~iacēre~ (“to lie, to be stretched out”), from Proto-Italic *~jakēō~, from PIE *~yeh₁-~. This verb of resting belongs beside its causative twin ~iacere~ (short ~e~), “to throw” — what is thrown comes to lie. Cognate with Italian ~giacere~ and Spanish ~yacer~, both still living verbs of lying, and, through the Latin family, with the ~ject~ words English borrowed (~project~, ~reject~) from the throwing branch.

In Old French ~gesir~ was fully conjugated and ordinary, but it withered into one of French's most defective verbs, surviving chiefly in the present, the imperfect, and the participle ~gisant~ — which also became the noun for the recumbent stone effigy carved atop a tomb. Above all it fossilized in the funerary formula ~ci-gît~ (“here lies”), and its plural ~ci-gisent~, still cut into gravestones. The everyday roles of lying and resting passed to other verbs, leaving ~gésir~ a word of the deathbed and the tomb. The same Latin root seeded a wide French vocabulary of lying and location: ~adjacent~ (from ~ad-~ + ~iacēre~, “to lie next to”), the geological ~gisement~ (an ore deposit, where minerals “lie”), the ~gîte~ (a resting-place or lodging), and the learned ~jacent~.
en.habiter:
From Latin ~habitāre~ (“to dwell, inhabit, occupy”), the frequentative of ~habēre~ (“to have, hold”): to ~habitāre~ is, etymologically, “to keep having,” to hold a place continually. ~Habēre~ descends from the PIE root *~gʰeh₁bʰ-~ (“to grab, take hold of”). A common but false intuition pairs Latin ~habēre~ with English ~have~; the two merely resemble each other, for English ~have~ in fact descends from a different root, the one behind Latin ~capere~ (“to seize”). The true Romance heirs of ~habēre~ are French ~avoir~, Italian ~avere~, and Spanish ~haber~; ~habitāre~ itself gave Italian ~abitare~ and Spanish ~habitar~.

The frequentative force — having again and again — is precisely what turns “to hold” into “to dwell”: a home is a place one has and has, steadily, over time. French kept the residing sense, ~habiter une ville~ (“to live in a city”), and extended it to occupation more broadly, even figuratively, as a feeling may ~habiter~ someone. From ~habitāre~ flow ~habitant~, ~habitation~, ~habitat~, and ~cohabiter~; from the parent ~habēre~ come ~habit~ (clothing, once “what one habitually wears”), ~habitude~, and ~avoir~ — the holding, the having, and the dwelling all branches of one grasping root.
en.haïr:
From Old French ~haïr~ (also ~hadir~, ~haier~), from Frankish/Old Low Franconian *~hatjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hatjaną~ (“to hate”), ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~kad-~ (“sorrow, hatred”). It is a direct cognate of English ~hate~, German ~hassen~, and Dutch ~haten~ — a rare Germanic intruder among the overwhelmingly Latinate verbs of French, carried in by the Franks who gave their name to France itself.

The meaning has held remarkably steady — to detest — but the conjugation of ~haïr~ is one of the strangest in the language, which is precisely what makes it memorable. The diaeresis (tréma) on the ~ï~ is kept throughout, forcing the disyllabic /a.i/ of ~nous haïssons~ and ~je haïssais~ — except in the three singular persons of the présent and the singular imperative, where it vanishes: ~je hais~, ~tu hais~, ~il hait~, imperative ~hais~, all pronounced as one syllable /ɛ/. The ~h~ is aspirate, so it blocks elision and liaison: one writes ~je hais~, never *j'hais*, and says ~le haïr~ with an audible gap. In the passé simple and imperfect subjonctif the tréma even usurps the circumflex other verbs take, yielding ~nous haïmes~, ~vous haïtes~, ~qu'il haït~ rather than *-îmes/-îtes/-ît*. Finally, a living regional variation: in Canadian French the singular keeps the tréma and stays disyllabic — ~je haïs~, ~tu haïs~, ~il haït~ /a.i/ — diverging from the standard monosyllabic ~je hais~. From the same Germanic root spring ~haine~, ~haineux~, and ~haïssable~.
en.hésiter:
From Latin ~haesitare~ (“to be stuck, to stammer, to waver, to hesitate”), the frequentative or intensive of ~haerere~ (“to stick, to cling, to remain fixed”). ~Haerere~ is of uncertain origin; scholars have proposed a Proto-Indo-European *~gʰays-~ (“to stick, to be fixed”), though the connection is debated. Through its prefixed compounds the verb is well attested in French and English: ~adhaerere~ gave ~adhérer~ and English ~adhere~, while ~cohaerere~ gave ~cohérent~ and ~cohere~.

The physical sense came first: ~haerere~ meant to stick fast, to be caught and unable to move on. ~Haesitare~ intensified this into repeated sticking — and the metaphor for the tongue and the mind was immediate, since one who ~hésite~ is, in effect, stuck, unable to move forward, caught between choices. Latin already used it both of a stammering speaker and of a wavering mind, and French ~hésiter~ inherited the figurative core directly.
