The shared root gives French ~adhérer~, ~adhésif~, ~adhérence~, ~cohérent~, and ~incohérent~ — all built on the same idea of clinging or sticking together.
en.identifier:
Coined in the eighteenth century from the noun ~identité~ plus the verb-forming suffix ~-ifier~ (“to make, to render”). ~Identité~ comes from Late Latin ~identitas~, built on ~idem~ (“the same”) with the abstract suffix ~-itas~ — itself a calque, a loan-translation, of Ancient Greek ~ταὐτότης~ (~tautótēs~, “sameness”). The suffix ~-ifier~ descends from Latin ~-ificare~, from ~facere~ (“to make, to do”), so to ~identifier~ is etymologically “to make the same.” Italian ~identificare~ and Spanish ~identificar~ are parallel formations.

The oldest sense is exactly that literal one: to ~identifier~ two things was to treat them as one and the same, to equate them, a usage that survives in ~s'identifier à un personnage~ (“to identify with a character”). From “to declare identical” grew the everyday sense “to recognize, to establish who or what something is,” as a witness might ~identifier~ a suspect. The computer age added a further twist: ~s'identifier~ now also means “to log in,” to prove to a machine that one is the same person as the holder of the account, by means of an ~identifiant~ (“username, login”). The same family includes ~identité~, ~identique~, ~identification~, and ~identifiant~ — all turning on the simple Latin notion of ~idem~, sameness.
en.ignorer:
From Latin ~ignōrāre~ (“to not know, to be unacquainted with”), built on ~ignārus~ (“unaware, ignorant”), which is the negative prefix ~in-~ (“not”) attached to a root meaning “knowing,” related to ~gnārus~ (“acquainted with”) and the verb ~(g)nōscere~ (“to come to know”). That root goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~ǵneh₃-~ (“to know, to recognize”) — one of the great word-families of Indo-European, cognate with English ~know~ and ~can~, Greek ~gignṓskō~, and the whole Latin clan of ~nōscere~, ~nōtus~, and ~nōbilis~ (“knowable, hence noteworthy”).

The word's core sense is privative: ~ignorer~ means, first, “to be unaware of, to not know” — a quiet statement of a gap in knowledge, as in ~j'ignore son nom~ (“I don't know his name”). Curiously, English ~ignore~, borrowed from the same Latin verb, drifted toward a deliberate act — to know perfectly well and pay no heed — and modern French ~ignorer~ has, under English influence, picked up that second sense too: to snub, to disregard on purpose. From the root *~ǵneh₃-~ spring a vast French family, among them ~connaître~, ~reconnaître~, ~noble~, ~notice~, and the noun ~ignorance~ itself.
en.illustrer:
From Latin ~illustrāre~ (“to light up, illuminate,” figuratively “to make clear, explain, render distinguished”), from ~in-~ (“in, upon”) + ~lustrāre~ (“to shed light over, brighten”), kindred with ~lūx~ (“light”) and ~lūmen~, all from Proto-Indo-European *~lewk-~ (“bright, to shine”). Cognate, through that root, with Ancient Greek ~λευκός~ (“white, bright”), English ~light~ and ~lucid~, and French ~lumière~ and ~luire~.

The whole history of the verb is a metaphor of light. To ~illustrer~ was first to shine light upon something; from there it meant to make a matter clear — to ~illustrate~ an idea with an example — and also to cast luster on a name, to make it famous, which is why an ~homme illustre~ is a distinguished, “brilliant” man and ~illustre~ means renowned. The now-dominant sense, to ~illustrer~ a book with pictures, is the most recent layer, the images “lighting up” the text. The same *~lewk-~ glows through a wide French family: ~lustre~ (both the chandelier and the sheen of polish), ~lustrer~, ~élucider~ (to “light out” a difficulty), ~lucide~, ~translucide~, and ~lumineux~ — every one of them, at bottom, a word about shining.
en.imaginer:
From Latin ~imaginari~ (“to picture to oneself, to form a mental image”), a verb derived from the noun ~imago~ (genitive ~imaginis~, “image, likeness, copy, statue”). ~imago~ belongs to the same family as ~imitari~ (“to imitate, to copy”) and ~aemulus~ (“rival, emulator”), tracing back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂eym-~ (“to copy, to imitate”). Through ~imago~ and ~imaginari~ the French verb is cognate with Italian ~immaginare~, Spanish ~imaginar~, and English ~imagine~ and ~image~.

The Latin ~imago~ was first a concrete thing — a portrait, a wax ancestral mask, a statue — and ~imaginari~ meant to carry such an image in the mind. French took the verb in the 13th century already abstract: to picture, then to suppose or invent. The kinship with ~imitari~ is telling, for to imagine was, at root, to make an inner copy of the world. From the same family French keeps ~image~, ~imaginaire~, ~imagination~, ~imaginatif~, and ~imagerie~; the surprising cousin ~émule~ (“emulator, rival”) comes from ~aemulus~ at the far end of the same root, so that one who imagines and one who emulates are, etymologically, both makers of likenesses.
en.impliquer:
From Latin ~implicare~ (“to fold in, to entangle, to involve”), a compound of ~in-~ (“in”) and ~plicare~ (“to fold”). ~Plicare~ traces to Proto-Indo-European *~pleḱ-~ (“to plait, to weave”), itself an extension of *~pel-~ (“to fold”), and is closely allied to ~plectere~ (“to braid”). Cognates of the family include English ~ply~, ~pleat~, and ~fold~, and the Latin verb survives directly in Spanish ~plegar~ and Italian ~piegare~ (“to fold”).

The literal picture is of folding one thing into another until the two are tangled together — and from that knot of entanglement grew the figurative senses. To ~impliquer~ someone is to fold them into an affair, hence “to implicate, to involve, to accuse”; to ~impliquer~ a consequence is to have it folded in, hence “to imply.” French inherited a rich cluster from ~plicare~: ~appliquer~ (to fold toward, to apply), ~compliquer~ (to fold together, to complicate), ~expliquer~ (to unfold, to explain), ~plier~ and ~ployer~ (to fold), and ~pli~ (“a fold, a crease”). The doublet ~employer~ also belongs here, from ~implicare~ by the popular route, while ~impliquer~ is the learned borrowing.
en.importer:
French ~importer~ holds two verbs of separate descent. The commercial sense (“to bring in goods”) comes from Latin ~importare~ (“to bring in, carry in”), built from the prefix ~in-~ (“into”) and ~portare~ (“to carry”). ~Portare~ traces back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to lead across, pass over”), the same root behind Latin ~portus~ (“harbour”) and ~porta~ (“gate”), and cognate with English ~ford~ and German ~Furt~ — a ford being, like a harbour, a place of crossing. The sense “to matter, to be important” was instead borrowed from Italian ~importare~ (“to be of consequence”).

The two strands meet in the underlying idea of something being ~carried in~. Goods carried into a country are ~imported~; a matter that ~carries~ weight, that bears upon us, is one that ~imports~ — hence the adjective ~important~ and the noun ~importance~, which entered French through the Italian sense. The impersonal construction ~il importe~ (“it matters”) preserves that figurative line: the thing weighs on the situation, it is borne in. From the same *~per-~ root and the ~portare~ family French draws a crowd of carrying words: ~porter~, ~exporter~, ~transporter~, ~rapporter~, and ~portable~, alongside the harbour-words ~port~ and ~porte~.
en.imposer:
From Latin ~imponere~ (“to place upon, to set on”), a compound of the prefix ~in-~ (here assimilated to ~im-~, “on, upon”) and ~ponere~ (“to put, to place”). Old French reshaped the learned Latin verb on the model of ~poser~ — itself from Late Latin ~pausare~ (“to halt, to set down”) — so that French ~imposer~ formally belongs to the ~poser~ family rather than continuing ~ponere~ directly. Cognate formations appear in Italian ~imporre~ and Spanish ~imponer~, while English borrowed ~impose~ from the same French stem.

The literal sense of laying one thing on another lived on in religious usage — ~l'imposition des mains~, the laying on of hands — and in printing, where pages are ~imposées~, arranged on the sheet. From physical placing the verb moved to figurative burdens: to impose a duty, a silence, a will upon someone. A fiscal branch gave Latin-derived ~impôt~ (“tax”), literally something “placed upon” the taxpayer, and ~imposition~. The reflexive ~s'imposer~ shifted again, to mean “to assert oneself” or “to be unavoidable.” The same ~ponere~ underlies a great cluster of French verbs — ~poser~, ~composer~, ~disposer~, ~proposer~, ~exposer~ — and the nouns ~position~ and ~dépôt~.
en.imprimer:
From Latin ~imprimere~ (“to press into, press upon, stamp, imprint”), a compound of the prefix ~in-~ (“into, upon”) and ~premere~ (“to press”), itself from a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to press, strike.” The Latin verb is cognate with the source of Italian ~imprimere~ and Spanish ~imprimir~, and through ~premere~ it is kin to the English borrowings ~press~ and ~print~.

Long before machines, ~imprimere~ described pressing a seal into wax or stamping a mark — leaving a physical impression. When movable type arrived in the fifteenth century, French applied this verb to the new craft of pressing inked letters onto paper, and the modern sense “to print” was born; the older sense survives in figurative phrases like ~imprimer un mouvement~ (“to impart a motion”). The same Latin ~premere~ seeded a wide family in French: ~empreinte~ (“imprint, footprint”), ~épreuve~ (“proof, test”), ~exprimer~ (“to express,” literally to press out), ~comprimer~, and ~pression~. Even ~printemps~ (“spring”) belongs to a neighboring story of pressing forth. Most charmingly, the noun ~imprimante~ (“printer”), the household machine, is a direct descendant of a verb that once meant nothing more than mashing a signet ring into hot wax.
en.inclure:
From Latin ~includere~ (“to shut in, enclose, include”), from ~in-~ (“in”) and ~claudere~ (“to close, shut”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~klau-~ (“hook, peg,” used as a bar or bolt, hence “to close”). Cognate with Italian ~includere~ and Spanish ~incluir~, and — through ~claudere~ — with English ~include~, ~exclude~, and ~close~.

The literal image is concrete: to ~inclure~ is to “shut something in,” to enclose it within a boundary so that it counts as part of the whole. The Latin ~claudere~ rests on a homely Indo-European notion — the hook or peg that bars a door — which also yielded ~clavis~ (“key”) and ~clavus~ (“nail”). The modern French ~inclure~ has a famously defective conjugation, and a curious irregularity sets it apart from its sibling ~conclure~: its past participle is ~inclus~, with an ~-s~, against the ~conclu~ of ~conclure~. From the same ~claudere~ family French inherited a remarkable crowd of words — ~clé~, ~clore~, ~conclure~, ~exclure~, ~enclore~, ~clôture~, and ~cloître~ — all of them turning, etymologically, on the act of shutting.
en.indexer:
From the noun ~index~ with the verbal suffix ~-er~, a twentieth-century coinage. French ~index~ comes from Latin ~index~ (“one who points out, an informer; a forefinger; a title or list”), a deverbal noun built on ~indicāre~ (“to point out, indicate”), itself ~in-~ + ~dīcāre~ (“to proclaim”), from the PIE root *~deyḱ-~ (“to show, point out”). The very same root, in its bare finger-sense, produced Latin ~digitus~ (“finger”) — so the pointing forefinger and the pointing word-list share an ancestry. Cognate in spirit with English ~index~ and ~indicate~, borrowed from the same Latin stock.

The Latin ~index~ already wandered from the human hand to the printed page: the finger that points became the marginal mark, then the table that points a reader to a passage. French inherited this bookish sense, and the modern verb ~indexer~ — to enter something into such a list — is younger still, a workaday derivative born of catalogues and archives. The computer age stretched it further, to the indexing of databases and search engines, and economics borrowed it for pegging wages or prices to a reference figure, as in ~indexer sur l'inflation~. From the same Latin family French also keeps ~indice~, ~indication~, ~dédier~, and ~digital~ — a tidy cluster all tracing back to the gesture of pointing.
en.indiquer:
From Latin ~indicāre~ (“to point out, to make known”), a denominal verb formed on ~index~ (“pointer, forefinger, informer”), which joins the prefix ~in-~ (“toward”) to the root of ~dīcere~ (“to say”). That root is Proto-Indo-European *~deyḱ-~ (“to point out, to show”), the same source as Greek ~deiknumi~ (“I show”) and, through Germanic, English ~teach~ and ~token~. The Romance heirs include Italian ~indicare~ and Spanish ~indicar~.

The original gesture is literally that of the index finger pointing something out, and from “to point at” the verb broadened to “to indicate, to show, to suggest.” The shared root *~deyḱ-~ is astonishingly fertile in French: ~dire~ itself, ~dicter~, ~indice~ (“clue”), ~indication~, and the legal ~juridiction~ (from ~iūs dīcere~, “to declare the law”) all descend from the idea of pointing or pronouncing. English doublets such as ~indicate~, ~dictate~, ~predict~, and ~verdict~ travel the same path. Each time one ~indique~ the way, one is, in effect, extending an ancient pointing finger across several thousand years of language.
en.informer:
From Latin ~informare~ (“to give shape to, to fashion, to form an idea of”), built from the prefix ~in-~ (“into”) and the verb ~formare~ (“to shape”), itself from the noun ~forma~ (“shape, form, figure”). The ultimate origin of ~forma~ is uncertain; it may be connected to Ancient Greek ~morphē~ (“shape”), perhaps through a swapping of sounds, but this is not secure, so it is best left as a Latin word of debated parentage. English borrowed the same verb as ~inform~.

The original meaning was concrete and even philosophical: to ~informer~ was to give form to matter, to impress a shape upon it — in scholastic thought the soul was said to “inform” the body. From “shaping someone's mind” grew the modern sense of furnishing the mind with knowledge: to inform is to form a person's understanding by pouring facts into it. The hugely productive ~forma~ seeded a whole French family: ~forme~, ~former~, ~format~, ~formule~, ~uniforme~ (“of one form”), ~transformer~, ~conforme~, and ~difforme~. And from ~informer~ itself, by way of the data that shapes our knowledge, came ~information~ — and, in our own century, the entire vocabulary of ~informatique~.
en.inscrire:
From Latin ~īnscrībere~ (“to write in, to write upon, to enroll”), a compound of the prefix ~in-~ (“in, into, upon”) and the verb ~scrībere~ (“to write”). The French form was reshaped on the model of ~écrire~, the inherited descendant of ~scrībere~. Latin ~scrībere~ originally meant “to scratch, to incise marks,” for the earliest writing was cutting signs into wood, stone, or clay; it comes from Proto-Italic *~skreiβō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)kreybʰ-~ (“to scratch, to cut”). The same Latin verb spread far: French ~écrire~, Italian ~scrivere~, German ~schreiben~, and English ~scribe~ and ~script~ all descend from it.

The prefix ~in-~ gives the verb its particular flavor: not merely to write, but to write something *into* a register, a stone, a list. From that come the two faces of modern ~inscrire~ — the monumental sense of carving a name into marble, and the bureaucratic sense of entering a name on a roll, hence “to sign up, to register.” The reflexive ~s'inscrire~ means to enroll oneself, and the legal phrase ~s'inscrire en faux~ means to formally contest a claim. The ~scrībere~ family richly stocks French with ~écrire~, ~écriture~, ~inscription~, ~scribe~, ~prescrire~, ~décrire~, and ~manuscrit~.
en.insister:
From Latin ~insistere~ (“to stand on, to set foot upon, to persist”), built from the prefix ~in-~ (“on, upon”) and ~sistere~ (“to cause to stand, to set”). ~sistere~ is itself a reduplicated form of ~stāre~ (“to stand”), going back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”). Through this same ~sistere~ family, French gained ~consister~, ~persister~, ~résister~, and ~subsister~; the English cognate ~insist~ comes by the same Latin road, alongside ~persist~ and ~desist~.

The image hiding inside ~insister~ is physical: to insist was once to literally plant your feet on something and stand there. Latin ~insistere~ meant to step onto a path or to take up a firm position, and from that bodily stance grew the figurative sense of dwelling on a point and refusing to budge — exactly what we mean when we say someone insists. French borrowed the verb from learned Latin rather than inheriting it through everyday speech, which is why it keeps its clean Latin shape. The far-flung relatives of the root *~steh₂-~ are everywhere in French: ~stable~, ~station~, ~statue~, ~constant~, and even the past participle ~été~ of ~être~. To insist, etymologically, is simply to keep standing your ground.
en.inspirer:
From Latin ~inspīrāre~ (“to breathe into, to blow upon”), a compound of the prefix ~in-~ (“into”) and the verb ~spīrāre~ (“to breathe”). Latin ~spīrāre~ is usually traced to a Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)peys-~ (“to blow, to breathe”), though some scholars suspect it is simply an onomatopoeia imitating the sound of breathing. Italian ~ispirare~ and Spanish ~inspirar~ continue the same Latin verb, and English ~inspire~ arrives through Old French.

The twin meanings of ~inspirer~ — “to breathe in” and “to inspire” — are not a coincidence but the original picture: a god or a Muse was imagined literally breathing an idea into a person. From that physical inhalation grew the figurative “inspiration,” the in-breathed thought, and French keeps both senses side by side: one inspires deeply at the doctor's, and a landscape inspires a poet. The same root yields a remarkably breathy family — ~expirer~ (to breathe out, hence also to die), ~respirer~, ~aspirer~, ~soupirer~, ~conspirer~ (to “breathe together,” i.e. to plot in whispered agreement), and the nouns ~esprit~ and ~spiritueux~. Most striking of all, French ~esprit~ and English ~spirit~ descend from ~spīritus~ (“breath”): the soul itself was, to the Roman mind, a kind of breathing — a thought ~inspirer~ quietly preserves.
en.installer:
From Medieval Latin ~installāre~ (“to put into a stall, to establish in office”), from the prefix ~in-~ (“in”) and ~stallum~ (“stall, seat”), itself borrowed from Frankish *~stall~ (“place, position, stable-stall”), from Proto-Germanic *~stallaz~. That Germanic word goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~stel-~ (“to place, to set, to stand firm”). Old French had the related noun ~estal~ (“position, market-stall, standing-place”).

The earliest sense was strikingly ceremonial: to ~installer~ was to seat a canon or bishop in his ~stall~ in the choir, formally placing him in his office. From that solemn enthroning the verb spread to ordinary placing — to install a person in a post, to set up furniture or a machine, and reflexively ~s'installer~, to settle in, to make oneself at home. The Germanic *~stall~ that entered French here is, distantly, kin to the native ~stalle~ (a choir-stall) and to English ~stall~ and ~install~ themselves. So the French ~installer~ is a Germanic stall dressed in Latin robes, carried into French by the ceremonies of the medieval Church.
en.interdire:
From Old French ~entredire~, ~entredire~, a learned reshaping after Latin ~interdicere~ (“to forbid, to pronounce a prohibition”), itself ~inter-~ (“between, among”) + ~dicere~ (“to say, to speak”). ~dicere~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~deyḱ-~ (“to show, to point out”), the same root that gives Latin ~index~ and ~iudex~ (“judge,” one who points out the law) and, through Germanic, English ~teach~ and ~token~. Cognate Romance forms include Italian ~interdire~ and Spanish ~interdecir~.

In Roman usage an ~interdictum~ was a magistrate's formal order — quite literally a thing pronounced ~inter~ the parties — and the verb kept this flavour of authoritative speech that closes off an action. Old French ~entredit~ named the ecclesiastical ~interdit~, the dread papal sanction that suspended public worship over a whole realm. The modern legal and everyday senses (“forbid, ban”) grew directly from this idea of a prohibition laid down by spoken authority; a related figurative sense, “to dumbfound,” survives in ~rester interdit~ (“to be left speechless”), as if struck silent by a verdict. From the same ~dicere~ French inherited a large family: ~dire~, ~prédire~, ~contredire~, ~maudire~, ~verdict~, ~juge~, and ~indice~.
en.interpréter:
From Latin ~interpretārī~ (“to explain, expound, translate”), the verb formed on ~interpres~ (genitive ~interpretis~, “a go-between, agent, translator”). The noun splits into ~inter-~ (“between”) and a second element widely connected to ~pretium~ (“price, value”) and beyond it to the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to traffic in, to sell”) — an ~interpres~ being one who stands between two parties and brokers meaning across them. French preserves the noun directly as ~interprète~. Italian ~interpretare~ and Spanish ~interpretar~ descend from the same Latin verb, as does English ~interpret~.

The original ~interpres~ was a middleman — a negotiator, a broker, then a translator standing between two tongues. From mediating between languages the sense broadened to mediating between a text and its reader: to ~interpréter~ is to draw out a hidden or disputed meaning, whether of a law, a dream, or a difficult passage. A further branch carries the word into performance, where to ~interpréter~ a role or a sonata is to render it, to make its meaning audible. The exact second root remains debated, which is why scholars hedge it; still, the same Latin family yields French ~interprète~, ~interprétation~, and ~interprétariat~.
en.interroger:
From Latin ~interrogare~ (“to ask, to question”), a compound of the preposition ~inter~ (“between, among”) and the verb ~rogare~ (“to ask, to request”). ~rogare~ is usually traced to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₃reǵ-~ (“to straighten, to direct in a straight line”), so that asking may have begun as a figurative “stretching out” of the hand toward someone. The same root gives Latin ~regere~ (“to rule, to keep straight”) and ~rex~ (“king”). Cognate through ~rogare~ with English ~rogation~ and, more distantly through the root, with English ~right~ and ~regent~.

The ~inter~ in ~interrogare~ carries the idea of an exchange passing back and forth between two parties — the to-and-fro of question and answer. From the neutral Latin sense “to ask,” French ~interroger~ specialized toward pointed, official, or insistent questioning: a judge, a teacher, or a police officer ~interroge~, where one merely ~demande~ a favor. The same ~rogare~ surfaces across French in a string of relatives: ~rogatoire~ (as in a ~commission rogatoire~, a judicial request), ~abroger~ (to repeal, literally to “ask away” a law), ~déroger~, ~proroger~, and ~prérogative~ — a cluster in which the original gesture of asking has hardened into the formal vocabulary of law and authority.
en.intervenir:
From Latin ~intervenīre~ (“to come between, intervene, interrupt, occur”), a compound of the prefix ~inter-~ (“between, among”) and ~venīre~ (“to come”). ~venīre~ descends from Proto-Italic *~gʷenjō~ and Proto-Indo-European *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to go”), one of the most widely attested roots in the family: it is cognate with English ~come~, Greek ~baínein~ (“to go, step”), and Sanskrit ~gam-~ (“to go”). To intervene is, literally, “to come between.”

The Latin verb already carried both senses the French keeps: a physical or social one — to step in, to interpose oneself in an affair — and a temporal one, “to happen, to occur in the meantime,” as when an event ~intervient~ unexpectedly. French inherited the prefixed family richly: ~venir~ (“to come”), ~devenir~ (“to become”), ~revenir~ (“to come back”), ~prévenir~ (“to forewarn, to prevent,” literally “to come before”), ~parvenir~, ~survenir~, and ~convenir~ (“to suit, agree”). The same root surfaces in the learned ~avenir~ (“future,” what is “to come”) and in ~événement~ (“event,” something that “comes out”). Even ~aventure~ — what is “about to come” — belongs to this kin. To intervene is thus to insert a coming squarely in the middle of events.
en.intituler:
From Latin ~intitulare~ (“to give a title to, to inscribe”), a derivative of ~titulus~ (“title, label, inscription, placard”). The origin of ~titulus~ is uncertain — possibly borrowed from Etruscan, the substrate that gave Latin several culture-words of obscure pedigree. From ~titulus~ the Romance languages drew their words for “title”: Spanish ~título~, Italian ~titolo~, and French ~titre~ (via Old French ~title~); English ~title~ and the tiny ~tittle~ (the dot over an i) are doublets from the very same Latin word.

In Roman use a ~titulus~ was an inscribed tablet or notice — the placard naming a thing, even the board nailed above the cross. To ~intituler~ a work is therefore literally to fix a heading on it, to give it its name, and the verb still means “to title, to entitle.” The reflexive ~s'intituler~ means “to be called, to bear the title of.” French keeps a rich family around ~titre~: ~titrer~ (“to give a title, to assay”), ~sous-titre~ (“subtitle”), ~titulaire~ (“holder, titular”), and the verb ~intituler~ itself, all turning on the idea of the name set at the head of a thing.
en.introduire:
From Latin ~introducere~ (“to lead in, to bring inside”), a compound of the adverb ~intro~ (“inward, within”) and the verb ~ducere~ (“to lead, to conduct”). ~intro~ is itself built on ~in~ (“in”); ~ducere~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dewk-~ (“to lead, to draw”), which in the Germanic branch yields English ~tow~ and ~tug~ and German ~ziehen~ (“to pull”). Old French reshaped the inherited form as ~entreduire~ before the learned ~introduire~ prevailed. Cognate with English ~introduce~ and Italian ~introdurre~.

The literal image is of leading something or someone inward — across a threshold, into a room, into a society. French ~introduire~ keeps this concretely physical sense (to insert, to put in: ~introduire une clé dans la serrure~) alongside the social one (to introduce a person, to bring them into a circle) and the abstract (to introduce an idea, a law, a custom). The root *~dewk-~ is one of the great engines of French vocabulary through ~ducere~ and its participle ~ductus~: ~conduire~, ~produire~, ~réduire~, ~séduire~, ~traduire~, plus the nouns ~duc~ (“duke,” a leader), ~conduite~, and ~aqueduc~. To ~introduire~ is, etymologically, simply to lead in.
en.intégrer:
From Latin ~integrāre~ (“to make whole, restore, renew”), a verb built on the adjective ~integer~ (“whole, intact, untouched”). That adjective is itself ~in-~ (“not”) plus a form related to ~tangere~ (“to touch”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~tag-~ (“to touch, handle”): the ~integer~ is literally the “untouched,” the thing left whole. The same root gives Latin ~tangere~, ~tactus~, and ~intāctus~. French ~intégrer~ entered first as a learned, rare word in the 14th century, then was effectively reborrowed in the 19th.

The arc of meaning runs from “to restore to wholeness” to “to make something part of a whole.” That second sense powers all the modern uses: to incorporate elements into a system, to admit a person into a group, to ~intégrer~ a prestigious school (a peculiarly French use meaning to gain admission), and the mathematician's “to integrate.” Because ~integer~ meant “untouched, undivided,” its descendants split between two notions — moral wholeness and numerical wholeness — so that the same root underlies both ~intégrité~ (uprightness) and the English ~integer~ (a whole number). Kin in modern French include ~intégral~, ~intégration~, ~réintégrer~, ~désintégrer~, and, through Old French, the everyday ~entier~ (“entire, whole”).
en.intéresser:
From Latin ~interesse~ (“to be among, to be between, to take part in”), a compound of the preposition ~inter~ (“between, among”) and ~esse~ (“to be”), the latter from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₁es-~ (“to be”) that also underlies English ~is~ and German ~ist~. From the impersonal Latin phrase ~interest~ (“it matters, it concerns”) French drew both the noun ~intérêt~ and, in the fourteenth century, the verb ~intéresser~. Italian preserves the parallel ~interessare~ and Spanish ~interesar~.

The semantic thread runs from physical position to mental engagement: what is literally “among” us, what lies “between” the parties, is what concerns them — and what concerns us is what holds our attention. So ~intéresser~ came to mean both “to involve someone, to give them a stake in something” and “to arouse someone's curiosity.” The financial sense survives in ~intéressement~, an employee profit-sharing scheme — a share, quite literally, in what is at stake. The same ~inter~ prefix runs through a crowd of French words such as ~international~, ~intervalle~, ~interrompre~, and ~entretenir~, while ~esse~ surfaces, much disguised, in ~essence~ and in the irregular forms of ~être~. To find something ~intéressant~ is, at root, to feel oneself caught up in the midst of it.
en.inventer:
From Latin ~inventum~, the supine of ~invenīre~ (“to come upon, to find, to discover”), a compound of ~in-~ (“on, upon”) and ~venīre~ (“to come”). Latin ~venīre~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to step”) — the very same root, remarkably, that surfaces in the Germanic branch as English ~come~ and German ~kommen~. To ~invenīre~ was literally to “come upon” something. The Spanish ~inventar~ shares the ancestry.

The semantic pivot is the difference between finding and making up: in Latin and early French the word leaned toward discovery — you ~invent~ a thing by coming upon what was already there (a sense fossilized in the legal and archaeological ~invention~, the “finding” of relics or treasure). Only by about 1450 did French ~inventer~ tip toward the modern sense of contriving something that did not exist before, and thence to the pejorative “to make up, to fabricate” (~inventer une excuse~). From the same ~venīre~ come a great crowd of French words — ~venir~, ~avenir~, ~événement~, ~convenir~, ~revenir~, ~souvenir~, ~prévenir~ — all turning on the idea of coming.
en.investir:
From Latin ~investīre~ (“to clothe, dress, surround”), from ~in-~ (“in, into”) + ~vestīre~ (“to dress, clothe”), itself from ~vestis~ (“garment, clothing”), from Proto-Indo-European *~wes-~ (“to clothe”). Cognate with Italian ~investire~ and Spanish ~investir~, and — through the same ~vestis~ — with French ~vêtir~, ~veste~, and ~vêtement~, and with English ~vest~ and ~vestment~.

The oldest sense was literal then ceremonial: to ~investir~ someone was to robe them in the insignia of an office, hence “to install in a dignity” — the source of ~investiture~, the medieval rite by which a lord clothed a vassal in his fief. From the garment that wraps came the military ~investir~ (“to besiege, to surround a place”), the army girding a town as cloth girds a body. The financial sense — to ~investir~ capital — is the most surprising turn: it arose in the early 1600s, probably through Italian ~investire~ in the East-Indies trade, on the image of giving money a new “dress,” clothing it in a fresh and profitable form. The same root yields ~travesti~ (“cross-dressed”), literally a re-clothing, and ~transvestisme~.
en.inviter:
From Latin ~invītāre~ (“to invite, to summon, to entertain”), attested in French from 1356. The deeper origin is genuinely uncertain: some scholars connect it to ~invocāre~ (“to call upon”), while others reconstruct a Proto-Indo-European base *~weyh₁-~ (“to chase, to pursue”), via a participle *~weyh₁-to-~ (“pursued, sought after”). Because the etymology is disputed, the chain beyond Latin is best left open. The Latin verb passed regularly into Italian ~invitare~, Spanish ~invitar~, and Catalan ~invitar~; English ~invite~ came in through Middle French ~inviter~.

The Latin sense already blended hospitality with enticement — to ~invītāre~ was to summon a guest but also to allure — and French preserves both shades, from ~inviter à dîner~ (“to invite to dinner”) to ~inviter à la prudence~ (“to urge caution”). A striking offshoot survives in card-playing: from the “luring” sense came Spanish and Catalan ~envidar~ (“to stake, to bet”), as a player invites the others to wager. In modern French the family stays compact — chiefly ~inviter~, ~invitation~, ~invité~, and ~invite~ (“an enticement”) — a small but sociable corner of the lexicon.
en.isoler:
Borrowed in the seventeenth century from Italian ~isolare~ (“to make into an island, to set apart”), itself from ~isola~ (“island”), from Latin ~īnsula~ (“island”). The French adjective ~isolé~ was attested slightly earlier, taken from Italian ~isolato~ (Latin ~īnsulātus~, “made into an island”), and the verb was back-formed from it. Latin ~īnsula~ is also the source of Italian ~isola~, Spanish ~isla~, and — through a different route, with ~île~ — French itself; English ~isle~ and ~isolated~ belong to the same family.

The earliest French use, in 1653, was strictly geographic: a branch of the Nile was said to ~isoler~ a meadow, to cut it off like an island. From there the meaning spread to any setting-apart — a hermit who ~s'isole~, a patient in quarantine, a wire that one must ~isoler~ electrically (hence the English “insulate”). The development was reinforced by the native French ~seul~ (“alone”), which colored the verb toward solitude. The same ~īnsula~ yields a large modern family: ~isolation~, ~isolement~, ~isolant~ (“insulator, insulating”), ~isolateur~, ~isoloir~ (the voting booth), and the geographic ~presqu'île~ (“peninsula,” a calque of Latin ~paene īnsula~, “almost an island”).
en.issir:
From Old French ~issir~ (also ~eissir~, “to go out, to come forth”), from Latin ~exīre~ (“to go out, depart”), a compound of ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~īre~ (“to go”). ~Īre~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₁ey-~ (“to go”), one of the great verbs of motion, cognate with Greek ~eîmi~ (“I will go”) and Sanskrit ~éti~ (“he goes”). Romance kin include Italian ~uscire~ (from a related ~ūsciō~) and the Spanish noun reflexes of the same family.

In medieval French, ~issir~ was a fully living verb meaning “to exit.” Then it lost its own battle for survival: in the sixteenth century speakers increasingly preferred ~sortir~, and by the seventeenth ~issir~ had all but vanished as a conjugated verb. Yet it did not die — it fossilized into its participles. The past participle ~issu~ (“descended from, born of”) is thoroughly alive (“~issu~ d'une famille modeste”), and the noun ~issue~ (“a way out, an outcome”) is one of the most common words in French. The same ~exīre~ surfaces in the borrowed ~exit~, and the root ~īre~ underlies a scattered band of French descendants from the verb ~aller~’s suppletive forms to nouns like ~transit~ and ~initiation~ — wanderings, all, of a verb that simply meant “to go.”
en.jeter:
From Old French ~jeter~ (“to throw”), from Vulgar Latin *~jectāre~, from Latin ~jactāre~ (“to toss about, to fling repeatedly”), the frequentative of ~jacere~ (“to throw”). A frequentative names a repeated or vigorous action, so ~jactāre~ is “to keep throwing, to hurl about.” ~Jacere~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~Hyeh₁-~ (“to throw, to let fly”). Cognate with Italian ~gettare~ and Spanish ~echar~.

That buried ~jacere~ is one of the most productive roots in the learned vocabulary: its past participle ~iactus~ and its compounds gave a long file of French words in ~-jeter~ and ~-jecter~ — ~projeter~ and ~projet~ (“thrown forward”), ~rejeter~ (“thrown back”), ~injecter~ (“thrown in”), ~objet~ (“thrown before”), ~sujet~ (“thrown under”), and ~trajet~ (“a throwing across”). The plain verb keeps the simple gesture of casting away, which is why ~jeter~ ranges from hurling a stone to throwing out the rubbish to “casting” a glance (~jeter un coup d'œil~). Even ~jeton~ — a thrown-down counter — and the cast water of a ~jet~ d'eau trace back to the same Latin act of letting fly.
en.joindre:
From Latin ~jungere~ (“to join, yoke, unite”), descended from the Proto-Indo-European root *~yewg-~ (“to join, yoke”). This is one of the deepest and most widespread roots in Indo-European: it gives Sanskrit ~yoga~ (“union, yoking”), Greek ~zeugnumi~ (“I yoke”), German ~Joch~, and the English ~yoke~ — itself a cognate of the very root, alongside the borrowed ~join~ and ~junction~. Italian ~giungere~ and Spanish ~juntar~ are the Romance kin.

The Latin sense of binding two things together survives intact: one can ~joindre~ two pieces of wood, ~joindre les mains~ (clasp the hands), ~joindre l'utile à l'agréable~ (combine the useful and the pleasant), or — in a thoroughly modern turn — ~joindre~ someone by telephone, that is, reach them. The past participle ~joint~ also names a seal or gasket. The root's reach in French is enormous: ~jonction~, ~adjoindre~, ~rejoindre~, ~conjoint~ (“spouse,” one yoked together with you), ~disjoindre~, ~jointure~, and the borrowed joindre-family aside, even ~joug~ (“yoke”) itself descends from ~jungere~'s noun ~jugum~. From an ox's wooden yoke to a married couple to a phone call, all are, at heart, a yoking-together.
en.jouer:
From Old French ~joer~, ~jouer~ (“to play, jest, frolic”), from Latin ~iocāre~ (“to joke, jest”), a Vulgar-Latin variant of the Classical deponent ~iocārī~, from the noun ~iocus~ (“joke, jest, game”). Cognate with Italian ~giocare~, Spanish ~jugar~, and Portuguese ~jogar~; from ~iocus~ English borrowed ~joke~ and ~jocular~.

A quiet upset hides here: Classical Latin's main verb for playing was ~lūdere~ (the source of ~ludique~ and ~illusion~), while ~iocārī~ meant narrowly to jest with words. Over Late Latin the joking verb broadened to cover all play and amusement and ousted ~lūdere~ from everyday speech, so French inherited ~jouer~ for everything from children's games to performing music and acting a role. From the related noun ~iocus~ came ~jeu~ (“game”), and from ~jeu~ the reduplicated nursery word ~joujou~ and the toy ~jouet~. The word's reach in modern French is remarkably wide — one ~joue~ a sport, an instrument, a part on stage, a card, the stock market, and even, figuratively, with someone's feelings — all descended from a Roman word that meant, at first, merely a good-natured joke.
en.juger:
From Latin ~jūdicāre~ (“to judge, to pass sentence”), itself transparently a compound of ~jūs~ (“law, right”) and the verb ~dīcere~ (“to say, to pronounce”) — so that to judge was literally “to speak the law.” Latin ~jūs~ derives, through Proto-Italic ~jowos~, from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂yew-~ (“right, justice”), while ~dīcere~ goes back to *~deyḱ-~ (“to show, to point out”), the root that also gives English ~teach~ and ~token~. Italian ~giudicare~ and Spanish ~juzgar~ are the sister forms; English ~judge~ arrives through Old French ~jugier~.

The agent noun ~jūdex~ (“one who declares the law,” our ~judge~) shows the compound at work: in Roman practice what made law was not the doing of it but the solemn pronouncing of it, and from this act of speech sprang a whole vocabulary — ~iūdicium~, ~iūrisdictio~, and the rest. In French the verb spread from the courtroom into everyday assessment: to judge a distance, to deem something wise, to form an opinion. The same root family flowers richly: ~jugement~, ~juge~, ~préjuger~ and its noun ~préjugé~, ~déjuger~, ~méjuger~, and the legal ~jury~ borrowed back through English. Tucked inside this common verb is a memory of the Roman magistrate gravely speaking the right into being.
en.justifier:
From Latin ~justificare~ (“to make just, to render righteous”), a compound of ~justus~ (“just, righteous”) + the combining form of ~facere~ (“to make, to do”). ~Justus~ derives from ~jus~ (“law, right”), from an Old Latin ~iovestos~ resting on the stem of ~jus~, and ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂yew-~, denoting binding law or sacred formula. Through ~jus~ the word is cognate with English ~just~, ~jury~, and ~judge~; through ~facere~, from PIE *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to put, to set”), it joins a vast Indo-European family.

First a theological and legal word — “to make righteous,” “to clear of a charge” — ~justifier~ then drifted toward the everyday “to give good reason for, to vindicate.” A delightful late detour is the typographer's sense: to ~justifier~ a line of text is to make its margins straight and “right,” spacing the words until the edge is even — the abstract idea of rectitude turned into the literal squaring of a column. The root ~jus~ runs deep in French through ~juste~, ~justice~, ~juger~, ~jury~, and ~juridique~, while ~facere~ surfaces in countless verbs in ~-fier~, among them ~vérifier~, ~modifier~, and ~simplifier~.
en.laisser:
French ~laisser~ descends through Old French ~laissier~ — already attested in the ninth-century ~Séquence de sainte Eulalie~, one of the oldest texts in the language — from Latin ~laxare~ (“to loosen, to slacken, to let go”). That verb is built on the adjective ~laxus~ (“loose, slack, wide”), which traces to Proto-Indo-European *~sleg-~ (“to be slack, to be languid”). The same root surfaces in English ~slack~, ~lax~, and ~relax~, and in Latin ~languere~ (“to be faint”), so the verb of letting-go is, at its core, a verb of going loose.

The semantic path is intuitive: to loosen one's grip is to let go, and to let go is to leave behind or to permit. Old French ~laissier~ already spanned “to let, to allow, to abandon,” and modern ~laisser~ keeps all of these — ~laisse-moi~ (“leave me alone”), ~laisser faire~ (“to let happen”). From ~laxare~ French also drew the doublet ~lâcher~ (“to release, to let go”), the noun ~laisse~ (a dog's leash, literally something slackened out), and, more distantly through ~laxus~, the learned ~laxatif~ and ~relâcher~. The economic doctrine of ~laissez-faire~ preserves, fossilized in a slogan, the verb's oldest instinct: loosen your hold and let things run.
en.lancer:
From Old French ~lancier~, from Late Latin ~lanceāre~ (“to wield or hurl a lance”), derived from ~lancea~ (“lance, light spear”), a word the Romans likely borrowed from a Celtic or Iberian source. The throwing weapon gave its name to the act of throwing. Italian ~lanciare~ and Spanish ~lanzar~ are cognate, and English ~lance~ and ~launch~ both descend, through French, from the same ~lancea~.

The verb began as something a soldier did with a spear — to cast it forward — and from that violent gesture generalized to any forceful throw, then to figurative launches: one ~lance~ a rocket, a product, a fashion, or a rumor. English ~launch~ is the same word, borrowed from Old French and specialized into the sense of setting a ship or a venture in motion. The native family is rich: the noun ~lance~, plus ~lancement~, ~lanceur~ (a “launcher,” including a rocket), ~relancer~ and ~relance~ (to throw again, to revive), and ~élancer~ / ~élan~, where the idea of hurling oneself forward yields both a sudden pain and a surge of momentum.
en.lever:
From Latin ~levare~ (“to lift, to raise, to make light”), a denominative verb from the adjective ~levis~ (“light, not heavy”). That adjective descends from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ (“light, nimble”), the root that also gives English ~light~ and ~lung~. Romance kept the verb everywhere: Italian ~levare~, Spanish ~llevar~ (“to carry”), Portuguese ~levar~, Catalan and Occitan ~levar~.

The core idea is making something rise. From it French grew an unusually large family. The everyday ~lever~ covers raising, lifting, and getting up; with prefixes it yields ~élever~ (“to raise, to bring up”), ~enlever~ (“to remove, to carry off”), ~relever~ (“to lift again, to note”), and ~soulever~ (“to heave up”). The past participle ~levé~ and the action noun ~levée~ gave English ~levee~ (a raised riverbank) and ~levy~ (a raising of troops or taxes), while the tool that raises a load came through Old French as English ~lever~. Even bread remembers the root: ~levure~ (“yeast”) and ~levain~ (“leaven, sourdough”) are what make dough ~lever~ — rise. And the curtain-raising sense survives in ~le lever du soleil~, the sunrise, and historically in ~le lever du roi~, the king's ceremonial rising.
en.libérer:
French ~libérer~ comes from Latin ~līberāre~ (“to set free”), a verb built on the adjective ~līber~ (“free”). That adjective traces to a Proto-Indo-European *~h₁léwdʰeros~ (“free,” literally “belonging to the people”), from the root *~h₁lewdʰ-~ (“to grow, to rise; people”). The cognates are illuminating: Ancient Greek ~eleútheros~ (“free”) shares the exact formation, while German ~Leute~ and Russian ~ljúdi~ (both “people”) preserve the “people” sense of the same root — freedom, anciently, was the condition of those who belonged to the folk. Notably, Latin ~līberāre~ also descended by the popular route into French ~livrer~ (“to deliver, to hand over”), so ~libérer~ and ~livrer~ are doublets, one learned, one inherited.

~Libérer~ is the relearned, bookish form, restored close to its Latin shape, and it kept the core sense “to set free”: to free a prisoner, to release from an obligation, and — a more mundane modern turn — to vacate, as one ~libère~ a hotel room or a parking space. The adjective ~līber~ and its verb seeded a large French family: ~liberté~, ~libéral~ and ~libéralité~, ~libération~ and ~libérateur~, ~délivrer~ (through ~livrer~), and the borrowed ~libertin~. The motto ~Liberté, égalité, fraternité~ enshrines the noun at the heart of French identity. To liberate, etymologically, is to restore someone to the free condition of the people.
en.lier:
From Latin ~ligāre~ (“to bind, to tie”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~leyǵ-~ (“to bind”). Old French had both the popular form ~lier~ and learned spellings, and the verb passed into English as the borrowed ~liable~ and ~liaison~. Cognates from the same root include Albanian ~lidh~ (“I bind”); within Romance the verb lives on as Italian ~legare~ and Spanish ~ligar~.

The meaning stays close to its root: to ~lier~ is to bind, to fasten, to tie together — ropes, ideas, people. From physical binding grew the abstract senses: ~lier conversation~ (“to strike up a conversation”), to be ~lié~ to someone by friendship, and even a cook's sense, to ~lier~ a sauce by thickening it so the ingredients hold together. The root *~leyǵ-~ produced a dense French family: ~lien~ (“bond, link”), ~liane~ (the binding jungle vine), ~ligament~, ~ligature~, ~ligoter~ (“to tie up”), ~allier~ and ~alliance~, ~obliger~, and ~religion~ — often explained as that which binds the faithful. English ~ally~, ~oblige~, and ~rely~ share the same knot.
en.limiter:
From Latin ~līmitāre~ (“to bound, to fix a boundary”), a verb formed on the noun ~līmes~ (genitive ~līmitis~), meaning “a boundary path, a balk between fields, a frontier.” The deeper root is debated: ~līmes~ is sometimes linked to ~līmen~ (“threshold”) and to an idea of a transverse or slanting line, but the connection is uncertain and best left open. Cognate with Italian ~limitare~ and Spanish ~limitar~.

The surprising history lives in the noun. To a Roman surveyor a ~līmes~ was first a balk, the strip of unploughed earth marking off one field from the next; the same word then named the great fortified frontiers of the Empire — the ~Limes~ along the Rhine and the Danube — the very limit of the Roman world. From “the edge as a marked line” the verb ~limiter~ kept both the literal “to mark out, to bound” and the figurative “to restrict, to limit.” French preserves the whole boundary family: ~limite~ (“limit, boundary”), ~limitrophe~ (“bordering, adjacent”), ~illimité~ (“unlimited”), and ~délimiter~ (“to delimit, to mark the bounds of”).
en.lire:
From Old French ~lire~, from Latin ~legere~ (“to gather, to collect; to read”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~leǵ-~ (“to gather, to collect, to pick up”). The literal Latin meaning was “to gather,” and “to read” developed from the idea of gathering up the letters of a text with the eye, or picking out words one by one. The cognate Greek verb ~λέγω~ (légō) shows the same root branching toward “to pick out” and then “to say, to speak.” Reflexes of ~legere~ survive as Italian ~leggere~, Spanish ~leer~, and Portuguese ~ler~, and the root entered English through Latin borrowings like ~lecture~ and ~legible~.

The semantic journey from “gathering grain or wood” to “reading a page” is one of the most evocative in the Latin vocabulary: the reader is a kind of harvester, going along the line and collecting meaning. The prefixed forms of ~legere~ are everywhere in modern French — ~élire~ (“to elect,” literally “to gather out, to pick out”), ~cueillir~ (“to gather, to pick”), and the learned ~collecter~ and ~sélectionner~. From the same root come ~lecture~ (“reading”), ~lecteur~ (“reader”), ~légende~ (“legend; caption,” originally “things to be read”), ~légion~, and the family of ~-lège~ words such as ~florilège~ (“anthology,” a “gathering of flowers”), and even ~intelligent~ (“choosing between, discerning”).
en.livrer:
From Old French ~livrer~, from Latin ~liberare~ (“to set free, to release”), the verbal derivative of ~liber~ (“free”). Latin ~liber~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~louðeros~ and Proto-Indo-European *~h₁lewdʰero-~, from the root *~h₁lewdʰ-~ (“people, to grow”). The same root produced Greek ~eleutheros~ (“free”) and, through the “people” sense, German ~Leute~ (“people”). The Latin verb itself survives doubly in French: as learned ~libérer~ and as inherited, worn-down ~livrer~.

The semantic drift is remarkable. ~Liberare~ first meant “to free, to release,” then “to release something into someone's hands” — that is, to hand over. From “release into another's keeping” came the everyday sense “to deliver” a parcel or goods, whence the noun ~livraison~ (“delivery”). The darker sense “to betray, to give up to the enemy” is the same gesture turned sinister: to ~livrer~ someone is to hand them over. The reflexive ~se livrer~ means “to give oneself over” to an activity or to surrender. The compound ~délivrer~ keeps the original sense “to set free.” Other descendants of *~h₁lewdʰ-~ include ~libre~, ~liberté~, and ~libéral~.
en.louer:
In the sense “to rent, to lease,” from Old French ~loer~, ~loier~, ~luer~, from Latin ~locāre~ (“to place, to let out, to lease”), a verb built on ~locus~ (“place, spot”). ~locus~ comes from Old Latin ~stlocus~, perhaps from a Proto-Indo-European root *~stel-~ (“to place, to set”), though de Vaan judges this derivation uncertain and leaves the origin open; the same *~stel-~ would link it, through Germanic, to English ~stall~. To ~locāre~ was literally to put something in a place — and so to place it at someone's disposal for a price.

The leasing sense flows directly from “to place”: letting a field, a house, or one's labour is placing it in another's hands for hire. Modern ~louer~ covers both renting from and renting to, and the root ~locus~ runs through a tidy French family — ~loyer~ (“rent payment”), ~location~ (“rental”), ~locataire~ (“tenant”), ~locatif~, and ~allouer~ (“to allocate”). A famous homonym shadows this verb: the other ~louer~, “to praise,” descends instead from Latin ~laudāre~ (“to praise”) by way of Old French ~loer~, and survives in ~louange~ (“praise”) and ~laudatif~. The two ~louer~s fell together in sound and spelling but were never related — one places a thing for hire, the other lifts a person with praise.
en.lutter:
From Old French ~luitier~ (also ~loitier~, ~luiter~; “to wrestle, to struggle”), attested in the Chanson de Roland around 1080, from Latin ~luctārī~ (“to wrestle, to struggle”). The Latin verb goes back to Proto-Italic *~lukto-~ and ultimately to a Proto-Indo-European root *~lewg-~ (“to bend, to twist”) — fittingly, since wrestling is a contest of twisting bodies. Cognate with Italian ~lottare~ and Spanish ~luchar~ (in which Latin ~-ct-~ regularly became ~-ch-~), and, through the prefixed Latin ~reluctārī~ (“to struggle against”), with English ~reluctant~.

The Old French diphthong ~-ui-~ held on for centuries; the reduction to plain ~-u-~ in ~lutter~ is unusual and dates only to the early seventeenth century, paralleling the pairs ~fruit~/~fruité~ and ~nuit~/~nuitée~. The sense has stayed remarkably stable: from the literal grappling of two wrestlers it broadened to any struggle, physical, moral, or political — one may ~lutter~ against an illness, against injustice, or against sleep. Words of the same family in modern French include the noun ~lutte~ (the sport and any struggle), the agent noun ~lutteur~ (“wrestler”), and the adjective ~luttable~. The learned English borrowing ~reluctant~ preserves, etymologically, the image of someone wrestling back against what is asked of them.
en.maintenir:
From Old French ~maintenir~ (“to protect, to defend, to uphold”), from Medieval Latin ~manutenere~, a fusion of the Latin phrase ~manu tenere~ (“to hold in the hand”) — ~manu~, the ablative of ~manus~ (“hand”), plus ~tenere~ (“to hold”). ~manus~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~man-~ (“hand”), while ~tenere~ rests on *~ten-~ (“to stretch”). Through this compound the verb is cognate with Italian ~mantenere~, Spanish ~mantener~, and English ~maintain~.

To maintain was first, literally, to keep a thing in hand — hence to support, protect, or defend, the dominant Old French sense. From “to hold up” grew the modern senses “to keep in a given state,” “to assert (an opinion),” and “to keep up, to service.” The last sense, the upkeep of machines and roads, is reinforced in modern French as a calque on English ~maintain~. From ~manus~ French draws ~main~, ~manuel~, ~manier~, and ~manœuvre~; from ~tenere~ come ~tenir~, ~retenir~, ~contenir~, and ~maintenance~ — so that the simple act of holding something in one's hand stands, etymologically, behind a vast machinery of holding, keeping, and managing.
en.manger:
From Middle French ~manger~, from Old French ~mangier~, from Late Latin ~manducare~ (“to chew, to eat”), a derivative of ~manducus~ (“glutton”), itself from ~mandere~ (“to chew”). ~mandere~ is traced to Proto-Indo-European *~mendʰ-~ (“to chew”). Cognate with Italian ~mangiare~ and Occitan ~manjar~, both Gallo-Romance reflexes, and — through ~mandere~ — with English ~mandible~ and ~mange~.

The choice of word is itself a small comedy. Where Spanish and Portuguese kept a verb for plain eating (~comer~, from ~comedere~), Gaul preferred ~manducare~, a coarse, popular term meaning roughly “to munch, to champ.” Its source noun ~manducus~ named a glutton, and in Roman farce a ~Manducus~ was a grotesque masked figure with great clacking jaws — so French speakers, etymologically, do not dine so much as chomp. The same root *~mendʰ-~, by way of ~mandibula~, gives the anatomical ~mandibule~ (the jaw), while ~manducare~ itself yielded the learned ~manducation~ and the homely ~mangeoire~, a feeding trough — the very word English borrowed as ~manger~. Modern French ~mâcher~ (“to chew”), by contrast, comes from a different Latin verb, ~masticare~.
en.manifester:
From Latin ~manifestāre~ (“to make plain, reveal”), from the adjective ~manifestus~ (“caught in the act, evident, palpable”). That adjective opens with ~manus~ (“hand”), but its second element ~-festus~ is genuinely disputed. One long-standing reading links it to a verb of striking, also seen in ~īnfestus~ (“hostile”) and the family of ~dēfendere~ — so ~manifestus~ would picture a wrongdoer literally ~struck~ or ~seized~ by the hand, caught red-handed. ~Manus~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~manus~ and continues in French ~main~.

The word entered French around 1100. From “caught in the act” the adjective broadened to “plainly visible,” and the verb ~manifester~ to “make evident, show openly.” Its most public modern sense — to ~demonstrate~ in the streets, to ~protest~ — is a nineteenth-century political development: a crowd makes its will manifest by becoming visible. From the same Latin stock French keeps ~manifeste~ (both adjective and the noun ~manifeste~, a public declaration), ~manifestation~, and ~manifestant~. English ~manifest~, ~manifesto~ (through Italian), and ~manifestation~ are cousins, all carrying that ancient image of something laid bare to the hand.
en.manquer:
Unlike most French verbs, ~manquer~ is not inherited but borrowed, from Italian ~mancare~ (“to lack, to be wanting”), itself from the Latin adjective ~mancus~ (“maimed, crippled, defective”). ~mancus~ originally described a hand maimed or missing, and is connected to ~manus~ (“hand”); both are referred to a Proto-Indo-European *~mh₂nkó-~ (“maimed in the hand”), beside ~manus~ from the root for “hand.” The same adjective survives as Italian ~manco~ and Spanish ~manco~ (“one-handed, maimed”).

From “maimed” the Italian word generalized to “to be missing, to fall short,” and French took it over in the sixteenth century, gradually pushing aside the older native ~faillir~. The most surprising development is grammatical rather than semantic: in the sense “to be missed,” ~manquer~ inverts subject and object, so that ~tu me manques~ literally says “you are lacking to me” yet means “I miss you” — the person felt as absent is the grammatical subject, and the verb agrees with them, as in ~mes amis me manquent~ (“I miss my friends”). The same root gave French the noun ~manque~ (“lack”), the verb ~manquement~ (“a failing, a breach”), and ~manchot~ (“one-armed,” also the name for a penguin), all circling the ancient idea of a defective, incomplete hand.
en.marcher:
From Middle French ~marcher~, from Old French ~marchier~ (“to trample, to tread underfoot”), attested around 1170. The most widely accepted source is Frankish *~markôn~ (“to mark, to press with the foot”), reaching French through Old High German ~marcôn~ — a Germanic verb of the Franks rather than a Latin inheritance. A competing hypothesis, going back to Scheler, instead derives it from Latin ~marcus~ (“a hammer”), through the image of feet hammering the ground; the question is genuinely unsettled.

The oldest sense was “to trample, to tread on,” preserved in the related ~marc~ (“the pressed residue of grapes or olives”), the pulp left underfoot. From that came the ordinary “to walk,” then a remarkable spread of figurative uses: an army on the ~marche~, a plan that ~marche~ (“works, goes well”), and the slangy ~ça marche~ (“all right, it's a deal”). One must not confuse this verb with the noun ~marché~ (“a market,” from Latin ~mercatus~), an unrelated homonym. From the verb the language draws ~marche~ (“a step, a march”), ~démarche~ (“a gait; a course of action”), and ~marcheur~ (“a walker”).
en.marier:
Attested from 1145, from Latin ~maritare~ (“to marry, to give in marriage”), derived from ~maritus~ (“husband, married man”). ~Maritus~ is generally connected to ~mas~ (“male”) and perhaps to an Indo-European element meaning “young woman, bride,” though the deeper derivation is debated and best left open. Italian keeps the noun in ~marito~ (“husband”) and the verb ~maritare~, while Spanish has ~maridar~ beside ~marido~; English borrowed French ~marier~ as ~marry~.

In its earliest French use ~marier~ meant “to give someone in marriage,” the active role of the parent or matchmaker; the reflexive ~se marier~ (“to get married”) appears by around 1170. Beyond the wedding, the verb developed a graceful figurative life: one can ~marier~ colors, flavors, or ideas — that is, blend them harmoniously, as in ~ce vin se marie bien avec le poisson~. The same root gives French ~mari~ (“husband”), ~mariage~, ~marié~ and ~mariée~ (“groom” and “bride”), and ~remarier~. It is worth noting that ~marier~ (“to wed”) and ~Marie~ (the given name) are unrelated despite the resemblance — a coincidence that has confused many a learner, but never the etymology.
en.marquer:
French ~marquer~ comes from Old French ~merchier~ (“to make a mark to distinguish something”), an Anglo-Norman and Picard form. Its ancestor is the Germanic noun *~marka~ (“boundary, border, sign set at a limit”), of Frankish origin, from Proto-Indo-European *~merǵ-~ (“boundary, edge”). The modern vowel ~a~ was pulled in by ~marcher~ and by the Italian ~marcare~ and Spanish ~marcar~, themselves from the same Germanic root. Cognate forms abound: English ~mark~, Dutch ~mark~, German ~Mark~, Swedish ~mark~, and even Latin ~margo~ (“edge,” whence French ~marge~).

The oldest sense was territorial: a *~marka~ was a frontier zone, and a ~marquis~ was originally the count of such a borderland, the ~marche~. From “a sign planted at a boundary” the noun ~marque~ generalized to any distinguishing sign, and ~marquer~ to the act of inscribing one — branding cattle, noting down a figure, scoring a goal (this last sense reinforced in the nineteenth century by English ~to mark~). The root spread widely in French: ~marche~ (a border province, as in les Marches), ~marquise~, ~démarquer~, and ~marqueterie~ all belong to it, and through English so do ~demarcation~ and ~marquee~. What began as the line dividing two lands now means simply leaving a trace.
en.maîtriser:
Coined in the late fourteenth century from the noun ~maîtrise~ (“mastery”), itself derived from ~maître~ (“master”). ~maître~ comes from Old French ~maistre~, from Latin ~magister~ (“master, chief, teacher”), which is a comparative formation: ~magis~ (“more”) plus the contrastive suffix ~-ter~, literally “the one who is greater.” ~magis~ rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~meǵ-~ (“great”), the same root behind Latin ~magnus~ (“great”). From ~magister~ also descend Italian ~maestro~, Spanish ~maestro~, German ~Meister~, and, through Norman French, English ~master~ and ~mister~.

The verb means to make oneself master of something: to ~maîtriser~ a language, a fire, one's emotions, an adversary. The chain ~maître~ → ~maîtrise~ → ~maîtriser~ shows French building a verb on a noun on a noun, each step adding the idea of acquired control. The deep root *~meǵ-~ fans out into a huge French family beyond the ~maître~ line: ~majeur~ (“major”), ~maxime~, ~maximum~, ~magnitude~, ~magnat~, ~magnifique~, ~majesté~, and even ~maire~ (“mayor”) — all carrying, like ~maîtriser~ itself, the ancient sense of greatness, of being more. To master is, etymologically, to make oneself the greater party.
en.menacer:
From Vulgar Latin *~mināciāre~, a verb built on Latin ~mināciae~ (“threats”), which displaced Classical Latin ~minārī~ (“to threaten”). ~Minārī~ derives from ~minae~ (“threats”), a noun whose oldest, concrete sense referred to projecting or jutting points — the menacing overhang of a cliff or a battlement. The same Gallo-Romance form gave Italian ~minacciare~, Spanish ~amenazar~, Portuguese ~ameaçar~, and Catalan ~amenaçar~.

The striking detail is the bridge between architecture and intimidation: ~minae~ first named the things that loom and jut forward, and only then the abstract sense of a threat that looms over someone. To threaten was, etymologically, to thrust something forward menacingly. The verb settled into modern French ~menacer~ with senses ranging from open intimidation to the impersonal “to endanger” (a deadline that threatens, a wall that threatens to collapse). From the same Latin root come ~menace~ itself, the adjective ~menaçant~, and — through ~minārī~ in its sense of driving cattle forward with shouted threats — the verbs ~mener~ (“to lead”) and its compounds ~amener~, ~emmener~, and ~promener~, in which the old idea of driving forward survives stripped of any hostility.
en.mener:
From Old French ~mener~ (“to lead, to drive”), from Late Latin ~minare~ (“to drive animals”), from Latin ~minari~ (“to threaten”), derived from ~minae~ (“threats, menaces”). ~Minae~ traces to PIE *~men-~ (“to project, to jut out”) — the same root behind Latin ~mons~ (“mountain,” something jutting up) and ~eminere~ (“to stand out”). A threat, in this image, is something that “looms” over you. Cognate with Spanish ~menear~ and Italian ~menare~.

The semantic journey is vivid: a herdsman drove his beasts forward by shouting threats at them, so ~minari~ (“to threaten”) slid in Late Latin into ~minare~ (“to drive, to lead livestock”), and finally into the neutral French ~mener~ (“to lead, to conduct, to take somewhere”). The threatening sense did not vanish — it survives in the doublet ~menacer~ (“to threaten”) and the noun ~menace~, both from the same ~minae~. From the “jutting” root also come ~mont~, ~montagne~, ~éminent~, and ~proéminent~, while ~mener~ itself heads a busy family: ~amener~, ~emmener~, ~ramener~, ~promener~, and ~démener~.
en.mentionner:
From the noun ~mention~, from Latin ~mentio~ (“a calling to mind, a making mention”), an action noun built on the root of ~mens~, ~mentis~ (“mind”). The verb itself was reinforced by Italian ~menzionare~ before settling into French. ~mens~ descends from PIE *~men-~ (“to think”), one of the great roots of the family. Cognate, through that root, with English ~mention~ and Italian ~menzionare~.

To ~mentionner~ is, at root, to bring a thing “to mind” by naming it aloud — the act of speech and the act of memory folded together. The PIE *~men-~ behind ~mens~ is astonishingly fertile across French and beyond: it gives ~mental~, ~mémoire~, ~commentaire~, ~démence~, ~réminiscence~, and the borrowed ~mantra~, and through other branches reaches English ~mind~ and Greek ~mania~. Even the Latin ~memento~ (“remember!”) and the legal ~compos mentis~ spring from the same source. ~mentionner~ sits at the verbal edge of this vast mental family — where thinking, remembering, and saying meet — and has bred its own small offshoots, ~mentionnable~ and the rather charming negative ~immentionnable~, for the thing one had better not bring to anyone's mind at all.
en.mesurer:
From Late Latin ~mensurare~ (“to measure, to estimate”), derived from ~mensura~ (“a measuring, a measure”), itself from ~mensus~, the past participle of the classical verb ~metiri~ (“to measure”). ~metiri~ traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~meh₁-~ (“to measure”). That same root, through the noun *~mḗh₁n̥s~ (“moon, month” — the moon being an early measure of time), connects ~mesurer~ to English ~moon~, ~month~, and ~Monday~, and to the Greek-derived ~meter~. Cognate with Italian ~misurare~; Spanish and Portuguese instead kept the old ~metiri~ in their ~medir~.

Late Latin ~mensurare~ gradually displaced classical ~metiri~ from the fourth century on, and it is the ancestor of the ordinary measuring verbs of Gallo-Romance. From the literal act of measuring, French ~mesurer~ extended to weighing or judging (~mesurer ses mots~, to weigh one's words) and, reflexively, ~se mesurer à quelqu'un~, to pit oneself against someone. The root family is wide in French: ~mesure~, ~démesure~ (excess, the lack of measure), ~commensurable~, and the learned ~dimension~. Through the moon branch of *~meh₁-~ come ~mois~ (“month”) and the scientific ~-mètre~ of ~kilomètre~ and ~thermomètre~ — all of them, in the end, ways of taking the measure of the world.
en.mettre:
From Old French ~metre~, from Latin ~mittere~ (“to send, to let go, to release”). The Latin verb likely goes back to Proto-Italic *~meitō~ and to a Proto-Indo-European root *~meyth₂-~ (“to exchange, to remove”), an extension of *~mey-~ (“to change”) — though the deepest reconstruction is debated, and accuracy here counsels caution. Through ~mittere~, ~mettre~ is cognate with Italian ~mettere~ and Spanish ~meter~, and with the English borrowings ~mission~ and ~transmit~.

The surprise is the meaning. Classical ~mittere~ meant “to send,” yet modern French ~mettre~ means “to put.” The shift from sending something away to setting it down took place in Vulgar Latin, where “let go” drifted toward “place,” and French carried the new sense forward completely. The original idea of sending survives instead in the prefixed forms, which French rebuilt on the learned stem ~-mettre~: ~permettre~ (“to permit,” “to let through”), ~promettre~ (“to send forth, to promise”), ~transmettre~ (“to transmit”), ~soumettre~ (“to submit”), and ~émettre~ (“to emit”). The supine stem ~miss-~ yields the noun family ~mission~, ~message~, ~messe~ (“Mass,” from the dismissal ~ite, missa est~), and ~mise~ (“a placing, a stake”) — the latter showing the “put” sense, the former the older “send.”
en.mobiliser:
French ~mobiliser~ is a young verb, formed in 1765 from the adjective ~mobile~ plus the suffix ~-iser~ (“to render mobile”). ~Mobile~ continues Latin ~mobilis~ (“movable”), itself a worn-down form of an earlier *~movibilis~, built on the verb ~movēre~ (“to move”) with the suffix ~-bilis~. ~Movēre~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~mewh₁-~ (“to move, push away”). Through ~movēre~ and ~mobilis~, French is kin to Italian ~mobile~, Spanish ~móvil~, and English ~move~, ~mobile~, and ~motion~.

The word was born military: to ~mobiliser~ was to put an army on the move, to call reserves from civilian rest into active war — the opposite of ~démobiliser~. From the parade-ground it spread to any rousing of dormant resources: one ~mobilise~ public opinion, funds, energy, or a team. French even reborrowed ~mobile~ as the noun for a hanging sculpture and, lately, for the ~téléphone mobile~. The same restless root *~mewh₁-~ moves through a crowd of French words: ~mouvoir~ and ~mouvement~ (the inherited line), ~émouvoir~ and ~émotion~ (“to move out,” to stir feeling), ~automobile~, ~immobile~, ~meuble~ (“furniture,” the “movable” goods of a household), and ~mobilier~ — all, like ~mobiliser~, refusing to stand still.
en.modifier:
Attested in 1355, from Latin ~modificāre~ (“to set a measure to, to regulate, to limit”), a compound of ~modus~ (“measure, manner, limit”) and the verb-forming element ~-ficāre~, from ~facere~ (“to make, to do”). Latin ~modus~ is generally connected to the Proto-Indo-European root *~med-~ (“to measure, to take appropriate measures”). Through ~modificāre~, French ~modifier~ is matched by Italian ~modificare~ and Spanish ~modificar~, and the underlying ~modus~ surfaces in English borrowings such as ~mode~, ~model~, and ~modify~.

The original idea was not change but limit: to ~modificāre~ was to fix the measure or bounds of something, to keep it within proper proportion. The modern sense of altering grew naturally from this — to set new measures is, in effect, to change. ~Modus~ proved astonishingly fertile in French, branching into ~mode~ (both “fashion” and “grammatical mood”), ~modèle~, ~moule~ (“mould,” the inherited popular form of ~modulus~), ~module~, ~modeste~, ~modéré~, ~commode~, and ~accommoder~ — a whole family circling the idea of fitting a thing to its proper measure.
en.monter:
From Vulgar Latin *~montāre~ (“to go up, to climb”), a verb coined from the Latin noun ~mons~ (genitive ~montis~, “mountain, mount”). ~Mons~ traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~men-~ (“to project, jut out”) — a mountain being, precisely, the land that sticks up. Through that root it is cognate with Latin ~ēminēre~ (“to stand out”) and ~prōminēre~ (“to project”). Romance kin include Italian ~montare~ and Spanish ~montar~, and the borrowed English ~mount~.

The coinage is delightfully literal — to “mountain” one’s way along, that is, to go up. From physical ascent the verb climbed into a remarkable range of senses: prices ~montent~ (rise), one ~monte~ a horse, a stage, or a flight of stairs, and one ~monte~ a machine, a tent, or a play by assembling its parts. The same ~mons~ underlies ~mont~, ~montagne~, ~monticule~, and ~amont~ (“upstream”), while compounds give ~remonter~, ~surmonter~ (“to overcome,” to rise above an obstacle), ~démonter~, and ~monture~ (a mount or a frame). The wider PIE root *~men-~ resurfaces in the borrowed ~éminent~ and ~proéminent~, words for things that, like mountains, stand out from their surroundings.
en.montrer:
From Middle French ~monstrer~, from Old French ~mostrer~ (an early past participle ~mostret~ is attested around 936), from Latin ~mōnstrāre~ (“to show, point out, indicate”). The verb derives from the noun ~mōnstrum~ (“a divine sign, portent, omen”), itself built on ~monēre~ (“to warn, advise, remind”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~men-~ (“to think”). Cognate with Italian ~mostrare~, Spanish ~mostrar~, and Portuguese ~mostrar~, all of which kept the plain sense “to show.” English borrowed from the same family in ~demonstrate~ and ~monster~.

The semantic thread is striking: a ~mōnstrum~ was something the gods caused to appear in order to *show* humans a warning — a portent. From that idea of an alarming sign came the English ~monster~, while French held instead to the neutral act of pointing something out. The link back to ~monēre~ (“to warn”) ties showing to thinking and remembering: to show is to make someone notice. From the same root *~men-~ spread a wide family of French words, among them ~montre~ (“watch,” literally a device that *shows* the time), ~démontrer~, ~remontrer~, ~monument~, and — through ~monēre~ — ~admonester~.
en.mourir:
From Old French ~mourir~, ~morir~, from Vulgar Latin *~morīre~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~morī~ (“to die”), a deponent verb. This goes back to Proto-Italic *~morjōr~ and to the Proto-Indo-European root *~mer-~ (“to die”). The root is one of the most widespread in the family: cognates include Sanskrit ~mṛtyú~ (“death”), Lithuanian ~mirti~, Proto-Slavic *~merti~, and, through Proto-Germanic *~murþaz~, words for “murder.” The Romance sisters ~morire~ (Italian) and ~morir~ (Spanish) descend from the same Vulgar Latin verb.

The classical verb ~morī~ was deponent — passive in form, active in meaning — and Vulgar Latin regularized it into an ordinary active *~morīre~, the ancestor of every Romance form. From the same root Latin built the noun ~mors~ (“death”) and the participle ~mortuus~ (“dead”), which together seeded a large French family: ~mort~, ~mortel~, ~mortalité~, ~immortel~, ~mortuaire~, ~morgue~, and the borrowed ~moribond~ (“dying”) from ~moribundus~. A curious offshoot is ~amortir~ (“to deaden, to cushion, to amortize”), literally “to bring toward death,” whence the financial ~amortissement~ — a debt slowly “put to death” by repayment.
en.multiplier:
From Old French ~multiplier~, from Latin ~multiplicāre~ (“to multiply, to increase, to repeat”), formed from ~multi-~ (“many,” from ~multus~) and ~plicāre~ (“to fold”). ~plicāre~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~plek-~ (“to plait, to fold”), which also gave Greek ~plékein~ (“to braid”) and, through Germanic, English ~fold~ and ~ply~. So to multiply is, at root, to “many-fold” — to lay something over itself again and again.

The folding image is wonderfully concrete: a cloth doubled and redoubled grows in layers exactly as a number grows under multiplication. Latin ~multiplicāre~ served both the arithmetical sense and the broader “to increase in number,” and modern ~multiplier~ keeps both, from the times table to multiplying one's efforts. The *~plek-~ root unfolds across a large French family built on ~plicāre~ and its noun ~plicātūra~: ~plier~ and ~ployer~ (“to fold, to bend”), ~pli~ (“a fold, a crease”), ~plisser~ (“to pleat”), ~appliquer~, ~compliquer~, ~impliquer~, ~expliquer~, ~répliquer~, and ~déployer~ (“to deploy,” literally to “unfold”). The learned ~-plicāre~ verbs and the popular ~plier~ are two streams from the same source, so the schoolchild's ~multiplication~ and the seamstress's ~pli~ share one ancient act of folding.
en.mélanger:
A denominal verb from the noun ~mélange~ (“mixture”), which is itself derived from ~mêler~ (“to mix, to mingle”). ~mêler~ comes from Vulgar Latin *~misculāre~, a frequentative of Classical Latin ~miscēre~ (“to mix, to mingle, to stir up”). Cognate, through *~misculāre~, with Spanish ~mezclar~, Occitan ~mesclar~, and Italian ~mescolare~; through ~miscēre~, with English ~mix~ and ~miscellaneous~.

The chain is tidy: ~miscēre~ gave the frequentative *~misculāre~, which became Old French ~mesler~ and then ~mêler~; from ~mêler~ French formed the noun ~mélange~, and from that noun, in turn, the verb ~mélanger~. So ~mélanger~ is etymologically a verb made from a noun made from a verb. French usage keeps a delicate distinction: ~mêler~ tends toward intermingling or muddling things together, while ~mélanger~ leans toward deliberately combining them into a blend. The Latin root ~miscēre~ left a wide trail, including ~mixte~ (“mixed”), ~mixture~, ~miscible~ (“mixable”), ~promiscuité~ (“promiscuity”), ~méli-mélo~ (“jumble”), and the learned ~immiscer~ (“to interfere,” literally to mix oneself in).
en.mériter:
A denominal verb built on the noun ~mérite~, itself from Latin ~meritum~ (“that which is earned, reward, desert”), the past participle of the verb ~merere~ / ~mereor~ (“to earn, to deserve, to serve for pay”). That verb descends through Proto-Italic from the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)mer-~ (“to assign, to allot”), which also gave Ancient Greek ~méros~ (“share, portion”) and ~meíromai~ (“to receive as one's due”). To deserve, in this light, is to receive the portion allotted to you.

The Latin sense sat squarely in the world of wages and military service — a soldier ~merere~ his pay, literally earning it by serving. French narrowed and abstracted this into the modern moral sense: to ~mériter~ is to be worthy of a reward or, increasingly, of anything at all, good or bad — one can ~mériter~ a prize or a punishment. The root remains visible in a tight French cluster: ~mérite~, ~méritoire~, ~méritant~, ~démériter~, and ~émérite~ (“emeritus,” one whose long service has earned honorable retirement). The Latin verb's military flavor also survives, distantly, in the English borrowing ~merit~ and in ~mercenary~, a soldier who serves purely for what he earns.
en.naître:
From Middle French ~naistre~, from Old French ~naistre~, ~nestre~, from Vulgar Latin ~*nascere~ (“to be born,” and figuratively “to originate, to arise”), a remodeling of the Classical Latin deponent ~nāscī~ (first-person ~nāscor~), earlier ~gnāscī~ (~gnāscor~), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵenh₁-~ (“to beget, give birth, produce”). That root is one of the most fertile in Indo-European, cognate with Greek ~gígnomai~ (“to be born, become”), English ~kin~ and ~kind~, and visible in Latin ~genus~ and ~gignere~ (“to beget”).

Classical Latin had a *deponent* verb — passive in form but active in meaning — which Vulgar Latin reshaped into an ordinary active infinitive ~*nascere~, the ancestor of the modern French verb. The figurative sense was present early: rivers, ideas, and disputes could all “be born,” and French keeps this fully, speaking of a doubt or a friendship that ~naît~. From the same root *~ǵenh₁-~ French draws a vast harvest: ~naissance~, ~né~, ~nature~, ~natif~, ~nation~, ~genre~, ~gène~, ~générer~, and ~ingénu~ (literally “native-born, freeborn”).
en.nommer:
From Old French ~nomer~, ~nommer~ (attested in the 10th century), from Latin ~nōmināre~ (“to name, to call by name, to appoint”), derived from ~nōmen~ (“name”). Latin ~nōmen~ descends through Proto-Italic *~nōmn̥~ from the Proto-Indo-European *~h₁nómn̥~ (“name”), one of the most widely preserved words in the whole family. Its cognates ring out across the languages: Ancient Greek ~ὄνομα~ (~ónoma~), Sanskrit ~nā́man~, German ~Name~, and English ~name~ all reflect the same ancient root, as do Italian ~nominare~ and Spanish ~nombrar~ for the verb.

Latin ~nōmināre~ carried from the start the double duty the French verb still bears: simply “to call by a name” and, more formally, “to designate someone for an office.” That second sense gives modern ~nommer~ its use in appointments — to ~nommer~ a minister or a director is to name them to the post. English borrowed the same Latin verb twice over, as ~nominate~ and, through French, as ~name~ in spirit.
