From ~nōmen~ French also inherited ~nom~, ~nominer~, ~nomination~, ~nominal~, ~pronom~, ~renom~, and ~surnom~ — a whole constellation turning on the act of naming.
en.noter:
From Latin ~notare~ (“to mark, to write down, to indicate”), a denominal verb formed on the noun ~nota~ (“a mark, sign, written note”). Tradition long linked ~nota~ to the verb ~noscere~ (“to know”), making it “a mark by which something is known” and connecting it to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵneh₃-~ (“to know”); modern scholars such as de Vaan judge this uncertain, and the deeper origin of ~nota~ is best treated as unresolved. Related forms include Italian ~notare~ and Spanish ~notar~, and English borrowed both ~note~ and ~notation~.

The meanings of the French verb all radiate from making a mark. To ~noter~ is to jot something down, to notice or remark a fact, to mark a musical pitch with notes — and, distinctively in French schools, to grade: a teacher ~note~ a pupil's work by assigning it a mark out of twenty. The same ~nota~ survives whole in the scholarly tag ~nota bene~ (“note well”), abbreviated ~N.B.~ The wider family in French includes ~note~ (“note, grade, bill”), ~notable~ (“noteworthy”), ~notaire~ (“notary,” one who records), ~notice~ (“an explanatory note”), and ~annoter~ (“to annotate”); through Latin, English ~notable~, ~notary~, and ~notorious~ share the root.
en.nourrir:
From Latin ~nūtrīre~ (“to suckle, to nurse, to feed”), which evolved into French around the year 1000 with the assimilation of the cluster ~-tr-~ into ~-rr-~ — giving the doubled ~r~ that the modern spelling faithfully preserves. ~Nūtrīre~ is reconstructed from a Proto-Indo-European form *~(s)neu-~ (“to suckle, to flow”), possibly tied to the root *~sneh₂-~ (“to flow”), the same watery idea behind mother's milk. It is cognate with the Romance family of Italian ~nutrire~ and Spanish ~nutrir~, and, through Old French ~norrir~, with English ~nourish~, ~nurse~, and ~nutrition~.

The original sense was intimate — a mother or wet-nurse (Latin ~nūtrīx~) giving the breast. From there it widened to “to feed” in general, then to “to raise, to bring up” a child, and finally to a host of figurative uses: one ~nourrit~ a hope, a grudge, an illusion, a conversation, feeding it so it grows. The metaphor of feeding-as-sustaining runs deep in French. From the same root descend ~nourriture~ (“food”), ~nourrisson~ (“infant, suckling”), ~nourrice~ (“wet-nurse”), and, through the learned Latin doublet, ~nutrition~ and ~nutriment~.
en.nécessiter:
From Medieval Latin ~necessitare~ (“to constrain, to compel”), a verb built on Latin ~necessitas~ (“necessity, inevitability”), itself from ~necesse~ (“unavoidable, needful”). ~Necesse~ is usually analysed as ~ne-~ (“not”) + a form related to ~cedere~ / ~cessus~ (“to yield, to give way”) — literally “that from which one cannot withdraw,” that which cannot be escaped. The French verb is attested from the fourteenth century and has Romance siblings: Italian ~necessitare~, Spanish ~necesitar~, and Portuguese ~necessitar~, all expressing need or compulsion.

The meaning has stayed remarkably close to its Latin core of inescapability. To ~nécessiter~ something is to render it unavoidable — to require it as a consequence one cannot dodge. There is a faint logic embedded in the word: if ~necesse~ is what one cannot yield from, then to necessitate is to box a situation in until only one outcome remains. From the same family French keeps ~nécessité~, ~nécessaire~, and the adverb ~nécessairement~, while the underlying ~cedere~ also gave French ~céder~, ~cesser~, and ~succès~ — a reminder that the very idea of necessity is built, etymologically, on the refusal to give ground.
en.obliger:
From Old French ~obligier~, from Latin ~obligāre~ (“to bind, to tie up, to bind by obligation”), a compound of the prefix ~ob-~ (“to, toward, against”) and ~ligāre~ (“to bind, to tie”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~leyǵ-~ (“to bind”). The same root, through ~ligāre~, gives a startling spread of words: Italian ~obbligare~, Spanish ~obligar~, and in English ~ligament~, ~ligature~, ~liable~, ~ally~, ~rely~, and ~league~.

The metaphor is physical: an obligation is a ~bond~ presented symmetrically — French ~lien~, English ~bond~ — a tie that binds a person to a duty, exactly as a rope binds a thing. Latin ~obligāre~ could even be used of binding a wound or of making a liquid set, but the figurative sense of binding by law, promise, or gratitude prevailed. French keeps two faces of this: ~obliger~ can compel (~la loi m'oblige~, the law requires me to) or, more gently, do a favour that places someone under a pleasant debt — hence ~obligé~ as “much obliged” and ~obligeant~ as “obliging, helpful.” The same ~ligāre~ underlies the everyday ~lier~ (“to bind, to link”), ~allier~ (“to ally”), ~relier~ (“to connect, to bind a book”), and the legal ~obligation~. Even ~religion~ may belong here, if it is, as some held, a “binding back” to the divine.
en.observer:
From Latin ~observare~ (“to watch over, attend to, guard, comply with”), a compound of the prefix ~ob-~ (“toward, before”) and ~servare~ (“to keep, guard, watch over, preserve”). The Latin verb was borrowed into French as a learned word as early as the tenth century. ~servare~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~ser-~ (“to protect, watch over”), so that to observe is, at root, to keep watch toward something. The same Latin verb gives Italian ~osservare~, Spanish ~observar~, and, through Old French, English ~observe~.

The meaning fans out neatly from “keeping one’s gaze on”: one ~observes~ the stars (watches), ~observes~ a custom (keeps, complies with), and ~observes~ that it is raining (notices and remarks). The protective sense of ~servare~ survives more plainly in its many French descendants: ~conserver~ (“to keep”), ~réserver~ (“to set aside”), ~préserver~ (“to shield”), and the noun ~observatoire~. Curiously, the same root *~ser-~ that meant “to guard” also stands behind Latin ~servus~ (“slave, servant”) — probably first a “guardian” or “herdsman” before the sense darkened — and thus, distantly, behind French ~serf~ and ~servir~. The watcher and the servant share an ancestor.
en.obtenir:
French ~obtenir~ comes from Latin ~obtinēre~ (“to hold firmly, to possess fully, to maintain”), a compound of the prefix ~ob-~ (“against, toward”) and the verb ~tenēre~ (“to hold”). Rather than evolving by regular sound change, it was refashioned in French on the model of the inherited verb ~tenir~ (“to hold”), so that its endings march in step with its everyday relative. The root ~tenēre~ descends from Proto-Italic *~tenēō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch, draw”), the link between stretching and holding being the idea of keeping something taut. That root is cognate with Ancient Greek ~teínō~ (“I stretch”) and Sanskrit ~tanóti~ (“stretches”).

The sense narrowed from the Latin “hold fast, keep possession of” to the modern French “to get, to come to have,” the result of an effort that secures and keeps something. The prefix ~ob-~ adds the nuance of reaching toward a goal — one obtains by holding on against resistance. French is unusually rich in cousins built on ~tenir~: ~contenir~ (“contain,” to hold together), ~maintenir~ (“maintain,” to hold by hand), ~retenir~ (“retain”), ~soutenir~ (“sustain”), ~détenir~ (“detain”), and ~appartenir~ (“belong”). Even the adverb ~maintenant~ (“now”) hides the root, from Latin ~manū tenendō~ (“holding in the hand”).
en.occuper:
From Latin ~occupāre~ (“to seize, take possession of, take before others”), an intensive compound of ~ob-~ (“toward, over”) and ~capere~ (“to grasp, seize, take”). ~Capere~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~kapjō~ to the Proto-Indo-European root *~kap-~ (“to grasp”), which — perhaps unexpectedly — also gave English ~have~ and ~heave~, and underlies Latin ~captīvus~ and ~capax~. Romance kin include Italian ~occupare~ and Spanish ~ocupar~.

The Latin verb already carried a competitive edge: to ~occupāre~ was to grab something *before someone else could*, to get there first. That sense of prior seizure survives in the military meaning — an army that ~occupe~ a territory has taken and now holds it. But French let the word soften and spread. Today one can ~occuper~ a seat, ~occuper~ a job, or ~occuper~ an afternoon, and the reflexive ~s'occuper de~ means simply “to busy oneself with, to take care of.” The same ~capere~ seeded an enormous family of French words through its many prefixed forms: ~capter~, ~accepter~, ~concevoir~, ~recevoir~, ~capable~, ~captif~, and the noun ~préoccupation~ — an anxiety that has, quite literally, seized the mind in advance.
en.offrir:
From Old French ~ofrir~, ~offrir~ (“to offer, present”), from Late Latin ~offerīre~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~offerre~ (“to bring before, present, offer”), itself ~ob-~ (“before, toward”) + ~ferre~ (“to carry, bear”), from Proto-Italic *~ferō~, from PIE *~bʰer-~ (“to carry, to bear”). Cognate with Italian ~offrire~, Spanish ~ofrecer~, and Catalan ~oferir~; through the same root *~bʰer-~ it is kin to English ~bear~ and ~birth~, and to German ~gebären~ (“to give birth”).

The Latin sense was concretely physical — to carry something forward and set it before another — and the verb early took on a religious coloring, the ~oblātiō~ laid before a god, so that to offer was to present a gift or sacrifice. French kept both the everyday sense of giving a present and the ceremonial one. The root *~bʰer-~ is among the most fertile in Indo-European, and through ~ferre~ it seeded a vast French family: ~offrande~ and ~offre~, but also ~conférer~, ~transférer~, ~souffrir~ (literally “to bear from below”), and the nouns in ~-fère~ such as ~mammifère~ (bearer of breasts). Even ~fertile~ — that which bears — belongs to the same stock.
en.opposer:
From Latin ~opponere~ (“to set against, to place opposite, to oppose”), refashioned in French after the verb ~poser~. ~opponere~ is a compound of the prefix ~ob-~ (“against, toward”) and ~ponere~ (“to put, to place”), whose past participle ~positus~ supplies the form ~oppositus~. The origin of ~ponere~ is debated — traditionally read as ~po-~ + ~sinere~ (“to set down, to let lie”) — so the deep root is best left open. Through ~opponere~ the verb is cognate with Italian ~opporre~, Spanish ~oponer~, and English ~oppose~ and ~opponent~.

The image inside ~opponere~ is spatial: to set one thing facing another, hence to set them against each other. French ~opposer~ kept this — to place in contrast, to set in conflict, to raise an objection. A neat detail is that French rebuilt the inherited verb on native ~poser~ rather than carrying the Latin stem intact, a common Old French habit that also reshaped ~composer~, ~déposer~, and ~exposer~. From the ~ob-~ + ~ponere~ family French keeps ~opposé~, ~opposition~, ~opposant~, and ~opposable~ (as in the opposable thumb) — a small constellation of confrontation all built on the simple idea of “placing toward.”
en.opter:
Borrowed from Latin ~optare~ (“to choose, to wish for”). ~Optare~ is a frequentative built on a lost verb *~opere~, surviving chiefly in its derivatives, and is connected to ~optio~ (“choice, free choice”) and to ~opinor~ (“to weigh, to suppose”). The same circle of ideas — choosing, seeing, preferring — links it loosely to Ancient Greek ~ὄψομαι~ (“I shall see”), so that to opt was, at root, to fix one's gaze on what one wants.

Latin ~optare~ meant both to wish and to choose, and that double sense survives faintly in French ~opter~, which has narrowed to the act of choosing between alternatives: ~opter pour~ (“to opt for, to plump for”) one option over another. It is a measured, almost administrative word — one ~opte~ for a tax regime or a nationality. The prefixed Latin ~adoptare~ (“to choose to oneself”) gave French ~adopter~, to take a person or an idea as one's own. The ~optare~ family runs through modern French in ~option~, ~optionnel~, ~optant~, ~adopter~, and ~cooptation~ — every one of them turning on the act of deliberate choice.
en.optimiser:
A learned modern coinage. French ~optimiser~ (attested 1906) was modeled on English ~optimize~ (1844, “to make as good as possible”), itself built on Latin ~optimum~, the neuter of ~optimus~ (“best”), the irregular superlative of ~bonus~ (“good”). Latin ~optimus~ is generally connected to ~ops~ / ~opes~ (“wealth, resources, abundance”) — the “best” being, originally, the richest or most resourceful — and so to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₃ep-~ (“to work, to produce in abundance”), which also gives ~opus~ (“work”) and ~copia~ (“plenty”).

The word’s lineage is more philosophical than most: ~optimum~ entered learned use through Leibniz, who argued that ours is the “best of all possible worlds,” le meilleur des mondes possibles — the seed of ~optimisme~ (1737) and ~optimiste~. From the bare idea of the best, the verb ~optimiser~ generalized to “to make as good as possible,” and has since bred a small family of its own — ~optimisation~, ~optimiseur~, ~suroptimiser~ (“to over-optimize”), and the parallel ~optimaliser~ formed on ~optimal~. Through ~ops~ the same root also stands behind ~copieux~, ~opulent~, and ~œuvre~.
en.opérer:
A learned borrowing from Latin ~operārī~ (“to work, to labor, to be busy”), itself from the noun ~opus~ (“work, deed, accomplishment”). Latin ~opus~ goes back to a Proto-Indo-European root *~h₃ep-~ (“to work, to produce in abundance, ability”). It is the scholarly twin of the inherited French ~ouvrer~ and ~œuvrer~, which came down by oral transmission from the same ~operārī~ — a doublet, the bookish form beside the homegrown one. Italian ~operare~ and Spanish ~obrar~ are cognates; English ~operate~ and ~opera~ share the root.

From the plain sense “to work, to act,” ~opérer~ spread to specialized fields: a surgeon ~opère~ on a patient, a process ~opère~ a transformation, a change ~s'opère~ (“takes place”). The same ~opus~ family is everywhere in French: the inherited ~œuvre~ (“work, body of work”) and ~ouvrier~ (“worker”) on the popular side, and on the learned side ~opération~, ~opérateur~, ~opératoire~, ~coopérer~, and the borrowed Italian ~opéra~ (literally “a work”). Even ~opulence~ belongs here, through Latin ~ops~ (“wealth, resources”), kin to ~opus~: at the deepest level, work, abundance, and riches spring from one Indo-European idea of productive power.
en.ordonner:
From Latin ~ordināre~ (“to set in order, to arrange, to dispose in rows”), from ~ordō~ (“row, rank, order”). In Old French ~ordināre~ first gave ~ordiner~ and ~ordener~, which by around 1200 had become ~ordonner~, probably reshaped under the influence of ~donner~ (“to give”), as in “donner des ordres.” ~Ordō~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~ordō~ and is plausibly tied to the PIE root *~h₂er-~ (“to fit together”), the same family as Latin ~artus~ (“joint”).

~Ordō~ first named a *row of threads* on a weaver's loom, so the abstract idea of “order” is woven, literally, from textile work. This double parentage — Latin order plus French ~donner~ — gave the verb its split modern range: to arrange or sort out things, and to command or prescribe (a doctor who ~ordonne~ a remedy issues an ~ordonnance~). From ~ordō~ / ~ordināre~ French keeps a broad family: ~ordre~, ~ordinaire~, ~ordonnance~, ~ordinateur~ (the “orderer” that became the computer), ~coordonner~, ~subordonner~, and ~désordre~.
en.organiser:
A sixteenth-century verb formed from the noun ~organe~ plus the suffix ~-iser~, but resting on a much older base: Medieval Latin ~organizare~ meant “to play a musical instrument,” whence the fourteenth-century Middle French ~organiser~, “to play the organ.” The noun ~organe~ goes back to Latin ~organum~, borrowed from Ancient Greek ~ὄργανον~ (~órganon~), “tool, instrument, organ of the body,” itself derived from ~ἔργον~ (~érgon~), “work,” from the Proto-Indo-European root *~werǵ-~ (“to do, work”). Through that root it is cognate with English ~work~ and with the Greek-derived ~energy~.

The semantic journey is striking: from a bodily ~organe~ — a part that does its appointed work — to the idea of furnishing a living being with coordinated working parts, and finally to arranging any set of elements into a functioning whole. By the seventeenth century ~organiser~ had shed its musical and purely biological senses for the general meaning “to structure, set in order,” and the modern sense of planning an event followed naturally. The same root and family yield ~organisme~, ~organisation~, ~organe~ itself, and indirectly ~énergie~. To organize, etymologically, is to give something organs — to make it work like a body.
en.orienter:
A denominal verb (around 1485) built on the noun ~orient~ (“the East”), from Latin ~oriens~, the present participle of ~orīrī~ (“to rise, to arise”) — literally “the rising,” the quarter where the sun comes up. ~Orīrī~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~orjōr~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₃er-~ (“to stir, to rise”), shared with Ancient Greek ~ὄρνυμι~ (~órnūmi~, “to rouse, set in motion”) and Sanskrit ~ऋणोति~ (~ṛṇóti~).

The word's history is literally a matter of which way you turn. To ~orienter~ first meant to set a building — above all a church — facing ~east~, toward the rising sun and Jerusalem. From that act of physical alignment grew the figurative senses: to position something by the compass, then to ~guide~ a person, to steer choices and careers (~orientation~ scolaire). To lose that alignment is to ~désorienter~, “to disorient.” The same Latin verb yielded ~origine~ (a “rising,” a beginning), ~oriental~, and ~orientation~; English ~orient~, ~origin~, ~original~, and ~abort~ (etymologically “to fail to rise”) all spring from the same idea of rising into being.
en.oser:
From Late Latin ~ausāre~, a verb built on ~ausus~, the past participle of Classical Latin ~audēre~ (“to dare, to be bold”). The classical verb was itself a stative formed on the adjective ~avidus~ (“eager, greedy”), so that to dare was, at root, to be hungry for something. The reconstructed deeper source is a Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂weh₁-~ (“to be eager, to desire”). Italian ~osare~, Spanish ~osar~, and Portuguese ~ousar~ are direct Romance cognates.

Late Latin reshaped the irregular ~audēre~ into the regular ~ausāre~, and from this came Old French ~oser~, unchanged in form to this day. The meaning has held firm: ~oser~ is to venture, to have the audacity to act or speak. Because the classical adjective family survived alongside it through learned borrowing, French keeps a striking split: the everyday ~oser~ next to the bookish ~audace~ (“audacity”), ~audacieux~ (“audacious”), and ~avide~ (“eager, avid”) — all ultimately the same Latin nest. Modern French derivatives of ~oser~ include the adjective ~osé~ (“daring, risqué”), the agent noun ~oseur~, and the rare ~réoser~ (“to dare again”). English ~audacious~ reaches the same root by the learned path, through ~audāx~.
en.oublier:
From Old French ~oblier~, ~oublier~ (attested early as ~oblider~, around 980), from Vulgar Latin *~oblītāre~, a verb built in Gaul and Iberia on ~oblītus~, the past participle of Latin ~oblīvīscī~ (“to forget”). Classical Latin had no simple successor that survived into Romance, so the Romance languages reached for the participle ~oblītus~ and forged a fresh verb upon it — a common workaround. The same *~oblītāre~ gives Italian ~obliare~ and the poetic ~oblio~, Spanish ~olvidar~, and Portuguese ~olvidar~, and the learned doublet ~oubli~ / English ~oblivion~ preserves the Latin form more visibly.

The Latin ~oblīvīscī~ is often connected to ~lēvis~ (“smooth”), as though to forget were to let a memory be rubbed smooth, wiped from the surface of the mind — a vivid image whether or not the link is certain. Old French ~oblier~ softened its first vowel under the influence of rounding to become ~oublier~, the modern shape. Alongside the everyday verb the language kept the more literary ~oubli~ (“forgetfulness, oblivion”) and the learned borrowing ~oblitérer~ (“to obliterate, to cancel”), which returns directly to the same Latin root for its sense of effacement.
en.ouvrer:
From Latin ~operārī~ (“to work, labor, be busy”), a verb built on ~opus~, genitive ~operis~ (“work, deed, labor”). The Latin noun descends from Proto-Italic *~opos~ and Proto-Indo-European *~h₃ep-os~ (“work”), from the verbal root *~h₃ep-~ (“to work, produce”), cognate with Sanskrit ~ápas~ (“work”) and underlying English ~opulent~ and ~opus~. The inherited French verb ~ouvrer~ thus means simply “to work,” and it is the popular doublet of the learned borrowing ~opérer~ (“to operate”).

~ouvrer~ was once an everyday verb but has largely faded, partly because its conjugated forms collided with those of ~ouvrir~ (“to open”); to avoid the confusion it was often refashioned as ~œuvrer~ (“to work, labor at”). The root nonetheless seeded a large French family: the agent noun ~ouvrier~ (“worker”), the adjective ~ouvrable~ (as in ~jour ouvrable~, “working day”), ~œuvre~ (“work, creation”), ~chef-d'œuvre~ (“masterpiece”), and ~main-d'œuvre~ (“labor, workforce”). Through the learned branch come ~opérer~, ~opération~, and ~coopérer~. A single PIE notion of “doing work” thus spans the laborer’s working day and the artist’s finished masterpiece.
en.ouvrir:
French ~ouvrir~ (attested near 980 in the form ~obrirent~, “they opened”) comes from Latin ~aperīre~ (“to open”), by way of a Vulgar Latin *~operīre~. The vowel change from ~a-~ to ~o-~ was no accident: spoken Latin remodeled ~aperīre~ on its antonym ~cooperīre~ (“to cover up,” source of French ~couvrir~), so that the verb for opening and the verb for covering came to rhyme. Latin ~aperīre~ itself is generally traced to a prefix *~ap-~ (“off, away”) plus a root meaning “to cover,” the whole literally “to un-cover.” Through the Romance line it is cognate with Italian ~aprire~ and Spanish ~abrir~, the everyday words for “to open.”

The paired remodeling of ~ouvrir~ and ~couvrir~ is a tidy case of two opposites pulled into the same shape by their very oppositeness — to this day they conjugate alike. The basic sense “to open” has stayed remarkably stable across two millennia, branching into figurative uses (to open a debate, a heart, a road). From ~ouvrir~ French derives ~ouverture~ (“opening, overture”), ~ouvert~ (“open”), and ~ouvre-boîte~ (“can-opener”); the learned Latin stem reappears in ~apéritif~ (a drink that “opens” the appetite), while ~apertura~ lives on in the technical ~aperture~ shared with English.
en.ouïr:
From Middle French ~ouïr~, from Old French ~oïr~, “to hear,” from Latin ~audīre~ (“to hear, to listen”), the present infinitive of ~audio~, from PIE *~au-~ (“to perceive”). Cognate with Italian ~udire~ and Spanish ~oír~, both of which remain the everyday verbs for hearing in their languages — unlike their French sibling, which lost that office. In French the ground was taken over by ~entendre~ (originally “to direct one's attention toward”), which slid from “to attend to” into plain “to hear.”

As ~entendre~ expanded, ~ouïr~ contracted into a defective relic, fossilized in a handful of set phrases: ~ouï-dire~ (“hearsay,” literally “heard-say”), the herald's archaic cry ~oyez~ (“hear ye!”), still echoed in courtrooms, and the perfect ~j'ai ouï dire~ (“I have heard tell”). The adjective ~inouï~ (“unheard-of, extraordinary”) preserves the old participle, and the related noun ~ouïe~ now names the very sense of hearing. The Latin root remains hugely productive in French through later borrowings: ~audience~, ~auditeur~, and the modern ~audio~. Even ~obéir~ belongs here — from Latin ~oboedīre~ (~ob-~ + ~audīre~), literally “to lend one's ear to,” so that to obey is, at root, to listen.
en.paraître:
From Middle French ~paroistre~, from Old French ~paroistre~, ~pareistre~, from Vulgar Latin *~parēscere~, an inchoative (“begin to…”) verb formed on Latin ~pārēre~ (“to appear, to show oneself, to be visible”). ~Pārēre~ traces through Proto-Italic *~pāzēō~ to PIE *~peh₂s-~ (“to watch, to look out for”), an s-extension of *~peh₂-~ (“to protect”). Cognate with Italian ~parere~ and Spanish ~parecer~, both meaning “to seem.”

The inchoative ending *~-ēscere~ is the heart of the story: it turned the plain ~pārēre~ (“to be visible”) into “to come into view, to begin to appear,” capturing the very moment a thing surfaces — which is exactly the nuance modern ~paraître~ keeps in “to appear, to seem, to come out” (a book ~paraît~ when it is published). ~Pārēre~ also combined with prefixes to give ~apparēre~, source of French ~apparaître~ (“to appear”) and the noun ~apparition~, and ~comparēre~, source of ~comparaître~ (“to appear in court”). From the same family French keeps ~apparence~ (“appearance”) and ~transparent~, all turning on the simple idea of being seen.
en.parcourir:
From Old French, a compound of the prefix ~par-~ (from Latin ~per-~, “through, thoroughly”) and the verb ~courir~ (“to run”), modeled on Latin ~percurrere~ (“to run through, traverse”). The base ~courir~ descends from Latin ~currere~ (“to run”), from Proto-Indo-European *~ḱers-~ (“to run”). The whole family is mirrored in Italian ~percorrere~ and Spanish ~recorrer~.

The prefix ~par-~ here is intensive and exhaustive: not merely to run, but to run over the whole extent of something. So ~parcourir~ kept a literal sense — to travel across or traverse a region — while developing the figurative one English borrows in “to skim”: to run one's eyes over a page, to ~parcourir~ a book. The same ~currere~ is astonishingly fertile in French, giving ~courir~, ~courrier~ (“mail,” once carried by a running messenger), ~courant~ (“current, running”), ~concourir~, ~secourir~ (“to succour,” literally “to run to the aid of”), and the noun ~cours~. Through Latin it also seeded English ~current~, ~course~, ~corridor~, and even ~car~ — all, at root, things that run.
en.parer:
From Latin ~parāre~ (“to make ready, to prepare, to arrange”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to produce, to procure”). The same root yields English ~prepare~, ~apparatus~, and ~separate~, Spanish ~parar~, and Italian ~parare~. In French, ~parer~ split into senses borrowed back from those neighbors: the defensive “to parry, to ward off” owes to Italian ~parare~ (“to block”), while the equestrian “to halt a horse” echoes Spanish ~parar~ (“to stop”).

Underneath all these meanings lies a single old idea: to get something ready. From “prepare” the verb slid easily toward “arrange attractively,” and so ~parer~ came to mean to adorn or bedeck — a bride is ~parée~ for her wedding, a room ~paré~ for a feast. The fighting sense took a different path through Italy: to make yourself ready against a blow is to fend it off, hence to parry. French keeps both alive at once, and the root *~per-~ blossoms across the language in ~préparer~, ~appareil~, ~séparer~, ~rempart~, ~parade~, and ~parure~ (“finery”). One Latin verb meaning simply “to make ready” thus learned to decorate, to defend, and to bring a galloping horse to a clean stop.
en.parler:
From Old French ~parler~, from Late Latin ~parabolāre~ (“to speak, to converse”), a verb built on Latin ~parabola~ (“comparison, parable; speech”). ~parabola~ was borrowed from Ancient Greek ~παραβολή~ (parabolḗ, “a placing side by side, comparison”), from ~παρά~ (pará, “beside”) and ~βάλλειν~ (bállein, “to throw, to cast”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷelH-~ (“to throw, to reach”). To “speak,” then, is etymologically to “throw words side by side.” Cognate verbs survive as Italian ~parlare~, while Spanish chose a different reflex of the same noun in ~palabra~ (“word”); English took ~parable~, ~parley~, and ~palaver~ from the same Greek source.

The striking detail is that the ordinary French word for talking comes not from Latin's neutral ~loquī~ (“to speak”) but from a churchy, rhetorical word for a “comparison” or “parable” — the speech of sermons and figures. In the mouths of Late Latin speakers ~parabola~ shed its lofty sense and came to mean simply “word, speech,” which is why French ~parole~ means “(the spoken) word” and ~parler~ means “to talk.” From the same root come a large family, including ~parole~, ~parleur~, ~pourparler~ (“negotiation, parley”), and the borrowed ~parabole~ (“parable; parabola”), which kept the geometric and biblical senses the everyday verb left behind.
en.partager:
French ~partager~ is a relatively young verb, coined around 1553 from the noun ~partage~ (“division, sharing”) plus the verbal suffix ~-er~. Behind it stands ~part~ (“share, portion”), from Latin ~pars~, accusative ~partem~ (“a part, a share”), which traces to Proto-Indo-European *~perh₂-~ (“to grant, to allot”). The root is widely fertile, related to Latin ~portiō~ (“portion”) and surfacing in English ~part~, ~portion~, and ~parcel~. Greek ~péprōtai~ (“it has been allotted”) carries the same notion of a share handed out by fate.

The coinage answered a real gap in the language. The older inherited verb ~partir~, from Latin ~partīre~ (“to divide”), had drifted away to mean only “to depart, to leave” — a share of a journey split off, as it were, from the rest. With ~partir~ no longer usable for “to divide,” French built a fresh verb on the noun ~partage~ to recover the lost sense. The root still ramifies widely: ~part~, ~partie~, ~parti~, ~partager~, ~partenaire~ (“partner”), ~parcelle~, ~départ~ and ~répartir~. The fixed phrase ~à part~ (“apart, aside”) and the verb ~partir~ itself remind us that to depart and to share a portion spring from one and the same idea of dividing.
en.participer:
From Latin ~participāre~ (“to share, to take part in”), a compound of ~pars~ / ~partis~ (“a part, a share”) and ~capere~ (“to take, to seize”) — literally “to take a part.” ~Capere~ descends from PIE *~kh₂p-~ (“to grasp, to seize”), through which ~participer~ is cognate with English ~have~ and ~heave~ and with German ~haben~. ~Pars~ also gives French ~part~, ~partie~, and ~partir~.

The Latin verb fused the having of a portion with the act of seizing it, so that to participate is to take one's share of a common thing. From ~capere~ flows an immense French family — ~captif~, ~capter~, ~recevoir~, ~concevoir~, ~accepter~, ~capable~ — while ~pars~ supplies ~partager~, ~parti~, ~partiel~, and ~répartir~. From ~participāre~ itself come the close relatives ~participant~, ~participation~, and the grammatical ~participe~ (the participle, so called because it “takes part” in the natures of both verb and adjective). Thus the dry term of grammar and the lively act of joining in a cause are, etymologically, the very same gesture of taking a share.
en.partir:
From Old French ~partir~ (“to divide, to share, to separate”), from Latin ~partīre~, a variant of Classical Latin ~partīrī~ (“to divide, to share out”), a verb built on ~pars~, ~partis~ (“a part, share, portion”). The noun is generally traced to a Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ in the sense “to grant, to allot.” Through ~pars~, ~partir~ is kin to Italian ~partire~ and Spanish ~partir~, and, by way of Old French, to English ~part~, ~party~, and ~depart~.

The modern meaning hides an older one. For centuries ~partir~ meant “to divide” — a sense still alive in the saying ~avoir maille à partir~ (“to have a bone to pick,” literally a tiny coin to split). The notion of leaving grew from the reflexive ~se partir~, “to separate oneself” from a person or place; the reflexive faded but its directional sense stayed, leaving ~partir~ to mean simply “to depart.” The dividing sense lives on in ~répartir~, ~partager~, ~partage~, ~partie~ (“part, match”), ~parti~ (“party, side”), ~partition~, and ~particule~ — and, more distantly through ~pars~, in ~parcelle~ and the borrowed ~portion~.
en.parvenir:
From Latin ~pervenire~ (“to arrive, to reach”), a compound of ~per-~ (“through, all the way”) and ~venire~ (“to come”). ~venire~ descends from Proto-Italic *~gʷeniō~ and from Proto-Indo-European *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to go”). Cognate with Italian ~pervenire~ and Spanish ~prevenir~ in form, and — through *~gʷem-~ — with English ~come~ and the learned ~advent~.

The prefix ~per-~ supplies the crucial nuance: not merely to come, but to come through, to come all the way to the end — hence “to reach, to attain.” French keeps both the literal and the figurative: a letter ~parvient~ to its addressee, and a person ~parvient~ to wealth or to a goal. From this last sense comes the loaded noun ~parvenu~, literally “one who has arrived,” used scornfully of the newly rich who reached high station from low birth — a social judgment hiding inside a participle. The shared root *~gʷem-~ links ~parvenir~ to the whole French ~venir~ family — ~devenir~, ~revenir~, ~convenir~, ~survenir~, ~prévenir~ — every one of them a different way of coming.
en.passer:
Attested from around 1050, from Vulgar Latin *~passāre~ (“to cross, to step”), derived from Latin ~passus~ (“step, pace”), which is the past participle of ~pandere~ (“to spread, to stretch out [the legs]”), from PIE *~peth₂-~ (“to spread”). A step is, etymologically, a “spreading” of the legs. Cognate with Italian ~passare~ and Spanish ~pasar~; the noun ~passus~ also gave French ~pas~ and, by borrowing, English ~pass~ and ~pace~.

Few French verbs sprawl across so many meanings: one ~passe~ a river (to cross), ~passe~ an exam, ~passe~ the salt, ~passe~ time, and time itself ~passe~. All trace back to the simple image of stepping across a threshold. The same root *~peth₂-~ surfaces in the Latin ~pandere~ family, from which French draws ~expansion~ and ~épandre~, and the ~passus~ branch yields a crowd of everyday words: ~passage~, ~passager~, ~passeport~ (literally a document for “passing a port”), ~dépasser~, ~surpasser~, ~repasser~, and ~trépasser~ — “to pass beyond,” the old, gentle word for dying. To “pass away” is thus, in French as in English, to take a final step across.
en.payer:
From Old French ~paiier~, ~payer~, from Latin ~pācāre~ (“to pacify, to appease”), a verb built on ~pāx~ (“peace”). ~Pāx~ goes back to PIE *~peh₂ḱ-~ (“to fasten, to join, to settle”), the root that also gave Latin ~pangere~ (“to fix, to drive in”) and ~pacīscī~ (“to make a pact”); through its Germanic line the same root underlies English ~fang~. Cognate with Italian ~pagare~ and Spanish ~pagar~.

The semantic leap is the memorable part. In Classical Latin ~pācāre~ meant “to make peaceful, to subdue,” but in the money-minded Latin of the Middle Ages it narrowed to a very particular kind of appeasement: to pacify a creditor by settling the debt. To pay was, literally, to make peace with the person you owed. That sense crossed into Old French and crowded out the older one entirely, so that French ~payer~ now means only “to pay.” The peaceful origin is still visible in the cousins of ~pāx~ that French preserves — ~paix~ (“peace”), ~apaiser~ (“to appease”), ~pacifier~, ~pacte~ — a quiet reminder that a settled bill and a settled quarrel were once the same act.
en.peindre:
From Middle and Old French ~peindre~, from Latin ~pingere~ (“to paint, to dye, to embroider, to tattoo, to embellish”), from a nasal-infixed Proto-Indo-European *~pi-n-ḱ-~, from the root *~peyḱ-~ (“to mark, to color; spotted”). The deep sense is marking or cutting a sign into a surface. Through this root ~peindre~ is cognate with Greek ~poikílos~ (“dappled, embroidered”), Sanskrit ~piṃśáti~ (“he cuts, adorns”), and the Old English ~fāh~ (“stained, variegated”).

That the same Latin verb meant both “to paint” and “to embroider” and “to tattoo” recalls a time when all of these were ways of marking a surface with color. French narrowed ~peindre~ to the brush, but kept the figurative reach: one can ~peindre~ a character or a scene in words, “to depict” it. The Latin participle ~pictus~ and noun ~pictūra~ entered French as the learned doublets ~pictural~ and the borrowed ~pictura~ behind ~peinture~, while ~pingere~ itself gave ~peintre~ (“painter”) and ~peinture~. From ~pigmentum~ (“coloring matter”) came both ~pigment~ and, by a worn-down popular route, ~piment~ (“chili pepper”); the same root colors Italian ~pingere~ and Spanish ~pintar~.
en.penser:
From Middle and Old French ~penser~ (also spelled ~panser~), from Latin ~pensāre~ (“to weigh carefully, to ponder”). ~pensāre~ is the frequentative of ~pendere~ (“to weigh, to hang”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)pend-~ (“to stretch, to pull, to spin”) — the image of weights hanging from the arm of a balance. So to “think” is, at root, to “weigh” — to hold ideas in the scales of the mind. ~penser~ is a learned doublet of French ~peser~ (“to weigh”), which descends from the very same Latin verb by the ordinary popular route; the two split apart, one keeping the literal weight, the other the mental one. Cognate reflexes survive as Italian ~pensare~ and Spanish ~pensar~, and the family entered English as ~pensive~ and ~ponder~.

The metaphor of mental weighing is ancient and vivid, and French preserves it throughout the vocabulary of thought. Curiously, the alternate spelling ~panser~ peeled off into a separate verb meaning “to dress a wound, to groom (a horse)” — originally “to take thought for, to attend to” an animal or an injury, the same act of careful consideration applied to care. From ~pensāre~ and ~pendere~ come a wide French family, including ~pensée~ (“thought; pansy”), ~pensif~ (“pensive”), ~penseur~ (“thinker”), and, through the weighing sense, ~dépenser~ (“to spend, to weigh out money”) and ~récompense~ (“reward”).
en.percevoir:
French ~percevoir~ comes from Latin ~percipere~ (“to seize wholly, to take in, to perceive”), formed from ~per-~ (“thoroughly, completely”) plus ~capere~ (“to seize, to take”). ~Capere~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~kapjō~ to a Proto-Indo-European root *~kap-~ (“to grasp, to seize”), which also surfaces in the Germanic family — English ~have~ and ~heave~ are distant cousins through Proto-Germanic *~habjaną~. The French form was remodeled on ~recevoir~ and ~apercevoir~, all sharing the ~-cevoir~ stem from ~capere~; Italian keeps the more transparent ~percepire~.

The Latin verb already united the physical and the mental: to “take in completely,” whether grasping an object or grasping an idea. French inherited both. ~Percevoir~ still means “to take in through the senses or the mind” — to perceive a sound, to make out a meaning — but it also carries a strikingly concrete administrative sense, “to collect, to levy,” as when the state ~perçoit~ a tax: a literal seizing of money. From this comes ~percepteur~, the tax collector. Around ~capere~ and its compound French clusters ~perception~, ~perceptible~ and ~imperceptible~, ~apercevoir~ and ~apperçu~, plus the wider ~capter~, ~capable~, ~capture~, and ~recevoir~. To perceive, etymologically, is to grasp a thing whole — with the hand, the ear, or the understanding.
en.perdre:
From Latin ~perdere~ (« to destroy, to ruin, to lose utterly »), formed from the prefix ~per-~ (« through, to the end, to destruction ») and the verb ~dare~ (« to give »). ~dare~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~deh₃-~ (“to give”), shared by Ancient Greek ~dídōmi~ (“I give”), Sanskrit ~dádāti~, and — through Latin — English ~donate~ and ~date~. Italian ~perdere~ and Spanish ~perder~ are its Romance siblings.

The literal sense is striking: ~per-~ + ~dare~ is to « give thoroughly away », to give until nothing is left — hence « to destroy » and then « to lose ». The intensive prefix turned the act of giving into the act of losing, a quiet reminder that loss is open-handedness pushed past return. French keeps the whole spread: to ~perdre~ a key, a battle, one's time (~perdre son temps~), or one's way (~se perdre~). From the same verb come ~perte~ (« loss »), ~perdition~, and the adjective ~perdu~, while the root ~dare~ also yields ~donner~, ~rendre~, ~vendre~, and ~pardonner~.
en.permettre:
From Latin ~permittere~ (“to let pass, to allow, to entrust”), formed from the prefix ~per-~ (“through, thoroughly”) and the verb ~mittere~ (“to send, to let go”). French reshaped it on the native model of ~mettre~, giving ~permettre~. Since ~mittere~ rests on a Proto-Indo-European root *~meyth₂-~ (“to exchange, to remove”), ~permettre~ shares its deep ancestry with Italian ~permettere~ and Spanish ~permitir~, and with English ~permit~, borrowed straight from the Latin.

The literal force of ~per-~ + ~mittere~ is vivid: to permit is to “let through,” to send a thing all the way along its path rather than block it. From the same supine stem ~permiss-~ French draws ~permis~ (both the past participle “permitted” and the noun “permit, licence,” as in ~permis de conduire~, “driving licence”) and ~permission~. The verb keeps the company of its siblings built on ~mettre~ — ~promettre~, ~soumettre~, ~transmettre~, ~émettre~ — a tidy set in which a single prefix recolors the shared idea of sending: ~pro-~ forward, ~sub-~ under, ~trans-~ across, ~ex-~ out, and ~per-~ all the way through.
en.personnaliser:
From the adjective ~personnel~ (“personal”) plus the verb-forming suffix ~-iser~ — a modern, regular coinage rather than an inherited verb. ~Personnel~ itself goes back, through Old French, to Latin ~persōnālis~ (“of a person”), from ~persōna~. That noun first meant the ~mask~ worn by an actor on the Roman stage, then the dramatic ~role~ played behind it, and only later the human being who plays a role in life. Its own origin is debated: many trace ~persōna~ to Etruscan ~phersu~, itself perhaps linked to Ancient Greek ~πρόσωπον~ (~prósōpon~, “face, mask”).

The suffix ~-iser~ descends from Latin ~-izāre~, borrowed from Greek ~-ίζειν~ (~-ízein~), the same productive ending behind English ~-ize~. To ~personnaliser~ is thus, etymologically, to stamp something with the ~mask~ of an individual — to make it bear a particular face. The Latin root spread widely in French: ~personne~, ~personnage~, ~personnalité~, ~personnel~ (both the adjective and the noun “staff”), and the legal ~personnalité juridique~. English ~person~, ~personnel~, and ~persona~ share the same theatrical ancestor, a reminder that the modern idea of a unique self began as a hollow wooden face.
en.peser:
From Old French ~peser~ (“to weigh”), from Vulgar Latin *~pesare~, an alteration of Latin ~pēnsāre~ (“to weigh carefully, weigh out”), the frequentative of ~pendere~ (“to weigh, to hang”), from Proto-Italic *~pendō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)pend-~ (“to pull, to stretch, to spin”). Cognate with Italian ~pesare~ and Spanish ~pesar~, and — through the same Latin verb — with the noun ~poids~ and, more distantly, English ~pension~ and ~pendant~.

The Latin ~pēnsāre~ split into two French verbs that are, etymologically, twins: ~peser~ kept the concrete sense of placing something on a balance, while its learned doublet ~penser~ (“to think”) took up the figurative “to weigh in the mind, to ponder.” To think, in this old image, was to weigh ideas against one another. The metaphor still surfaces in ~peser ses mots~ (“to weigh one's words”) and in the impersonal ~peser~ meaning “to weigh upon, to burden.” From the same root *~(s)pend-~ French inherited a broad family: ~pendre~ (“to hang”), ~suspendre~, ~dépenser~ (literally “to weigh out, to pay out”), ~compenser~, and the noun ~poids~ itself, whose silent ~d~ was added by scribes who wrongly linked it to Latin ~pondus~ (“weight”).
en.placer:
A denominal verb of the 16th century, ~placer~ was formed from the noun ~place~ plus the verbal suffix ~-er~. The noun ~place~ comes from Old French ~place~, from Vulgar Latin *~plattĕa~, an altered form of Latin ~platēa~ (“a broad street, an open space”), itself borrowed from Ancient Greek ~πλατεῖα~ (~plateîa~, “broad,” in ~plateîa hodós~, “a broad way”). The Greek adjective rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~pleth₂-~ (“flat, broad, to spread”). Through that root ~place~ is cousin to Italian ~piazza~ and Spanish ~plaza~, and to English ~place~ and ~flat~.

The journey of meaning runs from “a wide road” to “an open square in a town” to, more abstractly, “a spot, a position, a rank.” Once the noun could name any position whatever, French speakers naturally coined a verb for putting something into its position — and so, around 1564, ~placer~ appeared, meaning “to set in a place, to seat.” From the same broad-and-flat root *~pleth₂-~ French gathered ~plat~, ~plateau~, ~platane~ (the plane tree, named for its wide leaves), ~plaine~, and ~aplatir~, while ~place~ itself spun off ~placement~, ~remplacer~, and ~déplacer~.
en.plaire:
Modern French ~plaire~ comes, through Old French, from Latin ~placēre~ (“to please, to be agreeable, to satisfy”). In Old French the infinitive was long ~plaisir~ (the source of the noun ~plaisir~, “pleasure”), and ~plaire~ is most likely a later analogical reshaping modeled on verbs like ~faire~, working back from forms such as ~il plaît~. The deeper origin is debated: the Latin verb is tied to ~plācāre~ (“to soothe, to appease”), and many trace both to a Proto-Indo-European root *~pleh₂k-~ (“flat, to be calm”); De Vaan instead reconstructs *~pleHk-~ (“to please, to permit”), with secure cognates only in Tocharian ~plāk-~ (“to please, to agree”). Romance kept the verb alive everywhere: Italian ~piacere~ and Spanish ~placer~ are its siblings, and English borrowed the family as ~please~ and ~pleasure~.

From the start the sense was “to give satisfaction,” and it never strayed far. A striking trace is the fossilized impersonal ~plaît-il ?~ (“pardon?”) and the courteous ~s'il vous plaît~ (“if it pleases you,” i.e. “please”), where the verb survives in a frozen subjunctive-flavored politeness formula. The same Latin stock seeded a wide family in French: ~plaisir~, ~complaire~ and ~déplaire~, the adjective ~plaisant~, ~complaisant~, and ~placide~ (“placid, calm,” straight from ~plācidus~). Through Latin ~plācāre~ French also gained the learned apaiser's cousin ~placation~ and the verb ~placer~ in its rarer sense. Few verbs so thoroughly stayed put: ~plaire~ has meant essentially the same thing for two thousand years.
en.plonger:
From Old French ~plongier~ (twelfth century), from Vulgar Latin *~plumbicāre~, a verb derived from Latin ~plumbum~ (“lead, the metal”). The idea is vivid: to ~plonger~ is to drop like a lead weight, to sink as a plumb-line does. The same *~plumbicāre~ underlies Italian ~piombare~ (“to fall heavily, to plunge”). ~plumbum~ itself is a word of uncertain, probably non-Indo-European origin, perhaps borrowed from an extinct language of the western Mediterranean where lead was mined; scholars doubt the old guess linking it to Greek ~mólybdos~.

The metaphor of the lead sinker runs all through the word's history. A sounding-lead dropped to measure depth gave both the act of plunging into water and, figuratively, the act of immersing oneself — one is ~plongé~ in thought, in darkness, in despair. From the same ~plumbum~ French drew a tightly related cluster: ~plomb~ (“lead”), ~plomber~ (“to fill a tooth, to seal with lead”), ~plombier~ (“plumber,” once a worker in lead pipes), ~plombage~ (“filling”), and ~aplomb~ (“perpendicularity, poise” — literally “to the plumb”). English ~plumb~, ~plummet~, and ~plunge~ are cousins through the same metallic root, so the diver, the plumber, and the person of great ~aplomb~ all owe their words to a lump of lead.
en.porter:
French ~porter~ descends directly from Latin ~portāre~ (“to carry, to bring”), kept intact through Old French ~porter~. The Latin verb belongs to Proto-Indo-European *~per-~ (“to lead across, to pass over”), a root deeply concerned with passage and crossing: from it come Latin ~porta~ (“gate”) and ~portus~ (“harbor, passage”), Greek ~póros~ (“ford, passage”), and — through the Germanic branch — English ~fare~ and ~ford~ and German ~fahren~ (“to travel”). To carry, in this old view, is to make something cross a distance.

Latin ~portāre~ was so productive that its prefixed compounds flooded into French: ~apporter~, ~emporter~, ~rapporter~, ~supporter~, ~transporter~, ~comporter~, ~exporter~, and ~importer~ — the last giving us both “to import” and the impersonal ~il importe~ (“it matters”), since what is “carried in” bears weight. The plain verb meanwhile spread far beyond literal carrying into “to wear” (~porter une robe~) and “to bear” a feeling or a name. From the same family come the nouns ~port~ (a harbor, the place of passage) and ~portail~, and the French fashion phrase ~prêt-à-porter~ (“ready to wear”). A single root for crossing thus links harbors, gates, journeys, and the clothes on one's back.
en.poser:
From Late Latin ~pausare~ (“to halt, to cease”), from the noun ~pausa~ (“a pause, a stop”), itself borrowed from Ancient Greek ~παῦσις~ (~paûsis~, “a stopping”), from the verb ~παύω~ (~paúō~, “to make cease”). The original idea was rest and cessation — ~pausare~ appears on early Christian epitaphs in the phrase “here he rests in peace.” English ~pose~ and ~pause~ both trace to this family, alongside Italian ~posare~ and Spanish ~posar~.

The great surprise of ~poser~ is that its modern meaning is not really its own. The Latin verb meaning “to put, to place” was ~ponere~, with past participle ~positus~; but ~ponere~ and ~pausare~ collided in spoken Latin, and ~poser~ swallowed the sense of placing that by rights belonged to ~ponere~. The fingerprint of this merger is everywhere: every compound of ~ponere~ surfaces in French as ~-poser~ — ~déposer~, ~exposer~, ~imposer~, ~proposer~, ~composer~ — yet their nouns keep the older Latin stem and end in ~-ition~ (~position~, ~exposition~, ~proposition~), not the ~-ation~ a verb in ~poser~ would predict. So ~poser~ today means “to put down, to set, to ask a question, to pose for a portrait,” carrying a sense it borrowed wholesale from a verb it eclipsed.
en.posséder:
A learned borrowing from Latin ~possidēre~ (“to own, to hold, to occupy”), which supplanted the inherited Old French verb ~posseoir~. Latin ~possidēre~ is a compound of ~potis~ (“able, having power, master”) and ~sedēre~ (“to sit”): to possess was literally “to sit as master,” to occupy a place with authority. The element ~potis~ comes from the Proto-Indo-European *~pótis~ (“lord, master”), and ~sedēre~ from the root *~sed-~ (“to sit”) — the very root behind English ~sit~ and German ~sitzen~. Roman jurists used ~possidēre~ first of all for landholding, and the image of an owner seated firmly on his ground sits at the bottom of the word.

That picture of sitting-in-power proved durable: to ~posséder~ is to hold a thing so firmly that one is settled upon it, and the verb stretched from land to goods to qualities and, eerily, to people “possessed” by spirits. The ~sedēre~ root seeded a large French family — ~asseoir~, ~séance~, ~sédentaire~, ~résider~, and ~siège~ — while ~potis~ surfaces in ~pouvoir~, ~puissant~, and ~possible~. From ~possidēre~ itself French keeps ~possession~, ~possesseur~, ~possessif~, and ~déposséder~.
en.poster:
The verb ~poster~ (“to mail”) is denominal, formed from the noun ~poste~. That noun was borrowed in the fifteenth century from Italian ~posta~ (“relay station”), descended from Latin ~posita~, the feminine past participle of ~ponere~ (“to put, place”). A ~posta~ was a place where fresh horses were “posted” along a route — a relay station for couriers — and from this came the whole vocabulary of the mail. The chain links to Italian ~posta~ and Spanish ~posta~, and to the English ~post~.

French has, in fact, several verbs ~poster~ that look alike. One, also denominal from ~poste~ in its military sense, means “to station, to position” (~poster une sentinelle~); a more recent one borrows English ~post~ in the sense of publishing online. But the postal ~poster~ — to drop a letter into the mail — flows straight from those relay stations where messages were laid down and relayed onward. The base verb ~ponere~ is a giant of French word-formation, yielding ~poser~, ~position~, ~composer~, ~déposer~, ~exposer~, ~proposer~, and the noun ~poste~ in all its senses, from a post office to a job ~poste~ to a radio ~poste~ — every one of them, at root, something “placed.”
en.poursuivre:
From Old French ~pursivre~ (twelfth century), later ~porsuivir~, a compound of ~por-~ / ~pour-~ (“forth, forward”) and ~suivre~ (“to follow”), refashioned on the model of Latin ~prosequi~ (“to follow after, to pursue, to escort”), from ~pro-~ (“forward”) and ~sequi~ (“to follow”). ~sequi~ derives through Proto-Italic *~sekʷōr~ from a Proto-Indo-European *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”), cognate with Ancient Greek ~hépomai~ and Sanskrit ~sácate~ (both “to follow”). From the same Latin verb English has ~pursue~.

The sense fans out from the simple act of following hard after someone. To ~poursuivre~ is to chase a fleeing enemy, to carry on a task one has begun, to hound or torment someone (a memory can ~poursuivre~ you), and, in law, to prosecute — to pursue a defendant through the courts. That last sense is the living trace of the Roman ~prosequi~, the formal “following up” of a legal action. The shared root *~sekʷ-~ is astonishingly fertile in French: ~suivre~ itself, ~suite~ (“that which follows”), ~ensuite~ (“next”), ~conséquence~ (“that which follows from”), ~séquence~ (“sequence”), and ~poursuite~ (“pursuit, prosecution”) all grow from it, as do English ~sequel~, ~suite~, and ~second~.
en.pousser:
From Old French ~poulser~ (also ~polser~), from Latin ~pulsare~ (“to beat, to strike, to push repeatedly”), the frequentative of ~pellere~ (“to drive, to beat, to strike”). ~pellere~ rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~pel-~ (“to thrust, to strike, to drive”). Through this root the French verb is cognate with Italian ~pulsare~ and, more distantly, with English ~push~ (likewise from ~pulsare~ via Old French) and with English ~felt~ (the matted fabric beaten together).

The Latin ~pulsare~ was forceful and repetitive — to strike again and again — which is why its descendants spread across the senses of beating, throbbing, and driving forward. French ~pousser~ kept the “drive forward” thread: to push a cart, to urge someone on, even to sprout (a plant ~pousse~). From the same ~pellere~ family French gathers a striking crowd: ~pouls~ (“pulse,” the beating of the blood), ~pulsation~, ~pulsion~, ~repousser~ (“to push back, to repel”), ~propulser~, ~expulser~, and ~compulser~ — so that a heartbeat, a psychological drive, and a shove all answer to one ancient verb meaning “to strike.”
en.pouvoir:
From Old French ~pooir~ (also ~povoir~), from Vulgar Latin *~potēre~, a regularized infinitive built to replace the irregular Classical Latin ~posse~ (“to be able”). ~Posse~ was itself a fusion of ~potis~ (“able, capable, master of”) and ~esse~ (“to be”) — literally “to be capable.” The root is Proto-Indo-European *~poti-~ (“powerful, master, lord”). Romance cousins include Spanish ~poder~, Portuguese ~poder~, and Italian ~potere~, each serving as both verb and noun.

Because ~posse~ was so irregular, late spoken Latin rebuilt a tidy ~potēre~ on the model of its present participle ~potēns~ (“being able”), and from this regularized stem French ~pouvoir~ descends; the medial ~-v-~ crept in later to break the awkward hiatus of ~pooir~. The same PIE root *~poti-~ powers a remarkable spread of words: ~puissant~ and ~puissance~ (through a parallel Vulgar Latin *~possiēns~), the borrowed doublets ~potent~ and ~potentiel~, ~potentat~, and even ~despote~ (Greek ~despótēs~, a “house-master,” from the same *~poti-~). And ~pouvoir~ shows the language’s habit of freezing an infinitive into a noun: ~le pouvoir~, “power” itself.
en.pratiquer:
Built in French on the noun ~pratique~ with the verbal ending ~-er~. ~Pratique~ comes from Late Latin ~practicus~ (“practical, active”), a borrowing of Greek ~praktikós~ (“fit for action, effective”), from ~praktós~, the verbal adjective of ~prássein~ (Attic ~práttein~, “to do, to act, to accomplish,” literally “to pass through, to traverse”), which goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to cross, to pass over”). It is cognate with English ~practice~ and ~practical~, themselves drawn from the same Greek source through Medieval Latin ~practicare~.

The Greek word carried a strong philosophical charge — Aristotle opposed the ~praktikós~ life of action to the contemplative — and that sense of “doing rather than merely knowing” still colors the French verb. ~Pratiquer~ means to carry something out in deed: to practise a craft, a religion, or a sport, to do rather than to theorize. From the same root the modern language keeps ~pratique~ (both noun and adjective), ~praticien~ (“a practitioner”), the learned doublet ~praxis~, and ~pragmatique~ (“pragmatic”) — a cluster of words all insisting, against mere theory, on the act itself.
en.prendre:
From Middle and Old French ~prendre~, also ~prindre~ (attested 842), from Latin ~prēndere~, a contracted form of ~prehendere~ (“to grasp, to seize”). That verb breaks down into the prefix ~prae-~ (“before, in front of”) and a root ~-hendere~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ǵʰend-~ (“to seize, to take”). Through that root ~prendre~ is cognate with Greek ~khandánein~ (“to hold, to contain”) and — strikingly — with English ~get~ and ~forget~.

In Late Latin ~prēndere~ pushed aside the classical ~capere~ (“to take”) in Gaul, becoming the ordinary verb for taking, and its Italian sibling ~prendere~ did the same. The Latin past participle ~prēnsus~ left its own descendants, and the supine stem ~prehēns-~ resurfaces in the learned French ~préhension~ and ~appréhender~ (“to apprehend, to dread”). ~Prendre~ also anchors one of the busiest prefix families in French: ~apprendre~ (“to learn,” literally “to take to oneself”), ~comprendre~ (“to understand,” “to take together”), ~surprendre~ (“to surprise,” “to overtake”), ~entreprendre~ (“to undertake”), and ~reprendre~ (“to resume”). The shared image — a hand or a mind closing around its object — runs quietly through them all.
en.priver:
From Latin ~privare~ (“to deprive, to free from, to set apart”), derived from the adjective ~privus~ (“single, individual, one's own”), from Proto-Italic *~preiwos~, ultimately from PIE *~per-~ (“in front, forward”), via a sense shift from “(being) in front” to “standing apart, separate.” Cognate with Italian ~privare~ and Spanish ~privar~, and the source, through French, of English ~deprive~ and ~private~.

The key is that ~privus~ meant “set apart, belonging to one alone” — hence ~privare~ first meant “to set apart,” then “to take away from, to deprive.” The same adjective gave the idea of what is withdrawn from public view: ~privé~ (“private”) and, with a darker color, ~privation~ (“hardship, want”). Roman ~privilegium~ — a law (~lex~) made for a single (~privus~) individual — became French ~privilège~, originally a right set apart for one person. The literary ~privauté~ (“familiarity, intimacy”) preserves the sense of the private and personal. To deprive someone is, etymologically, to set what they had apart from them.
en.privilégier:
Denominal verb from the noun ~privilège~, which comes from Latin ~prīvilēgium~ (“a law affecting a single person”), a compound of ~prīvus~ (“private, belonging to one individual”) and ~lēx~, stem ~lēg-~ (“law”). ~Prīvus~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~preiwos~, the same source as ~prīvātus~; ~lēx~ derives from the root *~leǵ-~ (“to gather, collect”), the laws being what is “laid down” or “collected.” Cognate with Italian ~privilegiare~ and Spanish ~privilegiar~, and — borrowed — with English ~privilege~. Literally, then, a ~privilège~ is private legislation: a rule carved out for one.

The Roman ~prīvilēgium~ was originally a neutral, even pejorative, term — a special law against an individual — but it drifted toward favorable exception, the sense of a granted advantage that medieval charters and royal grants entrenched. From the noun French built the verb ~privilégier~, “to favor, to give precedence,” now common in the bland modern sense of choosing one option over another, as in ~privilégier une approche~ (“to favor an approach”). The two halves seeded large families: ~prīvus~ gave ~privé~, ~priver~, and ~privation~, while ~lēx~ gave ~légal~, ~loi~, ~légiférer~, and ~privilège~ itself — the personal and the legal forever fused in a single word.
en.procéder:
From Latin ~procedere~ (“to go forward, to advance, to result”), compounded from ~pro-~ (“forward, before”) and ~cedere~ (“to go, to withdraw, to yield”). ~Cedere~ descends from a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to go, to yield,” and ~pro-~ from the root *~per-~ (“forward, through”). Cognate, through ~cedere~, with the whole prefixed family that gave Italian ~procedere~ and Spanish ~proceder~, and with English ~proceed~ and ~process~.

The literal Latin sense — “to step forward” — branched in French into two living strands. One keeps the spatial and temporal idea of advancing: a ceremony, an investigation, or a chemical reaction ~procède~ in orderly steps. The other, “to behave, to act,” comes from the notion of how one goes about a thing. ~Cedere~ is among Latin's most generous roots, supplying French with ~céder~, ~accéder~, ~concéder~, ~précéder~, ~succéder~, ~procès~, and ~procédure~ — the last two preserving the legal sense of a matter that goes ~forward~ through stages. The English noun ~process~ and the verb ~proceed~ are the same word twice borrowed, and a ~procession~ is, etymologically, simply a going-forward made into a ceremony.
en.produire:
From Latin ~producere~ (“to lead forward, bring forth”), a compound of the prefix ~pro-~ (“forward, before”) and the verb ~ducere~ (“to lead, draw”). ~Ducere~ comes from Proto-Italic *~doukō~ and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~dewk-~ (“to lead, draw, pull”), which also gives English ~tow~ and German ~Zug~ (“a pull, a draught”). Within French, ~ducere~ is the engine of a whole verb family: ~conduire~, ~réduire~, ~traduire~, and ~produire~ all share the same “leading” core.

The Latin sense was literally agricultural and theatrical at once — to “lead forth” crops from the earth, or to bring a witness or an actor forward into view. French kept both strands: ~produire~ still means “to bring forth” (goods, fruit, an effect) and, in legal usage, “to produce, to bring forward” a document or witness. From the past participle ~produit~ comes the everyday noun for a “product,” and the root spreads further into ~production~, ~producteur~, ~productif~, and ~productivité~. Behind the modern factory floor lies an old image of pulling something out and leading it into the light.
en.profiter:
A denominal verb formed from the noun ~profit~. That noun comes from Latin ~profectus~ (“progress, growth, advantage”), the past participle of ~prōficere~ (“to advance, make progress, be useful”), built from ~prō-~ (“forward”) plus ~facere~ (“to make, do”). ~facere~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to put, place, set”), making French ~profiter~ a distant cousin of English ~do~ and ~deed~, of Greek ~tithēnai~ (“to put, place”), and of German ~tun~.

The literal Latin sense is “to make forward” — to advance. From “advance, gain ground” grew the noun's notion of material “profit, gain,” and the verb ~profiter~ took its main modern meanings from there: to profit from, to take advantage of, to make the most of (~profiter de la vie~, “to make the most of life”). A gentler branch keeps the older flavor of growth and benefit: ~profiter à quelqu'un~ means “to be beneficial to someone,” and regionally ~profiter~ can even mean “to thrive, grow well,” as a child or a plant does. The vast ~facere~ family fills modern French — ~faire~, ~profit~, ~bénéfice~, ~parfaire~, ~défaire~, ~confiture~ — so that to profit is, at root, simply to make headway.
en.progresser:
A late and learned formation, attested only in 1832, ~progresser~ was built within French on the stem of ~progrès~ and ~progression~ plus the verbal suffix ~-er~. The family traces to Latin ~prōgressus~, the past participle of ~prōgredī~ (“to step forward, to advance”), from the prefix ~prō-~ (“forward”) and ~gradī~ (“to step, to walk”), itself from ~gradus~ (“a step”), from Proto-Indo-European *~gʰredʰ-~ (“to walk, to stride”). Through that root it is cognate with Italian ~progredire~, Spanish ~progresar~, and English ~progress~ and ~grade~.

The image at the heart of the word is the footstep: to ~progresser~ is, etymologically, to keep putting one foot ahead of the other. The verb arrived late because French long made do with the noun ~progrès~ and the verb ~avancer~; the 19th century, with its faith in onward march, seems to have wanted a verb of its own for the idea. From the same Latin ~gradī~ and ~gradus~ French drew a stepping family: ~progrès~, ~progression~, ~grade~, ~gradin~, ~graduel~, ~dégrader~, and the cousins ~agresser~ (“to step toward, to attack”), ~régresser~, ~digresser~, and ~transgresser~ — each a different direction taken from the same Latin stride.
en.prolonger:
Attested from 1213, from Late Latin ~prōlongāre~ (“to lengthen, to extend”), a compound of the prefix ~prō-~ (“forward”) and ~longus~ (“long”). Latin ~longus~ descends from Proto-Italic *~dlongos~ and Proto-Indo-European *~dlongʰos~ (“long”), the same root that, through Germanic, yielded English ~long~ and German ~lang~. Cognate, through ~prōlongāre~, with Italian ~prolungare~ and Spanish ~prolongar~.

The image is spatial first: ~prō-~ + ~longus~ means to draw something out lengthwise, to make it longer in front. French applied that both to space — ~prolonger~ a road or a line — and, more often, to time, where to ~prolonger~ a stay, a contract, or a debate is to stretch its duration onward. A learned doublet exists: alongside ~prolonger~, French also has ~allonger~ (“to lengthen,” from ~ad~ + ~longus~), the more concrete, everyday verb for making a sleeve or a step longer. The root ~longus~ runs deep in the language: ~long~, ~longueur~ (“length”), ~longer~ (“to run alongside”), ~allonger~, ~élonger~, and the learned ~longitude~ all spring from it, as does ~lent~ in spirit through the Latin sense of drawn-out slowness.
en.promettre:
From Latin ~promittere~ (“to send forth, hold out, promise”), built on ~pro-~ (“forth, before”) plus ~mittere~ (“to release, let go, send”). The verb came down regularly through Old French ~prometre~. According to Michiel de Vaan, ~mittere~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~meyth₂-~ (“to exchange, remove”). It is matched by Italian ~promettere~ and Spanish ~prometer~, and English ~promise~ comes from the related participle ~promissum~.

The literal image is generous: to ~promittere~ is to “send forth” your word ahead of you, to put a thing forward for someone. The vast ~mittere~ family is one of Latin's most productive, and French inherits or borrows a crowd of its compounds — ~mettre~ (“to put,” the popular outcome of ~mittere~ itself), ~admettre~, ~commettre~, ~permettre~, ~soumettre~, ~transmettre~ — alongside the learned ~-mission~ words ~mission~, ~émission~, and ~promesse~ (from ~promissa~), the noun beside the verb. So a promise is, etymologically, something “sent ahead” — a word dispatched into the future to be honoured later.
en.promouvoir:
From Latin ~prōmovēre~ (“to move forward, to advance, to push on”), a compound of the prefix ~prō-~ (“forward, forth”) and ~movēre~ (“to move”). ~Movēre~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~mewh₁-~ (“to move, to push away”), and is cognate with English ~move~ (a Norman loan from the same Latin verb) and ~motor~, and with the Romance family of Italian ~muovere~ and Spanish ~mover~. The Latin verb reached French as a learned borrowing, which is why ~promouvoir~ keeps its irregular Latinate conjugation.

The image is bodily and concrete: to ~promote~ is to “move someone forward,” to push them up a rank. From this Roman sense of military and civil advancement, the verb broadened. One can ~promouvoir~ a colleague to a higher post, ~promouvoir~ a cause or an idea (to further its progress), and, in the most modern and commercial sense, ~promouvoir~ a product — to advertise it, an echo of the English ~promotion~ that re-entered French. Even in chess, a pawn that reaches the last rank is ~promu~. From the root *~mewh₁-~ descend a great crowd of French words, among them ~mouvoir~, ~émouvoir~, ~mouvement~, ~moteur~, ~émotion~, and ~mobile~.
en.prononcer:
From Old French ~prononcier~, from Latin ~prōnūntiāre~ (“to proclaim, announce, declare, recite”), from ~prō-~ (“forth, in public”) + ~nūntiāre~ (“to report, announce”), a verb built on ~nūntius~ (“messenger, message”), traditionally traced to Proto-Indo-European *~néw-~ (“to shout”). Cognate with Italian ~pronunciare~ and Spanish ~pronunciar~, and — through the same ~nūntiāre~ — with English ~pronounce~, ~announce~, and ~denounce~.

The core image is a herald carrying words ~forth~ before the public, which explains why the verb still spans both “to declare” (a court ~prononce~ a sentence) and “to articulate” (to ~prononcer~ a word aloud). The messenger ~nūntius~ seeded a wide family: with ~ad-~, ~annoncer~ (“to announce”); with ~dē-~, ~dénoncer~ (“to denounce”); with ~re-~, ~renoncer~ (“to renounce,” literally to report back that one withdraws); plus ~énoncer~ and the nouns ~nonce~ (the papal ambassador) and ~annonce~. A curious wrinkle: the noun ~prononciation~ keeps the ~-nci-~ of its Latin parent, while the verb worn smooth in the mouth became ~prononcer~ — the very word doing, in its own history, the softening it describes.
en.proposer:
From Old French ~proposer~, a French reshaping of Latin ~prōpōnere~ (“to set forth, to put before”), formed from ~prō-~ (“before, forward”) + ~pōnere~ (“to put, to place”). When the verb passed into French, its second element was remodeled on ~poser~ (“to place”) — which actually descends from Latin ~pausāre~, “to pause” — so that ~prōpōnere~ became ~proposer~ by analogy. Cognate with Italian ~proporre~ and Spanish ~proponer~, and, through ~pōnere~, with English ~propose~ and ~propound~.

The Latin ~pōnere~ has a debated deep origin — traditionally analyzed as ~po-~ + ~sinere~ (“to set aside, let”), with its participle ~positus~ giving the family its ~-pos-~ forms. The literal sense, “to place before” someone for consideration, drifted naturally into “to put forward an idea,” to suggest. French built a rich crowd of relatives on this ~poser~ pattern: ~proposition~, ~proposer~'s siblings ~composer~, ~disposer~, ~exposer~, ~imposer~, ~opposer~, ~supposer~, and the noun ~propos~ (“words, remarks, purpose”). The learned ~positus~ branch adds ~position~, ~posture~, and ~dépôt~. From “placing” one builds an entire grammar of arrangement, intention, and debate.
en.protéger:
From Latin ~protegere~ (“to cover in front, to shelter, to defend”), a compound of the prefix ~pro-~ (“before, in front of”) and ~tegere~ (“to cover”). The verb ~tegere~ comes from Proto-Italic *~tegō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)teg-~ (“to cover, to roof”), shared with Ancient Greek ~στέγειν~ (~stégein~, “to cover”), German ~Dach~ (“roof”), and English ~thatch~ and ~deck~. Italian ~proteggere~ and Spanish ~proteger~ are the Romance parallels; English ~protect~ comes from the same Latin verb.

The literal picture is a soldier holding a shield in front — covering before — and from this concrete shelter the sense rose to abstract protection, defense, and patronage. The earlier, more popular French outcome of ~tegere~ was distinct: through related forms French got ~toit~ (“roof,” from ~tectum~) and the verb couvrir comes from a different root, but ~protéger~ kept the learned Latin shape, borrowed late in the 14th century. The same *~(s)teg-~ root surfaces across French in ~tuile~ (“roof tile,” from ~tegula~), ~toge~ (the Roman ~toga~, a “covering” garment), ~protection~, ~tégument~, and ~détecter~ — the last literally an “un-covering.”
en.prouver:
From Latin ~probare~ (“to test, to examine, to approve, to demonstrate”), a verb derived from the adjective ~probus~ (“good, upright, of fine quality”). ~Probus~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~proβwos~ and to a Proto-Indo-European formation *~pro-bʰuH-~, literally “being in front, growing forward well,” from *~pro-~ (“forward”) and the root *~bʰuH-~ (“to be, to grow”). Cognate, through this root, with Sanskrit ~prabhú~ (“excellent, foremost”), and with English ~prove~ and ~probe~.

The Latin chain of meaning is elegant: what is ~probus~ is upright and sound; to ~probare~ is to test something to see whether it measures up, and then to approve or demonstrate that it does. French ~prouver~ kept the demonstrative end of that arc — to establish a truth — while the testing sense lingers in ~éprouver~ (“to test, to experience”) and ~épreuve~ (“trial, ordeal, proof-sheet”). The same root yields ~probe~ (“upright”), ~probité~, ~approuver~, ~réprouver~, and the printer's ~épreuve~. English inherited a whole cluster — ~prove~, ~proof~, ~probable~, ~probation~, ~approve~ — all turning on the old idea that to prove a thing is, at heart, to find it good.
en.provenir:
From Latin ~provenire~ (“to come forth, to arise, to grow up”), a compound of the prefix ~pro-~ (“forth, forward”) and the verb ~venire~ (“to come”). ~venire~ descends from Proto-Italic *~gʷeniō~ and from the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to go”), which makes ~provenir~ a distant cousin of English ~come~, Greek ~baínō~ (“I walk”), and Sanskrit ~gácchati~ (“he goes”). Italian ~provenire~ and Spanish ~provenir~ continue the same Latin compound.

The Latin verb was vividly literal — to come forth, even to sprout or be born — and the agricultural image of a crop “coming forth” underlies the noun ~provenance~, originally the yield or produce of a place, now its source or origin. Modern ~provenir~ keeps this sense of issuing from a point of departure, whether a word ~provient~ from Latin or a sound ~provient~ from the next room. The base ~venir~ is one of the most fertile verbs in French, throwing off a whole prefixed family: ~devenir~ (“to become”), ~revenir~ (“to come back”), ~convenir~ (“to suit, to agree”), ~prévenir~ (“to warn, to forestall”), ~survenir~ (“to occur”), and ~parvenir~ (“to reach, to attain”). The root *~gʷem-~ thus quietly powers a sizeable corner of the French lexicon.
en.provoquer:
From Latin ~provocare~ (“to call forth, to challenge, to appeal”), from ~pro-~ (“forth, forward”) + ~vocare~ (“to call”). ~vocare~ is built on ~vox~ (“voice”), from Proto-Indo-European *~wokʷ-~, the root of ~vox~ and of English ~voice~ and ~vocal~. Old French had a popular variant ~porvochier~; the learned ~provoquer~ established itself by the mid-fourteenth century, notably in Pierre Bersuire's translation of Livy. Romance cognates include Italian ~provocare~ and Spanish ~provocar~.

In Rome, to ~provocare~ was literally to “call someone out” — to summon, to challenge to combat, and, crucially, to ~provocare ad populum~, to appeal a magistrate's sentence to the people, a cornerstone of Republican liberty. The combative edge survived: the verb came to mean “to incite, to challenge, to bring about,” keeping a sense of summoning a reaction into being, whether a quarrel, an illness, or a result. From the same ~vocare~ and ~vox~ French inherited a chorus of words: ~évoquer~, ~invoquer~, ~convoquer~, ~révoquer~, ~vocation~, ~voix~, and ~voyelle~.
en.préciser:
French ~préciser~ is a verb formed from the adjective ~précis~ (“precise, exact”) with the first-conjugation suffix ~-er~. ~Précis~ comes from Latin ~praecīsus~ (“cut off, abridged, abrupt”), the past participle of ~praecīdere~ (“to cut off in front, to cut short”), itself ~prae-~ (“before, in front”) plus ~caedere~ (“to cut, to strike, to fell”). ~Caedere~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~kaidō~ to Proto-Indo-European *~keh₂id-~ (“to strike, to hew”), with cognates such as Sanskrit ~khídati~ (“to tear”) and Old High German ~heia~ (“mallet”).

The governing metaphor is sharpness: what is ~précis~ has had every excess pared away, leaving a clean cut. To ~préciser~ is thus to trim a vague statement down to its exact edge — to specify, to make sharp. The same ~caedere~ runs through a surprisingly bloody cluster of relatives: the suffix ~-cide~ in ~homicide~ and ~suicide~ (a “cutting down”), and the doublet ~concis~ (“cut together, concise”). English drew ~precise~, ~concise~, ~decide~, and even ~scissors~ from the same Latin stem. There is a quiet paradox here — the word for verbal exactness descends from a verb for hacking and felling, as if precision were a kind of clean amputation of everything imprecise.
en.précéder:
From Latin ~praecedere~ (“to go before, precede”), a compound of ~prae-~ (“before, in front of”) and ~cedere~ (“to go, to yield, to give way”). Latin ~cedere~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~kesdō~ to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ked-~ (“to go, to yield”). The same ~cedere~, fitted with different prefixes, produced a whole battery of French and English verbs of motion and giving-way: ~accéder~, ~concéder~, ~excéder~, ~procéder~, ~succéder~, and English ~accede~, ~concede~, ~recede~, and ~secede~.

The Latin sense was concrete and spatial — to physically walk in front of someone — and French ~précéder~ has kept that literal core while extending it to precedence in time, rank, and logic: one event precedes another, one dignitary precedes another in a procession. The supine stem ~cessum~ (rather than ~ced-~) supplies a parallel set of nouns, which is why French has ~procession~, ~succession~, and the astronomical ~précession~, the slow gyration of a spinning axis. From the same family come ~accès~, ~succès~, ~procédé~, and ~cesser~ (“to cease”), all turning on the idea of going, stepping, or giving way.
en.préférer:
From Latin ~praeferre~ (“to carry before, set before, prefer”), formed from the prefix ~prae-~ (“before, in front”) and the verb ~ferre~ (“to carry, bear”). ~Ferre~ descends from Proto-Italic *~ferō~ and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~bʰer-~ (“to carry, to bear”), one of the most widespread roots in the family. Through that root it is cognate with Ancient Greek ~phérein~ (“to carry”), Sanskrit ~bhárati~ (“he carries”), and the Germanic verbs of bearing — English ~bear~ and German ~gebären~ (“to give birth”).

The image behind the word is concrete: to prefer something is literally to ~carry~ it out in front, to hold it ahead of the rest. Latin already used ~praeferre~ in this figurative sense of valuing one thing above another, and French inherited the metaphor whole. The supine stem of ~ferre~ was the irregular ~lātum~, which is why the ~fer-~ family and the ~lat-~ family in French look unrelated despite sharing a verb: from ~prae-~ + ~ferre~ comes ~préférer~, and the parallel ~prae-~ formations gave ~préface~ and ~préférence~. The same root *~bʰer-~ runs through a large French vocabulary of carrying and bringing: ~offrir~, ~souffrir~, ~conférer~, ~transférer~, and ~référer~ are all, etymologically, manners of bearing a load.
en.préparer:
From Latin ~praeparare~ (“to make ready beforehand”), a compound of the prefix ~prae-~ (“before, in advance”) and ~parare~ (“to make ready, to prepare, to furnish”). The verb ~parare~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~perh₃-~ (“to grant, to allot, to provide”), which also yielded Latin ~parere~ (“to give birth, to produce”) and is glimpsed in Ancient Greek ~ἔπορον~ (~époron~, “I furnished”) and Sanskrit ~pṛṇāti~ (“he grants”). Italian ~preparare~ and Spanish ~preparar~ are the sister forms; English borrowed ~prepare~ from the same Latin source.

The kernel is ~parare~, “to get something ready,” and the prefix ~prae-~ simply pulls the readiness ahead in time — to arrange in advance. The bare root ~parare~ survives in French ~parer~, which split into two senses: “to adorn” (to make ready for display) and “to ward off” a blow, the source of ~parade~ and the fencing ~parer~.
