The same ~parare~ underlies a remarkable spread of French words: ~préparer~, ~réparer~ (“to make ready again,” hence “to repair”), ~séparer~, ~comparer~, ~emparer~, and ~appareil~ — and, through the related ~imperare~, the imperial vocabulary of ~empereur~ and ~empire~.
en.présenter:
From Old French ~presenter~, from Latin ~praesentāre~ (“to place before, to show”), a verb formed on the adjective ~praesēns~ (“present, at hand”). That adjective is itself the present participle of ~praeesse~ (“to be before, to be at the head of”), built from ~prae-~ (“before, in front of”) and ~esse~ (“to be”). The deep root is therefore the Proto-Indo-European verb of being, *~h₁es-~ (“to be”), the same root that gives Latin ~est~ and English ~is~. Cognate verbs survive across the Romance languages, among them Italian ~presentare~ and Spanish ~presentar~, and the family passed into English as ~present~.

The literal image behind ~praesentāre~ is spatial: to make something ~praesēns~ is to set it physically “before” someone, in front of their eyes. From “to place before” the verb naturally acquired the senses of showing, offering, and introducing, and French ~présenter~ still carries all of these — one presents a gift, a person, or an argument. The same adjective ~praesēns~ gave French the noun and adjective ~présent~, covering both “the present moment” and “a present, a gift,” the latter through the old idea of something placed before a recipient. From the prefix ~prae-~ and this same field come many related French words, including ~présence~, ~présentation~, and ~représenter~ (“to represent, to perform again”).
en.préserver:
From Latin ~praeservare~ (“to guard beforehand, keep safe in advance”), formed on ~prae-~ (“before, in advance”) plus ~servare~ (“to keep, guard, watch over, save”). Latin ~servare~ goes back through Proto-Italic to a Proto-Indo-European root *~ser-~ (“to protect, watch over”), with a likely cognate in Avestan ~haraiti~ (“heeds, protects”). The verb is matched by Italian ~preservare~ and Spanish ~preservar~, and English borrowed it as ~preserve~.

The prefix ~prae-~ carries the whole nuance: not merely to keep, but to keep something ahead of harm — to shield it in advance from a danger. Latin ~servare~ should not be confused with its near-homophone ~servire~ (“to serve,” from ~servus~, “slave”); they are distinct roots that French keeps apart. From ~servare~ French inherits and borrows a guarding family: ~conserver~ (“to keep, conserve”), ~observer~ (originally “to watch over, attend to”), ~réserver~ (“to keep back”), and ~préservatif~, which means a protective agent in general but, in everyday French, a condom — a “preserver” in the most literal sense.
en.prétendre:
From Latin ~praetendere~ (“to stretch forth, hold out before, allege as a pretext”), formed from ~prae-~ (“before, in front”) and ~tendere~ (“to stretch”). ~Tendere~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~tendō~ and, with a ~d~-extension, to the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch, draw out”). The kinship reaches far: Ancient Greek ~τείνω~ (~teínō~), Sanskrit ~तनोति~ (~tanóti~), and, through Germanic, English ~thin~ — all stretchings of one ancient thread. French took the verb in the 14th century.

The metaphor at the heart of ~prétendre~ is striking: to hold something stretched out before you, like a screen or a banner. From that came “to put forward as a claim” — to claim a right, to allege a fact, to pretend to (aspire to) a throne or a hand in marriage. English ~pretend~ drifted toward “feign,” but French keeps the older sense of asserting and aspiring; a ~prétendant~ is a suitor or a claimant to power. The *~ten-~ family is vast in French: ~tendre~, ~tension~, ~tente~, ~étendre~, ~intention~, ~attendre~, and ~prétention~ — every one of them, at root, a stretching.
en.prévenir:
From Latin ~praevenire~ (“to come before, anticipate, forestall”), a compound of the prefix ~prae-~ (“before, in front of”) and ~venire~ (“to come”). The verb ~venire~ traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷem-~ (“to come, go, step”), the same root that, through Germanic, yields English ~come~, and that in Greek gives ~bainō~ (“I go”) and in Sanskrit ~gam-~. Italian ~prevenire~ and Spanish ~prevenir~ are its Romance siblings.

The literal “to come before” spread into several modern meanings that still circle anticipation. To ~prévenir~ can mean to warn or notify someone in advance (~prévenir un ami~), to prevent or forestall something (~prévenir une maladie~ — hence the proverb ~mieux vaut prévenir que guérir~, “prevention is better than cure”), or, in older usage, to favorably or unfavorably predispose someone, surviving in the adjective ~prévenu~ (“biased,” and in law “the accused”). The same ~venire~ powers a huge French family: ~venir~, ~devenir~, ~revenir~, ~convenir~, ~parvenir~, ~survenir~, ~avenir~ (“future,” what is “to come”), and ~événement~. To warn a friend, in this light, is simply to arrive ahead of the trouble.
en.prévoir:
From Latin ~praevidere~ (« to see beforehand, to foresee »), formed from ~prae-~ (« before, in front ») and ~videre~ (« to see »). ~videre~ descends through Proto-Italic *~widēō~ from the Proto-Indo-European root *~weyd-~ (“to see, to know”) — a root whose sense slid from seeing to knowing. Through it ~prévoir~ is cognate with English ~wit~, ~wise~, and ~witness~, with German ~wissen~ (“to know”), and, by way of Greek, with ~idea~.

Latin already used ~praevidere~ both literally, « to catch sight of in the distance », and figuratively, « to anticipate ». French inherited only the figurative core: to ~prévoir~ is to see with the mind's eye what has not yet happened. The verb conjugates like ~voir~ except in the future and conditional, where it keeps a regular ~prévoirai~ rather than the irregular ~verrai~ — a small grammatical seam revealing its compound origin. The same root *~weyd-~ underlies a wide French family: ~voir~ itself, ~vision~, ~évident~ (« seen clearly »), ~prévision~, ~prévoyant~, and ~revoir~.
en.prêter:
French ~prêter~ comes through Old French ~prester~ from Latin ~praestare~ (“to stand before, to be at hand, to furnish, to provide”). The verb is ~prae-~ (“before, in front”) plus ~stāre~ (“to stand”), and so reaches the deep Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”) — making ~prêter~ a distant cousin of ~résister~, ~rester~, and ~stable~. The circumflex in ~prêter~ is a tombstone for a lost ~s~: it marks where the ~s~ of ~prester~ fell silent. Italian ~prestare~ and Spanish ~prestar~ carry the same Latin verb.

The sense-shift is a small drama of generosity. Latin ~praestare~ literally meant “to stand before,” hence “to stand surety, to make good, to supply” — to have something ready ~prae manu~, “at hand.” That readiness is preserved in the adjective ~prêt~ (“ready”), an etymological sibling of the verb. From “to furnish what is ready” the Romance languages narrowed to “to lend,” and French ~prêter~ now means putting something at another's disposal on the promise of return — money, a book, even, figuratively, ~prêter l'oreille~ (“to lend an ear”) or ~prêter serment~ (“to take an oath”). The same root supplies ~prêt~ (“a loan”), ~emprunter~'s partner in finance, and the learned ~prestation~ (“a service rendered, a benefit”).
en.publier:
From Latin ~pūblicāre~ (“to make public, to bring before the people”), a verb derived from the adjective ~pūblicus~ (“public, belonging to the people”), itself ultimately from ~populus~ (“the people, the populace”). The adjective's form was likely colored by ~pūbēs~ (“adult, grown men”), but its core is the people, ~populus~. Cognate Romance descendants include Italian ~pubblicare~ and Spanish ~publicar~; English ~publish~ came through Old French ~publier~ with its characteristic ~-sh~.

To ~pūblicāre~ originally meant to make something the property of the people — including, harshly, to confiscate private goods for the state. From this “to make common/open” the dominant sense narrowed to “to announce openly, proclaim,” and then, with the printing press, to “bring out in print, issue a book.” French ~publier~ now serves both the everyday “to publish” and the formal proclaiming of banns or laws. From ~populus~ and ~pūblicus~ descend a broad family in French: ~public~, ~publication~, ~publicité~, ~république~ (~rēs pūblica~, “the public thing”), ~peuple~, ~populaire~, and ~population~.
en.qualifier:
From Medieval Latin ~qualificare~, a compound of Latin ~qualis~ (“of what kind, of what sort”) and the verb ~facere~ (“to make”), via the suffix ~-ficare~ — literally “to make of a certain kind.” ~qualis~ derives from the PIE interrogative-relative stem *~kʷo-~, the same pronoun base that lies behind “who” and “which.” The abstract noun ~qualitas~ was famously coined by Cicero as a calque of Greek ~ποιότης~ (“quality”), itself minted by Plato from ~ποῖος~ (“of what nature”). Cognate with Italian ~qualificare~, Spanish ~calificar~, and English ~qualify~.

The original sense was to attribute a quality to something — to say what kind of thing it is. From there ~qualifier~ branched in two directions: grammatically, an adjective ~qualifie~ a noun by describing it; and socially and legally, to ~qualifier~ became to give a title or designation, then to deem someone fit, competent, “qualified.” The sporting sense — a team that ~se qualifie~ for the next round — is a modern extension of that “proving fit” idea. From the same ~qualis~ root spring ~qualité~, ~qualitatif~, and the interrogative ~quel~ itself; the ~-ficare~ ending, meanwhile, recurs across a whole regiment of French verbs such as ~modifier~, ~vérifier~, and ~simplifier~, each one a small act of “making.”
en.quitter:
Modern French ~quitter~ is a verb built from the adjective ~quitte~ (“free of an obligation, even, discharged”) with the verb-forming suffix ~-er~. ~Quitte~ descends from Latin ~quietus~ (“at rest, calm, undisturbed”), the perfect participle of ~quiēscere~ (“to repose”), from the noun ~quies~ (“rest”), all from Proto-Indo-European *~kʷyeh₁-~ (“to rest”). The very same Latin ~quietus~ gives French the doublet ~quiet~ (“calm”) and, through a popular variant ~quētus~, the archaic ~coi~ (“still, hushed”); English ~quiet~, ~quit~, and ~coy~ are cognate, the last borrowed from Old French ~coi~.

The semantic thread runs from “rest” to “release.” In medieval law a person who was ~quitte~ had been left “at peace,” freed from a debt or duty; to ~quitter~ someone was therefore to discharge them, to acquit them of what they owed — a sense still alive in “~quitter~ quelqu'un d'une dette.” From “to release” the verb slid to “to let go of, to part from,” and finally to the everyday “to leave.” The same root produced ~acquitter~, ~quittance~ (“a receipt”), and ~quiétude~, and is buried in English ~acquit~ and ~requite~. A fitting irony hides in the chain: a verb that now means restless departure, “to leave,” was born from a word meaning perfect tranquillity.
en.raconter:
From Old French ~raconter~ (attested c. 1130–40), formed with the prefix ~re-~ on ~aconter~, itself ~a-~ (from Latin ~ad-~) plus ~conter~ (“to tell, to count”). ~Conter~ descends from Latin ~computare~ (“to calculate, to reckon”), a compound of ~com-~ and ~putare~ (“to reckon, to estimate”). The very same Latin verb gave French its doublet ~compter~ (“to count”), so ~conter~ and ~compter~ are etymological twins, split apart by spelling and sense. Cognates include Spanish ~contar~ and Italian ~contare~, which likewise mean both “to count” and “to tell,” and English ~count~ and ~compute~.

The semantic thread is the link between enumerating and narrating: to “tell” a tale was once to “count off” its events one by one — English preserves the same metaphor in a bank ~teller~, who tells out money. ~Raconter~ adds an intensifying ~re-~ to ~aconter~, yielding “to recount, to relate in full.” Over time ~conter~ specialized toward fiction (a ~conte~ is a tale or fairy story), while ~raconter~ became the everyday verb for telling what happened. From the same ~computare~ flow ~compte~, ~comptable~, ~conte~, ~conteur~, and the learned ~computer~ (“to compute”).
en.rajouter:
Compound of the prefix ~re-~ and ~ajouter~ (“to add”). ~Ajouter~ comes from Old French ~ajoster~ (“to bring together, place side by side”), built on Latin ~juxta~ (“near, beside”) — the same word behind ~juxtaposer~. So to add was originally to set one thing beside another. ~Juxta~ is related to the family of ~jungere~ (“to join”), from Proto-Indo-European *~yewg-~ (“to join, yoke”), cognate with English ~yoke~ and ~join~.

Old French ~ajoster~ meant to assemble or unite; over time it narrowed to the arithmetic and everyday sense “to add, to put in addition.” Prefixing ~re-~ produced ~rajouter~, which adds again, puts back, or tops up — ~rajouter du sel~ is to add yet more salt. The two verbs coexist with a fine distinction much policed by purists: ~ajouter~ for a first addition, ~rajouter~ for a further one, though in speech ~rajouter~ has spread well beyond that rule. The buried root ~juxta~ also yields French ~juxtaposition~ and the borrowed adverb ~juxta~ in scholarly usage, while the deeper *~yewg-~ underlies ~joindre~, ~jonction~, and ~conjuguer~ — the very act of conjugation being a “yoking together” of forms.
en.ramener:
A French compound built from ~re-~ (here marking a return to a starting point) and ~amener~ (“to bring, to lead toward”), which itself is ~à~ (“to”) plus ~mener~ (“to lead, to drive”). The verb ~mener~ descends from Vulgar Latin *~minare~ (“to drive animals along, to herd”), a sense derived from Classical Latin ~minārī~ (“to threaten, to brandish”) — the herdsman drove his cattle forward with shouts and threats. Spanish and Italian preserve the same image in ~menar~ and ~menare~.

The path from “threaten” to “lead” runs through the drover: to threaten an ox into motion is to make it go where you wish, and so *~minare~ slid from menacing to simply conducting. French ~mener~ kept the neutral sense “to lead,” and its prefixed forms map out directions of travel: ~amener~ (lead toward), ~emmener~ (lead away), ~promener~ (lead about, hence “to walk, stroll”). With ~re-~ added, ~ramener~ means to bring someone or something back to where it began — and figuratively to restore, to draw back, or, in slang, to ~ramener sa fraise~ (“to butt in”). The same root underlies ~menace~ and ~ménager~.
en.rappeler:
Built in French from the prefix ~re-~ (“again, back”) and the verb ~appeler~ (“to call”). ~Appeler~ descends from Latin ~appellāre~ (“to address, to call by name”), a frequentative-style derivative of ~appellere~, ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) + ~pellere~ (“to push, to drive”), from PIE *~pelh₂-~ (“to approach, to drive”). To call someone was, in the root image, to “drive” your voice toward them. Through Old French ~apeler~, the same Latin verb gave English ~appeal~ and ~appellation~; Romance cousins include Italian ~appellare~ and Spanish ~apelar~.

The literal sense of ~rappeler~ is “to call back”: a general ~rappelle~ his troops, a country ~rappelle~ its ambassador, and one ~rappelle~ a phone caller. From summoning a person back came summoning a thought back — ~rappeler~ now most commonly means “to remind” or, reflexively in ~se rappeler~, “to remember,” literally to call something back into one's own mind. The noun ~rappel~ covers a striking spread of these: a reminder, an encore at a concert, a booster vaccine, a back-payment of wages, and the mountaineer's ~rappel~ (abseiling, “calling oneself back down” the rope). The shared root surfaces across French ~appeler~, ~appel~, ~rappel~, ~interpeller~ (“to call out to, to question”), and the borrowed ~appellation~.
en.rapporter:
Attested from the twelfth century, ~rapporter~ is formed from ~apporter~ (“to bring”) with the prefix ~re-~, marking a bringing back. ~Apporter~ comes from Latin ~apportāre~ (~ad-~ “to” + ~portāre~ “to carry”), and ~portāre~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to lead, to pass over, to carry across”). Cognate with Italian ~portare~ and Spanish ~portar~, and — through the same root — with English ~port~, ~portable~, and ~transport~.

The literal core is to carry a thing back to its place, which gives the everyday sense of retrieving or returning. From there the word branched widely: a hunting dog ~rapporte~ the game; a witness ~rapporte~ what was said, hence “to report”; a child who ~rapporte~ on a classmate is a tattletale; and money or land that ~rapporte~ “brings in” a yield — the source of ~rapport~ as “revenue” and, more abstractly, as “relation, connection.” That same carrying root *~per-~ runs through ~porter~, ~apporter~, ~emporter~, ~importer~, ~exporter~, ~port~, and ~portique~ — a whole family built on the simple act of bearing something from one place to another.
en.rapprocher:
A transparent compound of the prefix ~re-~ (here marking repetition and intensification, “again, anew”) and the verb ~approcher~ (“to approach, to bring near”). ~approcher~ itself descends from Late Latin ~appropiāre~ (“to draw near to”), formed from ~ad-~ (“to”) and the adverb ~prope~ (“near”), the same ~prope~ that yields French ~proche~ (“near, close”). The Old French form was ~aprochier~, attested from the twelfth century; English ~approach~ is borrowed from this same Old French verb, so the two languages share the ancestor.

Where ~approcher~ simply brings something near, ~rapprocher~ adds the idea of bringing things near once more or closer together — two people reconciled, two ideas compared, two dates moved nearer. From this comes the rich figurative life of the word: a ~rapprochement~ between former enemies, a diplomatic thaw, is one of the best-known French exports to other languages, English among them. Modern derivatives include the noun ~rapprochement~, the adjective ~rapprochable~, and the military expression ~garde rapprochée~ (“close protection”). At bottom, every use of ~rapprocher~ replays the humble Latin ~prope~: the act of making two things ~proche~ that were not so near before.
en.rassembler:
Formed from the verb ~assembler~ (“to assemble, gather”) with the prefix ~re-~, expressing repetition or intensification. ~Assembler~ comes from Vulgar Latin *~assimulāre~ (“to bring together, make alike”), built on ~ad-~ (“to, toward”) and a stem related to ~simul~ (“together”) and ~similis~ (“like, similar”). These trace to the PIE root *~sem-~ (“one, together”). Through that root ~rassembler~ is cognate with English ~same~, ~similar~, and ~assemble~ itself, and with Greek ~homós~ (“same”); Italian keeps ~assembrare~. The buried idea is gathering many into a likeness, a oneness.

Where ~assembler~ joins parts together, the ~re-~ of ~rassembler~ adds a sense of pulling scattered things back into one — regathering a dispersed crowd, mustering troops, collecting one's thoughts. Hence its slightly more emphatic, more deliberate ring: ~rassembler ses idées~ (“to collect one's thoughts”), ~rassembler une foule~ (“to gather a crowd”). The political noun ~rassemblement~ (“rally, gathering”) grew from it. The unifying root *~sem-~ runs through a remarkable spread of French: ~sembler~, ~ensemble~, ~assemblée~, ~simple~ (“one-fold”), ~singulier~, and ~simultané~ all descend from the ancient sense of oneness and togetherness that *~sem-~ names.
en.recevoir:
From Old French ~recevoir~, from Latin ~recipere~ (“to take back, to receive”), a compound of the prefix ~re-~ (“back, again”) and ~capere~ (“to take, to seize”). ~capere~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~keh₂p-~ (“to grasp, to seize”), through which ~recevoir~ is cognate with Ancient Greek ~káptō~ (“to gulp down”) and, on the Germanic side, with English ~have~ and ~heave~ — both, surprisingly, relatives of this Latin verb of taking. The shift from ~recipere~ to French ~recevoir~ follows the regular Latin ~-cipere~ to French ~-cevoir~ pattern.

Latin ~recipere~ meant to take back to oneself, hence to accept what is offered or returned. French kept the core sense of accepting and added social and abstract ones: to ~recevoir~ a guest (to welcome), to ~recevoir~ a blow, to ~recevoir~ a diploma. The root ~capere~ is one of the most fertile in French, surfacing in ~capable~, ~capter~, ~captif~, ~recette~ (a doublet of ~recevoir~'s noun), ~réception~, ~concevoir~, ~décevoir~, ~percevoir~, and ~apercevoir~. The verbs in ~-cevoir~ form a tidy set, all the same Latin ~capere~ taken back, taken up, taken in, or taken away by their prefixes.
en.rechercher:
From Old French ~cerchier~ (later Middle French ~chercher~), the source of modern ~chercher~ (“to look for, to search”), with the iterative prefix ~re-~. The base verb descends from Late Latin ~circāre~ (“to go around, to roam in search of”), from ~circa~ (“around”), itself from ~circus~ (“ring, circle”), tied to the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)ker-~ (“to turn, to bend”). The same Latin family gave Spanish ~cercar~ and Italian ~cercare~ (“to look for”), and, through ~circulus~, English ~circle~ and ~circus~.

The path of meaning is geometric at heart: one who searches once “went around” a place, and ~chercher~ originally meant “to roam, to ransack.” In the seventeenth century it displaced the older ~querre~ (which survives as ~quérir~). Prefixed with ~re-~, ~rechercher~ both intensifies and repeats: it can mean “to search thoroughly, to seek out” and “to look for again,” the second loop around the circle. From this productive ~re-~ comes the noun ~recherche~ (“research, inquiry”), borrowed into English as ~research~, and the adjective ~recherché~ (“refined, sought-after”). The underlying *~(s)ker-~ also turns up in ~cercle~, ~cerceau~, and ~recherche~ itself — a whole vocabulary spun from the idea of going round.
en.recommander:
From Old French ~comander~ (“to entrust, to command”), reinforced with the prefix ~re-~, from Late Latin ~commendare~ (a variant of ~commandare~), from ~com-~ (intensive) plus ~mandare~ (“to entrust, to commit to one's charge”). ~mandare~ is itself a compound of ~manus~ (“hand”) and ~dare~ (“to give”), so its literal sense is “to place into someone's hand.” ~manus~ goes back to PIE *~man-~ (“hand”) and ~dare~ to PIE *~deh₃-~ (“to give”). Cognate with Italian ~raccomandare~ and Spanish ~recomendar~, and, through ~commendare~, with English ~recommend~ and ~commend~.

The doubled prefix is telling: to ~recommander~ was first to “entrust again” or “entrust earnestly,” and from that idea of warmly committing a person or thing to another's care grew the modern sense of speaking in someone's favor, of endorsing. The military sense of ~commander~ — to give orders — comes from the same root, the commander being the one into whose hand authority is given. The “hand-giving” core of ~mandare~ seeds a wide family in French: ~commander~, ~demander~, ~mander~, ~mandat~, and ~commande~, alongside the learned ~mandataire~. Even English ~mandate~ and ~command~ trace back to that same Latin image of a charge placed, quite literally, in the palm.
en.reconnaître:
From Latin ~recognoscere~ (“to know again, to recall, to review”), ~re-~ (“again”) + ~cognoscere~ (“to get to know, to learn”), itself ~co(m)-~ (“thoroughly”) + ~gnoscere~ (“to know”), from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ (“to know, to recognize”). French passed through Middle French ~recognoistre~, then ~reconnoistre~ and ~reconnoître~, before settling on ~reconnaître~ (and since the 1990 reform, optionally ~reconnaitre~). The root *~ǵneh₃-~ is one of the most fertile in Indo-European, giving English ~know~ and the ~-gnostic~ of ~agnostic~ and ~diagnosis~, Greek ~gignṓskein~, and the Romance cousins Italian ~riconoscere~ and Spanish ~reconocer~.

The Latin idea was iterative: to ~recognoscere~ was to know a second time, to identify something already encountered — which is exactly the modern ~reconnaître un visage~ (“to recognize a face”). From there the verb branched into the moral and social: to ~reconnaître~ a fault is to admit it, to ~reconnaître~ a child or a state is to acknowledge it officially, and a person who shows gratitude is ~reconnaissant~ — for to be thankful is, etymologically, to “acknowledge” a kindness received. The military scout's ~reconnaissance~ (also borrowed into English) is yet another sense: knowing the terrain in advance. The same root threads through French ~connaître~, ~connaissance~, ~reconnaissance~, ~méconnaître~ (“to fail to recognize”), and the learned ~diagnostic~.
en.recruter:
A denominal verb from the late seventeenth century, formed from ~recrue~ (“fresh troops, a new recruit”), the past participle of the older verb ~recroître~ (“to grow again, to grow back”). ~Recroître~ is ~re-~ plus ~croître~, from Latin ~crēscere~ (“to grow”), from Proto-Indo-European *~ḱer-~ (“to grow”). Cognate, through ~crēscere~, with English ~crescent~ and ~increase~ and with the Roman harvest-goddess ~Cerēs~.

The logic is military and botanical at once: a ~recrue~ was the body of men by which an army was made to “grow again” after losses, and ~recruter~ meant to gather such reinforcements. The word's birth was controversial — purists long condemned ~recruter~ as a clumsy denominal coinage, the way ~solutionner~ is mocked today — yet it survived and broadened to any enlisting, from soldiers to volunteers to employees. From the same *~ḱer-~ / ~crēscere~ root French keeps a vigorous family of growth-words: ~croître~, ~croissant~, ~accroître~, ~décroître~, ~accroissement~, and ~crue~ (a river's “rise,” a flood). To ~recruter~ is, at root, to make a force grow once more.
en.recueillir:
From Old French ~recoillir~, ~recueillir~, from Latin ~recolligere~ (“to gather again, to collect, to reunite”), formed from ~re-~ (“again”) and ~colligere~ (“to gather together”). ~colligere~ is itself ~com-~ (“together”) + ~legere~ (“to gather, to pick, to read”), which descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~leg-~ (“to collect, to gather”). The same root gave Greek ~légein~ (“to gather, to say”) and, through Latin ~colligere~, English ~collect~ and ~cull~.

The primitive sense is the careful picking-up of things one by one — gleaning, harvesting, gathering. ~recolligere~ added the nuance of gathering back together what was scattered, and modern ~recueillir~ ranges over this whole field: one ~recueille~ rainwater, testimony, votes, a stray animal taken in, or — most tellingly — oneself, in ~se recueillir~, “to collect oneself, to gather one's thoughts in meditation or prayer.” From ~legere~ and its compounds French inherited a great harvest of words: ~cueillir~ (“to pick, to gather”), ~accueillir~ (“to welcome”), ~collecte~ and ~collection~, ~recueil~ (“an anthology,” a gathering of texts), ~élire~ and ~élection~ (to “choose out”), and ~lire~ itself, since reading was once the gathering up of letters with the eye.
en.refaire:
From the prefix ~re-~ (“again, back”) and ~faire~ (“to do, to make”). ~faire~ descends from Latin ~facere~ (“to make, to do”), which goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to set, to put, to place”). That root is astonishingly widespread: it gives Greek ~tithenai~ (“to put”), German ~tun~ and English ~do~, and within French the vast ~faire~ family — ~défaire~, ~satisfaire~, ~parfaire~, and the suffix in ~confiture~ and ~facile~.

~refaire~ means to do or make again — to redo a calculation, remake a bed, rebuild a wall. But French stretched it cheerfully into the figurative and the slang. One can ~se refaire~ — recoup one's losses at the gaming table, or restore one's health and spirits. More mischievously, ~se faire refaire~ can mean to be swindled, the “re-making” turned against the victim. The deep root *~dʰeh₁-~ originally meant not “do” but “put, set in place” — making was conceived as setting a thing down in the world — and that placing sense survives in cousins like ~thèse~ and ~thème~, both Greek borrowings from ~tithenai~. So ~refaire~, the homeliest of verbs, ultimately reaches back to one of humanity's oldest words for putting something where it belongs.
en.refuser:
From a blend of two Latin verbs. Old French had ~reüser~, regularly descended from Latin ~recūsāre~ (“to object to, to decline”), formed from ~re-~ (“back, against”) and ~causa~ (“cause, legal case”). But the modern form shows an ~f~ that ~recūsāre~ cannot supply; philologists since Diez have explained it as a contamination by Latin ~refūtāre~ (“to drive back, to refute”), built from ~re-~ + ~-fūtāre~ (“to beat”), perhaps from the Proto-Indo-European root *~bʰau-~ (“to strike”). French ~refuser~ thus carries the ~f~ of one verb and the ~s~ of the other.

The two ancestors are visible across the Romance languages: Italian ~rifiutare~ continues ~refūtāre~, while Spanish ~rehusar~ and Occitan ~refusar~ pattern with the French blend. The meaning has stayed close to its roots — to push back, to decline, to reject a request, an offer, or admittance — and figuratively to deny or withhold (« refuser son aide »). The pure ~refūtāre~ line survives separately in learned French as ~réfuter~ (“to refute”), so the same Latin verb reaches modern French twice: once openly in ~réfuter~, once hidden inside ~refuser~.
en.regarder:
From Middle French ~regarder~, from Old French ~regarder~ (also ~reguarder~), formed from the verb ~garder~ (“to guard, keep, watch over”) with the prefix ~re-~. ~garder~ comes from Late Latin *~wardāre~, borrowed from Frankish *~wardōn~ (“to watch, keep guard”), from Proto-Germanic *~wardōną~, ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~wer-~ (“to perceive, watch out for”). Through this Germanic line, ~regarder~ is kin to English ~ward~, ~warden~, and ~guard~ (the last itself reborrowed from French), and to German ~warten~ (“to wait”).

The sense moved from “to keep, watch over” toward “to direct one's eyes at.” The prefix ~re-~ here does not mean “again” but adds a sense of attentive, directed looking, so ~regarder~ became “to look at, to watch” — to turn one's guarding gaze onto something. A trace of the older meaning survives in the idiom ~cela ne te regarde pas~ (“that doesn't concern you,” literally “that doesn't watch over you”). From the same root come the noun ~regard~ (“gaze, look”), ~garder~, ~gardien~, ~garde~, and ~égard~ (“regard, consideration”). The shift from guarding to seeing is intuitive: to watch over something, you must keep your eyes on it.
en.regretter:
From earlier ~regreter~ (also ~regrater~), formed with the prefix ~re-~ on a Germanic verb of weeping: Old Norse ~gráta~ (“to weep, lament, bewail”), phonetically reinforced by Old English ~grētan~ of the same sense, rather than from Old Low Frankish *~greotan~. The Germanic root is cognate with Gothic ~grētan~ and survives in modern English dialect ~greet~ (“to weep,” in Scots), all from a Proto-Germanic verb for crying out in grief.

The striking thing about ~regretter~ is that, unlike most French verbs, its core is Germanic, not Latin: the Norse and Scandinavian raiders and settlers who reached the French coasts left behind a word for lamentation. Old French ~regreter~ meant to bewail, especially to mourn the dead — to lament loudly over a loss. From this funerary wailing the meaning softened into the modern range: to miss someone or something, to be sorry, to regret a decision. French still distinguishes ~regretter quelqu'un~ (“to miss a person”) from regretting an act. The Germanic origin left few French relatives; the chief descendant is the noun ~regret~ itself, which English in turn borrowed back — a word that crossed the Channel twice.
en.regrouper:
From ~re-~ (here a prefix of repetition and intensification) and ~grouper~ (“to group, to gather”), a verb attested from the late eighteenth century and formed from the noun ~groupe~. ~Groupe~ itself was borrowed from Italian ~gruppo~ (“knot, cluster, bunch”), which traces back through a Germanic source — Frankish *~kruppa~ — to Proto-Germanic *~kruppaz~ (“rounded mass, lump, body”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~grewb-~ (“to crumple, to bend, to curl up”). Cognate with German ~Kropf~ (“crop, craw, bulge”) and with English ~crop~.

The image at the heart of the word is concrete: a ~groupe~ was first a rounded mass of figures bunched together, a term of painting and sculpture before it became the abstract grouping of people or things. French built the verb ~grouper~ from it, then prefixed ~re-~ to mark a fresh act of gathering — bringing scattered elements back into one mass. The redoubled sense suits its modern uses, from regrouping troops to consolidating data. From the same Italian-Germanic stock French keeps ~groupe~, ~groupement~, ~groupuscule~, and ~regroupement~, while the financial sense of the old Italian word (a “bag of money”) is a reminder that a group was, from the start, simply things bound up together.
en.rejeter:
Attested from the twelfth century, ~rejeter~ comes from Latin ~rejectare~ (“to throw back, hurl back, vomit”), the frequentative of ~rejicere~ — itself the prefix ~re-~ (“back, again”) joined to ~jacere~ (“to throw”). Latin ~jacere~ goes back to a Proto-Indo-European root *~yeh₁-~ (“to throw, cast”). The family includes Italian ~rigettare~ and Spanish ~rechazar~, and through ~jacere~ the English borrowings ~reject~ and ~eject~.

The literal sense — throwing something back — fans out across modern French. The sea ~rejette~ debris onto the shore; a body ~rejette~ a transplanted organ; a plant ~rejette~ new shoots from its base (hence the noun ~rejet~, a sucker or offshoot); a committee ~rejette~ a proposal; and one may ~rejeter~ the blame onto another. The base verb ~jacere~, throw, is astonishingly prolific in French through its participle ~jactus~ / ~jectus~: ~jeter~ itself, ~jet~, ~projet~ and ~projeter~, ~sujet~, ~objet~, ~trajet~, ~injecter~, and ~adjacent~. Whether you reject an idea or skip a stone, you are, etymologically, simply casting something — and in ~rejeter~, casting it right back where it came from.
en.rejoindre:
From ~joindre~ with the prefix ~re-~ (“again, back”). ~Joindre~ descends from Latin ~iungere~ (~jungere~, “to join, to yoke, to harness”), from Proto-Italic *~jungō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~yewg-~ (“to join, to yoke”). This is one of the most transparent of all Indo-European roots: it gives Sanskrit ~yoga~ (“union”), Ancient Greek ~ζεύγνυμι~ (zeúgnumi, “to yoke”), English ~yoke~, and German ~Joch~ — and, through Latin, English ~join~ and ~junction~.

The literal image is agricultural: ~iungere~ meant to fit two oxen under a single ~iugum~, a yoke. From joining beasts it generalized to joining anything; ~re-~ then adds the nuance of coming back together. So ~rejoindre~ means to join up with, to catch up to, or to be reunited with — to re-yoke. French keeps a large ~yewg-~ family: ~joindre~, ~jointure~, ~conjoint~, ~adjoindre~, ~disjoindre~, and ~jonction~, alongside the inherited ~joug~ (“yoke”) and the learned ~conjuguer~ — for to conjugate a verb is, etymologically, to yoke its forms together, a fitting root for a verb-learning app.
en.relever:
French ~relever~ is the prefix ~re-~ (“again, back up”) joined to ~lever~ (“to lift, to raise”), and is also continued directly from Latin ~relevāre~ (“to lift again, to lighten, to relieve”). ~Lever~ descends from Latin ~levāre~ (“to raise, to make light”), a verb built on the adjective ~levis~ (“light, not heavy”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ (“light, nimble”). The same root surfaces in English ~light~ (the adjective) and ~lever~, and in the family of ~levity~.

The two senses of Latin ~levāre~ — “to lift up” and “to lighten” — both flow into French. ~Relever~ keeps the first vividly: to raise something fallen, to pick up, to stand back up (~se relever~). But the “lighten/relieve” strand survives too, in ~relever la garde~ (“to relieve the watch,” to take over so others may rest) and in nuance, where ~relever~ a dish means to heighten and brighten its flavour. The shared root has been astonishingly productive in French: ~élever~, ~soulever~, ~enlever~, ~prélever~, the noun ~levée~, and ~levain~ (“leaven,” which makes dough rise), alongside the borrowed ~levant~ (the east, where the sun “rises”). To raise, to lighten, to leaven, to relieve — all are one gesture of lifting.
en.relier:
From ~re-~ + ~lier~ (“to tie, bind”), the verb ~lier~ descending from Latin ~ligāre~ (“to bind, tie, fasten”), from Proto-Indo-European *~leyǵ-~ (“to bind, tie”). Cognate with Italian ~legare~ and Spanish ~ligar~, and — through the same ~ligāre~ — with English ~ligament~, ~liable~, and ~ally~.

The ~re-~ here carries the sense “back together,” so ~relier~ is to bind things one to another — to ~connect~, link, or join. The root threw off a strikingly broad family. From ~ligāre~ directly come ~lier~, ~liaison~, ~lien~, and ~liasse~; with ~ad-~, ~allier~ (“to ally,” to bind to) and its noun ~alliage~; with ~ob-~, ~obliger~ (“to oblige,” literally to bind toward); and the much-discussed ~religion~ — explained already by Roman writers as either a “binding back” of the soul to the gods or a careful “re-reading.” A neat near-pair sits in modern French: ~relier~ also names the craft of ~bookbinding~, in which loose sheets are bound back into a single volume — the etymology made literal on a workbench, gathering scattered leaves into one tied-together whole.
en.remarquer:
Formed in French from the prefix ~re-~ (here intensive) and the verb ~marquer~ (“to mark”). ~Marquer~ entered French from Italian ~marcare~, reinforcing an older native ~merchier~, and ultimately rests on a Germanic word — Frankish *~marka~ / Proto-Germanic *~markō~ (“boundary, sign, mark”), the same source as English ~mark~ and ~march~ (a border region) and as French ~marche~ (“frontier march”).

To ~marquer~ is to set a sign upon something; to ~remarquer~ is, etymologically, to “mark it again,” to mark it out a second time so that it stands out to the eye. From this came the two living senses: to ~remarquer~ is both to notice (the thing marks itself out to your attention) and to observe aloud, to remark upon. The noun ~remarque~ followed, and the adjective ~remarquable~ describes precisely what is “worth marking out.” The Germanic root proved astonishingly fertile in French: ~marque~, ~marquer~, ~démarquer~, ~marqueur~, ~démarche~, and ~marquis~ (originally the lord of a border ~marche~) all carry, somewhere within them, the ancient sense of a boundary traced and a sign set down.
en.remercier:
From Old French ~mercier~ (“to thank, to express gratitude”), with the prefix ~re-~ here intensive rather than iterative. ~Mercier~ is built on ~merci~, from Latin ~mercedem~, accusative of ~merces~ (“wages, price, reward”). In late and Christian Latin, ~merces~ slid from “reward” to “favour, grace, mercy” — the heavenly reward — and Old French ~merci~ carried both “mercy” (as in ~à la merci de~, “at the mercy of”) and “grace, favour.”

The path from “mercy” to “thanks” runs through gratitude for a favour granted: to say ~merci~ was to acknowledge a kindness received, and ~remercier~ became the standard verb for rendering that acknowledgment. A pointed modern wrinkle: French uses ~remercier~ as a polite euphemism for sacking an employee — to “thank” someone for their services is to dismiss them, gratitude doubling as the door. The Latin ~merces~ kept its commercial sense in a parallel French family: ~mercenaire~ (“mercenary”), ~commerce~, ~merci~ itself, and ~marché~ — all rooted in the idea of payment, price, and exchange.
en.remettre:
From Old French ~remetre~, from Latin ~remittere~ (“to send back, to let go, to relax”), composed of ~re-~ (“back, again”) and ~mittere~ (“to send, to release, to throw”). ~Mittere~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~meitō~ and to the Proto-Indo-European root *~meyth₂-~ (“to exchange, remove”), an extension of *~mey-~ (“to change”). Its Romance reflexes are everywhere — Italian ~mettere~, Spanish ~meter~ — and through Latin ~missa~ it gave English ~mass~, ~mission~, and ~message~.

French ~mettre~ shifted from “to send” to the broad “to put, to place,” and ~remettre~ inherited a remarkable spread of meanings from that base: to put back (~remettre en place~), to hand over (~remettre une lettre~), to postpone (~remettre à demain~), and, echoing the Latin sense of “letting go,” to forgive — ~remettre une dette~ (“to remit a debt”), the very root of English ~remit~ and ~remission~. From the same ~mittere~ family French draws a rich harvest: ~admettre~, ~permettre~, ~promettre~, ~soumettre~, ~émettre~, plus ~message~, ~mission~, and ~messe~ — the Mass, named from the dismissal that closed the service.
en.remonter:
Attested from the twelfth century, ~remonter~ is formed from the verb ~monter~ (“to go up, to climb, to mount”) with the prefix ~re-~, which here marks either repetition (“to climb again”) or a return to a former position (“to go back up”). ~Monter~ comes from Vulgar Latin *~montāre~, derived from Latin ~mōns~, ~montem~ (“mountain”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~men-~ (“to project, to stand up”). Cognate with Italian ~montare~ and Spanish ~montar~, and — through ~mōns~ — with English ~mount~ and ~mountain~.

The striking thing is that to “go up” is, etymologically, to “mountain”: the verb was coined straight from the noun for a peak, the very image of something projecting upward. ~Re-~ then layers on a second motion — climbing anew, going back up a slope or a staircase, but also winding up a clock or watch (remonter une montre), reassembling parts (remonter un moteur), tracing a river or a history upstream (remonter le temps), and reviving someone's spirits (remonter le moral). From ~mōns~ French keeps ~mont~, ~montagne~, ~montée~, ~monture~, ~amont~, and ~surmonter~ — a whole landscape risen from a single word for a height.
en.remplacer:
A French formation rather than an inherited Latin word: ~remplacer~ is built from the prefix ~re-~ and the verb ~emplacer~ (“to put in place”), itself ~en-~ (“in”) plus ~place~ — so the literal sense is “to put back in the place (of another).” The noun ~place~ descends from Latin ~platea~ (“broad street, open space”), borrowed from Ancient Greek ~plateîa~ (“broad way”), the feminine of ~platús~ (“broad, flat”), from a Proto-Indo-European *~pleth₂-~ (“flat, to spread”). Through the same ~platea~ come Italian ~piazza~, Spanish ~plaza~, and English ~place~.

The word thus contains a small history of substitution: to set one thing back into the space another occupied. From this came the sense of standing in for someone — a ~remplaçant~ is a substitute or stand-in — and the abstract notion of one thing superseding another. The deep root *~pleth₂-~ (“flat, broad”) underlies a surprising spread of French vocabulary: ~place~ and ~placer~, ~plat~ (“flat, a dish”), ~plaine~ (“plain”), ~plan~ (“flat surface, plan”), and ~plateau~ all carry the idea of flatness or spreading out, and English ~flat~, ~plate~, and ~plaza~ belong to the same family.
en.remplir:
From the twelfth century, a compound of the prefix ~re-~ and ~emplir~ (“to fill”). ~Emplir~ comes from Latin ~implere~ (becoming ~implīre~ in Late Latin), itself ~in-~ (“into”) plus ~plere~ (“to fill”), related to ~plenus~ (“full”). All trace to the Proto-Indo-European root *~pleh₁-~ (“to fill”), which also gave English ~full~ and ~fill~, German ~voll~, Greek ~plērēs~ (“full”), and Sanskrit ~pūrṇa~.

Here ~re-~ does not really mean “again”: it intensifies, so ~remplir~ simply means “to fill completely,” and it has all but replaced the simpler ~emplir~ in everyday French. The verb spans the concrete and the abstract: one fills a glass (~remplir un verre~), fills in a form (~remplir un formulaire~), and fulfills a duty or condition (~remplir une obligation~) — the same metaphor of completion English keeps in ~fulfill~. From ~plenus~ and *~pleh₁-~ comes a broad family of French words: ~plein~, ~plénitude~, ~complet~, ~complément~, ~supplément~, ~plus~, and the borrowed ~plénier~ — a whole cluster turning on the notion of fullness and abundance.
en.remporter:
From ~emporter~ (“to carry away”) with the prefix ~re-~. ~Emporter~ is itself ~porter~ (“to carry”) plus the prefix ~en-~ (Latin ~inde~, “away from there”). ~Porter~ comes from Latin ~portare~ (“to carry”), tied to ~porta~ (“gate”) and ~portus~ (“harbor, passage”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to lead, to pass over”). Cognates from this root include Greek ~poros~ (“passage”) and English ~fare~ and ~ford~.

~Emporter~ means “to carry off, to take away with oneself”; adding ~re-~ gives both “to carry back” and the idiomatic “to carry off again,” but its liveliest modern sense is figurative: ~remporter~ une victoire, un prix, un succès — “to carry off, to win” a victory or prize, as one bears away the trophy. The construction ~l'emporter sur~ (“to prevail over”) belongs to the same image of carrying the day. From ~portare~ flows a vast family: ~porter~, ~porte~, ~port~, ~porteur~, ~portable~, ~apporter~, ~exporter~, ~importer~, ~transporter~, ~comporter~, and ~rapporter~ — all built on the simple act of carrying.
en.renaître:
From the prefix ~re-~ (“again”) and ~naître~ (“to be born”). ~naître~ comes through Old French ~naistre~ from Vulgar Latin *~nascere~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~nāscī~ (“to be born”), itself from earlier *~gnāscī~ and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ǵenh₁-~ (“to beget, to give birth”). That root is one of the most fertile in the family, producing Latin ~genus~ and ~gignere~, English ~kin~, ~kind~, and ~genus~, and the Romance siblings Spanish ~nacer~ and Italian ~nascere~.

~renaître~ means literally to be born a second time, and French uses it with the full weight of that image. A landscape ~renaît~ in spring; hope, courage, or a city ~renaît~ from its ashes — the verb carries an idea of return from apparent death that the plain ~naître~ lacks. It is the everyday word behind the ~Renaissance~, the “re-birth” of art and learning. Defectively, ~renaître~ famously has no past participle in standard use, so one cannot easily say “has been reborn” — a fitting grammatical gap for a verb about endless beginning again. From the same root *~ǵenh₁-~ French draws ~genre~, ~nature~, ~nation~, ~génération~, and ~gendre~ (“son-in-law”), a whole vocabulary of birth, kind, and kinship.
en.rencontrer:
From Old French ~encontrer~ (“to come up against, to meet”), itself derived from ~encontre~ (“an encounter, a meeting”), from Late Latin ~incontrā~, built from ~in-~ (“in, against”) + Latin ~contrā~ (“facing, against, opposite”). The verb ~rencontrer~ adds the prefix ~re-~ to ~encontrer~ and is attested from the 14th century. The base ~contrā~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~komterād~, a feminine form meaning “the opposite one of two,” from PIE *~kom-~ (“with, together”) plus the comparative suffix *~-teros~ — making ~contrā~ a doublet of Latin ~cum~. Cognate with Italian ~incontrare~ and Spanish ~encontrar~.

The oldest French uses were martial: around 1175 ~encontrer~ meant “to clash, to do battle,” for to meet an adversary head-on is to oppose him. The softer sense “to come upon by chance” developed alongside, and once the reinforcing ~re-~ was added, ~rencontrer~ settled into its modern range — to meet a person, to come across a thing, to confront a difficulty. The reflexive ~se rencontrer~ can even mean “to coincide,” two minds meeting on the same thought. The “against” core of ~contrā~ remains visible in the noun ~rencontre~ (“an encounter,” but also “a clash”) and in the fixed phrase ~à l'encontre de~ (“contrary to”). The same root underlies a great brood of French words: ~contre~, ~contraire~, ~contredire~, ~rencontre~, and the borrowed ~contraste~.
en.rendre:
From Vulgar Latin *~rendere~ (“to give back”), an altered form of Classical Latin ~reddere~ (“to give back, to return”). ~reddere~ is itself ~red-~ (“back”) plus ~dare~ (“to give”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~deh₃-~ (“to give”) — the same root that gives Latin ~dōnum~ (“gift”) and English ~donor~ and ~date~. The irregular ~-nd-~ of the popular form almost certainly arose by contamination from ~prendere~ (“to take, to seize”), so that the verb for “giving back” reshaped itself on the verb for “taking.” Cognate verbs survive as Italian ~rendere~ and Spanish ~rendir~, and the family entered English as ~render~ and ~rent~.

The core idea is restitution — to hand back what one received — and French ~rendre~ still means “to give back” or “to return” something. From there the verb fanned out remarkably: to “render” an account, to “render” a service, to “render” a verdict, and, in the construction ~rendre quelqu'un heureux~, “to make someone happy” — to give them a quality, as it were. The reflexive ~se rendre~ even means “to surrender” or “to go (somewhere),” to take oneself off. From ~reddere~ and ~red-~ come French ~rendement~ (“yield, output”), ~reddition~ (“surrender”), and the noun ~rente~ (“income, annuity”), a sum regularly “given back.”
en.renforcer:
Derived from Old French ~enforcier~ (“to make strong”), formed with the prefix ~re-~ (here intensive or iterative). ~enforcier~ is itself ~en-~ plus the stem of ~force~, from Vulgar Latin ~fortia~, the neuter plural of Latin ~fortis~ (“strong, sturdy”) taken as a feminine noun. Cognate with Italian ~rinforzare~ and Spanish ~reforzar~, and — through ~fortis~ — with English ~force~ and ~fort~.

The building blocks are transparent: ~fortia~ gave French ~force~, ~en-~ turns the noun into a verb (“to put strength into”), and ~re-~ adds the idea of doing so anew or all the more — to ~renforcer~ is to strengthen what already had some strength. The same ~fortis~ underlies a broad family in French: ~fort~, ~forteresse~, ~fortifier~, ~effort~ (literally a “forcing-out” of strength), ~confort~ (originally a strengthening, a giving of courage), and ~renfort~, the noun of reinforcement. English ~comfort~ borrowed that last sense from Old French before it softened into mere coziness, so that an English ~effort~ and a French ~renfort~ are, at bottom, two ways of summoning the same Latin strength.
en.renouveler:
Formed from Old French ~nouvel~ (“new”) with the prefix ~re-~ (“again”) and a verbal suffix, paralleled by Late Latin ~renovellāre~. Behind ~nouvel~ stands Latin ~novellus~ (“young, new”), a diminutive of ~novus~ (“new”), from Proto-Indo-European *~newos~ (“new”) — the very root of English ~new~, German ~neu~, Ancient Greek ~νέος~ (~néos~), and Sanskrit ~náva~.

The verb means literally “to make new again,” and French has spread it across many domains: one ~renouvelle~ a passport or a subscription (extending its validity), ~renouvelle~ the air in a room, ~renouvelle~ a team or a wardrobe, and even ~se renouvelle~ as an artist by reinventing oneself. The reflexive also covers natural recurrence — a phenomenon that ~se renouvelle~ happens again. Note the doubled ~l~ in conjugation (~je renouvelle~), inherited from the ~-ell-~ of ~novellus~. From the same Latin ~novus~ descend a rich crop of French words: ~nouveau~ and ~nouvelle~, ~nouveauté~, ~renouveau~ (“renewal, revival”), ~rénover~ and ~rénovation~ (a learned doublet), ~innover~ and ~innovation~, and ~novice~.
en.rentrer:
From Old French ~entrer~ (“to enter, to go in”) prefixed with ~re-~, yielding the sense “to enter again, to go back in.” The base verb descends from Latin ~intrāre~ (“to enter, to go into”), itself built on ~inter~ (“among, between, within”) and ultimately on the Proto-Indo-European preposition *~h₁en~ (“in”). Cognates of the underlying family include Italian ~entrare~, Spanish ~entrar~, and — through the same root — English ~enter~ and ~interior~.

The prefix ~re-~ here is doubly productive: sometimes it means “again” (re-enter a room one has left), sometimes simply “back” or “inward,” and in many uses the iterative force has faded entirely, so that ~rentrer~ has become the ordinary verb for “to go home” (~rentrer chez soi~). It also developed transitive senses — to bring something in (~rentrer le foin~, to get the hay in before rain) and to hold something back (~rentrer ses larmes~, to hold back one's tears). From the same stem French built the noun ~rentrée~, used for the return to work or school after the summer holidays, a word with no neat English equivalent. The verb ~entrer~ and its relatives ~entrée~ and ~entremets~ all share the ancestor ~intrāre~.
en.renvoyer:
Formed in French from ~envoyer~ (“to send”) with the iterative prefix ~r-~ (a form of ~re-~, “again, back”). ~Envoyer~ itself comes from Latin ~inviare~ (“to set on the way, to travel”), a compound of ~in-~ (“into, onto”) and ~via~ (“way, road”). The future and conditional stems of ~envoyer~ were reshaped in the 18th century under the pull of ~voir~ (“to see”), which is why French says ~enverra~. ~Via~ survives directly in English ~via~ and in Italian and Spanish ~via~ / ~vía~.

If ~envoyer~ is to put someone or something on the road, ~renvoyer~ is to put them back on it — and the prefix's two readings, “again” and “back,” fan out into a remarkable spread of senses. To ~renvoyer~ can mean to send again, to send back, to return (a ball, a favour, a letter), to refer (a reader to a footnote, a case to another court), to postpone, and — pointedly — to dismiss someone from a job or expel a pupil: putting them, so to speak, back out the door. From the same ~via~ come French ~voie~, ~voyage~, ~dévoyer~, ~convoyer~, and ~envoi~.
en.reposer:
In the “rest” sense, from Old French ~reposer~, from Late Latin ~repausare~, from ~re-~ plus ~pausare~ (“to halt, to cease”), itself from Latin ~pausa~ (“a pause”), borrowed from Greek ~παῦσις~ (“a stopping”), from the verb ~παύειν~ (“to cease”). Cognate with Italian ~riposare~ and Spanish ~reposar~, and — through ~pausa~ — with English ~repose~ and ~pause~.

A tangle sits at the heart of this verb. Late Latin ~pausare~ (“to cease, to rest”) became so entwined with classical ~ponere~ (past participle ~positum~, “to place”) that the two fused: French ~poser~ inherits the form of ~pausare~ but much of the meaning of ~ponere~. That confusion is exactly why ~reposer~ today carries both senses at once — “to rest” (one ~se repose~) and “to put back down” (one ~repose~ a glass on the table). From ~pausare~ flow ~repos~, ~pause~, ~poser~, ~reposant~, and ~reposoir~; and the Greek ~παύειν~ behind it all surfaces in learned French through ~menopause~ and the musical ~pause~ — the silence that, etymologically, is simply a rest.
en.reprendre:
From Old French ~reprendre~ (attested early, “to take back, to blame”), from Latin ~reprehendere~ (“to seize again, to hold back, to reprove”), contracted in Vulgar Latin to ~reprendere~. The word is transparently ~re-~ (“back, again”) + ~prendere~, the popular form of ~prehendere~ (“to grasp, to seize”), itself ~prae-~ (“before”) + ~-hendere~ (“to grasp”). Latin ~prendere~ displaced classical ~capere~ across the Romance languages; cognate with Italian ~riprendere~ and Spanish ~reprender~.

The prefix ~re-~ carries both “again” and “back,” and ~reprendre~ exploits the whole spectrum: to take an object back, to resume an interrupted task (« reprendre le travail »), to take up a theme anew, to recover (« reprendre des forces »), and — preserving the Latin ~reprehendere~ sense of “to hold back, to check” — to reprimand someone. Commerce kept the literal grasp in ~repreneur~ (one who takes over a business) and ~reprise~ (a buy-back, a resumption, even a darned patch of cloth). The learned doublet ~répréhensible~ (“blameworthy”) descends from the same Latin verb, while ~prendre~, ~apprendre~, ~comprendre~, ~surprendre~, and ~entreprendre~ all share the grasping root.
en.reproduire:
Formed within French from ~produire~ (“to produce”) and the prefix ~re-~ (“again, anew”). ~Produire~ comes from Latin ~prōdūcere~ (“to lead forth, to bring forth”), from ~prō-~ (“forward”) and ~dūcere~ (“to lead”), from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ (“to draw, to pull”). Cognate, through *~dewk-~, with English ~tow~ and ~tug~ and German ~ziehen~ (“to pull”), and with the Italian and Spanish counterparts ~riprodurre~ and ~reproducir~.

The core picture is a hand drawing something out into view: to ~produire~ is to “lead forth,” and to ~reproduire~ is to bring it forth *again* — hence “to copy, to depict faithfully, to breed.” The biological sense (animals that ~se reproduisent~) is a metaphor of repeated bringing-forth. From the same ~dūcere~ French inherited a long ~-duire~ family — ~conduire~, ~traduire~, ~séduire~, ~réduire~, ~induire~ — alongside ~duc~ (“duke,” a “leader”), ~conduit~, and ~aqueduc~. So a faithful ~reproduction~ is, etymologically, the same scene led out before the eyes once more.
en.représenter:
From Latin ~repraesentare~, formed from the prefix ~re-~ and ~praesentare~ (“to make present, to present”). That verb derives from ~praesens~, the present participle of ~praesum~ — itself ~prae-~ (“before, in front of”) plus the verb of being, ~esse~ (“to be”). To “present” is thus, literally, to make something “be before” one's eyes; to ~repraesentare~ is to make it present again, to bring it back into view. The Romance descendants line up neatly: Italian ~rappresentare~, Spanish ~representar~, and French ~représenter~.

The Latin verb already carried the double charge that French keeps. In Roman usage ~repraesentare~ could mean “to pay on the spot, to deliver in person,” the sense of literal, physical presence; from there it slid toward “to depict, to portray, to stand for,” a sign standing in the place of an absent thing. Modern ~représenter~ holds both poles at once — an actor ~représente~ a character on stage, a deputy ~représente~ the voters, a painting ~représente~ a landscape. From the same family come ~présent~, ~présenter~, ~présence~, and the noun ~représentation~. To represent is to make the absent present once more.
en.requérir:
French ~requérir~ rests on Latin ~requirere~ (“to seek, to ask after, to need”), the prefix ~re-~ (here intensive, “anew, urgently”) joined to ~quaerere~ (“to seek, to ask, to inquire”). In French the verb was actually rebuilt on the native ~quérir~ (“to fetch, to seek”), modelled on its Latin parent — which is why it keeps the awkward, archaic conjugation of ~quérir~ rather than the smoother shape of a fresh borrowing. ~Quaerere~ is traced to a Proto-Italic *~kʷaizō~ and, more deeply, to the Proto-Indo-European root *~kʷeh₂-~ (“to acquire”). Through ~requirere~, French is cousin to English ~require~ and ~request~.

The sense slid from plain seeking to formal demanding. To ~requérir~ is to call for something with authority or need: a situation ~requiert~ caution, and in court the ~procureur~ (public prosecutor) ~requiert~ a sentence — the technical heart of the word, preserved in the noun ~réquisitoire~ (“closing speech for the prosecution”). Old French ~requerre~ also carried the softer “to entreat, to court,” a shade still felt in ~requête~ (“a request, a petition”). The bare ~quaerere~ seeds a remarkable French harvest: ~quérir~, ~acquérir~ (“to acquire”), ~conquérir~ (“to conquer”), ~s'enquérir~ (“to inquire”), and the nouns ~question~, ~quête~, ~enquête~, and ~conquête~ — a whole vocabulary of seeking radiating from the same root as ~requérir~.
en.respecter:
From Latin ~respectāre~ (“to look back at, to keep in view, to take into account”), the frequentative of ~respicere~ (“to look back, to regard”), itself ~re-~ (“back”) + ~specere~ (“to look”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~spek̑-~ (“to observe, to watch”). The same root, through Latin ~specere~ and its noun ~speciēs~ (“appearance, sort”), gave French ~espèce~, ~spectacle~, and ~inspecter~; through Germanic it surfaces in German ~spähen~ (“to spy”) and English ~spy~.

The metaphor is one of looking: to respect something is, at root, to look back at it and hold it in view. French attests the modern verb only from 1554, in the sense « considérer comme une chose dont on doit tenir compte »; the sense « honorer », to show deference, follows by 1566, and « préserver, ne pas porter atteinte à » by 1573. The deadline sense in English — respecter un délai, « to meet a deadline » — extends the idea of scrupulously observing what is laid down. Older Old French had cousins like ~respitier~ (“to spare”), the ancestor of ~répit~ (“respite”). The same root *~spek̑-~ stands behind a sprawling French family: ~aspect~, ~suspect~, ~perspective~, ~spécial~, and the noun ~respect~ itself.
en.ressembler:
French ~ressembler~ is built within French itself: it is ~sembler~ (“to seem”) plus the intensive prefix ~re-~, formed in Old French. ~Sembler~ descends from Latin ~similāre~ (a variant of ~simulāre~, “to imitate, to copy”), itself from the adjective ~similis~ (“like, similar”). That adjective goes back through Proto-Italic *~semalis~ to a Proto-Indo-European root *~sem-~ (“one, together”), the same root that yields notions of sameness and unity. Cognates run wide: Ancient Greek ~homós~ (“same”) and ~homalós~ (“even”), and through Romance, Italian ~rassomigliare~ and Spanish ~semejar~. English borrowed the learned ~similar~ and ~simulate~ from the same Latin stock.

In Old French ~sembler~ meant “to seem, to appear,” and adding ~re-~ pushed it toward “to seem like one another,” hence “to resemble.” The pair drifted apart in sense: today ~sembler~ is about appearance (“Il semble fatigué,” “he seems tired”) while ~ressembler~ is about likeness, and it keeps the telltale preposition à — one resembles ~à~ someone. The root *~sem-~ is astonishingly productive in French through several channels: the native ~sembler~, ~semblable~, ~ensemble~ (“together,” literally “in one”), and ~assembler~; and the learned ~similaire~, ~similitude~, ~simuler~, and ~dissimuler~. Even ~simple~ belongs here, “one-fold.” The humble verb for looking alike thus sits at the heart of a family all about oneness and sameness.
en.ressentir:
French ~ressentir~ was coined within French, around 1190, from ~sentir~ (“to feel, to sense”) plus the prefix ~re-~. ~Sentir~ descends from Latin ~sentīre~ (“to feel, to perceive, to be of an opinion”), which goes back through Proto-Italic *~sentjō~ to a Proto-Indo-European root *~sent-~ (“to feel, to go, to head for”). The root's reach is long: Old High German ~sinnan~ (“to strive, to desire”) and modern German ~Sinn~ (“sense, meaning”) are cousins, and through Old French ~sentir~ English drew ~sense~, ~scent~, and ~sentiment~. Italian ~risentire~ and Spanish ~resentir~ mirror the French compound.

The prefix ~re-~ here is intensive rather than repetitive: ~ressentir~ is to feel something deeply, inwardly, often an emotion — ~ressentir de la joie~, ~ressentir de la douleur~. That inward force gave the noun ~ressentiment~ (“resentment”), a feeling re-felt and brooded over, which French lent to English and which philosophers later adopted untranslated. The PIE root *~sent-~, with its double sense of “feeling” and “heading toward,” seeded a remarkably broad French family: ~sentir~, ~sentiment~, ~sensible~, ~sensation~, ~consentir~ (“to feel together,” to agree), ~pressentir~ (“to sense beforehand”), ~assentiment~, and the learned ~sens~ itself. To resent, in French, is simply to feel something all the way through.
en.restaurer:
From Latin ~restaurare~ (“to rebuild, repair, renew”), formed on ~re-~ (“again”) plus a ~-staurare~ element also seen in ~instaurare~ (“to set up, establish”). The verb passed through Old French and survives in Italian ~restaurare~ and Spanish ~restaurar~; English borrowed it twice over, as ~restore~ and ~restaurant~.

The culinary detour is the surprise. In the eighteenth century a ~restaurant~ was not a place but a thing: a ~restaurant~ was a rich, fortifying broth meant to “restore” the strength of the weak — a restorative. A Parisian bouillon-seller is traditionally said to have advertised such restoratives, and the word for the broth slid to the establishment that served it, then to any eatery, before being re-exported across the world. Meanwhile the older sense thrives: one ~restaure~ a painting, a building, a monarchy. From the same Latin source French keeps ~restauration~ (both “restoration” and “catering”), ~restaurateur~ (“restorer” and “restaurant-keeper” alike), and the doublet ~instaurer~ (“to institute, establish”), its near-twin built on the same root.
en.rester:
Attested from the twelfth century, borrowed from Latin ~restāre~ (“to stay behind, to remain, to be left over”), formed from the prefix ~re-~ and ~stāre~ (“to stand, to stand firm”). Latin ~stāre~ comes from Proto-Italic *~stāō~ and from the Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”), one of the most fertile roots in the language family. Through ~stāre~, ~rester~ is a sibling of Italian ~restare~ and Spanish ~restar~ and ~quedar~, and, through the same PIE root, a distant cousin of English ~stand~ and ~stay~.

The word's logic is spatial: what ~restāre~ described was standing back, holding one's ground while others moved on — hence “to remain.” Old French already had the inherited ~ester~ from ~stāre~, and ~rester~ (~re-~ + ~ester~) eventually displaced it as the everyday verb of staying. A trace of the “left over” sense survives in arithmetic, where French children say ~il reste~ for a remainder. The root *~steh₂-~ stocks French with a vast family: ~stable~, ~station~, ~statue~, ~constant~, ~rester~ itself, and — surprisingly — the past participle ~été~ of ~être~, which descends not from ~esse~ but from ~status~, the participle of ~stāre~.
en.retenir:
From Latin ~retinere~ (“to hold back, to keep”), a compound of the prefix ~re-~ (“back”) and ~tenere~ (“to hold”). Latin ~tenere~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch”), the notion being that to hold is to keep something stretched taut. The same root gave Latin ~tendere~ (“to stretch”), Greek ~teinein~ (“to stretch”), Sanskrit ~tanoti~ (“stretches”), and English ~thin~; the compound parallels Italian ~ritenere~ and Spanish ~retener~.

~Retenir~ fans out across many senses, all from “to hold back”: to retain or keep (~retenir une place~, “to reserve a seat”), to hold someone back (~retenir quelqu'un~), to hold back a feeling (~retenir ses larmes~, “to hold back one's tears”), in arithmetic “to carry” a digit, and — most usefully for a learner — “to remember,” to hold something in the mind. The noun ~retenue~ means both “restraint, reserve” and a school “detention.” From ~tenere~ comes a great family: ~tenir~, ~tenue~, ~contenir~, ~maintenir~, ~obtenir~, ~soutenir~, ~entretenir~, and ~appartenir~ — and, through the kindred ~tendere~, ~tendre~, ~tension~, and ~tente~.
en.retirer:
A French formation, ~re-~ (“back, again”) + ~tirer~ (“to pull, to draw”), built within Old French and gradually displacing the older ~retraire~ (from Latin ~retrahere~, “to draw back”). The base ~tirer~ has a famously disputed origin: many trace it, with Spanish ~tirar~, to a Germanic source, while others derive it from a reanalysis of Old French ~martirier~ (“to torture, to martyr”), from ~martyre~ — the present participle ~martirant~ supposedly re-cut as ~mar~ (“badly”) + a form linked to ~tirant~.

Whatever its root, ~tirer~ supplanted the Latin-derived ~traire~ (from ~trahere~, “to pull”) across much of its territory, and ~retirer~ rode that wave. The literal core is “to pull back”: one ~retire~ a hand, a sword, a candidate from a race. From there spread the senses “to remove, to take off” (clothing), “to withdraw” (money from a bank), and the reflexive ~se retirer~, “to withdraw, to retire from the world.” The same field of ~tirer~ and ~trahere~ yields ~attirer~, ~soutirer~, ~étirer~, ~tiroir~, and — from the Latin side — ~extraire~ and ~soustraire~.
en.retourner:
From Old French ~torner~, ~tourner~ (“to turn”) prefixed with ~re-~ (“again, back”). The base verb descends from Latin ~tornāre~ (“to turn on a lathe, to round off, to fashion”), from ~tornus~ (“lathe”), itself borrowed from Greek ~tórnos~ (“a lathe, a tool for drawing circles”), traced to the Proto-Indo-European root *~terh₁-~ (“to rub, to turn, to bore”). Through ~tornāre~ the family includes Italian ~tornare~, Spanish ~tornar~, and English ~turn~ and ~tourney~.

The original image is the craftsman's lathe, shaping wood or metal as it spins — so ~tourner~ began as “to turn on a wheel” and broadened to every kind of turning. With ~re-~, ~retourner~ covers both turning over (~retourner une crêpe~, to flip a pancake; ~retourner la terre~, to turn the soil) and going back (~retourner chez soi~, to return home), and reflexively ~se retourner~ is to turn around or, of a sleeper, to toss. The verb can even mean to shake someone emotionally — ~ça m'a retourné~, “it turned me upside down.” The lathe-root spread widely in French: ~tour~ (a turn, a lap, a trick — and, as a feminine noun, a tower by a different route), ~tournoi~ (a tournament, where contestants wheeled about), ~tournevis~ (screwdriver), ~contour~, ~détour~, and ~retour~ (a return) all turn on the same Greek lathe.
en.retrouver:
From ~re-~ (“again, back”) + ~trouver~ (“to find”), the latter from Vulgar Latin *~tropāre~ (“to compose, invent a melody,” hence “to invent, devise, find”), from Late Latin ~tropus~ (“figure of speech, melody”), borrowed from Greek ~tropos~ (“turn, way, mode”), from ~trepein~ (“to turn”). Cognate with Italian ~trovare~ and Occitan ~trobar~ — the verb that gave ~troubadour~, literally “one who finds (invents) verse.”

The leap from “to compose” to “to find” runs through poetry: to ~trobar~ was to invent a song, to hit upon the right turn of phrase, and from this creative finding came the ordinary sense of coming upon anything. The prefix ~re-~ adds the nuance of recovery — to find what was lost, or to find again — so ~retrouver~ means both “to locate once more” and, of people, “to be reunited”; ~se retrouver~ is to meet up, or to find oneself in a place or a state. The same Greek ~tropos~ surfaces in learned French words far from the verb's everyday warmth — ~trope~, ~tropique~ (the “turning point” of the sun), and ~héliotrope~ (the flower that turns toward the sun) — while ~trouvère~, the northern cousin of ~troubadour~, preserves the medieval poet-finder.
en.revenir:
Formed in Old French from the prefix ~re-~ (« back, again ») and the verb ~venir~ (« to come »), attested as early as the tenth century. ~venir~ comes from Latin ~venire~, from Proto-Italic *~gʷenjō~, from the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to step”). That root reached English through Germanic as the verb ~come~, and shows up in Ancient Greek ~baínō~ (“I go”) and Sanskrit ~gácchati~ (“he goes”).

The compound is transparent — « to come back » — and stayed close to that sense, but French let it bloom in idiom. ~revenir~ means to return in space, but also « to come to » after a faint, to « amount to » a price (~cela revient cher~), to « come down to » in argument (~cela revient au même~), and, in cooking, to brown lightly (~faire revenir des oignons~). Like its parent ~venir~, it takes the auxiliary ~être~ in compound tenses. The root *~gʷem-~ feeds a whole French clan built on ~venir~: ~devenir~, ~convenir~, ~prévenir~, ~parvenir~, the noun ~avenir~ (« what is to come »), and ~venue~.
en.revoir:
From Old French ~revoir~, ~reveoir~, from Latin ~revidere~ (“to see again, to go back to see”), compounded from the prefix ~re-~ (“again, back”) and ~videre~ (“to see”). ~Videre~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~weid-~ (“to see,” and by extension “to know”). Cognate, through ~videre~, with Italian ~rivedere~ and Spanish ~rever~, and — through the root *~weid-~ — with Greek ~eîdon~ (“I saw”), Sanskrit ~véda~ (“I know”), and English ~wit~ and ~witness~.

~Revoir~ is among the most transparent of French compounds: ~re-~ plus ~voir~, “to see again.” Its substantival use lies hidden inside the everyday farewell ~au revoir~ — literally “until the seeing-again,” a promise of reunion rather than a flat goodbye. The root *~weid-~ is one of the great Indo-European wellsprings, binding sight to knowledge: from it French draws ~voir~, ~vision~, ~évident~, ~prévoir~, ~revue~, and ~vue~, while the same root surfaces in Latin-borrowed English ~vision~ and ~evident~ and in the Germanic ~wise~, ~wisdom~, and ~idea~ (through Greek). To ~revoir~ someone is, etymologically, to let the eyes know them once more.
en.rire:
From Middle French ~rire~, from Old French ~rire~, from Vulgar Latin *~rīdĕre~, a shift of the Classical Latin verb ~rīdēre~ (“to laugh, to smile, to mock”) from the second to the third conjugation. The change of conjugation class is what kept ~rire~ short and irregular in French. Beyond Latin the trail goes cold: ~rīdēre~ has no securely established Proto-Indo-European etymology, so cautious etymologists decline to reconstruct a deeper root. Within Romance it is cognate with Italian ~ridere~, Spanish ~reír~, and Portuguese ~rir~.

Latin ~rīdēre~ already carried the double edge the French verb keeps — open laughter on one side, derision on the other, as in ~rire de~ quelqu'un (“to laugh at someone”). The smiling sense lived in the derived ~subrīdēre~ (“to smile,” literally “to laugh a little under”), which gave French ~sourire~, where ~sous-~ + ~rire~ still means a laugh held quietly beneath the surface. The same Latin stem furnished ~ridicule~ and ~ridiculiser~ (from ~rīdiculus~, “laughable”), ~dérision~ and ~dérisoire~, and the noun ~rire~ itself. So from one uncertain ancient verb the French language draws its whole vocabulary of mirth: the belly laugh, the quiet smile, and the cutting ridicule alike.
en.risquer:
Attested from the late sixteenth century, ~risquer~ is a denominal verb formed on the noun ~risque~, borrowed from Old Italian ~risco~ (modern ~rischio~). The Italian word's ultimate origin is disputed, but the favoured account derives it from Vulgar Latin *~resecum~, literally “that which cuts,” from ~resecare~ (“to cut off”) — first a rock or reef that could gash a ship's hull, then the peril such hazards posed to seaborne cargo, and at last “risk” in the abstract. Rival proposals invoke Greek ~rhíza~ (“root, cliff”) or Arabic ~rizq~ (“fortune, what providence allots”), but neither is secure.

The maritime stamp is the memorable part: ~risque~ was a merchant's and insurer's word before it was anyone else's, naming the gamble that goods might never reach port. From the concrete cutting edge of a reef the sense broadened to any exposure to loss, and the verb ~risquer~ now ranges from “to endanger” to the everyday “to risk doing.” If the *~resecum~ etymology holds, the noun shares its root with French ~réséquer~ (“to resect, cut away”) and, through ~secare~, with ~scier~, ~section~, and ~sécateur~ — a whole family centred on the act of cutting.
en.rouler:
From Old French ~roeler~ (around 1180), a verb built on ~roele~ (“little wheel”) with the ending ~-er~. ~Roele~ comes from Late Latin ~rotella~, a diminutive of Latin ~rota~ (“wheel”), crossed along the way with ~rôle~ (from Latin ~rotulus~, “a little rolled scroll”). ~Rota~ descends from Proto-Italic *~rotā~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~Hret-~ (“to run, roll”). Cognates abound: Sanskrit ~ratha~ (“chariot”), German ~Rad~ (“wheel”), and Lithuanian ~ratas~ (“wheel”).

The verb began concretely — to turn like a wheel, to make something revolve — and then rolled outward in every direction. It came to mean to travel by wheeled vehicle (~la voiture roule~), to coil or wind, and, in vivid slang, to ~rouler quelqu'un~, “to cheat someone,” as if spinning them around. The shared root left French a wheel-shaped vocabulary: ~roue~, ~rouleau~, ~roulette~, ~roulement~, the noun ~rôle~ (originally a rolled-up list of parts), and even ~contrôle~ (from a ~contre-rôle~, a counter-roll kept for checking). English ~roll~, ~rotate~, and ~round~ trace to the same turning root, as does the ~rotary~ motion that names it.
en.réagir:
Attested around 1500, ~réagir~ is a learned formation from Latin ~reagere~ (“to push back, to drive back”), built on the prefix ~re-~ (“back, in return”) and ~agere~ (“to drive, to do, to act”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive, to lead”). Its sense was later reshaped on the French verb ~agir~ (“to act”), so that ~réagir~ reads transparently as ~ré-~ + ~agir~, “to act back.” Through ~agere~ it is cognate with Italian ~reagire~, Spanish ~reaccionar~, and English ~react~, and through the PIE root with English ~act~, ~agent~, and Greek ~ágein~ (“to lead”).

The word began in the language of physics and chemistry: one substance ~réagit~ upon another, exerting a counter-force or counter-effect. From this technical “acting back” it spread into ordinary life, where to ~réagir~ is to respond — to a remark, a crisis, a provocation — with an action of one's own. The noun ~réaction~ followed the same path and even acquired a political coloring, ~réactionnaire~ naming those who wished to act *back* against revolutionary change. The fertile root ~agere~ seeds a vast French family: ~agir~, ~agent~, ~agile~, ~agiter~, ~action~, ~acte~, and ~rédiger~ — all turning on the idea of setting something in motion.
en.réaliser:
Unlike the other verbs here, ~réaliser~ is not a direct inheritance from Latin but a French formation of the fifteenth century, built from the adjective ~réel~ (“real”) and the suffix ~-iser~ (“to make, to render”) — literally “to make real.” ~réel~ descends from Medieval Latin ~reālis~ (“relating to a thing, actual”), from Latin ~rēs~ (“thing, matter, affair”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~reh₁-~ (“property, goods”). So at bottom ~réaliser~ means to turn an idea into a ~rēs~, a real thing. Cognates of ~réel~ survive as Italian ~reale~ and Spanish ~real~, and the verb entered English as ~realize~.

The verb's earliest French sense was the concrete one still central today: to bring a project, a dream, or a sum of money into reality — to “carry out,” to “make happen,” and in cinema to ~réaliser~ a film is to direct it. The mental sense, “to realize, to become aware of,” is a calque of English ~realize~; though long criticized by purists as an anglicism, it was defended by André Gide and has been current since the late nineteenth century. Interestingly, the English meaning echoes an old metaphor already latent in Latin ~reor~ (“to reckon, to think”), where “fixing” a thing in the mind shades into thinking it. From ~rēs~ come a large French family, including ~réel~, ~réalité~, ~réalisme~, ~réalisateur~ (“film director”), and ~république~ (~rēs pūblica~, “the public thing”).
en.réclamer:
From Old French ~reclamer~, from Latin ~reclāmāre~ (“to cry out against, to protest loudly”), formed from the prefix ~re-~ (“back, against”) and ~clāmāre~ (“to call, to shout”), from Proto-Indo-European *~kelh₁-~ (“to call, to shout”). Cognate with Italian ~reclamare~ and Spanish ~reclamar~, and — through ~clāmāre~ — with English ~claim~ (itself borrowed from Old French) and, through the same PIE root, with English ~low~ (“to moo”) and Latin ~calāre~.

The Latin verb was originally adversarial: to ~reclāmāre~ was to shout ~re-~, “back,” at a speaker — to clamor in opposition. That confrontational sense survives in modern ~réclamer~ when it means “to protest, to object,” but the dominant modern meaning shifted from crying against something to crying for it: to ~réclamer~ is now above all to demand, to claim, to call insistently for what one is owed — ~réclamer son dû~. A child ~réclame~ its mother; a creditor ~réclame~ payment. From the same ~clāmāre~ French built a loud family: ~clamer~, ~acclamer~, ~proclamer~, ~déclamer~, ~s'exclamer~, and the noun ~clameur~ (“clamor, outcry”) — all of them, like ~réclamer~, rooted in the raised voice.
en.récupérer:
