A learned re-borrowing (around 1495) from Latin ~recuperare~ (“to regain, to recover, to get back”), itself the prefix ~re-~ (“back, again”) on a stem related to ~capere~ (“to take, to seize”) — recovery being, etymologically, a “taking back.” The same Latin verb had already entered French much earlier by ordinary phonetic descent as ~recouvrer~ (“to recover”), so ~récupérer~ and ~recouvrer~ form a doublet: two French verbs, one inherited and worn down, the other re-cut directly from the Latin. Spanish ~recuperar~, Italian ~recuperare~, and English ~recuperate~ are parallel learned descendants.

Because it was reshaped from the Latin rather than passed down by mouth, ~récupérer~ keeps a transparent, almost technical feel, and its meanings fan out widely: to get back a lost object, to recover one's strength after effort (~récupérer~ after a hard week), to reclaim or recycle materials, and — in a distinctly modern political sense — to co-opt or appropriate a cause or a movement for one's own ends. The noun ~récupération~ covers all of these, from physical recovery to salvage to ideological appropriation. Beside it the older ~recouvrer~ survives in more formal registers, as when one ~recouvre~ a debt or ~recouvre~ one's sight.
en.rédiger:
From Latin ~redigere~ (“to bring back, drive back, reduce”), a compound of the prefix ~re-~ (“back, again”) and ~agere~ (“to drive, do, act”). ~Agere~ derives from the PIE root *~h₂eǵ-~ (“to drive”), source also of Greek ~ágō~ (“I lead”) and, through Latin borrowings, of English ~act~, ~agent~, and ~agile~; Italian and Spanish keep the parent verb in ~agire~ and the like. The literal Latin sense is to “drive back” or “reduce” a thing to some state — to bring it down to order.

The path to “writing” runs through that idea of reduction. Medieval Latin used ~redigere in scriptis~, “to reduce into writing,” to set a matter down in fixed, orderly form — and from this phrase the “write up, compose” sense crystallized. So ~rédiger~ never meant mere scribbling: it means to compose with care, to draft a contract, an article, a report, marshalling loose material into finished prose. Its agent and act are ~rédacteur~ (“editor, writer”) and ~rédaction~ (“drafting; an editorial office; a school essay”). The driving root *~h₂eǵ-~ powers a huge French family beyond it — ~agir~, ~agent~, ~agile~, ~action~, ~rédiger~ standing among them as the one that drives words into shape.
en.réduire:
From Old French ~reduire~, from Latin ~redūcere~ (“to lead back, to bring back”), which is ~re-~ (“back”) + ~dūcere~ (“to lead, to draw”). ~dūcere~ comes from Proto-Italic *~doukō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~dewk-~ (“to pull, to lead”). That root reaches far into Germanic: it gives English ~tow~, German ~ziehen~ (“to pull”), and even the ~-zog~ of German ~Herzog~ (“duke,” literally “army-leader,” a calque of Greek ~stratēgós~). Latin built ~dux~ (“leader”) from the same root — the ancestor of ~duke~ and Italian ~duce~.

The meaning traveled a long way. ~redūcere~ first meant simply “to lead back,” and Old French ~reduire~ could mean “to bring back, to restore.” The modern sense “to make smaller” grew from the idea of bringing a thing back to a smaller, simpler, or controlled state — reducing an army, a price, a text, or a rebel “brought back” to obedience. The ~dūcere~ family is one of the richest in French: ~conduire~, ~produire~, ~traduire~, ~introduire~, ~séduire~, ~déduire~, plus the nouns ~duc~, ~conduite~, and ~aqueduc~ (“water-leader”).
en.réfléchir:
From Latin ~reflectere~ (“to bend back, to turn back”), a compound of the prefix ~re-~ (“back, again”) and ~flectere~ (“to bend, to curve”). The ultimate root of ~flectere~ is uncertain and has no secure Indo-European cognates, though a Proto-Italic *~flektō~ is reconstructed. The verb is cognate with Italian ~flettere~ and survives, through its derivatives, in English ~reflect~, ~flexible~, and ~inflect~. French inherited a popular form ~réflechir~ alongside the learned doublet that gave the noun ~réflexion~.

The metaphor at the heart of this verb is light bending back from a surface. From the optical sense — a mirror ~reflects~ an image — French drew an inward, mental one: to “turn one's thought back” upon itself, to ponder. So ~réfléchir~ means both “to reflect (light)” and “to think hard,” and the two senses illuminate each other — the thinker turns ideas over the way a mirror turns back a beam. A reflexive verb in French is a verbe ~réfléchi~, one whose action bends back onto the subject. From the same ~flectere~ stem descend ~fléchir~ (“to bend, to yield”), ~flexible~, ~réflexe~, and ~circonflexe~ — the accent “bent around” a vowel.
en.régler:
French ~régler~ is a denominal verb, formed in the thirteenth century from the noun ~règle~ (“rule, ruler”), which comes from Latin ~rēgula~ (“straight stick, ruler, rule”). ~Rēgula~ is a derivative of ~regere~ (“to keep straight, to direct, to rule”), and that verb rests on a Proto-Indo-European root *~h₃reǵ-~ (“to straighten, to make right”). The root is one of the great ones: Sanskrit ~rā́jati~ (“to rule, to direct”), Latin ~rēx~ (“king”), and through Germanic the English ~right~ and German ~recht~ all spring from it. Italian ~regolare~ and Spanish ~reglar~ are the Romance cousins of the verb; English borrowed ~regulate~ from the related Latin ~regulāre~.

The oldest concrete sense is wonderfully literal: to ~régler~ a sheet of paper is to rule lines on it with a straightedge — the ruler doing exactly what its name says. From “to draw straight lines” the meaning fanned out to “to set right, to adjust, to put in order”: one ~règle~ a watch, a machine, a thermostat; one ~règle~ a quarrel or a bill (~régler ses comptes~, “to settle one's accounts”). The same root underlies a sprawling French family: ~règle~, ~règlement~, ~régulier~ and ~réguler~, ~régime~ and ~régir~, ~roi~ and ~royal~ (through ~rēx~), and ~rectitude~ and ~direct~ (through ~rectus~, “straight”). To regulate is, at heart, to make crooked things straight.
en.répandre:
From Old French ~espandre~, from Latin ~expandere~ (“to spread out, unfold, expose to the air”), formed from the prefix ~ex-~ and ~pandere~ (“to spread, stretch, open”), from Proto-Italic *~pandō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~peth₂-~ (“to spread out”). The modern French verb is more immediately ~épandre~ (the direct heir of ~expandere~) extended by the iterative prefix ~re-~. The Latin root ~pandere~ is cognate with ~pateō~ (“to lie open”) and, through the same Indo-European root, with Ancient Greek ~πέταλον~ (“leaf, a spread-out thing”).

In Latin, ~expandere~ literally meant to unfold something so that it lay flat and open — wings, cloth, grain set out to dry. Old French ~espandre~ carried the sense of pouring out or scattering, and adding ~re-~ in ~répandre~ intensified rather than reversed it, yielding “to spread widely, to scatter, to shed.” Today the verb covers everything physical and figurative that can be diffused: ~répandre une odeur~ (“to give off a smell”), spilling a liquid, or spreading a rumor. The same root *~peth₂-~ surfaces across modern French in a botanical and spatial family of words, among them ~pétale~ (“petal,” the spread-out part of a flower), ~expansion~, and ~patent~ — all sharing the ancient image of something opening out flat.
en.répartir:
A French formation: the verb ~partir~ in its old sense “to divide, to share” plus the prefix ~ré-~ (“again, thoroughly”). That older ~partir~ descends from Latin ~partīre~ / ~partīrī~ (“to divide, to share out”), built on ~pars~ (genitive ~partis~, “a part, a share”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to grant, to allot”). The same ~pars~ gives Italian ~parte~ and Spanish ~parte~, and through Latin a great spread of English words including ~part~, ~partition~, ~portion~, and ~partner~.

In early French ~partir~ meant “to split” — a sense now almost wholly displaced by “to leave, to depart,” which itself grew from the idea of separating oneself from a place. The compound ~répartir~ preserved the original meaning, the prefix ~ré-~ stressing a complete or renewed dividing-up: to ~répartir~ is to apportion, to distribute shares among many. (A near-twin, ~repartir~ without the accent, means “to set off again” and is conjugated differently — a reminder of how the “depart” sense overtook the “divide” sense.) From the same ~pars~ French inherited ~partie~, ~partage~, ~parti~, ~partition~, ~portion~, and ~répartition~.
en.répertorier:
From French ~répertoire~ (“repertory, index, list”), with the verbal suffix ~-er~. ~Répertoire~ descends from Latin ~repertorium~ (“inventory, catalogue”), a noun built on ~reperīre~ (“to find, to discover”), itself ~re-~ plus ~parīre~ (“to bring forth, to produce, to obtain”), from Proto-Indo-European *~perh₃-~ (“to produce, to beget”). Cognate, through ~parīre~, with English ~parent~ and ~repertory~, and with Italian ~repertorio~ and Spanish ~repertorio~.

A ~repertorium~ was literally a place where things are found again — an index that lets you retrieve an item, the etymological heart of both a theatre troupe's ~répertoire~ of plays and a list one keeps. The verb ~répertorier~ is a young, practical coinage meaning “to index, to catalogue, to draw up a list of.” Its buried root is surprisingly fertile: ~parīre~, “to give birth,” also produced ~parent~ (one who begets), the medical ~parturition~, and — through the related ~parāre~, “to make ready” — a sprawling family including ~préparer~, ~séparer~, ~apparaître~, and even ~parure~. To ~répertorier~ something is, etymologically, to arrange it so it can be brought forth and found anew.
en.répondre:
From Old French ~respondre~ (attested 980), from Vulgar Latin ~*respondĕre~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~respondēre~ (“to answer, to reply”). The verb is ~re-~ (“back, in return”) plus ~spondēre~ (“to pledge, to promise solemnly”), from Proto-Italic ~*spondejō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~spend-~ (“to make an offering, to perform a rite, to pledge”). The same root gives Greek ~spéndō~ (“to pour a libation”) and ~spondḗ~ (“libation, treaty”), and through Latin underlies English ~sponsor~ and ~spouse~ (one pledged in marriage).

The buried metaphor is legal and ceremonial: to ~respondēre~ was originally to *pledge in return* — to answer a sponsor's solemn promise with one's own. From this reciprocal pledging came the everyday sense “to answer, to reply,” and then the wider “to correspond to, to be answerable for.” French keeps all of these: one answers a question, and one ~répond de~ (“is answerable for”) one's acts. From ~spondēre~ and its participle ~sponsus~ French inherited ~réponse~, ~répons~, ~correspondre~, ~responsable~, ~épouser~ and ~époux~ (the betrothed), while English ~sponsor~ goes back to the same root.
en.répéter:
Attested from the twelfth century, ~répéter~ comes from Latin ~repetere~ (“to seek again, attack anew, demand back, reclaim”), formed from the iterative prefix ~re-~ (“again”) and ~petere~ (“to seek, aim at, strike, fall upon, demand”). Latin ~petere~ goes back to a Proto-Indo-European root *~peth₂-~ (“to fly, rush, fall”), the same root behind Greek ~pteron~ (“wing, feather”) and Latin ~penna~ (“feather”). Italian ~ripetere~ and Spanish ~repetir~ share the descent.

The Latin verb's “seek again” lay behind two strands of meaning that French still keeps. One is reclaiming: in law one may ~répéter~ a sum, that is, demand its repayment. The other, far more common, is doing over: to say or do something again — and, by a charming specialization, to rehearse, so that actors and musicians ~répéter~ a play or a piece, and a rehearsal is a ~répétition~. The base ~petere~, with its sense of seeking and striving toward, gave French a rich crop: ~appétit~ (a striving-toward, hunger), ~compétition~, ~pétition~, ~impétueux~, ~perpétuel~, and ~compétent~. Behind the schoolchild's tiresome repetition lies an ancient image of rushing, again and again, toward a target.
en.réserver:
From Latin ~reservare~ (“to keep back, hold in reserve”), a compound of the prefix ~re-~ (“back”) and the verb ~servare~ (“to keep, guard, preserve”). ~servare~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~ser-~ (“to protect, watch over”). Cognate with Italian ~riservare~ and Spanish ~reservar~, and — through the same Latin verb — with English ~reserve~ and ~conserve~.

The Latin verb already carried the idea of setting something aside for later, of holding it back from immediate use. French inherited this sense intact: to ~réserver~ a thing is to withhold it from the general flow, whether a seat, a table, or a right. The same root *~ser-~ is unusually productive: from ~servare~ French draws ~conserver~ (“to keep together, to preserve”), ~observer~ (originally “to watch over, to keep before oneself”), and ~préserver~ (“to guard beforehand”), as well as the nouns ~réserve~ and ~réservoir~, a vessel that literally keeps water back. Etymologists distinguish this family carefully from that of ~servir~ and ~serviteur~, which trace to ~servus~ (“slave”); though some link ~servus~ too to *~ser-~ in the sense of a guardian, the two strands diverged early and should not be confused.
en.résister:
French ~résister~ continues Latin ~resistere~ (“to stand back against, take a stand, oppose”), made of the prefix ~re-~ (here “against, back”) and ~sistere~ (“to make stand, to halt, to come to a stand”). ~Sistere~ is itself a reduplicated present of the verb ~stāre~ (“to stand”): from the root ~st-~ Latin built ~si-st-ere~, “to set standing.” Both go back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”), one of the most fruitful roots in the language. ~Resistere~ gave Italian ~resistere~, Spanish ~resistir~, and English ~resist~.

The literal picture is a soldier who plants his feet and refuses to be pushed back — to ~résister~ is to stand one's ground while a force presses. From that, the meaning broadened to withstanding pressure, temptation, disease, or wear, and even to physical durability (a material that ~résiste~). History gave the word a charged second life in ~la Résistance~, the underground that stood against the Occupation. The reduplicating ~sistere~ seeded a whole rank of French verbs that all turn on standing: ~consister~, ~persister~, ~insister~, ~subsister~, and ~exister~ (“to stand forth”) — while the simpler ~stāre~ branch yields ~stable~, ~station~, and ~constant~, kin to ~résister~ through the same standing root *~steh₂-~.
en.résoudre:
French ~résoudre~ descends from Latin ~resolvere~ (“to untie, to loosen, to dissolve”), formed from ~re-~ (“back, again”) plus ~solvere~ (“to loosen, to release”). ~Solvere~ is itself a compound, ~se-~ (“apart, away”) plus ~luere~ (“to release, to loosen”), and traces to a Proto-Indo-European root *~lewh₁-~ (“to cut off, to loosen”). The Latin verb left a rich Romance and learned legacy: English ~solve~, ~resolve~, and ~dissolve~ all draw on this stock, as do Italian ~risolvere~ and Spanish ~resolver~. An older, fully popular French form, ~rasoudre~ / résoudre, coexisted with the relearned shape we use today.

The literal sense was physical: ~resolvere~ meant to untie or dissolve, to take a thing apart. French preserves both threads. The concrete one survives where a fog or a tumour ~se résout~ — dissolves, breaks up. From “to take apart” grew the abstract “to settle a question by analysis,” hence ~résoudre un problème~ (“to solve a problem”), and reflexively ~se résoudre à~ (“to resolve oneself to,” to make up one's mind). The same ~solvere~ underlies a wide French family: ~résolution~, ~soluble~, ~solution~, ~dissoudre~ and ~dissolution~, ~absoudre~ (“to absolve”), and the financial ~solvable~ (“solvent,” able to pay — to “loose” one's debts). To resolve, at root, is to loosen a knot, whether of rope, of fog, or of doubt.
en.résulter:
From Latin ~resultāre~ (“to spring back, to rebound, to echo”), a frequentative formed on ~resultum~, the supine stock of ~resilīre~ (“to leap back”), itself ~re-~ (“back”) + ~salīre~ (“to leap, to jump”). The verb ~salīre~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~sel-~ (“to jump, to leap”). The same ~salīre~ underlies Italian ~salire~ and Spanish ~salir~, and through its frequentative ~saltāre~ gives the dance-word at the root of English ~saltation~ and the Spanish ~saltar~.

The literal Latin sense was physical bounce: a ball, a sound, or a beam of light ~resultāre~ — springs back from a surface. From that image of something that rebounds came the figurative idea of an effect that springs out of a cause, an outcome that leaps forth from what precedes it — so a conclusion, a profit, or a disaster may ~résulter~ from given conditions. Today the verb is almost entirely abstract, conjugated chiefly in the third person, and frequently impersonal: ~il en résulte que~ (“it follows that”). The same ~salīre~ left a lively French family, among them ~saillir~, ~sauter~, ~assaillir~, ~tressaillir~, and ~résilier~, the last sharing the very stem ~resilīre~ that produced ~résulter~.
en.résumer:
From Latin ~resūmere~ (“to take back, to take up again, to resume”), from ~re-~ (“again”) and ~sūmere~ (“to take”). ~Sūmere~ is itself ~sub-~ (“up from under”) plus ~emere~ (“to take, to buy”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁em-~ (“to take, to distribute”). Cognate, through ~resūmere~, with English ~resume~ and with Italian ~riassumere~ and Spanish ~resumir~.

The classical sense was simply “to take up again,” but in scholastic Latin ~resūmere~ acquired the narrower meaning “to take up again *briefly*” — to go back over an argument in short — and it is this learned sense that French inherited: to ~résumer~ is to restate the essential in few words. From the same ~sūmere~ French keeps ~assumer~, ~consumer~, and ~présumer~, while the underlying ~emere~ (“to take, to buy”) seeded a surprisingly large family — ~exemple~, ~rédempteur~, ~prompt~, ~rançon~ (a worn-down ~redemptio~), and ~péremptoire~. A ~résumé~, then, is literally what has been “taken up again” in miniature — fittingly, English borrowed the past participle whole as the name for a curriculum vitae.
en.réunir:
From ~unir~ (“to unite”) with the prefix ~ré-~ (“again, back”), so literally “to unite again, to bring back together.” ~unir~ descends from Latin ~unire~ (“to make one, to join”), a verb built directly on ~unus~ (“one”). ~unus~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~h₁óynos~ (“one”), the same root that gives Ancient Greek ~oîos~ (“alone”), Old Irish ~óen~, and — through Proto-Germanic — English ~one~ and ~an~. ~réunir~ is thus a close relative of Italian ~riunire~ and Spanish ~reunir~.

The semantics are transparent: to ~réunir~ people or things is to make them ~one~ once more. The prefix ~ré-~ does real work, since it presumes a prior unity broken and now restored — friends reunited, scattered papers gathered up. From the same Latin ~unus~ and its PIE root *~h₁óynos~ French drew a wide vocabulary: ~un~ (“one”), ~unique~ (from ~unicus~, “sole”), ~union~, ~unité~, ~unanime~ (“of one mind,” ~unus~ + ~animus~), and ~univers~ (from ~universus~, literally “turned into one”). The reflexive ~se réunir~ — for a committee or assembly “to meet” — preserves the image perfectly: many individuals momentarily becoming one body.
en.réussir:
From Italian ~riuscire~ (“to come out again,” hence “to succeed, to manage”), borrowed into French around 1531. The Italian verb is ~ri-~ (“again”) + ~uscire~ (“to go out, to exit”), and ~uscire~ descends from Latin ~exire~ (~ex-~, “out” + ~ire~, “to go”), the verb ~ire~ tracing to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₁ey-~ (“to go”). The word is also paralleled by Old French ~reissir~ (“to come back out, to emerge”), so the core image is consistent across both Romance lines.

The semantic leap is delightfully concrete: to ~réussir~ is, literally, to emerge — to come back out of a venture rather than getting stuck in it. Success was framed not as reaching a peak but as finding the exit. The prefix is telling: ~ri-~ adds the nuance of coming out again, a second emergence after entering the fray. From the same root *~h₁ey-~ French inherits a scattered family — among them ~issue~ (“exit, outcome”), ~initier~, ~ambition~ (etymologically a “going around” to canvass votes), and ~transitoire~ — while the everyday ~sortir~ later crowded ~réussir~ out of its spatial meaning, leaving it to specialize entirely in the sense of triumph.
en.révéler:
From Latin ~revēlāre~ (“to unveil, to uncover, to disclose”), compounded from ~re-~ (here reversive, “un-”) and ~vēlāre~ (“to cover, to veil”), from ~vēlum~ (“sail, curtain, covering”). ~Vēlum~ traces back through Proto-Italic *~wekslom~ to the Proto-Indo-European root *~weg-~ (“to weave”), so a veil is etymologically a woven thing. The same Latin verb gave English ~reveal~ and, more transparently, the French doublet of ~vēlum~ — ~voile~ (“veil; thin woven fabric”). Italian ~rivelare~ and Spanish ~revelar~ are siblings.

The literal sense could not be plainer: to reveal is to pull the veil off something. The word entered French heavy with religious weight — révélation is the divine unveiling of truth, God lifting the veil on His will — before broadening to any disclosure of what was hidden or secret (« révéler un secret »). A delightful technical sense survives in photography: to ~révéler~ a film is to develop it, the ~révélateur~ being the developer bath that lifts the latent image out of darkness into view — quite literally unveiling it. From ~vēlum~ French also keeps ~voile~, ~voiler~ (“to veil”), and ~dévoiler~ (“to unveil”), a near-synonym of ~révéler~.
en.rêver:
From Old French ~resver~ (“to wander, to rave”), of disputed origin. The likeliest account derives it from an unattested Vulgar Latin *~exvagare~ or a Gallo-Romance *~esver~, ultimately from Latin ~ex-~ (“out of”) plus ~vagus~ (“wandering, roaming, vague”). On this view ~rêver~ is, at root, “to stray off, to wander” — and its Old French sibling ~desver~ meant “to go out of one's mind.” Latin ~vagus~ also gives French ~vague~ and English ~vague~ and ~vagabond~.

The semantic journey is striking. Originally ~resver~ meant “to rave, to talk wildly, to be delirious” — a wandering of the mind, not its rest. Only later did ~rêver~ glide toward the gentler sense of dreaming in one's sleep, displacing the older ~songer~ in that role, and then toward daydreaming and idle reverie. The mental-wandering sense still echoes in the noun ~rêverie~ (“reverie, musing”) and in ~délirer~ (“to be delirious”), and ~rêver~ keeps a faint edge of unreason: ~je rêve !~ means roughly “I must be dreaming — this can't be real!” The dreamer and the raver were, long ago, the same wanderer.
en.sacrer:
From Latin ~sacrāre~ (“to consecrate, make holy, dedicate to a divinity”), from the adjective ~sacer~ (“sacred”). ~Sacer~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~sakros~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~seh₂k-~ (“to sanctify, to make binding by treaty”), the same root behind ~sānctus~ (“holy”) and the verb ~sancīre~ (“to ratify”). A famous quirk of ~sacer~ is its double edge: it could mean both “consecrated” and “accursed,” for what is set apart for the gods is untouchable in either direction.

In French, ~sacrer~ first named the sacred rite of crowning a king or ordaining a bishop — the king was ~sacré~, anointed and consecrated, at Reims. From there it broadened to “to proclaim solemnly” (~sacré champion~, crowned champion). The most colorful development belongs to Québec French, where ~sacrer~ became “to swear, to curse” — precisely by profaning the sacred, hurling church words as oaths, the ~sacres~ of Quebec. So the same verb spans crowning a monarch and blaspheming. From ~sacer~ French also keeps ~sacré~, ~sacrement~, ~consacrer~, ~sacrifice~, and ~sacerdoce~ (“priesthood”).
en.saillir:
From Old French ~saillir~, from Latin ~salīre~ (“to leap, to jump,” and in the language of breeders “to mount, to cover a female”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~sel-~ (“to jump”). In its plainest sense ~salīre~ was crowded out early by its own frequentative ~saltāre~ (whence ~sauter~), and the palatal ~-ll-~ that marks French ~saillir~ spread from the third-person plural ~saliunt~ → ~saillent~ across the rest of the paradigm. Cognate French words include ~saut~, ~sauter~, ~assaillir~, and ~tressaillir~; through Old French ~saillie~ and ~assalir~, English borrowed ~salient~, ~sally~, and ~assault~.

Every modern meaning is a metaphor of leaping. The oldest, attested in the ~Chanson de Roland~ around 1100, is « to gush, to spring forth with force » — water or blood that leaps from a wound. From the image of a male animal that leaps upon a female came, by the 14th century, the breeding sense « to cover, to mate » and the noun ~saillie~. A stone or a beam that seems to leap out from a wall gave the architectural sense « to jut out, to project », also medieval. Modern French splits ~saillir~ across two conjugations: in the breeding sense it has gone over to the regular second group, conjugating like ~finir~ (ils ~saillissent~), whereas the project sense keeps the older, irregular pattern (il ~saille~, ils ~saillent~). From the same root come ~saillant~, ~saillie~, ~assaut~, and ~ressaut~.
en.saisir:
From Old French ~saisir~, from Medieval Latin ~sacire~ (“to take possession, to put in legal possession”), itself of Germanic origin — most likely from Frankish *~sakjan~ or, by another account, from Old High German *~sazjan~ (“to set, to place someone in possession”), related to modern German ~setzen~ (“to set, to place”) and to English ~set~. The legal Low Latin phrase ~ad proprium sacire~ (“to put into one's own”) mirrors the native ~ad proprium ponere~, betraying the Germanic verb dressed in Latin clothing.

The earliest sense was the formal, legal one — to put someone in possession of property — which survives in French law: a court is ~saisi~ of a case, and goods can be ~saisis~ (“seized”) by a bailiff. From the idea of taking legal hold the verb broadened to physical grasping (to grab, to seize), then to mental grasping (to understand, as in ~saisir une idée~), and finally to the modern act of entering data — to ~saisir~ text on a keyboard. The verb is the source of ~saisie~ (“seizure, data entry”), ~saisissant~ (“striking, gripping”), ~dessaisir~ (“to dispossess”), and ~ressaisir~, as in ~se ressaisir~ (“to pull oneself together”).
en.satisfaire:
From Latin ~satisfacere~ (“to do enough, to satisfy a claim, to give satisfaction”), a compound of ~satis~ (“enough, sufficiently”) and ~facere~ (“to do, to make”). ~satis~ comes from the Proto-Indo-European root *~seh₂-~ (“to satiate, to be full”), seen also in ~satur~ (“full, sated”) and English ~sad~ (originally “sated, weary”). ~facere~ rests on the root *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to set, to put, to do”), which gives Greek ~τίθημι~ (~títhēmi~) and English ~do~. The French form was remodelled on the native verb ~faire~. Cognate with Italian ~soddisfare~ and Spanish ~satisfacer~.

The literal sense, “to do enough,” is wonderfully plain: to satisfy is to do as much as is required and no more. In Roman law ~satisfacere~ meant to discharge an obligation, to make good on a debt — a meaning that survives in the legal and financial uses of French ~satisfaire~ (~satisfaire à ses engagements~, to meet one's commitments) alongside the everyday sense of pleasing someone. From ~satis~ French also draws ~satiété~ (“satiety”), ~rassasier~ (“to sate”), and ~saturer~; from ~facere~ comes the immense family of ~faire~ — ~défaire~, ~refaire~, ~parfaire~ — so that ~satisfaire~ sits at the meeting point of “enough” and “do.”
en.sauvegarder:
A verb built on the noun ~sauvegarde~, itself a transparent compound of ~sauve~ (the feminine of ~sauf~, “safe,” from Latin ~salvus~) and ~garde~ (“guard, protection”). The two halves come from opposite ends of Europe. ~Salvus~ is solidly Latin; but ~garde~ and its verb ~garder~ descend from a Germanic root carried into Gaul by the Franks — Frankish *~wardōn~ (“to watch over, to guard”), latinized as ~wardāre~. Through that Germanic root ~garde~ is cognate with English ~ward~, ~warden~, and ~guard~, and with German ~warten~. A “safe-guard,” then, is etymologically a “safe-watching.”

In medieval and legal French, a ~sauvegarde~ was the formal protection a lord or sovereign extended over a person or property — a safe-conduct, a guarantee of safety. The verb ~sauvegarder~ generalized this to “to protect, to preserve from harm,” and in the computer age it acquired its now-dominant sense: to ~save~ a file or back up data, keeping it safe from loss. The Germanic half lives on in ~garder~, ~gardien~, and ~regarder~ (“to look at,” originally “to watch over again”), while the Latin half survives in ~sauf~, ~sauver~, and ~salut~. To safeguard is to keep watch so that something stays whole.
en.sauver:
From Old French ~salver~, ~sauver~ (“to save, keep safe, protect”), attested as early as the ~Serments de Strasbourg~ of 842 in the form ~salvar~ — among the oldest French words on record. It descends from Late Latin ~salvāre~ (“to make safe, rescue”), a verb built on Classical Latin ~salvus~ (“safe, sound, unharmed, in good health”), from Proto-Indo-European *~solh₂wós~, from the root *~solh₂-~ (“whole, well-kept”). Cognate with Italian ~salvare~, Spanish ~salvar~, and — through Old French — with English ~save~ and ~safe~.

The sense has stayed remarkably stable: to keep something whole is to keep it safe, and the same notion of intact wholeness shows in Greek ~ὅλος~ (“whole, entire”) and Sanskrit ~sarva~ (“all, whole”), both from the same root. From ~salvus~ Latin also drew the greeting ~salvē~ (“be well, hail”), and from the family French inherited ~salut~, ~santé~, ~sauf~, ~sauveur~, ~sauvetage~, and ~sauvegarde~. English ~salvage~ and ~salvation~ are learned borrowings of the same Latin stock. A fine doublet survives in French itself: alongside the inherited ~sauver~ stands the later, scholarly form preserved in ~salvateur~ and in religious ~Sauveur~ — the Savior who makes the soul whole again.
en.savoir:
From Old French ~savoir~, earlier ~saveir~ and ~savir~ (the form recorded in the 842 ~Serments de Strasbourg~), from Vulgar Latin *~sapēre~, a remodeling of Classical Latin ~sapĕre~ (“to taste, to have flavor,” figuratively “to be wise, to understand”), from Proto-Italic *~sapiō~, from PIE *~seh₁p-~ (“to taste, to perceive”). The Latin verb already carried both senses, reinforced by the participle ~sapiēns~ (“wise”). Cognate with Italian ~sapere~ and Spanish ~saber~, both meaning “to know,” and — through the same root — with English ~savor~ and ~savvy~.

The semantic drift is striking: a verb that first meant “to taste” came to mean “to know,” the tongue's discernment standing in for the mind's. In doing so, *~sapēre~ pushed aside the classical verb for knowing, ~scīre~, which vanished from French. That lost ~scīre~ left a curious scar: from the 16th to the 18th century the verb was misspelled ~sçavoir~, a learned but false back-formation that wrongly assumed descent from ~scīre~; the Académie française restored ~savoir~ only in 1740. The same PIE root underlies a wide family of French words, among them ~sage~, ~saveur~, ~savant~, ~sapience~, and the species name ~Homo sapiens~ — the “knowing,” or rather “tasting,” human.
en.sembler:
From Old French ~sembler~ (“to seem, resemble”), from Latin ~similāre~ / ~simulāre~ (“to make like, imitate, resemble”), from the adjective ~similis~ (“like, similar”), from Proto-Italic *~semalis~, from PIE *~sem-~ (“one, together, as one”). Cognate with Catalan ~semblar~ and Italian ~sembrare~; through ~similis~ and the same root *~sem-~, English has ~similar~, ~assemble~, ~resemble~, and ~simultaneous~.

The semantic core is likeness: to ~sembler~ is, at bottom, to present an appearance like something — hence “to seem.” The root *~sem-~ (“one”) ties this whole family to the idea of oneness and togetherness, which is why ~ensemble~ (“together,” from ~in~ + ~simul~, “at once”) is a cousin of ~sembler~. French also kept ~semblable~ (“similar; a fellow human being”), ~semblant~ (as in ~faire semblant~, “to pretend,” literally “to make a seeming”), and ~ressembler~ (“to resemble”). The borrowed doublet ~simuler~ (“to simulate, feign”) preserves the Latin form more transparently. So the gulf between merely seeming, genuinely resembling, and deliberately pretending all unfolds from a single Latin verb meaning to make one thing like another.
en.sentir:
From Latin ~sentīre~ (“to perceive, feel, be of an opinion”), which traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~sent-~ — whose oldest sense was not “to feel” but “to head for, to go.” That same root surfaces, through Germanic, in German ~Sinn~ (“sense, mind”) and the verb ~sinnen~ (“to ponder”), and in Old English ~sīþ~ (“journey, way”). Romance kin are everywhere: Italian ~sentire~, Spanish and Portuguese ~sentir~, all alive and common.

The semantic leap is the memorable part. If the root first meant “to go,” then “to find one’s way,” the step to “to perceive, to sense” looks like a mind *going mentally toward* something — an act of mental travel that lands on a perception. From that core, French ~sentir~ branched in two directions at once: outward, to the body’s senses (to ~sentir~ a perfume, to smell), and inward, to feeling and intuition (to ~sentir~ that something is wrong). The reflexive ~se sentir~ reports one’s own state (“to feel well”). The same ~sentīre~ and its participle ~sēnsus~ gave French a rich crop: ~sens~, ~sentiment~, ~sensible~, ~sensation~, ~consentir~ (“to feel together, agree”), ~ressentir~, ~pressentir~ (“to have a foreboding”), and the borrowed-back English ~sentiment~ and ~resent~.
en.servir:
From Latin ~servire~ (“to serve, to be a slave, to live in servitude”), a verb derived from ~servus~ (“slave, servant”). The deeper origin of ~servus~ is uncertain — some link it cautiously to a Proto-Indo-European root *~ser-~ (“to guard, watch over”), on the idea of the slave who keeps the household, while others suspect a pre-Latin, perhaps Etruscan, source — so the chain below the Latin level is best left open. The verb survives almost unchanged across Romance: Italian ~servire~, Spanish ~servir~, and French ~servir~, and English ~serve~ was borrowed from Old French.

The semantic spread of ~servir~ is unusually wide because it kept the full Latin range. From the bondage of the slave it climbed to honourable senses — to wait at table, to be of use, to render service — and even to the abstract “to serve a purpose.” The same root threw off a dense family in French: ~serviteur~ and ~servante~ for the people, ~service~ and ~serviette~ for the acts and objects (a ~serviette~ being literally what a servant brings), ~servile~ and ~servitude~ for the condition, and ~asservir~, ~desservir~, ~resservir~ among the compounds. Few words travel so far from slavery to a napkin.
en.signaler:
From the noun ~signal~ (attested 1572 as a verb), itself from Late Latin ~signalis~ (“serving as a sign”), an adjective derived from Latin ~signum~ (“mark, sign, military standard, signal”). ~Signum~ may go back, by one influential account, to the Proto-Indo-European root *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”) — a standard being literally that which one follows into battle — though some scholars instead connect it to a root meaning “to cut” (a mark cut into something). Cognate with Italian ~segnalare~ and Spanish ~señalar~, and with English ~signal~.

French borrowed ~signaler~ partly under the influence of Italian ~segnalare~, and its earliest sense was “to distinguish oneself, to make oneself notable” — to set oneself apart by some conspicuous mark. From this came the reflexive ~se signaler~ (“to distinguish oneself”) before the verb settled into its modern, more neutral senses of pointing out, notifying, or flagging. The root ~signum~ is extraordinarily prolific in French: ~signe~, ~signal~, ~signature~, ~désigner~, ~insigne~, ~enseigne~, and ~enseigner~ all spring from it, as do English ~ensign~, ~sign~, and ~insignia~. A ~signal~ and a teacher's lesson, etymologically, are the same gesture of marking something out for attention.
en.signer:
From Latin ~signare~ (“to mark with a sign, to seal, to point out, to distinguish”), derived from ~signum~ (“sign, mark, token, military standard”). ~Signum~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~seknom~ to a Proto-Indo-European root that is genuinely disputed — either *~sek-~ (“to cut,” a sign being a notch or incision) or *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow,” a sign being something one follows); scholars have not settled which, and it is honest to leave the question open. Italian ~segnare~, Spanish ~señar~, and the borrowed English ~sign~ all share the source.

The Latin verb already covered a wide field — to mark, to seal, to signal — and French ~signer~ narrowed onto the most consequential mark of all: the signature, the personal token by which one binds oneself to a document. Religious French kept the older gesture in ~se signer~, “to make the sign of the cross.” The family is large: ~signe~, ~signal~, ~signature~, ~signataire~, ~contresigner~, ~désigner~, ~assigner~, ~résigner~, and the learned ~insigne~. Even ~enseigner~ (“to teach”) and ~enseigne~ (“sign, shop-sign”) belong here, from Latin ~insignire~, “to mark out” — for to teach was once, literally, to point out and make plain.
en.signifier:
From Latin ~significāre~ (“to make a sign, to indicate, to mean”), a compound of ~signum~ (“sign, mark, token”) and ~facere~ (“to make, to do”) — so that to signify is literally “to make a sign.” Latin ~signum~ is generally derived from the Proto-Indo-European root *~sek-~ (“to cut”), through Proto-Italic ~seknom~, the original sign being a cut or incised mark, though some link it instead to *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”). Italian ~significare~ and Spanish ~significar~ continue the verb unchanged; English ~signify~ comes through Old French ~signifier~.

The drift from the concrete to the abstract is the whole story: a ~signum~ began as a physical mark — a notch, a military standard, a seal pressed in wax — and ~significāre~ meant making such a mark visible. From “to indicate by a sign” the verb glided easily into “to mean,” which is the dominant sense of ~signifier~ today, alongside a precise legal use, “to notify formally” (signifier un congé). The root spreads through a large French family — ~signe~, ~signal~, ~signaler~, ~signature~, ~signer~, ~désigner~, ~insigne~, ~assigner~, and the learned ~signifiant~ and ~signifié~ of linguistics. The neat detail is that a word now wholly about meaning carries, deep down, the image of a blade cutting a mark into wood or stone.
en.situer:
French ~situer~ is a learned borrowing from Medieval Latin ~situāre~ (“to place in a location, to establish, to assign”), a verb built from the noun ~situs~ (“position, site, location”). That noun is the past participle of the Latin verb ~sinere~ (“to let, to allow, to set down, to leave in place”) — so a ~situs~ is, etymologically, a thing that has been let stand where it is. The same ~situs~ gives English ~site~ and ~situate~, and the legal term ~situs~ survives untouched in English for the place where something legally belongs.

Unlike the battered everyday verbs of French, ~situer~ entered late and bookish, keeping the clean shape of its Latin model. Its meaning has stayed close to the source: to fix a thing in space, and figuratively to place it in a context — ~situer un événement~ (“to date or place an event”), ~se situer~ (“to be located, to position oneself”). The family that grew around it is modern and tidy: ~situation~ (“situation, location, post”), ~situé~ (“located”), ~resituer~ (“to re-place, to put back in context”), and the adjective ~situable~. From a verb of simply letting something stay put, French built its whole vocabulary of placement and circumstance.
en.soigner:
Of disputed origin, ~soigner~ most likely comes from Frankish *~sunnjōn~ (“to occupy oneself with, to attend to”), borrowed into Low Latin as ~soniāre~ and related to the noun ~soin~ (“care”). The Germanic source *~sunnja~ carried the idea of “need, necessity, care.” A rival hypothesis links it instead to Latin ~somniāre~ (“to dream”), which independently gave ~songer~ (“to think, to muse”); under that view, to care for someone began as thinking attentively about them. Cognate, on the Germanic side, with forms in the older Dutch and German care-words.

Whichever root is right, the meaning settled firmly: to ~soigner~ is to take care of, to look after, to treat — a patient, a garden, a wound, or one's appearance. The reflexive ~se soigner~ means to take care of oneself or to undergo treatment. The verb belongs to a tight French family built on ~soin~: ~soin~ itself (“care”), ~soigneux~ (“careful, painstaking”), ~soigneusement~ (“carefully”), and ~soignant~ (“caregiving,” as in ~le personnel soignant~, “the nursing staff”). The lingering uncertainty between a Germanic “need” and a Latin “dreaming” is itself the memorable detail: French may have built its word for nursing either on duty or on attentive thought.
en.sommer:
A denominal verb built on the noun ~somme~ with the suffix ~-er~. The relevant ~somme~ descends from Latin ~summa~ (“the highest thing, the total, the sum”), the feminine of ~summus~ (“highest, topmost”), itself a superlative of ~superus~ (“upper”) and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~upo~ (“up, under”). Cognate nouns survive everywhere: Italian ~somma~, Spanish ~suma~, German ~Summe~, and English ~sum~, ~summary~, and ~summit~.

The two modern senses of ~sommer~ — “to sum up” and “to summon, to call upon” — both spring from ~summa~, by different routes. Roman accountants wrote the running total at the top of a column, the ~summa~ or highest line; to sum was to reach that crowning figure, which gives the arithmetic verb. The other sense passed through the medieval idea of a final, conclusive demand — the upshot of an argument pressed on someone — attested at Douai around 1250 as “to put under obligation, to enjoin,” which is why ~sommer~ today means to formally order someone to act. The same family gives French ~somme~ (“sum”), ~sommet~ (“summit”), ~sommaire~, ~consommer~, and ~suprême~, all reaching, in their way, for the top.
en.sortir:
French ~sortir~ gathers two senses — to go out and to take out or obtain — under one verb, and its origin is debated. The dominant view derives it from Vulgar Latin *~surctus~, from Latin ~surgere~ (“to rise, to spring up”), itself a contraction of *~surrigere~, from ~sub-~ (“up from below”) and ~regere~ (“to keep straight, to direct”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₃reǵ-~ (“to straighten, to rule”). The literal early meaning was “to spring forth, to gush,” which unifies both senses: what springs out goes out, and what one makes spring forth one obtains. Cognate with Spanish ~surtir~ and Italian ~sortire~.

Around the sixteenth century ~sortir~ displaced the older ~issir~ (from Latin ~exire~) as the ordinary verb for leaving. The transitive sense — ~sortir~ a card, ~sortir~ a result, the legal ~sortir son effet~ (“to take effect”) — preserves the original notion of bringing something forth. A separate Latin verb, ~sortiri~ (“to draw lots,” from ~sors~, “fate, lot”), reinforced the “obtain” branch and may have blended in; from ~sors~ French also kept ~sort~ (“fate, spell”) and ~sorte~ (“kind, sort”). The same root *~h₃reǵ-~ that gave ~regere~ underlies ~régir~, ~direct~, ~roi~, and ~règle~.
en.souffrir:
From Vulgar Latin *~sufferīre~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~sufferre~ (“to bear, to endure, to undergo”), a compound of the prefix ~sub-~ (“under”) and the verb ~ferre~ (“to carry, to bear”). ~ferre~ goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~bʰer-~ (“to carry, to bear”), one of the most widespread of all roots, giving Ancient Greek ~φέρω~ (~phérō~), Sanskrit ~bhárati~, and — in the Germanic line — English ~bear~. To ~sufferre~ was thus literally to “carry from underneath,” to take a weight on one's shoulders.

That image of bearing a load from below is the whole of the word: the sufferer is the one who carries the burden. French kept both halves of the sense — to ~souffrir~ is to undergo pain, but also, in older and literary usage, to tolerate or permit (~souffrir une exception~, to allow an exception). The root *~bʰer-~ is enormously productive in French through ~ferre~ and its participle ~lātus~: ~offrir~, ~conférer~, ~différer~, ~transférer~, ~préférer~, and the nouns ~relation~ and ~translation~. Note that ~souffrir~ takes a single ~f~ in the infinitive but doubles it across much of its conjugation (~je souffre~) — a fossil of that Vulgar Latin *~sufferīre~ with its geminate from ~sub-~ + ~ferre~.
en.souhaiter:
From Old French ~sohaidier~ (attested 1175, in Chrétien de Troyes), reshaped into ~souhaiter~ by the mid-14th century under the influence of the noun ~souhait~ (which hardened the medial ~-d-~ to ~-t-~). The source is a Gallo-Romance ~*subtus-haitare~ (“to promise without committing oneself too far”), a hybrid built from Latin ~subtus~ (“under, beneath”) and Old French ~haitier~. That second element is Germanic: ~haitier~ goes back to a Frankish ~*haitan~ (“to call, to command, to vow”), cognate with Gothic ~haitan~ and Old High German ~heizzan~, both “to call, command.”

The blend is a tidy snapshot of how French was made — a Latin preposition welded to a Frankish verb. The ~subtus~ (“under”) lent a shade of reserve, as if a wish were a half-spoken, under-the-breath vow. In Old French ~haitier~ also meant “to gladden, to please,” and the related adjective ~haitié~ meant “cheerful, in good health,” so the family carried a warm sense of well-wishing well suited to its modern descendant. Today ~souhaiter~ keeps the noun ~souhait~ at its side, surviving also in the set phrase ~à souhait~ (“to perfection, as much as one could wish”).
en.souligner:
A transparent compound of ~sous~ (“under,” from Latin ~subtus~) and ~ligne~ (“line”), literally “to put a line under.” The noun ~ligne~ descends from Latin ~linea~ (“a linen thread, a string, a line”), originally the feminine of an adjective meaning “made of flax,” from ~linum~ (“flax, linen”). It is cognate with Italian ~linea~, Spanish ~línea~, and — borrowed through French and Latin — English ~line~ and ~linen~; the carpenter's chalked string that snapped a straight mark gave the word its geometric sense.

The literal meaning is the original one: to draw a line beneath a word on the page. From there the verb rose easily into the figurative, “to emphasize, to stress,” since underlining is how writers point a reader to what matters. French today says ~souligner un mot~ for the pen-stroke and ~souligner un fait~ for calling attention to it, the two senses living comfortably side by side. The flaxen root of ~linum~ runs through a wide field of French words: ~ligne~ and ~aligner~, ~ligature~ and ~linéaire~, and even ~lin~ (“flax”) and ~linceul~ (“a linen shroud”) — a reminder that the humble line began as a thread.
en.soumettre:
From Old French ~suzmetre~, from Latin ~submittere~ (“to lower, put under, yield”), from ~sub-~ (“under”) and ~mittere~ (“to send, let go, throw”). In French the verb was remodeled on ~mettre~ (“to put”), with the prefix ~sous-~ (“under”). The Latin ~mittere~ is traced to a Proto-Indo-European root *~meyth-~ (“to exchange, remove”). Cognate with Italian ~sottomettere~ and Spanish ~someter~, and — through ~mittere~ — with English ~submit~, ~mission~, and ~commit~.

The literal sense is to “put under,” and the verb still flexes between two directions: to ~soumettre~ a people is to subjugate it, to place it beneath one's power; but to ~soumettre~ a proposal is to lay it before someone for judgment — to “submit” it, deferentially. The reflexive ~se soumettre~ completes the picture, meaning to yield or comply. From the rich ~mittere~ family, whose participle ~missus~ gives the ~-mis~ of so many forms, French draws ~mettre~, ~mission~, ~message~, ~promettre~, ~permettre~, ~admettre~, and ~commettre~ — a whole verbal household built on the idea of sending and placing.
en.soutenir:
From Old French ~sostenir~ (later ~soustenir~), from Vulgar Latin *~sustenīre~, a reshaping of Classical Latin ~sustinēre~ (“to hold up, bear, endure”). The verb is transparently ~sub-~ (“up from below”) + ~tenēre~ (“to hold”), which descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch”). Through that root it is cognate with Latin ~tendere~ (“to stretch”) and ~tenuis~ (“thin”), with Greek ~teínein~ (“to stretch”), and with English ~thin~. Its Romance siblings include Italian ~sostenere~ and Spanish ~sostener~, both still everyday verbs.

The literal Latin sense was physical: to hold something up from underneath, to prop it. From that base the meaning fanned out into nearly every figurative direction in which a weight can be borne — to ~soutenir~ an argument is to uphold it, to ~soutenir~ a friend is to back them, and to ~soutenir~ a siege is to withstand it. The same ~tenēre~ underlies a whole French battalion of compounds built on ~tenir~: ~contenir~, ~détenir~, ~maintenir~, ~obtenir~, ~retenir~. A surprising offshoot is the noun ~souteneur~, literally “one who supports,” which French narrowed to mean a pimp — the man who, with grim irony, “sustains” a prostitute by living off her. The plainer noun ~soutien~ (“support”) and the adjective ~tenace~ (“tenacious”) keep the root’s honest sense of holding firm.
en.souvenir:
From Latin ~subvenīre~ (“to come up, come to one's aid”), formed from ~sub-~ (“under, from below”) and ~venīre~ (“to come”). ~Venīre~ descends from Proto-Italic *~gʷenjō~ and the Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to step”), the very root behind English ~come~, Ancient Greek ~βαίνω~ (~baínō~, “to walk”), and Sanskrit ~गच्छति~ (~gácchati~, “goes”). Old French inherited it as ~sovenir~.

The image is wonderfully concrete: a memory ~comes up~ from below into the mind, “se présente à la mémoire.” What in Latin meant “to come to the rescue” narrowed in French to this mental rising — a recollection surfacing unbidden. The verb is normally reflexive, ~se souvenir de~, “to remember,” and its infinitive was nominalized into ~le souvenir~, a memory or a keepsake — the noun English borrowed wholesale as ~souvenir~. The ~venīre~ family is enormous in French: ~venir~, ~devenir~, ~revenir~, ~convenir~, ~prévenir~, ~survenir~, and ~subvenir~ (which kept the older “to provide for, come to aid”). Each is, at heart, a different way of coming.
en.spécialiser:
A modern French derivative, formed from the adjective ~spécial~ plus the productive verbal suffix ~-iser~. ~Spécial~ comes from Latin ~speciālis~ (“belonging to a particular kind”), from ~speciēs~ (“appearance, sort, kind”), the noun of ~specere~ (“to look at”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~spek̑-~ (“to observe, to watch”). The suffix ~-iser~ descends through Latin ~-izāre~ from Greek ~-ízein~. English ~specialize~ and Italian ~specializzare~ are parallel formations on the same Latin adjective.

The semantic thread runs through ~speciēs~: a “species” is a particular kind set apart by its appearance, so to specialize is to narrow oneself to one particular kind of thing. The reflexive ~se spécialiser~ means to confine one's training to a single field; the transitive sense — to assign a specialized task, to earmark — keeps the same logic of singling out. Because the root is *~spek̑-~, “to look,” ~spécialiser~ belongs to the great visual family of French: ~espèce~ (the direct descendant of ~speciēs~), ~spécimen~, ~spécifique~, ~spécieux~ (“specious,” fair only in appearance), and — through the looking sense itself — ~spectacle~, ~aspect~, and ~respecter~.
en.subir:
From Latin ~subire~ (“to go under, to approach, to undergo, to submit to”), a compound of the preposition ~sub~ (“under”) and the irregular verb ~ire~ (“to go”). ~ire~ descends from Proto-Italic *~eō~ and ultimately from PIE *~ey-~ (“to go”), a root of remarkable productivity. Cognate with Italian ~subire~ and Spanish and Portuguese ~subir~ — though the Iberian verbs, by a curious twist, came to mean “to go up” while French kept the Latin sense of going under and enduring.

The metaphor at the heart of ~subir~ is bodily: to place oneself beneath a weight and bear it. Latin ~subire~ already covered both the neutral “to undergo” and the heavier “to suffer, to be subjected to,” and French inherited this passive, enduring colour — one ~subit~ a defeat, an interrogation, an operation, rarely anything one would have chosen. The PIE root *~ey-~ behind ~ire~ surfaces all across French through Latin compounds: ~transir~, the noun ~issue~, and learned forms such as ~ambition~, ~initial~, ~transit~, and ~sédition~ all trace to that single small verb “to go.” The closely related ~subit~ (“sudden”) and ~subitement~ preserve another shade of ~subire~ — the abrupt arrival of what “comes up” on us unawares.
en.succéder:
From Latin ~succēdere~ (“to go under, to come up to, to follow after, to take the place of”), from ~sub-~ (“under, up to”) and ~cēdere~ (“to go, to withdraw, to yield”). ~Cēdere~ is of disputed origin, perhaps from Proto-Indo-European *~ḱe~ (“here”) plus *~sed-~ (“to sit”), giving “to go and sit.” Cognate, through ~cēdere~, with English ~cede~ and the doublets Italian ~succedere~ and Spanish ~suceder~.

The image inside ~sub-~ + ~cēdere~ is of stepping up *under* someone — moving into the place they vacate — which neatly yields two of the verb's senses: to follow in order, and to inherit a throne or office. From the same ~cēdere~ French built a dense network of motion-and-yielding verbs: ~céder~, ~accéder~, ~précéder~, ~procéder~, and ~succéder~ itself, plus the nouns ~succès~, ~successeur~, ~succession~, and ~procès~. Strikingly, ~succès~ (“success”) is just the past-participle stem of ~succēdere~: a success is literally an outcome that has “followed on,” a “coming-after” that turned out well — the neutral idea of sequence narrowing, over centuries, into the bright modern sense of triumph.
en.suffire:
From Old French ~soufire~, from Vulgar Latin *~suffīcere~ (with a lengthened ~ī~ that drew it into the same class as ~dīcere~, “to say”), from Classical Latin ~sufficere~, a compound of ~sub-~ (“under, beneath”) and ~facere~ (“to make, to do”). ~Facere~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~dʰeh₁-~ (“to put, to place”), the same root that gives English ~do~ and German ~tun~. The form was later relatinized: the medieval ~soufire~ was reshaped toward its Latin source, isolatedly in the fourteenth century and then regularly from the sixteenth, yielding modern ~suffire~.

The Latin compound first meant “to put under, to set in place of, to supply,” and intransitively “to place oneself beneath” — that is, to be capable of bearing a load, hence “to be enough.” It is this image of standing under a weight without giving way that survives in the French sense “to suffice.” The same Latin ~facere~ underlies an enormous family of French words, among them ~faire~, ~défaire~, ~satisfaire~, and the learned doublet ~fabriquer~; the buried prefix ~sub-~ likewise surfaces in ~souffrir~, ~soutenir~, and ~soumettre~. A small curiosity: ~suffire~ keeps an irregular past participle, ~suffi~, that takes no agreement — a quiet reminder of its tangled Latin pedigree.
en.suggérer:
A learned borrowing from Latin ~suggerere~ (“to carry up, supply, furnish; to prompt, advise”), formed from the prefix ~sub-~ (“under, up from below”) and the verb ~gerere~ (“to carry, bear, conduct”). ~Gerere~ comes from Proto-Italic ~*gezō~; its supine stem ~gestum~ underlies a host of English words. The French verb was taken straight from Latin rather than worn down through Old French, which is why it preserves the Latin shape so cleanly.

The image inside ~suggerere~ is to “carry up” material from below — to supply or feed something to someone, and so to *prompt* a thought, to put an idea into another's mind without stating it outright. French ~suggérer~ keeps exactly that delicacy: one suggests rather than commands. The root ~gerere~ (“to carry, to carry on”) seeded a remarkably wide family across French and English, including ~geste~ and English ~gesture~, ~gestation~, ~digérer~ (“to digest,” to carry apart), ~registre~, ~congérer~, and the noun ~suggestion~ itself, built on the supine ~suggestum~.
en.suivre:
From Old French ~sivre~, later ~suivre~ — the modern form reshaped on the third-person present ~suit~ (earlier ~siut~) — from Vulgar Latin *~sequere~, a regularized form of Classical Latin ~sequī~ (“to follow”), a deponent verb, from Proto-Italic *~sekʷor~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ (“to follow”). Cognate with Italian ~seguire~, Spanish ~seguir~, and Occitan ~sègre~; through the same root come Greek ~hépesthai~ and Sanskrit ~sácate~, both “to follow.”

The verb's history is a tangle of irregular forms: the Latin ~q~ produced wildly different dialect outcomes, from Picard ~suire~ to Burgundian ~seugre~ and Norman ~suure~, before standard French settled on the ~-v-~ stem. The PIE root *~sekʷ-~ proved astonishingly fertile in Latin: from the participle ~secundus~ (“following,” hence “second”) French inherited ~second~, while the compounds gave ~poursuivre~, ~ensuivre~, ~exécuter~, ~persécuter~, and ~poursuite~. Even the everyday ~suite~ — a musical sequence, a hotel suite, a retinue “following” a dignitary — is the same root, as is ~conséquence~, literally a “following-together.” A single notion of one thing coming after another thus branches into law, music, logic, and hospitality.
en.supporter:
From Latin ~supportāre~ (“to carry, to bring up, to convey”), a compound of the prefix ~sub-~ (“under, from below”) and the verb ~portāre~ (“to carry”) — literally “to carry from underneath,” the posture of one who bears a load on the back. ~Portāre~ goes back to Proto-Italic *~portāō~ and ultimately to the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to cross, to pass through”), the same root that gave Latin ~porta~ (“gate”) and ~portus~ (“harbour”). It is cognate with the Romance family of Italian ~portare~ and Spanish ~portar~, and, through *~per-~, with English ~ford~ and ~fare~.

The Latin sense of physical bearing branched in two directions. One kept the literal idea of holding something up: a beam ~supports~ a roof. The other turned inward and moral — to “bear up under” hardship, hence “to endure, to tolerate.” Modern French keeps both: a wall ~supporte~ a weight, and one ~supporte~ a noisy neighbour, often grudgingly. A curious twist: the sporting sense “to support a team” was re-imported from English ~supporter~, itself a much older loan from French — a word that travelled the Channel and came home with new baggage. From the same root *~per-~ descend a crowd of French words, among them ~porter~, ~porte~, ~port~, ~apporter~, and ~transporter~.
en.supposer:
A French formation modelled on Latin ~supponere~ (“to put under, to substitute, to assume”), reshaped on the native verb ~poser~ (“to place”) under the influence of the prefix ~sub-~ (“under”). Strictly, ~poser~ descends from Late Latin ~pausare~ (“to pause, to set down”), which crossed paths with Latin ~ponere~ (“to put”); but the whole ~-poser~ family — ~supposer~, ~composer~, ~proposer~, ~disposer~ — was built in French to answer the Latin verbs in ~-ponere~, so ~supposer~ stands for ~supponere~, literally “to place under.” Cognate in sense with English ~suppose~ and Italian ~supporre~.

The modern abstract meaning — to assume, to conjecture, to take as a premise — grew in scholastic Latin, where ~suppositio~ rendered the logician's notion of a hypothesis, itself a calque of Greek ~ὑπόθεσις~ (~hupóthesis~, literally a “placing under,” a foundation laid beneath an argument). So both the Latin and the Greek term picture an assumption as something set underneath, a support propping up reasoning. French ~supposer~ thus spans the concrete and the mental: to place under, to substitute (~supposer un enfant~, in old law, to pass off a false heir), and to suppose, imply, or surmise. Kin in French include ~supposition~, ~présupposer~, ~suppositoire~ — literally a thing “placed underneath” — and the whole ~poser~ clan.
en.supprimer:
Borrowed from Latin ~supprimere~ (“to press down, to hold back, to stifle”), a compound of ~sub-~ (“under”) and ~premere~ (“to press”). ~Premere~ traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~per-~ (“to strike, to press”). It is cognate with Italian ~sopprimere~ and Spanish ~suprimir~, and — through the same Latin verb — with English ~suppress~ and ~press~ itself.

The Latin sense was physical, “to press something down so it cannot rise,” and the French verb keeps that gesture in the abstract: to suppress a revolt, to delete a line, to remove a clause is to push it out of sight. The arrival of computing gave ~supprimer~ a brisk new home as the everyday word for “to delete” a file. The root ~premere~ is one of the most fertile in French, surfacing in ~presser~ (“to press”) and its many kin — ~exprimer~ (“to express,” to press out), ~imprimer~ (“to print,” to press in), ~comprimer~ (“to compress”), ~opprimer~ (“to oppress”), and ~déprimer~ (“to depress”) — a family in which the simple act of pressing has been bent to a dozen meanings.
en.surprendre:
A French compound of the prefix ~sur-~ (“over, upon”) and the verb ~prendre~ (“to take”), modeled on Latin ~super~ + ~prehendere~. ~Prendre~ comes from the contracted Latin ~prendere~, from ~prehendere~ (“to seize, grasp”), which breaks into ~prae-~ (“before”) and a root ~-hendere~ akin to Ancient Greek ~χανδάνω~ (~khandánō~, “to hold, contain”) and, through Proto-Indo-European *~ǵʰend-~ (“to seize”), to English ~get~. Old French had several near-variants — ~sorprendre~, ~sousprendre~ — that later merged.

The literal image is to “take from above,” to overtake. Early on ~surprendre~ meant to seize or attack someone unawares, to take a town by surprise — a military sense of catching the enemy off guard. From this grew the two modern strands: to catch out (to take someone in the act, ~surprendre un secret~) and simply to surprise, to astonish, since the unexpected catches the mind unprepared. The noun ~surprise~ (originally a “taking unawares”) followed the verb into English. Relatives in the ~prendre~ family include ~prise~, ~apprendre~, ~comprendre~, ~entreprendre~, and ~surprenant~ — a whole web spun from the idea of grasping.
en.susciter:
From Latin ~suscitāre~ (“to rouse, to stir up, to awaken, to revive”), a compound of the prefix ~sub-~ (here “up from under”) and ~citāre~ (“to set in rapid motion, to summon”). ~citāre~ is the frequentative of ~ciēre~ (“to move, to stir, to call”), which goes back to the Proto-Indo-European root *~kei-~ (“to set in motion”). That root also yielded Greek ~kīneîn~ (“to move”), the source of ~cinéma~ and ~kinetic~. French borrowed ~susciter~ as a learned term, so its form stayed close to the Latin.

The literal image is of stirring something up from below — rousing a sleeper, reviving the dead, calling a force into being. Old French already used it for arousing emotions or provoking events, and modern ~susciter~ keeps that abstract reach: one ~suscite~ admiration, curiosity, opposition, or a vocation, always evoking something into existence rather than handling it directly. From the same ~ciēre~ family, French gathered a striking crowd of relatives: ~citer~ (“to cite, to summon”), ~exciter~ (“to excite”), ~inciter~ (“to incite”), ~solliciter~ (“to solicit”), ~ressusciter~ (“to resurrect”), and ~récit~ and ~réciter~ (a tale being something “called back”). The same primal idea of motion even powers ~cinématographe~, so the stirred verb and the moving picture share one ancestral impulse.
en.sécuriser:
A modern French formation, built from the stem of ~sécurité~ (or ~sécur-~) plus the productive verbal suffix ~-iser~. The ancestor is Latin ~sēcūrus~ (“free from care, untroubled, safe”), a transparent compound of ~sē-~ (“without, apart”) and ~cūra~ (“care, worry”): to be ~sēcūrus~ was literally to be “without a care.” Through Old French this same ~sēcūrus~ also gave the everyday adjective ~sûr~ (“sure, safe”), so that ~sûr~ and the learned ~sécurité~ are doublets, the popular and the scholarly descendants of one Latin word. English ~secure~ and ~sure~ form the very same pair.

The verb itself is strikingly recent: the adjective ~sécurisant~ appeared in the late 1950s, and the transitive ~sécuriser~ followed around 1969, first in a psychological sense — to ~sécuriser~ someone was to set their mind at ease, to free them from anxiety, true to the Latin “without care.” Only later did it broaden to the concrete senses now dominant — to ~sécuriser~ a building, a network, a payment. The same ~cūra~ underlies ~curieux~, ~cure~, ~soin~ via a different path, ~assurer~, and ~procurer~.
en.sélectionner:
A nineteenth-century formation from the noun ~sélection~, from Latin ~selectio~ (“a choosing out”), the action noun of ~seligere~ (“to choose out, to single out, to cull”). ~seligere~ is a compound of ~se-~ (“apart, aside”) and ~legere~ (“to gather, to pick, to read”), so its literal sense is “to gather apart.” ~legere~ goes back to PIE *~leǵ-~ (“to collect, to gather”). Cognate, through the same Latin verb, with English ~select~ and Italian ~selezionare~.

The ~legere~ at its core is one of the most generous roots in the language: the act of “picking out” specialized in Latin into reading (picking out letters), and *~leǵ-~ underlies a striking spread of French words — ~lire~, ~élire~, ~collection~, ~légende~ (“things to be read”), and, through the Greek branch of the same root, ~lexique~ and ~dialogue~. ~sélectionner~ itself is the youngest layer: where the older ~choisir~ (a Germanic borrowing) simply picks, ~sélectionner~ carries the cooler, more deliberate flavour of choosing the best from a set — a coach who ~sélectionne~ players, a process of natural ~sélection~. The doublet ~élire~ shows the same ~legere~ worn smooth by centuries of everyday use, while ~sélectionner~ keeps the crisp Latin consonants of a learned, modern coinage.
en.séparer:
Borrowed in 1314 from Latin ~separāre~ (“to pull apart, to divide”), a learned doublet of the inherited French verb ~sevrer~ (“to wean”). The Latin verb joins ~se-~ (“apart, aside”) to ~parāre~ (“to make ready, to procure”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~perh₃-~ (“to produce, to procure”). Cognates abound: Italian ~separare~, Spanish ~separar~, English ~separate~ and its Frenchified doublet ~sever~.

The curious point is that French inherited the same Latin verb twice. The ordinary popular descendant of ~separāre~ is ~sevrer~, which narrowed to the specific sense of separating a child or young animal from the breast — to wean — while the scholarly re-borrowing ~séparer~ kept the broad Latin meaning, to set apart. Such pairs, one worn down by centuries of speech and one taken straight from books, are doublets, and ~séparer~/~sevrer~ is a textbook case. The verb branched into ~séparation~ (including the legal ~séparation de corps~, a separation short of divorce), ~séparé~, ~séparément~, ~séparateur~, and the political ~séparatisme~. The second element ~parāre~ is itself remarkably fertile, lying behind ~préparer~, ~réparer~, ~emparer~, and — through the related ~parere~ (“to give birth, to bring forth”) — the family of ~parent~.
en.tendre:
From Latin ~tendere~ (“to stretch, extend, strain, aim, direct oneself toward”), from Proto-Indo-European *~ten-~ (“to stretch”). Cognate with Italian ~tendere~ and Spanish ~tender~, with Greek ~τείνειν~ (“to stretch”), and — through the same root — with English ~thin~ (a thing “stretched out”) and, more distantly, ~tend~ and ~tone~.

The physical idea of pulling taut radiates into every sense of the verb: one can ~tendre~ a rope (make it tense), ~tendre~ the hand (stretch it out), or ~tendre~ toward a goal (aim oneself at it). That last, directional sense — straining toward something — is the root of ~tendance~ (“tendency, trend”) and ~tension~. Latin ~tendere~ and the kindred ~tenēre~ (“to hold”) seeded an enormous progeny in French: with prefixes come ~attendre~ (to stretch one's mind toward, hence to wait), ~entendre~ (originally to direct one's attention, hence to hear), ~prétendre~, ~étendre~, ~détendre~, and ~sous-tendre~; the participle gives ~tente~ (a “stretched” canvas) and ~tendon~. Beware the homonym: the adjective ~tendre~ (“soft, tender”) comes instead from Latin ~tener~ — a separate word that merely fell together in spelling.
en.tenir:
French ~tenir~ comes from Latin ~tenēre~ (“to hold, to keep”), which passed through a Vulgar Latin *~tenīre~ before reaching Old French ~tenir~. The Latin verb rests on Proto-Indo-European *~ten-~ (“to stretch”), the same root behind Latin ~tendere~ (“to stretch”) and ~tenuis~ (“thin”), Greek ~teínein~ (“to stretch”) and ~tónos~ (“tension, pitch”), Sanskrit ~tanoti~ (“he stretches”), and English ~thin~. The bridge from “stretch” to “hold” runs through the idea of keeping something taut — to hold a thing is to maintain it under tension.

In Romance the verb stayed central: Italian ~tenere~, Spanish ~tener~, Portuguese ~ter~ all live on as everyday words, with Spanish even pressing ~tener~ into service for “to have.” French ~tenir~ is likewise one of the busiest verbs in the language, anchoring countless idioms — ~tenir bon~ (“to hold firm”), ~tenir à~ (“to be attached to”), ~tenir de~ (“to take after”). The root *~ten-~ also yields ~tendre~ (“to stretch, to hold out”), ~tension~, ~contenir~, ~maintenir~ (literally “to hold in the hand,” from ~manu tenēre~), ~obtenir~, and ~teneur~. Even the musical ~ténor~ belongs here: the voice that originally “held” the sustained melodic line.
en.tenter:
From Latin ~temptāre~ (also spelled ~tentāre~), “to feel, to handle, to test, to attempt, to attack.” The verb carries an iterative-frequentative force and rests on the Proto-Indo-European root *~ten-~ (“to stretch”); De Vaan, more cautiously, derives it from an extended base *~temp-~ (“to touch, to feel”). Cognates and descendants abound: Italian ~tentare~, Spanish ~tentar~, and — borrowed through French — English ~tempt~, ~taunt~, and ~tentative~.

The meaning travelled from the hand to the soul. To ~tenter~ originally meant to probe by touch, to test the strength of a thing — hence “to put to the test,” a sense kept in the religious ~tenter Dieu~ (“to tempt God,” i.e. to test divine patience) and in ~tenter sa chance~ (“to try one's luck”). From “to test someone” it slid easily to “to entice, to allure,” the dominant modern sense: temptation is, etymologically, a trial. If the root truly is *~ten-~, ~tenter~ belongs to one of the largest Indo-European word-families in French, alongside ~tendre~, ~tension~, ~tente~ (a stretched canvas), ~attenter~, and the borrowed ~tentative~ — all turning on the image of stretching toward something.
en.terminer:
From Latin ~terminare~ (“to set bounds to, to limit, to bring to an end”), a denominal verb built on the noun ~terminus~ (“boundary marker, limit, end”). ~terminus~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~termenos~ to a Proto-Indo-European *~térmn̥~ (“boundary”), and is cognate with Ancient Greek ~térma~ (“goal, finishing post”) and ~térmōn~ (“border”). The Romance siblings include Italian ~terminare~ and Spanish ~terminar~, both “to finish.”

The memorable detail is mythological: ~Terminus~ was also a Roman god, the deity of boundary stones, worshipped each February at the festival of the ~Terminalia~. His shrine on the Capitoline was so sacred that when the great temple of Jupiter was built around it, Terminus alone refused to be moved — a sign, the Romans said, that the bounds of their state would never recede. So at the root of “to finish” lies a stone planted in the earth to say “this far and no farther.” From the same source French has ~terme~ (“term, deadline, word”), ~terminaison~ (“ending”), ~déterminer~ (“to fix the limits of, to decide”), and ~exterminer~ (literally “to drive beyond the boundary”); English ~terminate~, ~term~, and the cinematic ~Terminator~ all share it.
en.tester:
The modern French ~tester~ (“to test, to try out”) was formed from the noun ~test~ with the suffix ~-er~, under the influence of English ~to test~. The English noun came, in the fourteenth century, from Old French ~test~ (“an earthen pot”), from Latin ~testum~ and ~testa~ (“earthenware vessel, potsherd, shell”) — the assayer's small cup in which metals were melted to prove their purity. Cognates of that Latin word include Italian ~testa~ and the French inheritance ~tête~, both “head.”

French thus has two unrelated verbs spelled ~tester~. The older, a legal term from Latin ~testārī~ (“to bear witness, to make a will”), means “to make a will” and gives ~testament~ and ~testateur~. The newer ~tester~, our verb, is the metallurgist's pot come full circle: the cup that tested gold lent its name to the noun ~test~, which English turned into a verb of trial and examination, and which French then re-imported. The most surprising relative is ~tête~: in Vulgar Latin the “little pot” ~testa~ was used jokingly for the skull, and that humorous slang ousted the classical ~caput~, so that the ordinary French word for “head” is, etymologically, a clay jug. From the same vessel come ~têt~ (a potsherd), ~tesson~, and the borrowed ~test~ and its newer kin ~tester~, ~retester~, and ~testeur~.
en.tirer:
The origin of ~tirer~ is debated. The leading hypothesis derives it from Gothic *~tairan~ (“to tear, to pull apart”), the same Germanic source that gave Spanish ~tirar~; this fits the kinship felt between ~tirer~, ~tirailler~, and ~déchirer~, and is paralleled by German ~zehren~ (“to consume, destroy”) and ~zerren~ (“to tug”). A rival Latin account (Wartburg) sees a clipping of Old French ~martirier~ (“to martyr, to torture”), from ~martyre~. In any case ~tirer~ largely displaced the older ~traire~, from Latin ~trahere~ (“to pull, to drag”).

The spread of meaning is wide: from the core “to pull” come ~tirer~ “to draw out” (~tirer un trait~, “to draw a line”), “to fire, to shoot” (a crossbow being drawn before release), and the printing sense (a ~tirage~ is a press-run, paper pulled off the plate). The torture connection, if true, is grimly apt — medieval racking stretched and dislocated limbs by pulling. Whatever ~tirer~'s own pedigree, the displaced ~traire~ left a productive Latin family in French: ~soustraire~, ~extraire~, ~soutirer~, ~étirer~, ~retirer~, and ~attirer~, all turning on the act of drawing.
en.tomber:
From an expressive Vulgar Latin *~tumbāre~ (“to fall, to tumble”), built on a root *~tumb-~ that imitates the sound and suddenness of a fall. In Old French this met and merged with ~tumer~ (“to do somersaults, to tumble”), a verb of Frankish origin from *~tūmōn~ (compare Old High German ~tūmōn~, “to turn, to reel”); through that Germanic stock the same idea survives in English ~tumble~. The two near-identical verbs blurred together, and ~tumer~ died out by the sixteenth century, leaving ~tomber~ alone.

What makes ~tomber~ remarkable is that it is an upstart. Old French already had a perfectly good verb for falling, ~choir~ (from Latin ~cadere~), but from the fifteenth century the livelier, half-onomatopoeic ~tomber~ pressed in, and by the seventeenth it had all but ousted ~choir~ from everyday speech. ~Choir~ now survives only in literary corners and in its compound ~déchoir~ (“to decline, to fall from grace”), while its participle lingers in ~échu~ and ~déchet~. ~Tomber~, meanwhile, spread into countless idioms — ~tomber amoureux~ (“to fall in love”), ~tomber malade~, ~laisser tomber~ — and spun off ~tombée~ (“nightfall”) and ~retomber~.
en.toucher:
From Old French ~tochier~ (“to touch, to strike, to mention”), from Vulgar Latin *~toccāre~ (“to knock, to strike, as a bell”). This *~toccāre~ is widely held to be of imitative origin, built on the sound ~toc~ — the sharp “knock” made when two hard things meet. It thus replaced the older Latin ~tangere~ (“to touch”). Romance kin include Italian ~toccare~ and Spanish ~tocar~, and through Old French it gave English ~touch~.

The semantic drift is a small marvel of physics: a blow at its very gentlest is nothing more than a touch, so “to strike” slid quietly into “to make contact with.” From bodily contact French ~toucher~ reached for figurative senses — to ~toucher~ a sum of money (to receive it), to ~toucher~ someone (to move them emotionally), and to ~toucher~ a subject (to broach it). The onomatopoeic ~toc~ left other traces: ~toquer~ (“to knock”), the alarm-bell ~tocsin~ (which once literally meant a struck signal), and the noun ~toucher~ itself, the sense of touch. The musical ~toccata~, borrowed into many languages, is from the Italian cousin ~toccare~ — a “touched” keyboard piece.
en.tourner:
From Old French ~torner~, from Latin ~tornare~ (“to turn on a lathe, to round off”), a verb built on ~tornus~ (“a lathe, a turner's chisel”). That noun was itself borrowed from Ancient Greek ~τόρνος~ (~tórnos~), a compass or lathe for tracing circles, ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~terh₁-~ (“to rub, to bore by twisting”). Through the same Latin source come Italian ~tornare~, Spanish ~tornar~, and — by way of Norman and Old French — English ~turn~ and ~tour~.

The earliest sense was thoroughly mechanical: to shape wood or metal by spinning it against a fixed blade, the craft of the ~tourneur~. From this image of rotation the meaning fanned out to cover any circular movement, then a change of direction (~tourner à gauche~), then figurative reversals of fortune and mood (~tourner mal~, “to turn out badly”). The culinary sense of stirring, and the cinematic sense of shooting a film — ~tourner un film~, a memory of the hand-cranked camera — both descend from the same idea of turning. The lathe-sense survives intact in ~tour~ (“lathe”) and ~tournage~. From the Greek-Latin root spring a wide family: ~tour~, ~tournoi~, ~tournevis~, ~contour~, ~détour~, and ~retourner~.
en.traduire:
From Latin ~traducere~ (“to lead across, to bring over”), from ~trans-~ (“across”) + ~ducere~ (“to lead, conduct”). ~ducere~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ (“to draw, to pull”), cognate with English ~tow~ and ~tug~ and with German ~ziehen~ (“to pull”). The Romance siblings are Italian ~tradurre~ and Spanish ~traducir~; French had also inherited the older, properly translation-meaning verb ~translater~, from Latin ~translatare~.

The modern sense rests on a famous misreading. In classical Latin ~traducere~ meant “to lead across, to transfer, to parade” — even “to disgrace,” by leading a culprit publicly across the forum. Around 1400 the humanist Leonardo Bruni, parsing a phrase of Aulus Gellius, took ~traducere~ to mean “to render from one tongue into another,” and the brilliant error spread across Europe, ousting ~translater~ in French by the sixteenth century. A ghost of the old “lead across” sense lingers in the legal ~traduire en justice~ (“to bring before a court”). From the same ~ducere~ French drew a teeming family: ~conduire~, ~produire~, ~réduire~, ~séduire~, ~duc~, and ~aqueduc~.
en.traiter:
From Latin ~tractāre~ (“to handle, to manage, to deal with”), the frequentative of ~trahere~ (“to draw, to pull”), whose past participle is ~tractus~. ~Trahere~ goes back to a PIE root often given as *~tregʰ-~ (“to draw, to drag”); the relationship to Germanic ~drag~ is debated, so it is left aside. Within French itself ~traiter~ is a doublet of the inherited ~traire~ (“to milk,” originally “to draw”). Cognate with Italian ~trattare~ and Spanish ~tratar~.

The frequentative force of ~tractāre~ is “to keep drawing, to handle repeatedly,” and from there “to handle” in every sense — physically, then socially, then intellectually.
