That spread explains the wide modern range of ~traiter~: to treat a person (well or badly), to treat a patient, to deal with a subject, to process a material or data, and, via ~traité~, to conclude a treaty. The participle ~tractus~ seeded ~trait~ (“line, feature, stroke”), and the same ~trahere~ family gives French ~tracter~, ~traction~, ~extraire~, ~abstrait~, and ~portrait~ — all built on the basic gesture of drawing something out.
en.transformer:
From Latin ~transformāre~ (“to change in shape, transform”), a compound of the prefix ~trans-~ (“across, beyond”) and ~formāre~ (“to shape, form”), itself from ~forma~ (“form, shape, figure”). The verb entered French around 1295. The origin of ~forma~ is debated: it may be a borrowing from Greek ~morphḗ~ (“form, shape, beauty”) — perhaps via Etruscan — with the consonants transposed, or both words may inherit a common Proto-Indo-European source. The Latin verb also gives Italian ~trasformare~, Spanish ~transformar~, and English ~transform~.

The sense has stayed remarkably stable: to carry a thing “across” into a new form. From that core, French applies it everywhere — from caterpillar into butterfly (a metamorphosis), from raw material into product, and, in rugby, the bonus kick that ~transforme~ a try into added points. ~forma~ seeded an enormous family in French: ~former~ (“to form, train”), ~forme~, ~formule~, ~formel~, ~conforme~, ~réformer~, ~déformer~, ~informer~ (literally “to give shape to,” to put into the mind), and ~uniforme~. The disputed link to Greek ~morphḗ~ means that ~transformer~ may be a distant cousin of ~métamorphose~ — two ways of speaking about a change of shape.
en.transférer:
A learned borrowing from Latin ~trānsferre~ (“to carry across, to bear over, to transport”), formed from the prefix ~trāns-~ (“across, beyond”) and ~ferre~ (“to carry, to bear”), from Proto-Indo-European *~bʰer-~ (“to carry, to bear”). That root is one of the most widespread in all Indo-European: it is cognate with English ~bear~, Greek ~phérein~ (“to carry”), and Sanskrit ~bhárati~ (“he carries”). The Latin ~trānsferre~ also gave the past participle ~trānslātus~, source of the English ~translate~ — itself a “carrying across,” of meaning from one tongue to another.

French remodeled the inherited stem on the pattern of regular ~-érer~ verbs, yielding ~transférer~. Its core image is literal transport — carrying a thing from one place to another — and it still serves for moving a prisoner, an employee, a sum of money, or a body from place to place. The figurative ~transfert~ went on to a distinguished career in psychology and law. From the same ~ferre~ French inherited a great convoy of words: ~conférer~, ~déférer~, ~différer~, ~offrir~, ~souffrir~, ~préférer~, and the noun ~fertile~ (“bearing, fruitful”) — every one of them, like ~transférer~, a matter of something carried.
en.transmettre:
From Latin ~transmittere~ (“to send across, to send over”), reshaped in French on the model of ~mettre~. The Latin verb joins the prefix ~trans-~ (“across, beyond”) and ~mittere~ (“to send, to let go”), which descends from Proto-Italic *~meitō~ and a Proto-Indo-European root *~mey-~ (“to change, to exchange”). The sense traveled from “exchange” to “give, let go” to “send.” English borrowed the same compound as ~transmit~.

The image is one of carrying something across a gap — a message, a disease, an inheritance, a radio wave — and handing it on. ~Mittere~ was one of Latin's most prolific verbs, and through its French heir ~mettre~ (“to put”) and its many compounds it shaped a huge vocabulary: ~admettre~, ~commettre~, ~permettre~, ~promettre~, ~soumettre~, and ~remettre~, alongside the nouns of the ~missus~ / ~missio~ branch — ~mission~, ~message~ (literally “a thing sent”), ~messager~, ~commission~, and ~mise~. So when we ~transmettre~ knowledge from one generation to the next, we are quite literally “sending it across,” performing in the abstract the same act a Roman did when he dispatched a courier over the frontier.
en.transporter:
From Latin ~transportāre~ (“to carry across, to convey”), composed of the prefix ~trans-~ (“across, beyond”) and ~portāre~ (“to carry”). The verb ~portāre~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~per-~ (“to pass through, to cross over”), the root behind Latin ~porta~ (“gate”) and ~portus~ (“harbour”), and behind English ~fare~, ~ford~, and ~ferry~ — all notions of passage and crossing. Italian ~trasportare~ and Spanish ~transportar~ are the direct Romance parallels.

The literal sense — to carry people or goods from one place to another — has stayed central since antiquity, and it powers a whole modern vocabulary of movement. But French also kept a striking figurative use: to be ~transporté~ is to be carried away by emotion, “transported” with joy, anger, or love, the soul borne off as if bodily. The noun ~transport~ likewise means both “transportation” and a “rapture” or surge of feeling (~un transport de joie~). From the shared ancestor ~portāre~ flows a vast family of French words: ~porter~ itself, ~porteur~, ~apporter~, ~emporter~, ~rapporter~, ~importer~ and ~exporter~, ~portatif~, and ~portable~ — all built on the simple idea of carrying.
en.travailler:
French ~travailler~ comes from Old French ~traveillier~ (“to torment, to torture, to cause suffering”), from Vulgar Latin *~tripāliāre~ (“to torture with the ~tripalium~”). The ~tripalium~ was, on the most widespread account, an instrument of restraint or torture made of three stakes — ~tri-~ (“three”) plus ~pālus~ (“stake”) — to which slaves were bound for punishment or large animals were tied to be shod. The same source gives Spanish ~trabajar~, Portuguese ~trabalhar~, and Occitan ~trabalhar~, all the ordinary words for “to work.”

The semantic journey is startling: from “to torture” the verb passed to “to suffer, to toil painfully,” then to the neutral “to labor,” and finally to today's “to work” of every kind. The older sense of anguish has not wholly vanished — the noun ~travail~ still names the pains of childbirth, and ~se travailler~ can mean “to torment oneself.” A caution is in order: this tidy ~tripalium~ story, though repeated everywhere, is disputed, and some scholars instead derive the word from a Late Latin *~trabicula~ (“little beam,” a frame for holding animals). Either way the modern ~travailleur~ (“worker”) carries, etymologically, a faint echo of the rack.
en.traverser:
From Old French ~traverser~, from Late Latin ~traversare~, a worn-down form of ~transversare~, itself from the Latin adjective ~transversus~ (“lying across, turned crosswise”). ~transversus~ is the past participle of ~transvertere~, from ~trans~ (“across”) and ~vertere~ (“to turn”). ~vertere~ descends from PIE *~wert-~ (“to turn, to bend”), one of the most prolific roots in Indo-European. Cognate with Italian ~traversare~ and Spanish ~atravesar~.

The image is precise: to ~traverser~ is to move along a line “turned across” one's path, to cut from one side to the other. The same *~wert-~ root, through ~vertere~ and its participle ~versus~, fans out across French into ~tourner~, ~version~, ~vers~, ~envers~, ~divers~, and ~convertir~, and even into English ~-ward~, ~worth~, and ~versus~ — all of them carrying some ghost of turning. Closer to home, ~traverser~ has bred a small household of its own: the noun ~travers~ (“breadth, a turning aside”), the adverbial ~à travers~ and ~de travers~ (“askew”), the ~traversée~ (a sea crossing), and the ~traversin~, the long bolster that lies crosswise at the head of a French bed — a humble pillow still quietly “turned across.”
en.tromper:
Attested from the late 12th century, ~tromper~ is a denominal verb from the noun ~trompe~ (“horn, trumpet”), and its first meaning was simply “to play the horn,” jouer de la trompe. The noun ~trompe~ is of imitative or Germanic origin, echoing the instrument's blare. How a verb for trumpeting came to mean “to deceive” is one of the small mysteries of French etymology — and the favored explanation is delightfully concrete.

The usual account runs through the reflexive expression ~se tromper de quelqu'un~, literally “to play the horn at someone” — to blare derisively in a person's face, hence “to mock, to make sport of” them. From mockery the sense slid to misleading, then to outright deceiving, and finally the plain ~tromper~ settled into its modern meaning, “to deceive, to fool, to lead astray.” The reflexive ~se tromper~ took the gentler turn “to be mistaken, to make a mistake” — to be, as it were, fooled by oneself. ~Tromper~ also acquired the specific sense of marital infidelity, “to cheat on.” From the same ~trompe~ French keeps ~tromperie~ (“deceit”), ~trompeur~ (“deceiver”), ~trompette~ (“trumpet,” a little ~trompe~), and ~se détromper~ (“to undeceive oneself”).
en.trouver:
From Old French ~trover~, from Vulgar Latin *~tropāre~ (“to compose a melody, then to compose verse, then to invent, to find”), derived from Latin ~tropus~ (“a figure of speech, a trope”). The Latin word was borrowed from Greek ~trópos~ (“a turn, a manner, a turn of phrase”), from ~trépein~ (“to turn”), and ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *~trep-~ (“to turn”). So at its root, to find is to “turn” a phrase.

The path of meaning is unusually beautiful. *~tropāre~ first meant to compose tropes — the sung verses added to liturgical chant — and from “to compose, to invent a song” it widened to “to invent” and then to “to come upon, to find.” The poet who composed such songs was the ~trouvère~ in the north and the ~troubadour~ in the south of medieval France, both literally “finders, inventors.” The same stem gives French ~trouvaille~ (“a lucky find”), ~retrouver~ (“to find again, to meet again”), and ~controuver~ (“to fabricate, to invent falsely”). The learned ~trope~ and ~tropical~ (a zone where the sun “turns”) preserve the original Greek sense of turning, a quiet reminder that, etymologically, every discovery is a turn of phrase.
en.tuer:
From Old French ~tuer~ (“to strike, stun, extinguish”), from Late Latin ~tūtāre~ (“to put out a fire, to smother”), an altered form of Classical Latin ~tūtāri~ (“to protect, to guard against”), the frequentative of ~tuērī~ (“to watch over, protect”), a verb of uncertain deeper origin. The startling thread here is semantic: a verb that once meant “to protect” became the ordinary French word for “to kill.”

The bridge was fire. To “protect” against a blaze meant to ~tūtāre~ it — to smother it, put it out — and from “extinguishing a flame” the sense slid to “snuffing out” more generally: stunning a blow, then dealing a fatal one. By Old French, ~tuer~ already meant “to strike down,” and it eventually crowded out the inherited ~occire~ (from Latin ~occīdere~) as the everyday verb for killing. The protective ancestor ~tuērī~ left gentler descendants in French through its participle ~tūtus~: ~tuteur~ (“guardian, tutor”) and ~tutelle~ (“guardianship”) still mean “one who watches over.” So the same Latin root for watchful protection branches into both the guardian and the killer — a vivid reminder that to “put out” can mean to shield or to slay.
en.télécharger:
A modern French coinage, formed from the prefix ~télé-~ (“at a distance, far off”) and the verb ~charger~ (“to load”). The prefix ~télé-~ comes through scientific Latin from Ancient Greek ~τῆλε~ (~têle~, “far, far off”); ~charger~ descends from Late Latin ~carricare~ (“to load a wagon”), from ~carrus~ (“wheeled cart”), a word the Romans borrowed from Gaulish. So the verb is a hybrid: a Greek adverb of distance bolted onto a Celtic-Latin verb of loading. Cognate with English ~charge~ and ~cargo~, both also from ~carricare~.

Unlike most verbs here, ~télécharger~ has no Roman ancestor: it was minted in French in the early 1980s, in the first age of telematics and computer networks, to name the act of “loading at a distance” — moving a file between a remote machine and a local one. French, unusually, uses the one verb for both directions, distinguishing them as ~télécharger~ for downloading versus ~téléverser~ or ~télécharger vers le serveur~ for uploading. The ~télé-~ element ties it to a whole modern cohort — ~téléphone~, ~télévision~, ~télescope~, ~télégramme~ — while ~charger~ binds it to the older ~charge~, ~chargement~, ~décharger~, and ~surcharger~, the everyday vocabulary of loads and burdens.
en.témoigner:
A denominal verb from the noun ~témoin~ (“witness”), with the verb-forming suffix ~-er~. ~Témoin~ descends from Latin ~testimōnium~ (“testimony, evidence”), formed on ~testis~ (“witness”). The etymology of ~testis~ is striking: it goes back to a Proto-Indo-European compound *~tristh₂s~, “a third one standing by” — from *~tréyes~ (“three”) and the root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”). A witness, in this ancient image, is the impartial third party who stands beside two disputants. It is cognate with Italian ~testimone~ and Spanish ~testigo~, and with English ~testimony~ and ~testify~.

So to ~témoigner~ is to act as that standing-by third party — to attest, to bear witness. From the legal courtroom the sense broadened: a gift can ~témoigner~ gratitude, and symptoms ~témoignent~ a disease, the verb meaning “to show, to be evidence of.” The path from Latin ~testimōnium~ to French ~témoin~ ran through hard wear — the loss of the medial syllable and the softening of consonants typical of inherited words. From the same ~testis~ root come French ~témoignage~, ~testament~ (a document that “bears witness” to one's will), ~attester~, and ~protester~ (literally “to witness before, to declare publicly”).
en.unir:
From Latin ~unire~ (“to unite, to make one”), from ~unus~ (“one”), from Old Latin ~oinos~, from Proto-Italic *~oinos~, from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₁óynos~ (“one, single”). The same root, through different branches, surfaces as English ~one~ and ~an~, German ~ein~, Greek ~oios~ (“alone”), and Old Irish ~óen~ — a rare case where the kinship is plain to the eye across the whole family.

The meaning has stayed remarkably stable: to ~unir~ is to make things one, to join them into a single whole. Latin ~unus~ poured into French as the bare numeral ~un~, and around it gathered a dense family: ~union~, ~unique~ (“being only one of its kind”), ~unité~, ~unanime~ (“of one mind,” with ~animus~), ~uniforme~ (“of one form”), and the prefixed verbs ~réunir~ (“to bring together again”) and ~désunir~ (“to un-unite”). The English borrowings ~unite~, ~union~, and ~unique~ travel the same path. From “one” as a number grew “one” as togetherness, so that ~les États-Unis~ — the United States — are, etymologically, states made into a single one.
en.user:
From Vulgar Latin *~ūsāre~, a frequentative formed on ~ūsus~, the past participle of Classical Latin ~ūtī~ (“to make use of, employ, enjoy”) — a verb of uncertain, much-debated Indo-European origin, with no securely reconstructed root. Cognate with Italian ~usare~ and Spanish ~usar~, and — through the same ~ūtī~ — with English ~use~, ~utility~, and ~usual~.

The frequentative shape (“to use repeatedly”) left its mark on the meaning. Where ~utiliser~ (a later, learned coinage) simply means “to employ,” the older inherited ~user~ leans toward the consequence of repeated use: things ~usées~ by handling are worn out, and ~s'user~ is to wear away — so ~user~ de patience is to draw it down, and ~usure~ names both wear-and-tear and, by a separate route, the “using” of money for interest. Hence French keeps a telling pair: one ~utilise~ a tool but ~use~ a pair of shoes. The same Latin ~ūtī~ seeded ~usage~, ~usager~, ~usuel~, ~ustensile~, and — with ~ab-~ (“away, amiss”) — ~abuser~ and ~abus~, the “using up” turned into misuse.
en.utiliser:
A late and learned formation, dating from the end of the 18th century, derived from the adjective ~utile~ (“useful”) plus the verb-forming suffix ~-iser~ (“to make, to render”). Its model was Italian ~utilizzare~. The adjective ~utile~ itself comes from Latin ~ūtilis~ (“useful”), from the verb ~ūtī~ (“to use, to make use of”), whose past participle ~ūsus~ underlies a wide family of words. Cognate in formation with Italian ~utilizzare~ and Spanish ~utilizar~, and through the Latin verb with English ~use~ and ~utility~.

Because ~utiliser~ arrived so late, older French simply said ~user de~ or ~se servir de~; the newcomer's success was so complete that purists once denounced it as a needless neologism. Its ancestor ~ūtī~ — of uncertain deeper origin, with no secure PIE root — was nonetheless prolific in Latin. From the participle ~ūsus~ French draws ~user~, ~usage~, ~usine~ (originally a place put to “use”), ~ustensile~, and ~usure~ (“wear,” and also “usury”), while ~ūtilis~ yields ~utile~, ~utilité~, ~inutile~, and ~utilitaire~. A single notion of putting things to use thus spans tools, factories, wear, and worth.
en.valider:
From Latin ~validāre~ (“to make strong, to strengthen, to confirm”), built on the adjective ~validus~ (“strong, robust, effective”), which derives from the verb ~valēre~ (“to be strong, to be worth”) with the suffix ~-idus~. ~Valēre~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~walēō~ to the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂welh₁-~ (“to be strong, to rule”). Italian ~validare~ and Spanish ~validar~ share the same Latin source, and the root surfaces in English ~valid~ and ~value~, and even in the name ~Valerie~.

The earliest sense was bodily: a ~validus~ man was hale and vigorous, and to ~validāre~ was to fortify. From physical strength the meaning slid toward the strength of a thing's standing — its soundness, its force in law or logic. To ~valider~ today is to make something hold good: to confirm a ticket, ratify a decision, or certify a result as sound. The same ~valēre~ gave French the everyday ~valoir~ (“to be worth”), and the wider root spread into ~valeur~, ~valable~, ~valeureux~, ~prévaloir~, ~équivaloir~, and ~convalescence~, the last recalling the original sense of returning strength.
en.valoir:
From Latin ~valere~ (“to be strong, be well, be worth”), from Proto-Italic *~walēō~ and ultimately from the Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂welh₁-~ (“to be strong, to rule”). The kernel of meaning is bodily strength: the Latin farewell ~vale~ was a wish of “stay strong, keep well.” Through this root ~valoir~ is cognate with English ~wield~ (to handle with power) and, by borrowing from the same Latin stem, with English ~value~ and ~valour~.

The slide from “being strong” to “being worth” is the heart of the word: what is robust and sound is what has worth, and so ~valere~ moved from health to value. French kept both edges — se porter bien lingers in ~valétudinaire~, while the worth-sense dominates ~valoir~ today, as in ~cela vaut la peine~ (“that is worth the trouble”) and ~vaille que vaille~ (“come what may”). The verb is also notably irregular, with the present ~je vaux~ and the future ~je vaudrai~ preserving old stem shifts. From the same *~h₂welh₁-~ root flow a rich French family of strength and worth: ~valeur~, ~valable~, ~valide~, ~évaluer~, ~prévaloir~, ~équivalent~, and ~polyvalent~.
en.varier:
From Latin ~variare~ (“to change, to make different, to diversify”), a verb built on the adjective ~varius~ (“varied, spotted, of different colors”). ~varius~ is perhaps related to ~varus~ (“bent, knock-kneed”), with a notion of things turned different ways; its deeper Proto-Indo-European origin is uncertain and best left unstated. Through ~variare~ the French verb is cognate with Italian ~variare~, Spanish ~variar~, and English ~vary~ and ~various~.

The core idea was diversity of appearance — ~varius~ first described things speckled or many-colored, like a dappled animal — and ~variare~ meant to make such diversity, to alter. French ~varier~ kept both the transitive “to vary, to diversify” and the intransitive “to differ, to change.” A memorable kinsman is ~varicelle~ (“chickenpox”), so named for its spots, a faint diminutive echo of the “speckled” sense; the medical ~varice~ (“varicose vein”), however, belongs to a separate Latin word. From ~varius~ French keeps ~varié~, ~variété~, ~variation~, ~variable~, and ~variante~ — a whole vocabulary of change grown from an adjective that first meant simply “of more than one color.”
en.veiller:
From Latin ~vigilāre~ (“to stay awake, to keep watch”), itself derived from the classical Latin adjective ~vigil~ (“awake, alert, watchful”). The root traces to Proto-Indo-European *~weǵ-~ (“to be strong, lively, awake”). From this same root spring a remarkable set of English cognates: ~wake~, ~watch~, ~vigour~, and even ~vegetable~ (originally “lively, growing”). Italian ~vegliare~ and Spanish ~velar~ are the Romance siblings of ~veiller~.

The verb has always hovered around the idea of holding off sleep. To ~veiller~ is to stay up late, but also to sit up with someone — at a bedside, or beside the dead in a ~veillée funèbre~ (“wake”) — and, more broadly, to keep watch and look after. From the notion of watchfulness comes the construction ~veiller à~ (“to see to, to take care that”) and ~veiller sur~ (“to watch over, to protect”). The same Latin family yields a rich crop of French words: ~vigile~ (“the eve of a feast,” and now a “security guard”), ~vigilant~ and ~vigilance~, ~réveiller~ (“to wake up,” literally “to watch again”), ~surveiller~ (“to supervise, to watch over”), and the noun ~veille~ (“the day before; wakefulness”).
en.vendre:
From Latin ~vendere~ (“to sell, to trade, to praise”), a contraction of ~vēnum dare~ (“to give for sale”) — that is, ~vēnum~ (“sale, that which is for sale”) plus ~dare~ (“to give”). ~Vēnum~ goes back through Proto-Italic *~wesnom~ to PIE *~wes-~ (“to buy, to sell”), the same root behind Sanskrit ~vasna~ (“price”) and Ancient Greek ~ônos~ (“price”); ~dare~ descends from PIE *~deh₃-~ (“to give”). Cognate with Italian ~vendere~ and Spanish ~vender~.

The Romans built their word for selling out of two ideas welded together: a thing offered up, ~vēnum~, and the act of handing it over, ~dare~. Latin let the phrase fuse into one verb, and French inherited it whole, so that a sale is etymologically a giving. The buyer's side of the bargain left its own trace: from the same root, through ~vēnum~, French keeps ~vénal~ (“venal, for sale”), the adjective for whatever can be bought, a conscience included. The ~dare~ half seeded a vast family too — ~donner~, ~don~, ~date~, and the learned ~dation~ — while ~vendre~ itself produced ~vente~ (“sale”) and ~vendeur~ (“seller”).
en.venir:
From Old French ~venir~, from Latin ~venīre~ (“to come”), from Proto-Italic *~gʷenjō~, from PIE *~gʷem-~ (“to come, to step”). Cognate with Italian ~venire~, Spanish ~venir~, and Catalan ~venir~ or ~vindre~; through the same PIE root — but down the Germanic line, where *~gʷ-~ became *~kw-~ — come English ~come~, German ~kommen~, and Dutch ~komen~. French ~venir~ and English ~come~ are thus distant cousins despite sounding nothing alike.

The Latin verb's awkward conjugation made for a famously irregular French one: the future stem buckled and shifted, ~venirát~ giving ~venrat~, then ~vendra~, then the modern ~viendra~, an intrusive ~-d-~ wedging itself between ~n~ and ~r~. The root spread widely through Latin compounds, and French inherited a great cluster of them: ~devenir~, ~revenir~, ~prévenir~, ~convenir~, ~parvenir~, ~survenir~, and ~intervenir~. Their Latinate doublets supply ~avènement~, ~convention~, ~revenu~, and ~aventure~ — literally “what is about to come,” ~adventūra~. From a single verb of motion, French built much of its vocabulary of arrival, agreement, prevention, and chance.
en.verser:
From Old French ~verser~, from Late Latin *~versāre~, a variant of ~versārī~, the frequentative of Latin ~vertere~ (“to turn”). A frequentative names a repeated or intensive action, so ~versāre~ is “to keep turning, to turn over and over.” ~Vertere~ descends from the Proto-Indo-European root *~wer-~ (“to turn, to bend”). Cognate with Italian ~versare~ and Spanish ~verter~, and — through ~vertere~ — with English ~verse~ and ~convert~.

The thread tying the modern senses together is that founding idea of turning. To pour is to tip a vessel and turn its contents out; to capsize (~se verser~, said of a cart or a boat) is to be turned right over; and the financial sense, “to pay in, to deposit,” imagines money being turned out onto the counter — which is why a French salary is ~versé~ and a payment is a ~versement~. English ~verse~ preserves the oldest picture of all: the ploughman ~turning~ at the furrow's end to start the next line, the line of poetry being a “turning.” The same root *~wer-~ feeds a wide French family — ~version~, ~vers~, ~envers~, ~renverser~, ~déverser~, ~convertir~, and ~divers~ — all of them, somewhere, a turn.
en.viser:
From Latin ~vīsāre~, a frequentative of ~vīsere~ (“to look at attentively, to go to see”), itself an intensive of ~vidēre~ (“to see”). ~Vidēre~ descends from PIE *~weyd-~ (“to see, to know”), one of the most fertile roots in the family. Through its Germanic branch the same root gave English ~wit~ and ~wise~, and through Latin ~vīsiō~ it gave ~vision~; the Greek branch yielded ~idéa~. Cognate Romance reflexes include Italian ~visare~ and Spanish ~avisar~.

The progression of sense is tidy: from simply seeing, to looking hard at one thing, to fixing one's eye and weapon upon a target — hence the modern ~viser~ (“to aim at”) and its figurative “to aim for, to have designs on.” A separate, later ~viser~, “to stamp or endorse,” was coined in the seventeenth century from the noun ~visa~ (Latin ~vīsa~, “things seen, verified”), the official's mark that a document had been examined; the travel ~visa~ is the same word. From the rich *~weyd-~ stock French also keeps ~voir~, ~vue~, ~vision~, ~évident~, and ~revoir~, every one of them a way of seeing.
en.visiter:
From Latin ~visito~ (“to go to see, to visit often”), a frequentative of ~video~ (“to see”), the iterative form built on the participial stem ~vis-~. ~video~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~weyd-~ (“to see, to know”), one of the most productive roots in the family. Through that root ~visiter~ is cognate with Ancient Greek ~eîdon~ (“I saw”) and ~oîda~ (“I know”), with Sanskrit ~veda~ (“knowledge”), and with English ~wit~ and ~wise~, where the sense of seeing has shifted entirely to knowing.

The frequentative ~visito~ already implied not a single glance but repeated, attentive looking — the kind of seeing one does when inspecting, calling on, or watching over someone. That nuance survives: a doctor still ~visite~ patients, an inspector ~visite~ a site, and one ~visite~ a city by going round it thoroughly. The same root *~weyd-~ underlies a remarkable spread of French vocabulary: ~voir~ itself, ~vision~, ~visage~, ~visite~, ~visible~, ~évident~, ~revue~, and — through the knowing branch — words like ~idée~ and ~histoire~, both ultimately Greek relatives. Few roots have given French so many ways to talk about looking and learning.
en.vivre:
French ~vivre~ comes straight from Latin ~vīvere~ (“to live”), the present infinitive of ~vīvō~, inherited through Old French ~vivre~ with almost no change of shape. Behind the Latin lies Proto-Italic *~gʷīwō~ and, beyond it, Proto-Indo-European *~gʷíh₃weti~ (“to live, to be alive”). The cognates trace life across the whole family: Greek ~bíos~ (“life,” as in ~biology~), Sanskrit ~jīvati~ (“he lives”), Old Church Slavonic ~žiti~, and — strikingly — English ~quick~, whose old meaning was “alive,” preserved in the phrase “the quick and the dead.”

The sense has stayed remarkably stable: to be alive, and by extension to dwell, to subsist, to experience. French keeps both the bare existential sense and a worldlier one in ~savoir-vivre~ (“good manners,” literally knowing how to live). The root threw off a rich crop of French words: ~vie~ (“life”), ~vif~ (“lively, sharp”), ~vivace~ and ~vivant~, ~survivre~ (“to survive,” to out-live), ~revivre~, ~convive~ (a fellow liver, hence a dinner guest), and the borrowed cheer ~vive !~ (“long live!”). Through the Greek cousin ~bíos~ the same idea returns in ~biologie~, ~microbe~, and ~amphibie~ — so that the science of life and the act of living spring from one ancient verb.
en.voir:
From Old French ~veoir~, earlier ~vedeir~ and ~veder~ (c. 980), from Latin ~vidēre~ (“to see, to perceive, to witness”), the infinitive of ~videō~. The Latin verb goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~weyd-~ (“to see, to know”). Through that root ~voir~ is cognate with Italian ~vedere~ and Spanish ~ver~, and — by the same ancient stem — with English ~wit~ and ~wise~, German ~wissen~ (“to know”), and Greek ~oîda~ (“I know,” literally “I have seen”).

The pairing of seeing and knowing is no accident: across Indo-European languages, *~weyd-~ slid easily from “to perceive with the eye” to “to understand,” a metaphor French still uses when one says ~je vois~ to mean “I understand.” The intervocalic ~d~ of ~vidēre~ softened and then vanished as the word passed through Old French, leaving the smooth modern ~voir~. The root has seeded a large family in French: ~vue~ (“sight”), ~vision~, ~voyant~ (“seer, clairvoyant”), ~évident~ (“evident,” literally “seen out”), ~revoir~, ~prévoir~ (“to foresee”), and ~entrevoir~ (“to glimpse”). The learned doublet ~vidéo~, borrowed back from Latin in the twentieth century, restores the very ~d~ that ordinary speech had worn away centuries before.
en.voler:
From Latin ~volāre~ (“to fly”), attested in Old French as early as 881. The deeper origin is uncertain; De Vaan connects it to a Proto-Indo-European root *~gʷelH-~ (“to throw, to reach”), the flying sense perhaps developing from “to fling (the wings).” It is cognate with Italian ~volare~, Spanish ~volar~, and Portuguese ~voar~, all keeping the plain meaning “to fly.”

French is unusual in that this same verb means both “to fly” and “to steal” — and the bridge between them is falconry. A trained falcon ~vole~, taking its quarry on the wing; the bird seizes the prey in mid-flight. The transitive sense “to seize as a bird of prey does” specialized into “to take what is not yours,” and by the seventeenth century ~voler~ in the sense “to steal” had supplanted the older ~rober~ (“to rob by force,” itself from Frankish ~*raubōn~) and ~embler~ (“to steal by stealth”). So the thief and the flier share one verb, joined by the hawk. From ~volāre~ descend French ~vol~ (which means both “a flight” and “a theft”), ~voleur~ (“thief”), ~volée~, ~s'envoler~, and ~volaille~ (“poultry,” the flying farmyard).
en.voter:
A late arrival in French, ~voter~ was borrowed in the eighteenth century from English ~vote~. The English word goes back to Latin ~votum~ (“a vow, a solemn pledge, a prayer”), the noun from ~vovēre~ (“to vow, to promise solemnly”), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁wegʷʰ-~ (“to promise, to praise solemnly”). Through that root it is cognate with Greek ~eúkhomai~ (“to pray, to vow”) and Sanskrit ~vāghát~ (“one who makes a vow”). English may have built its verb directly on ~votum~ or taken it from Medieval Latin ~votare~.

The path from “vow” to “ballot” runs through religion and parliament: a ~votum~ was an offering pledged to a god, and to vote was first to declare one's solemn wish — the modern ballot is, etymologically, a vow of preference. Because French acquired the word ready-made from English, it sits a little apart from its own learned cousins drawn straight from ~votum~: ~vœu~ (the inherited descendant, “a wish, a vow”), ~votif~ (“votive”), and ~dévot~ and ~dévotion~, where the original sense of solemn pledging to the divine still rings clearly.
en.vouloir:
From Old French ~voloir~, from Vulgar Latin *~volēre~, a reshaping of the irregular Classical Latin ~velle~ (present ~volō~, “I want”), from Proto-Italic *~welō~, from PIE *~welh₁-~ (“to choose, to want”). Speakers smoothed the wildly irregular ~velle~ into a regular *~volēre~ built on the stem ~vol-~, already visible in the 8th-century attestation ~si voles~. Cognate with Italian ~volere~ and Romanian ~vrea~; through the same root, by the Germanic line, come English ~will~ and German ~wollen~. Spanish, tellingly, abandoned this verb for ~querer~.

The PIE root *~welh₁-~ meant “to choose” as much as “to want,” and that pairing of desire and selection runs through its descendants — to will something is to single it out. The French verb kept its irregularity to the end, with the curious imperative ~veuille~ and ~veuillez~ used in polite formulas (~veuillez agréer~). Modern French relatives include ~volonté~ (“will”), ~volontaire~ (“voluntary”), ~bienveillant~ and ~malveillant~ (“well-” and “ill-wishing”), and ~velléité~ — a faint, half-formed wish, from the Latin ~velleitās~. The same root even reaches ~voluptas~, “pleasure,” linking what one wants to what one enjoys.
en.vérifier:
From Medieval Latin ~verificare~ (“to make true, to prove true”), a compound of ~verus~ (“true”) and ~facere~ (“to make, to do”). Latin ~verus~ descends from Proto-Italic *~wēros~ and Proto-Indo-European *~weh₁ros~ (“true, trustworthy”). The same root gave German ~wahr~ (“true”) and English ~very~ (originally “true,” as in “the very man”), while the Italian and Spanish ~verificare~/~verificar~ are direct parallels.

The word is transparent once unpacked: to verify is, etymologically, “to make-true,” to establish that a thing is ~verus~. French inherited it as a learned term, keeping the bookish ~-ifier~ ending from ~facere~ found in a long series of verbs (~modifier~, ~clarifier~, ~justifier~). From the same ~verus~ comes a substantial family: ~vrai~ (the inherited, worn-down form of ~verus~), ~vérité~, ~véritable~, ~vraisemblable~, ~véridique~, and ~vérifiable~. Curiously, the everyday adverb ~voire~ (“indeed, even”) also descends from ~verus~ — a fossil of the Latin for “truly.” So ~vérifier~ and ~vrai~ are doublets of a kind, the learned and the popular reflexes of one Latin word for truth.
en.échanger:
A French compound: the prefix ~é-~ (from Latin ~ex-~, here intensive) joined to ~changer~ (“to change”). The base ~changer~ comes from Late Latin ~cambiāre~ (“to barter, to exchange”), a verb of Gaulish origin, from Proto-Celtic *~kambos~ (“crooked, bent”) and ultimately the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)kh₂emb-~ (“to bend”). Italian ~cambiare~ and Spanish ~cambiar~ are its Romance cousins, and English borrowed the same Gaulish word through Old French as ~change~ and ~exchange~.

The Celtic root meant “bent, crooked,” and the path of meaning ran from “to turn” to “to swap one thing for another” — a fitting image, since an exchange turns goods around between two hands. ~Cambiāre~ is one of the rare everyday Romance verbs that the Romans took from the Gauls rather than from their own Latin stock. The ~é-~ prefix in ~échanger~ underscores the mutual, two-way swap. From the same root French keeps a busy family — ~changer~, ~change~, ~changement~, ~échange~, ~rechange~, ~interchanger~, and the financial ~changeur~ — all turning, in their way, on the idea of exchange.
en.échapper:
From Old French ~escaper~, ~eschaper~ (attested around 1100), from Vulgar Latin *~excappare~, a verb built from the prefix ~ex-~ (“out of”) and Late Latin ~cappa~ (“cape, hooded cloak”). The image is vivid: to ~excappare~ was to slip out of one's cloak, leaving it in a pursuer's grasping hands. Cognate with Spanish and Portuguese ~escapar~, Catalan ~escapar~, Italian ~scappare~, and Romanian ~scăpa~, and with the English borrowing ~escape~.

The same ~cappa~ that lies behind ~échapper~ is one of Latin's most productive garments: it gave French ~chape~, ~chapeau~, ~chaperon~, and ~cape~, and through Italian even ~capot~. Old French ~escaper~ had a doublet ~eschaper~ that hardened into Modern French ~échapper~, while the un-prefixed family kept the cloak's literal sense. A curious relative survives in ~réchapper~ (“to come through, to survive”), and the noun ~échappatoire~ (“a way out, a loophole”) still carries the original gesture of wriggling free. English preserves the cloak-image twice over: ~escape~ from the French verb, and the rarer ~escapade~, a borrowing through Italian and Spanish, for a reckless break for freedom.
en.éclairer:
From Old French ~esclairier~, from Latin ~exclārāre~ (“to light up, make bright”), a compound of ~ex-~ (intensive) and the verb ~clārāre~, from the adjective ~clārus~ (“clear, bright, loud”). Latin ~clārus~ traces to the Proto-Indo-European root *~kelh₁-~ (“to call, to shout”) — and the link between sound and light is the surprising heart of this word, the spreading of a cry and the spreading of brightness imagined as one. From the same root come ~clamare~ and, in English, ~claim~, ~clear~, and ~declare~.

The earliest French attestations, around the year 980, are of weather: the sky ~esclaire~ when it lightens or flashes. From “to flash, to grow bright” the verb settled into its modern senses — to give light to a room, to illuminate a path, and figuratively to enlighten the mind, to ~éclairer~ a difficult question. The noun ~éclair~ (“flash of lightning,” and hence the glazed pastry, supposedly eaten in a flash) belongs to the same family, as do ~éclaircir~ (“to clarify”), ~clair~, ~clarté~, and ~déclarer~. The verb’s whole career runs along the double track of its root: light that shines and a voice that carries.
en.écouter:
From Middle and Old French ~escouter~ (also ~ascouter~), from Vulgar Latin *~escultāre~, a reshaping of Latin ~auscultāre~ (“to listen attentively, to give ear to”). The Latin verb is built on ~aus-~, the old stem of ~auris~ (“ear”), joined to a second element from the Proto-Indo-European root *~ḱlew-~ (“to hear”) — so the word is, transparently, “to ear-hear.” Cognates run across Romance: Italian ~ascoltare~, Spanish ~escuchar~, Romanian ~asculta~, and the Picard form ~acouter~, which kept the original initial vowel that standard French dropped.

The first vowel had a turbulent history: the classical ~au-~ weakened, the ~-l-~ vocalized, and ~escouter~ eventually smoothed into ~écouter~, the circumflex marking a vanished ~s~. A surprising side-branch reached English: in Old French an ~escoute~ was a person posted to listen and keep watch, and that noun, borrowed across the Channel, became English ~scout~. From the same Latin source French preserves the learned doublet ~ausculter~ — the physician's verb for listening to the chest through a stethoscope — beside the everyday ~écouter~. The root ~auris~ also surfaces in ~auriculaire~ (the little finger, small enough to fit the ear) and ~oreille~ itself, and the verb yields ~écouteur~ (earpiece, headphone) and ~écoute~ (a listening watch, or telephone tapping).
en.écrire:
From Middle French ~escrire~, from Old French ~escrivre~ (reshaped to ~escrire~ by analogy), from Latin ~scribere~ (“to write”), the infinitive of ~scribo~. ~scribere~ originally meant “to scratch, to incise,” and descends from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)kreybʰ-~ (“to scratch, to carve”), an extension of the root *~(s)ker-~ (“to cut”). Latin ~scribere~ is cognate with Ancient Greek ~skariphâsthai~ (“to scratch an outline”). Notably, it is not related to Greek ~gráphō~ (“to write”), which comes from a different root, *~gerbʰ-~, nor to English ~write~, from Proto-Germanic *~wrītaną~ — three unrelated words all born from the act of scratching marks on a surface.

The shift from scratching to writing reflects the ancient technology of the wax tablet and the stylus: to write was to scratch letters into wax or stone. That buried image survives in the modern French family, all built on the ~scrib-~ / ~script-~ stem: ~écrire~, ~écriture~, ~écrivain~, ~décrire~, ~inscrire~, ~prescrire~, ~souscrire~, ~manuscrit~, and ~scribe~. The doublet ~écrire~ (inherited, popular) beside ~scribe~ (learned, borrowed) shows the same Latin verb arriving in French by two different roads.
en.éditer:
From Latin ~ēdere~ (supine ~ēditum~, “to give out, put forth, publish”), a compound of ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~dare~ (“to give”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~deh₃-~ (“to give”) — the same root that gives Latin ~dōnum~ (“gift”) and ~dōs~ (“dowry”), and, in French, ~donner~ and ~don~. To ~ēdere~ was literally to “give out” a thing into the world; applied to a book or text, it meant to bring it forth before the public.

French took its bookish vocabulary first from the noun side — ~édition~ (from ~ēditiō~, “a bringing forth”) and ~éditeur~ — and the verb ~éditer~ arose later, around 1784, partly built back from those nouns. The same Latin family yielded ~édit~ (a royal edict, something “given out” by authority) and ~éditorial~. A second, homonymous ~éditer~ entered French in the twentieth century as a computing term, borrowed from English ~edit~; English had clipped that verb from ~editor~, itself ultimately the same ~ēdere~. So the two French ~éditer~ verbs — publishing a book and editing a file — are long-separated descendants of one Latin root meaning “to give out.”
en.élaborer:
Borrowed learnedly from Latin ~elaborare~ (“to work out, to take pains over, to produce by effort”), a compound of the prefix ~ex-~ (“out, thoroughly”) and the verb ~laborare~ (“to work, to toil”), from the noun ~labor~ (“work, toil, hardship”). The same ~labor~ underlies French ~labeur~ and ~labourer~ (“to plough”) — the farmer's toil — and was borrowed into English as ~labor~ / ~labour~. Romance kept the learned verb in Italian ~elaborare~ and Spanish ~elaborar~.

The force of the prefix ~ex-~ is “to the finish”: to ~elaborare~ was to work something out fully, to bring it to completion through effort. French ~élaborer~ preserves this exactly — one ~élaborer~ a plan, a theory, a strategy, or a document, building it up carefully piece by piece. Physiology borrowed it too: an organ is said to ~élaborer~ a secretion, working raw material into a finished product. The everyday English ~elaborate~ shares the ancestor but has drifted toward “add detail.” From ~labor~ the French family includes ~laboratoire~ — literally a “place for working” — along with ~collaborer~ (“to work with”) and ~laborieux~ (“laborious, painstaking”).
en.élever:
From Latin ~elevāre~ (“to lift up, to raise”), ~e-~ (a reduced form of ~ex-~, “out, up”) + ~levāre~ (“to lift, to make light”). ~Levāre~ is a verb formed on the adjective ~levis~ (“light, not heavy”), from PIE *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ (“light in weight”) — the same root, through Germanic, that gives English ~light~ (not heavy). To raise was, at root, to make something rise by relieving its weight. Through Old French ~lever~, ~levāre~ gave English ~lever~, ~levy~, and ~levee~; Italian ~elevare~ and Spanish ~elevar~ are direct cognates of ~élever~.

French split the simple verb ~lever~ (“to lift”) from the prefixed ~élever~, which specialized toward raising in degree, rank, and especially upbringing. One ~élève~ a wall or a price (raises it), one ~élève~ children (brings them up), and one ~élève~ livestock (rears it) — hence ~élevage~ (“animal husbandry”) and ~éleveur~ (“breeder”). The pupil one brings up educationally is, fittingly, an ~élève~ (“student”). The reflexive ~s'élever~ means “to rise” — a tower, a sum, or a protest. The ~levis~/~levāre~ family is enormous in French: ~lever~, ~levée~, ~élever~, ~élévation~, ~élève~, ~soulever~ (“to lift up”), ~enlever~ (“to take away, to lift off”), ~prélever~ (“to levy, to take a sample”), and ~léger~ (“light”) itself.
en.éliminer:
French ~éliminer~ is a learned borrowing of Latin ~eliminare~ (“to thrust out of doors, to expel”). Its anatomy is vivid: the prefix ~ex-~ (“out of”) plus ~limen~ (“threshold, doorsill”) — literally “to put across the threshold,” to show the door. ~Limen~ is probably bound up with Latin ~limes~ (“boundary, border-path”), the word behind ~limite~, and with ~limus~ (“sideways, oblique”); its ultimate Indo-European root is not securely established. Through ~eliminare~ and ~limen~, French is cousin to English ~eliminate~ and to the modern buzzword ~liminal~ (“on the threshold”).

The original image is almost domestic — sweeping something out over the doorsill, out of the house. From that literal expulsion ~éliminer~ broadened into the abstract “to remove, to do away with”: a candidate is ~éliminé~ from a contest, a hypothesis from an argument, an unknown from an equation. Physiology kept the bodily sense — the kidneys ~éliminent~ waste. Sport made it dramatic, in the ~éliminatoires~ (“qualifying rounds”) that knock teams out. The threshold itself, ~limen~, lurks beneath a small but pointed family: ~préliminaire~ (“preliminary,” what stands before the threshold), ~liminaire~ (an opening note, “on the doorsill”), and, through its sibling ~limes~, the whole field of ~limite~ and ~limiter~.
en.élire:
From Old French ~eslire~, ~eslire~, from Latin ~eligere~ (“to pick out, to choose”), from ~ex-~ (“out”) + ~legere~ (“to gather, to pick, to read”). ~legere~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~leǵ-~ (“to gather, to collect”), the same root behind Ancient Greek ~légō~ (“I gather, I say”) and the ~-logy~ of countless sciences. Romance cognates include Italian ~eleggere~ and Spanish ~elegir~.

The literal idea is to “gather out” the best from a heap — to single out by careful picking. Old French ~eslire~ covered ordinary choosing as well as formal election, and the past participle ~élu~ still means both “elected” and, in religious language, “chosen, one of the elect.” The plain sense “to choose” narrowed over time toward the political and ceremonial “to elect,” leaving everyday choosing to ~choisir~. A trace of the wider meaning lingers in the formal ~élire domicile~ (“to take up legal residence”). The fertile ~legere~ gave French a whole library of words: ~lire~, ~lecture~, ~élire~'s cousins ~sélectionner~ and ~collectionner~, plus ~légende~, ~collège~, and ~intelligent~ (“choosing between”).
en.éloigner:
From Middle French ~esloigner~, from Old French ~esloignier~ (“to move far off, to send away”), a derivative of ~loing~ (“far”) — modern ~loin~ — with the prefix ~es-~ (from Latin ~ex-~). The adverb ~loin~ descends from Latin ~longē~ (“far, at a distance”), the adverbial form of the adjective ~longus~ (“long, distant”), traced to Proto-Indo-European *~dlongʰos~ (“long”). The Italian ~lontano~ and Spanish ~lejos~ share the same Latin ancestry, while English ~long~ springs from a related branch of the same root.

The core idea is spatial: to ~éloigner~ something is to push it toward the ~loin~, the far horizon. Old French built the verb on the comparative notion of putting distance between things, and the ~es-~ prefix added the sense of separation or removal. Over time the verb gained figurative range — one can ~éloigner~ a suspicion, a danger, or a friend grown cold — so that distance became emotional and abstract as readily as physical. The reflexive ~s'éloigner~ (“to move away, to drift apart”) is now among its commonest uses. From the same Latin ~longus~ French inherited a wide family, including ~long~, ~longueur~, ~allonger~, ~prolonger~, and ~longtemps~, all turning on the measure of length or distance.
en.émettre:
From Latin ~emittere~ (“to send out, let go, produce”), a compound of the prefix ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~mittere~ (“to send, let go”). The base verb ~mittere~ is of debated origin, plausibly traced to a Proto-Italic *~mitō~ and a Proto-Indo-European root meaning “to exchange” or “let go.” It is the same Latin source behind Italian ~emettere~ and Spanish ~emitir~, and behind a swarm of English borrowings in ~-mit~.

The core idea is always a sending-forth, but the object sent has multiplied delightfully. One can ~émettre~ a sound, a light, a banknote, a stamp, a radio signal, an opinion, or a vote — each a thing released into the world. The everyday French verb ~mettre~ (“to put”) descends from the very same ~mittere~, its meaning having drifted from “send” to “place.” The Latin verb is one of the most fertile in French: through it come ~mission~, ~message~, ~admettre~, ~permettre~, ~promettre~, ~soumettre~, and ~remettre~, plus the nouns ~émission~ (a radio or TV broadcast) and ~émetteur~ (“transmitter”). A television program and a postage stamp thus share an ancestor whose single task was simply to let something go.
en.équiper:
From Old French ~esquiper~ (“to fit out a ship, to load aboard”), recorded around 1160 in the sense “to set sail,” itself borrowed through the Norman coast from Old Norse ~skipa~ (“to arrange, to put in order,” and especially “to fit out a vessel”), a verb built on ~skip~ (“ship”). The word thus entered French from the seafaring vocabulary of the Scandinavians who settled Normandy. It is cognate with English ~ship~ and ~skiff~, and with ~skipper~, the master of a ship, all reaching back to the same Germanic root.

The original meaning was strictly nautical: to provision and rig a boat before a voyage. Once the verb passed into Middle French as ~équiper~, it loosened its moorings and came to mean simply “to supply with what is needed,” whether a soldier, a kitchen, or a laboratory. The maritime memory survives only faintly today. From the same family French keeps ~équipage~ (“a ship's crew,” then any team or retinue), ~équipe~ (“a team”), and ~équipement~ (“gear, equipment”). It is a quiet irony that a word for outfitting a longship now describes a football ~équipe~ or the kit in a gym.
en.établir:
From Old French ~establir~ (“to set up, to found, to make firm”), from Latin ~stabilīre~ (“to make stand fast, to strengthen”), derived from the adjective ~stabilis~ (“steady, firm, stable”), which is built on ~stāre~ (“to stand”), from Proto-Italic *~stāō~ and ultimately the Proto-Indo-European root *~steh₂-~ (“to stand”). The same root yields Italian ~stabilire~ and Spanish ~establecer~, and, through other branches, English ~stand~, ~stay~, and ~stable~ itself. The Old French ~establir~ later lost its initial ~s~ before the ~t~ — a regular French change that also gave ~étable~, ~état~, and ~étage~ — and a circumflex now marks the vanished consonant in ~établir~.

The metaphor at the heart of the word is simple and physical: to ~établir~ something is to make it stand firmly, to give it a stable footing. From legal and political institutions “founded” in the Middle Ages, the sense broadened to settling people in a place, setting up a household or a business, and proving a fact beyond doubt — all variations on planting something so solidly it will not topple. The reflexive ~s'établir~ came to mean settling down in life or in trade. From the same *~steh₂-~ root the French language drew an immense family, including ~stable~, ~établissement~, ~état~, ~station~, ~constant~, and ~stade~.
en.étayer:
Denominal verb from the noun ~étai~ (“a prop, a stay, a shore”) plus the infinitive ending ~-er~; it appears in Old French as ~estaier~ (“to support a wall with props”), attested by 1213. The noun ~étai~ does not come from Latin but from Germanic: from Old French ~estache~ / ~estay~, from Frankish *~staka~ (“stake, post”). This belongs to the Germanic family of the “pointed post,” cognate with English ~stake~ and Dutch ~staak~. So a Latin-shaped French verb here rests, fittingly, on a Germanic timber.

The sense stayed remarkably concrete and then opened up. First a literal carpenter's act — to keep a leaning wall from falling with wooden ~étais~ — it broadened figuratively to “support, back up, bolster,” as one ~étaye~ an argument with evidence or a faltering currency with reserves. French keeps a small family around the timber image: the noun ~étai~ itself, the synonym ~étançon~ (“shore, prop”) with its verb ~étançonner~, and ~étayage~ / ~étaiement~ (“shoring, propping”). The same Frankish *~staka~ also surfaces in the place- and surname element ~estache~ and, through a parallel Germanic route, links French speakers — by way of the shared root — to the everyday English ~stake~.
en.étendre:
From Old French ~estendre~, from Latin ~extendere~ (“to stretch out, to spread”), from ~ex-~ (“out”) + ~tendere~ (“to stretch”). ~tendere~ descends from Proto-Indo-European *~ten-~ (“to stretch, to draw”), the root behind English ~tend~, ~tense~, and ~tent~, Ancient Greek ~teínō~ (“I stretch”), and Sanskrit ~tanóti~ (“stretches”). Romance cognates include Italian ~estendere~ and Spanish ~extender~.

The image is wonderfully concrete: to pull something taut and lay it open. From that single gesture French let the verb fan out across an unusually wide field — to ~étendre~ the limbs, to spread butter, to hang out the washing, to extend an empire, and, by a nice metaphor of thinning-by-spreading, to dilute or water down a wine. The reflexive ~s'étendre~ stretches the same way into time and talk: a plain may ~s'étendre~ to the horizon, and a speaker who ~s'étend sur un sujet~ dwells on it at length. From the same ~tendere~ French drew ~tendre~, ~attendre~, ~entendre~, ~prétendre~, ~tension~, and ~tente~.
en.étudier:
From Old French ~estudier~ (“to apply oneself, show zeal for, examine”), from Medieval Latin ~studiare~, itself from the classical noun ~studium~ (“zeal, eagerness, application”). ~Studium~ belongs to the verb ~studere~ (“to be eager, to apply oneself diligently”), traced to the Proto-Indo-European root *~(s)teu-~ (“to push, to thrust, to strike”). The semantic thread is “pressing forward, thrusting toward” — striving after a thing. Through Latin ~studium~ the word is cognate with English ~study~ and ~studio~ and with Italian ~studio~ and Spanish ~estudio~.

The surprise hidden in ~étudier~ is that learning was first imagined not as quiet contemplation but as eager pressure — a thrusting of the will toward an object of desire; ~studium~ in classical Latin could mean enthusiasm or even partisan passion before it narrowed to bookish application. Old French ~estudier~ lost its initial ~s~ before ~t~, the consonant leaving its trace as the accent of ~étudier~ — the very same shift that turned ~estoile~ into ~étoile~. From ~studium~ and ~studere~ French keeps a small but pointed family: ~étude~ (also the musical étude), ~étudiant~, ~studieux~, and the borrowed ~studio~.
en.évaluer:
From Old French ~esvaluer~, a verb formed with the prefix ~é-~ (from Latin ~ex-~, “out, thoroughly”) on the noun ~value~ (“worth, value”), the feminine past participle of ~valoir~. ~valoir~ comes from Latin ~valere~ (“to be strong, to be worth”), and through its Proto-Indo-European root *~h₂welh₁-~ (“to be strong, to rule”) the family is cognate with English ~wield~, German ~walten~ (“to govern”), and the borrowed ~valid~, ~valour~, and ~value~ itself.

The verb's logic is to draw out the worth of a thing — to fix how much it is worth — and from that concrete appraisal it broadened to “to assess, to estimate” in every sense, from valuing a property to evaluating a risk or a student's work. French keeps a dense cluster around the root: the noun ~valeur~ (“value, worth, valour”), the verb ~valoir~ (“to be worth”), ~évaluation~ (“assessment”), ~réévaluer~ (“to reassess, to revalue”), ~vaillant~ (“valiant,” an old present participle of ~valoir~), and ~valable~ (“valid”). The loanword ~valide~ and the medical ~convalescent~ (one “growing strong” again) reach back to the same ~valere~, all turning on the ancient idea of strength translated into worth.
en.éviter:
A learned borrowing from Latin ~ēvītāre~ (“to avoid, to shun”), ~ē-~ / ~ex-~ (“out of, away from”) + ~vītāre~ (“to avoid”). It enters French around 1324 with the sense “to guard oneself against,” later (1459) “to flee a thing.” The origin of ~vītāre~ itself is uncertain. One proposal traces it through an unattested *~vītus~ to the PIE root *~weyh₁-~ (“to chase, pursue”); others connect it to *~weyk-~ (“to yield, give way”). Because the detail is genuinely disputed, the safest statement is that ~vītāre~ is Latin of unclear deeper origin.

What is clear is the family it founded. From ~vītāre~ and its compound ~ēvītāre~ Latin built ~vītābilis~ (“avoidable”) and its negation ~inēvītābilis~, the source of French ~inévitable~ and English ~inevitable~ — literally “what cannot be avoided.” French keeps ~éviter~, ~évitable~, ~inévitable~, and the noun ~évitement~ (“avoidance,” as in a railway ~voie d'évitement~, a siding). A false friend to watch: Latin ~invītāre~ (“to invite”) looks like a relative but is a separate verb, so the modern French ~inviter~ and ~éviter~ only resemble each other by accident.
en.évoluer:
A learned sixteenth-century derivative built on the stem of ~évolution~, ultimately from Latin ~evolvere~ (“to unroll, unfold”), from ~ex-~ (“out”) and ~volvere~ (“to roll, turn”), from the Proto-Indo-European root *~wel-~ (“to turn, revolve”). Cognate with English ~evolve~ and ~revolve~, with Italian ~evolvere~, and — through *~wel-~ — with English ~walk~ and the German ~walzen~ (“to roll, waltz”).

The image of unrolling explains an unexpected first meaning: when ~évoluer~ first appears, in 1536, it is a military and naval term for troops or ships executing manoeuvres — wheeling and turning across the field, as a scroll unrolls. The biological sense of “transforming through stages” arrives only in 1845, and the broad modern sense of passing through a series of changes around 1864. So when something ~évolue~ in today's French — a situation, a dancer, a species — it is etymologically still “rolling outward.” From the same ~volvere~ French draws ~révolution~, ~révolte~, ~voluble~, ~volute~, and ~développer~, this last literally an “un-wrapping.” The senses of moving gracefully about a space and of biological evolution thus share one rolling root.
en.évoquer:
From Latin ~evocare~ (“to call out, to summon forth”), a compound of the prefix ~e-~ (a form of ~ex-~, “out, forth”) and ~vocare~ (“to call”), which derives from ~vox~ (“voice”). Both ~vocare~ and ~vox~ trace to the Proto-Indo-European root *~wekʷ-~ (“to speak, to call”); the noun ~vox~ reflects Proto-Italic *~wōks~ from PIE *~wṓkʷs~. Through this root ~évoquer~ is cognate with Sanskrit ~vā́c~ (“speech”) and Ancient Greek ~óps~ (“voice”), and shares its immediate ancestor with English ~evoke~, ~voice~, and ~vowel~.

The literal force of ~evocare~ was to call something out into the open. In Roman religion it named a solemn rite: before besieging a city, generals would perform an ~evocatio~, ceremonially summoning the enemy's protective gods out of their walls to switch sides. From this came the senses of summoning spirits and, far more gently, calling a memory or image to mind — the dominant modern meaning. The same root *~wekʷ-~ seeded a remarkable family of French verbs built on ~vocare~, each with its own prefix: ~invoquer~ (“to call upon”), ~provoquer~ (“to call forth, to provoke”), ~révoquer~ (“to call back, to revoke”), and ~convoquer~ (“to call together”). Alongside them stand ~voix~, ~vocal~, and ~vocation~ — a “calling” in the most literal sense.
en.être:
From Old French ~estre~, from Vulgar Latin *~essere~ (a regularization of Classical Latin ~esse~, “to be”), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁es-~ (“to be”). The same root yields Italian ~essere~, Spanish ~ser~, English ~is~ and ~am~, and German ~ist~. Yet ~être~ is famously suppletive: its paradigm is stitched together from more than one Latin verb, and several of its commonest forms descend not from ~esse~ at all but from ~stāre~ (“to stand”), from PIE *~steh₂-~.

The verb ~esse~ was already defective in Latin, lacking many forms, so speakers borrowed pieces from ~stāre~ to fill the gaps. The most striking survivor is the past participle ~été~: it comes not from ~esse~ but from ~status~, the participle of ~stāre~, so that the French “having been” is etymologically a “having stood.” Time wore that form down to almost nothing: of Latin ~status~, only the /t/ survives in ~été~. The initial ~s-~ was shed — it lingered for a while as the prosthetic é- of Old French ~esté~ — and the intervocalic ~-t-~ lenited away to silence. The imperfect (~étais~) likewise leans on ~stāre~, while ~suis~, ~es~, ~est~, and ~sont~ keep the old root of ~esse~. The future and conditional (~serai~, ~serais~) descend from yet another formation. From the ~stāre~ side French also inherited ~été~ the season, ~stable~, and ~station~; from ~esse~ come ~essence~ and, more distantly, ~présent~.

fr.abandonner:
Attesté vers 1100, ~abandonner~ est un verbe dénominal formé sur le nom de l'ancien français ~bandon~, tiré de la locution ~mettre à bandon~, « mettre à bandon ». ~Bandon~ signifiait « pouvoir, juridiction, discrétion », et dérive de ~ban~, terme d'origine francique (*~ban~ / *~bannan~, « proclamation, commandement, pouvoir de convoquer »). Mettre une chose ~à bandon~, c'était la laisser ouverte au pouvoir ou à la volonté de quiconque — à la disposition de tous. Le francique ~ban~ est apparenté à l'anglais ~ban~ et à l'allemand ~Bann~.

La logique du sens est d'une discrète élégance : si une chose est abandonnée à la discrétion de tous, son propriétaire l'a, de fait, lâchée — et ainsi « mettre à la disposition de tous » devint simplement « abandonner, délaisser ». Le réfléchi ~s'abandonner~ garda une trace plus douce de l'origine, « se livrer » à une émotion ou à une personne. L'anglais emprunta le verbe sous la forme ~abandon~, et l'ancien nom subsiste, fossilisé, dans la locution adverbiale française ~à l'abandon~, « laissé sans soin, en déshérence ». Du même francique ~ban~, le français tient aussi ~bannir~ (« bannir »), ~banal~ (à l'origine « relevant du ~ban~ du seigneur », commun à tous) et ~banlieue~.
fr.aborder:
Formation française, ~a-~ (« à, vers », du latin ~ad~) + ~border~ (« longer, venir le long du bord »), attestée dès 1306. ~border~ dérive de ~bord~ (« bord, côté ; planche, flanc d'un navire »), emprunt au francique *~bord~, du proto-germanique *~burdą~ (« planche »). Il est apparenté à l'anglais ~board~ et à l'allemand ~Bord~ — le mot même qui donne l'anglais nautique ~aboard~.

La scène maritime est la clef : ~aborder~ un navire, c'était amener son propre ~bord~ contre celui d'un autre — venir le long, pour accoster, monter à bord ou attaquer. De ce concret « se ranger contre le flanc » naquirent les sens figurés modernes : ~aborder~ une personne, c'est l'approcher et l'accoster, et ~aborder un sujet~, c'est le « traiter » ou l'entamer, comme si l'on dirigeait son vaisseau vers son rivage. Le germanique ~bord~ se révéla un riche emprunt, semant ~bord~, ~border~, ~bordure~, ~abordage~, ~abordable~, ~babord~ (« côté gauche ») et ~tribord~ (« côté droit »).
fr.aboutir:
À la différence de la plupart des verbes de cette application, ~aboutir~ n'a pas d'ancêtre latin unique : il fut bâti à l'intérieur même du français, attesté dès 1319. C'est un dérivé parasynthétique du nom ~bout~ (« extrémité, pointe »), encadré par le préfixe ~a-~ (du latin ~ad~, « à, vers ») et le suffixe verbal du deuxième groupe ~-ir~ — littéralement « mener à un bout / à une pointe ». Le nom ~bout~ est lui-même un déverbal de l'ancien français ~bouter~ (« pousser, frapper »), emprunté au francique *~bōtan~ (« pousser, battre »), de souche germanique *~bautaną~ (« battre »). Par cette racine germanique, il est cousin de l'anglais ~beat~ et du ~butt~ du bouc.

La métaphore au cœur d'~aboutir~ est celle d'un trajet qui atteint son ~bout~ lointain. Concrètement, une rue ou un tuyau peut ~aboutir~ quelque part — y déboucher physiquement ; abstraitement, un effort, une négociation ou un projet ~aboutit~ quand il parvient enfin à son résultat, d'où « réussir ». La tournure négative ~ne pas aboutir~ est la façon courante de dire qu'un plan « n'a rien donné ». Le même humble nom ~bout~ engendre une large famille : ~embout~ (« pointe, bec »), ~about~ (l'assemblage en bout du charpentier), ~rebouter~ (« remettre un os »), et le ~rebouteux~ qui le pratique — tous tournant, comme ~aboutir~, autour de l'idée d'un bout poussé contre quelque chose.
fr.abriter:
Verbe dénominal (attesté en 1489) tiré de ~abri~ (« abri »), qui remplaça un verbe plus ancien, ~abrier~ (« couvrir, abriter »). L'origine profonde est débattue : ~abrier~ est souvent rattaché au bas latin ~aprīcārī~ (« se chauffer, se tenir au soleil »), de ~aprīcus~ (« ensoleillé, exposé au soleil ») ; certains étymologistes proposent plutôt une source franque. Apparenté, selon l'hypothèse latine, à l'italien ~aprico~ (« ensoleillé »).

Si la dérivation latine tient, l'histoire du mot est charmante : un abri fut d'abord un endroit *ensoleillé*, un recoin chaud où l'on venait se réchauffer, et seulement plus tard l'idée générale d'une protection contre le vent, la pluie ou le danger. Le glissement de sens va de « chaleur » à « protection ». ~Abriter~ supplanta entièrement ~abrier~, réapparut comme terme de jardinage au XVIIIe siècle (protéger les plantes fragiles), puis s'élargit à son registre moderne — abriter une personne, loger une institution, voire au figuré abriter un secret. De ~abri~ le français conserve aussi ~abribus~ et le réfléchi ~s'abriter~, « se mettre à couvert ».
fr.accepter:
Du latin ~acceptare~ (« recevoir volontiers, prendre régulièrement »), fréquentatif de ~accipere~ (« recevoir, prendre à soi »), lui-même ~ad-~ (« vers ») plus ~capere~ (« prendre, saisir »). Le verbe ~capere~ remonte à la racine indo-européenne *~keh₂p-~ (« saisir, empoigner »), et par la branche germanique est un véritable apparenté de l'anglais ~have~ et ~heave~. L'italien ~accettare~ et l'espagnol ~aceptar~ en sont les frères romans ; l'anglais ~accept~ est un emprunt au même radical latin.

Parce qu'~acceptare~ est un fréquentatif, sa nuance intrinsèque est celle d'une prise répétée ou habituelle — non un seul geste mais une disposition durable à recevoir. C'est cette nuance de consentement que le français retint : accepter, c'est recevoir ce qui est offert, et par extension consentir ou tolérer. La base ~capere~ est l'une des racines les plus fécondes du français, affleurant dans ~capter~, ~capturer~, ~captif~, ~recevoir~ et ~concevoir~ (par la forme de composition ~-cipere~ de ~capere~), et dans ~capable~ et ~capacité~. Le latin ~acceptare~ donna aussi le nom ~acceptation~ et, dans le commerce, l'~acceptation~ d'un effet — la « prise » formelle d'une traite par le créancier.
fr.accompagner:
De l'ancien français ~acompaignier~, dérivé de ~compaing~ / ~compagnon~ avec le préfixe ~a-~ (du latin ~ad-~, « vers »). ~Compagnon~ vient du bas latin ~companio~ (accusatif ~companionem~), littéralement ~cum~ (« avec ») + ~panis~ (« pain ») — « celui avec qui l'on partage le pain ». Le mot latin fut sans doute un calque du gotique ~gahlaiba~ (~ga-~ « avec » + ~hlaifs~ « pain »), compagnon d'armes de la même gamelle. Apparenté à l'espagnol ~compañero~ et, par l'ancien français, à l'anglais ~company~.

Ainsi ~accompagner~ signifie, à la racine, aller aux côtés de quelqu'un comme un partageur de pain — tenir compagnie. De là le sens s'élargit : escorter, aller avec, ajouter à (« accompagner un plat d'un vin »), et en musique soutenir un soliste d'une partie secondaire. Le pain reste visible dans toute la famille : le même ~companio~ donna le cas sujet ancien français ~compain~, devenu le familier ~copain~, et le collectif ~compagnie~ (militaire, théâtrale ou commerciale). Peu de mots courants portent si clairement leur origine conviviale : accompagner quelqu'un, c'est étymologiquement rompre le pain avec lui.
fr.accomplir:
De l'ancien français ~complir~, ~acomplir~, du latin ~complēre~ (« remplir, achever, compléter ») renforcé par le préfixe ~ad-~ (« à, vers »). ~Complēre~ est lui-même ~com-~ (« ensemble, entièrement ») plus le verbe lié ~plēre~ (« remplir »), de l'indo-européen *~pleh₁-~ (« remplir »). Cette racine est extrêmement féconde : elle donne le latin ~plēnus~ (« plein »), et elle est apparentée au grec ~plērēs~ (« plein »), à l'anglais ~full~ et à l'anglais ~plenty~ (ce dernier emprunté à la même famille latine).

Le chemin du sens va du remplir à l'achever : *remplir* une tâche, c'est la *compléter*, et la compléter pleinement, c'est l'*accomplir* — le préfixe ~ad-~ ajoutant l'idée de mener jusqu'au bout. Le latin bâtit toute une série sur ~plēre~ en changeant de préfixe — ~implēre~ (« emplir »), ~explēre~ (« remplir entièrement »), ~replēre~ (« remplir de nouveau ») — et le français en hérita l'ensemble. De la même racine viennent les mots français ~plein~, ~complet~, ~remplir~, ~accomplissement~, ~complément~, ~plénitude~, et même ~plus~ (du latin ~plūs~, « davantage », même idée de plénitude).
fr.accorder:
Du latin vulgaire ou tardif ~accordare~ (« mettre d'accord »), composé du préfixe ~ad-~ (« vers ») et de ~cor~, génitif ~cordis~ (« cœur »), avec la désinence verbale ~-are~. Le latin ~cor~ vient de l'italique commun *~kord~, de la racine indo-européenne *~ḱerd-~ (« cœur »). Cette même racine donna le grec ~kardia~ (« cœur »), l'anglais ~heart~ et l'allemand ~Herz~, tandis que le composé latin réapparaît dans l'italien ~accordare~ et l'espagnol ~acordar~.

Littéralement, ~accorder~ signifie « porter cœur à cœur » — faire battre deux cœurs à l'unisson. De cette image découlent ses sens modernes : mettre des choses en accord (l'accord grammatical, ~l'accord du participe~), accorder un instrument pour que ses cordes s'harmonisent, et, par extension, « octroyer » une demande, comme si on l'accordait du fond du cœur. Le nom ~accord~ fut emprunté en anglais sous la forme ~accord~ et, en musique, est à l'origine de ~chord~. Le même cœur *~ḱerd-~ sous-tend une famille frappante de mots français : ~courage~ (de ~cor~, siège du sentiment), ~cordial~, ~concorde~ et ~discorde~, ~record~ (d'abord « rappeler au cœur, se souvenir »), et même ~croire~ (de ~credere~, « mettre son cœur en »).
fr.accroître:
Du moyen français ~accroistre~, de l'ancien français ~acroistre~ (« croître, augmenter »), du latin ~accrēscere~ (« grandir, augmenter »), composé du préfixe ~ad-~ (« vers ») et du verbe ~crēscere~ (« croître »). Le verbe latin remonte à la racine indo-européenne *~ker-~ (« croître, nourrir »). La même racine donna le latin ~creāre~ (« produire, créer ») et le nom divin ~Cerēs~, déesse des moissons. Par le latin, il est apparenté à l'anglais ~accrue~, ~crescent~, ~increase~, et même à ~croissant~, la pâtisserie en forme de lune croissante.

En latin, ~crēscere~ décrivait une croissance organique — les plantes, les enfants, la lune montante — et le préfixé ~accrēscere~ en précisa le sens : une croissance ajoutée à ce qui existait déjà. L'ancien français ~acroistre~ conserva cette nuance, et le moderne ~accroître~ signifie encore augmenter une quantité ou une intensité plutôt qu'agrandir simplement. Une curiosité de sa conjugaison est l'accent circonflexe hérité d'un ~s~ disparu (~accroistre~ → ~accroître~), ainsi que le participe passé ~accru~, qui le distingue de ~cru~. La racine *~ker-~ épanouit en français toute une famille : ~croître~ lui-même, ~croissance~, ~accroissement~, ~recrue~ et ~recrutement~ (une recrue étant une croissance nouvelle pour l'armée), ~concret~, ~créer~ et ~croissant~ — autant de rameaux d'une même idée de gonflement et de venue à l'être.
fr.accueillir:
De l'ancien français ~acoillir~, ~acueillir~ (« recevoir, rassembler, attaquer »), du latin vulgaire *~accolligere~, formé du préfixe ~ad-~ (« vers ») et du latin ~colligere~ (« recueillir, rassembler »). ~Colligere~ est lui-même ~con-~ (« ensemble ») + ~legere~ (« cueillir, ramasser, lire »), qui descend de l'indo-européen *~leǵ-~ (« recueillir, rassembler ») — la racine qui donna aussi le grec ~légein~ (« choisir, dire ») et le latin ~lēx~ (« loi »). Apparenté à l'italien ~accogliere~ et à l'espagnol ~acoger~.

Le verbe de base ~cueillir~ signifie « cueillir, ramasser » (fleurs, fruits) ; ajoutez le directionnel ~ad-~ et l'image devient « recueillir quelqu'un vers soi », le prendre chez soi — d'où « accueillir, recevoir ». L'ancien français ~acoillir~ pouvait même signifier « assaillir », autre façon d'aller vers quelqu'un, sens depuis longtemps disparu. Le ~accueillir~ moderne s'est fixé sur l'hospitalité et produit le chaleureux ~accueil~ (« accueil, réception »). De la même racine *~leǵ-~, le français garde ~cueillir~, ~recueillir~, ~collecte~, ~lire~ (« lire », par ~legere~), ~légende~ et ~élire~ (« élire, choisir »).
fr.accuser:
Du latin ~accūsāre~ (« appeler à rendre compte, porter une accusation contre »), formé sur ~ad-~ (« vers, à l'égard de ») et le nom ~causa~ (« cause, procès, affaire judiciaire ») — de sorte qu'~accuser~ quelqu'un, c'était littéralement porter une ~causa~ contre lui, l'appeler devant la justice. Le même ~causa~ est à la base d'~excuser~ (« disculper », de ~ex-~ + ~causa~) et, par l'ancien français, de l'anglais ~cause~ et ~accuse~. L'italien ~accusare~ et l'espagnol ~acusar~ en sont les cognats directs.

Le latin ~causa~ donne aussi le terme grammatical ~accusatif~, par une suite de méprises de traduction : les grammairiens grecs nommaient ce cas celui de la chose affectée, mais le latin ~accūsātīvus~ rattacha le grec à l'idée d'~accusation~. Les dérivés français regroupés autour de ~causa~ comprennent ~accusation~, ~accusateur~, ~accusatoire~ et ~inaccusable~, à côté de ~cause~, ~causer~ et ~à cause de~. L'origine de ~causa~ avant le latin reste obscure et mal reconstituée ; mieux vaut laisser sa préhistoire en suspens. Ce qui est sûr, c'est la saveur judiciaire à la racine d'~accuser~ : le mot ne signifia jamais simplement blâmer, mais porter une charge formelle — et en français il garde ce poids d'une convocation à répondre.
fr.accéder:
Du latin ~accedere~ (« s'approcher de »), formé du préfixe ~ad-~ (« vers », assimilé en ~ac-~ devant c) et du verbe ~cedere~ (« aller, se mouvoir, se retirer, céder »). ~Cedere~ vient de l'italique commun *~kezdō~ ; son origine profonde est discutée, peut-être une racine indo-européenne signifiant « s'en aller, céder ». Par les composés latins, le verbe engendra toute une série anglaise — ~cede~, ~access~, ~recede~, ~precede~ et ~succeed~.

Le sens latin était physique : ~accedere~, c'était marcher vers une chose, s'en approcher. Le français garda ce mouvement mais le tourna en atteinte et en entrée : ~accéder~ à un lieu, c'est y parvenir, et le substantif ~accès~ est la voie d'entrée. De là naquirent les sens figurés — ~accéder à~ une demande, c'est s'y rendre, la satisfaire ; ~accéder~ à un trône ou à une charge, c'est y parvenir. La racine ~cedere~ parcourt une vaste famille française : ~céder~, ~accès~, ~succéder~, ~procéder~, ~précéder~, ~excéder~, et les substantifs ~succès~ et ~procédure~. Accéder, c'est donc simplement avoir marché jusqu'à la porte.
fr.acheter:
De l'ancien français ~achater~ (« acheter »), du latin populaire *~accaptāre~, forme renforcée bâtie avec ~ad-~ (« vers ») sur le latin ~captāre~ (« chercher à saisir, pourchasser »). ~captāre~ est le fréquentatif de ~capere~ (« prendre, saisir, tenir »), de la racine indo-européenne *~kap-~ (« saisir »). Par cette racine, ~acheter~ est un lointain cousin de l'anglais ~have~ et ~heave~, et des empruntés ~capable~, ~captive~ et ~capture~. Le verbe latin propre de l'achat, ~emere~, ne survécut pas ici en français.

Le chemin du sens est un rétrécissement : de « chercher à prendre » à « obtenir », puis enfin à « obtenir contre de l'argent » — acheter. Cela reflète ce qui arriva à ~emere~ lui-même, qui signifiait « prendre » avant de se spécialiser en « acheter » au latin classique. La prononciation voyagea aussi : le médiéval ~achater~ s'adoucit en ~acheter~ moderne. De la même famille de ~capere~, le français garde une foule remarquable de mots — ~captif~, ~capable~, ~capter~, ~recevoir~ (par ~recipere~) et ~chasser~ (par *~captiāre~) — si bien qu'acheter, capturer et chasser sont tous, au plus profond, des manières de saisir.
fr.achever:
De l'ancien français ~achever~ (« mener à terme, achever »), dérivé formé avec le préfixe ~a-~ (du latin ~ad-~, « vers ») sur ~chief~ (« tête, bout »), l'aboutissement en ancien français du latin ~caput~ (« tête »). Le verbe appartient à la locution ~venir a chief~ (« venir à bout, mener à sa fin »). ~Caput~ descend de l'italique commun *~kaput~ et de l'indo-européen *~kaput~, et est un véritable apparenté de l'anglais ~head~ et de l'allemand ~Haupt~.

L'image centrale est celle d'atteindre la tête ou le sommet d'une chose : achever une tâche, c'est la mener à son point culminant, à son ~chief~, à son bout. De là naquit le sens plus sombre de « donner le coup de grâce », porter le coup mortel qui mène une bête blessée à son terme — le français dit encore ~achever une bête~. L'anglais emprunta le verbe sous la forme ~achieve~, où le sens glissa vers la réussite plutôt que la simple fin. Le même ~caput~ parcourt une remarquable famille française : ~chef~ (« tête, dirigeant, chef de cuisine »), le reflet moderne de ~chief~ ; ~chevet~ (« tête de lit ») ; ~achèvement~ ; ~capitaine~ ; ~chapitre~ ; et ~cap~ (« promontoire »), chacun conservant la tête comme la partie antérieure ou culminante.
fr.acquérir:
Du latin ~acquirere~ (latin populaire ~acquaerere~), composé de ~ad-~ (« à ») et de ~quaerere~ (« chercher, demander »). L'ancien français connaissait la forme héritée ~acquerre~ (attestée en 1148) ; le moderne ~acquérir~, refait sur le modèle d'autres verbes en ~-ir~, apparaît vers 1370. Apparenté à l'italien ~acquisire~ et à l'espagnol ~adquirir~, et — par ~quaerere~ — à l'anglais ~acquire~, ~quest~ et ~query~.

La force d'~ad-~ transforme un simple « chercher » en une recherche qui atteint son objet : ~acquérir~, c'est chercher jusqu'à l'obtention. Le supin ~acquisitum~ donna le nom ~acquisition~ et le participe passé ~acquis~, devenu aussi un nom désignant le savoir ou les avantages que l'on a gagnés. Le verbe-racine nu subsiste en français sous la forme archaïque ~quérir~ (« aller chercher »), encore sensible dans ~aller quérir~. Le même ~quaerere~ se ramifie largement : ~conquérir~ (« chercher à fond, remporter »), ~requérir~ (« réclamer »), ~enquérir~ et ~quérable~. Fait frappant, l'espagnol laissa ~quaerere~ glisser du chercher au vouloir puis à l'aimer — ~querer~ — tandis que le français garda le tranchant plus dur et plus possessif de l'original.
fr.activer:
Attesté dès le XVe siècle, ~activer~ est une formation française bâtie sur l'adjectif ~actif~ et le suffixe verbal ~-er~. ~Actif~ descend du latin ~āctīvus~ (« actif, pratique »), dérivé de ~āctus~, participe passé de ~agere~ (« pousser, faire, agir »), de l'indo-européen *~h₂eǵ-~ (« pousser, mener »). Par cette racine il est apparenté à l'anglais ~act~, ~agent~ et ~agile~, au grec ~ágein~ (« mener, pousser ») et — fait surprenant — à l'anglais ~ambassador~, au terme d'un long détour celto-latin.

~Activer~, c'est littéralement rendre ~actif~, mettre une chose en mouvement ou en hâter le cours. Dans le français courant il signifia longtemps surtout « se hâter, se dépêcher » — ~activer le pas~, ou le réflexif familier ~s'activer~, « s'affairer ». L'âge des machines et des écrans lui offrit ensuite une vie technique neuve : on ~active~ aujourd'hui un interrupteur, un compte, une fonction ou une alarme. Le prolifique ~agere~ nourrit, aux côtés d'~activer~, une vaste famille française : ~agir~, ~action~, ~acte~, ~agent~, ~agiter~, ~exiger~ et ~rédiger~ — tous une forme de faire ou de pousser.
fr.adapter:
Du latin ~adaptare~ (« ajuster à »), formé de ~ad-~ (« vers ») + ~aptare~ (« ajuster, préparer »), fréquentatif d'~apere~ (« attacher, fixer »). Le participe ~aptus~ (« ajusté, propre à ») donna le français ~apte~ et l'anglais ~apt~ ; toute la famille repose sur l'indo-européen *~h₂ep-~ (« joindre, ajuster »). Apparenté à l'italien ~adattare~ et à l'espagnol ~adaptar~, et source, par le français, de l'anglais ~adapt~.

L'image sous-jacente est mécanique — faire qu'une chose s'emboîte exactement dans une autre — et le verbe la conserva : ajuster une pièce à une machine, puis, par extension, ajuster quelqu'un aux circonstances, et dans les arts transformer un roman en film. Le réfléchi « s'adapter » fait de l'organisme, non de l'objet, celui qui s'ajuste.
